topics.title: Introduction to Failure Modes and Theories of Failure: Identification of Failure Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Failure Modes and Theories of Failure: Identification of Failure Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of engineering design and materials science, a **Failure Mode** is defined as the specific manner, mechanism, or physical process by which a component, structure, or material ceases to perform its intended function. The term **"failure"** does not exclusively imply catastrophic fracture; it encompasses any deviation from the design specification, including unacceptable deformation, loss of stiffness, wear, corrosion, fracture, or fatigue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Failure Analysis is the systematic process of determining the operating conditions that caused a failure, identifying the failure mode (the *how*), and the failure mechanism (the *why* at a microstructural level).

The discipline of failure analysis is built upon three foundational pillars:

1. **Failure Mode:** The observable *manner* of failure (e.g., ductile fracture, brittle fracture, fatigue, creep, wear).
2. **Failure Mechanism:** The underlying *microstructural* or *physical* process responsible (e.g., microvoid coalescence, cleavage, pitting).
3. **Failure Cause:** The design, material, manufacturing, or service-related *root reason* (e.g., stress concentration, wrong material selection, overload).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Doctor vs. Symptom vs. Disease" Framework

Imagine a patient visiting a doctor with a broken leg.

- **Failure Mode** corresponds to the **observable symptom**: the leg is broken, swollen, and non-functional. This is the *manner* in which the body has failed.
- **Failure Mechanism** corresponds to the **medical diagnosis**: a transverse fracture of the tibia with bone fragment displacement. This is the *biological process* that caused the symptom.
- **Failure Cause** corresponds to the **root cause inquiry**: a high-speed motorcycle accident, or a pre-existing condition like osteoporosis that made the bone weak.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Insight:** A single symptom (failure mode) can have multiple causes. For example, a broken bone could be due to an accident (overload) or osteoporosis (material deficiency). Likewise, a fractured shaft could be due to cyclic fatigue, overload, or material embrittlement. KTU examiners love this distinction — always identify all three.

### 1.3 Classification Overview of Failure Modes

Engineering failures are broadly classified into the following primary categories, each of which will be elaborated in subsequent modules:

| S.No. | Failure Mode Category | Core Physical Manifestation |
|-------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Ductile Fracture** | Significant plastic deformation prior to separation |
| 2 | **Brittle Fracture** | Sudden separation with negligible plastic deformation |
| 3 | **Fatigue Failure** | Progressive damage under cyclic/ fluctuating stresses |
| 4 | **Creep Failure** | Time-dependent deformation at elevated temperatures |
| 5 | **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** | Combined action of tensile stress and corrosive environment |
| 6 | **Wear Failure** | Material loss due to relative motion (adhesive, abrasive, erosive) |
| 7 | **Buckling/Instability** | Geometric instability under compressive loading |
| 8 | **Thermal Failure** | Distortion or damage from thermal cycling or shock |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module 1 mandates explicit identification of these modes along with their macroscopic and microscopic signatures, because subsequent design calculations (using theories of failure) depend entirely on the *correct identification* of the dominant failure mode.

### 1.4 The Engineering Significance

Why does identifying the failure mode matter? Because **the design countermeasure is mode-specific**:

- A ductile fracture is mitigated by limiting the equivalent stress (von Mises / Tresca criteria).
- A fatigue failure requires a **fatigue strength** approach (S-N curves, Goodman, Gerber, Soderberg).
- A buckling failure requires an **Euler column stability** analysis — stress alone is insufficient.
- A creep failure demands a **time-temperature-parameter** (Larson-Miller, Manson-Haferd) framework.

Using a ductile-failure criterion (like Maximum Shear Stress) to design against a fatigue problem leads to unsafe, oversized, or economically inefficient designs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of Failure Identification

Failure mode identification follows a **three-tier hierarchical logic** that mirrors the autopsy procedure in forensic engineering:

**Tier 1 — Service History Reconstruction:**
- Operating loads (static, dynamic, cyclic, impact)
- Environmental conditions (temperature, humidity, corrosive media)
- Service duration and loading history
- Maintenance records

**Tier 2 — Macroscopic Examination (Visual & Dimensional):**
- Identification of fracture surface location, orientation, and topography
- Measurement of permanent deformation, wear scars, crack patterns
- Detection of discoloration, oxidation, corrosion products
- Observation of plastic flow, necking, or shear lips

**Tier 3 — Microscopic and Chemical Examination:**
- Fractography using SEM (Scanning Electron Microscopy)
- Microstructural analysis (grain size, phase distribution)
- Hardness mapping and chemical composition verification
- Identification of striations, dimples, cleavage facets, or intergranular features

### 2.2 The Five Primary Theories of Failure (Preview of Design Equations)

The **theories of failure** provide mathematical criteria to predict the onset of failure under combined stress states. Each theory is essentially a *failure-mode-specific* design hypothesis.

#### 2.2.1 Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine's Theory)
Applicable to **brittle materials** (cast iron, concrete, ceramics).
- Hypothesis: Failure occurs when the maximum principal stress reaches the ultimate tensile strength.
- **Why it works for brittle materials:** Brittle materials fail by cleavage along planes of maximum tensile stress; they do not yield or shear significantly.
- **Why it fails for ductile materials:** It ignores shear yielding, which is the dominant ductile failure mechanism.

#### 2.2.2 Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca Criterion)
Applicable to **ductile materials**.
- Hypothesis: Failure occurs when the maximum shear stress in the component equals the maximum shear stress at yield in a simple tension test.
- **Why it works:** It correctly predicts yielding in ductile materials where shear-driven slip dominates.

#### 2.2.3 Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises Criterion)
The most accurate and widely used criterion for **ductile materials**.
- Hypothesis: Failure occurs when the distortion energy per unit volume equals that at the yield point in a simple tension test.
- **Why it is superior:** It accounts for the complete three-dimensional stress state and is thermodynamically consistent with observed yielding.

#### 2.2.4 Coulomb-Mohr Theory (Modified Mohr)
Applicable to **brittle materials** with different tensile and compressive strengths (e.g., concrete, cast iron, rock).
- Hypothesis: Combines a modified normal stress limit with a shear stress limit using a Mohr circle envelope.

#### 2.2.5 Modified Mohr Theory
A linearized Coulomb-Mohr envelope that is mathematically tractable for design handbooks.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Isolation:** All pipe symbols inside table cells use `\vert` to prevent markdown table syntax corruption.

**Table 1: Governing Equations for Theories of Failure**

| S.No. | Theory Name | Governing Equation (in terms of principal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$) | Material Applicability | Failure Stress Limit |
|-------|-------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Max Normal Stress (Rankine) | $\sigma_1 \leq S_{ut}$ (if $\sigma_1 > 0$); $\vert \sigma_3 \vert \leq S_{uc}$ (if $\sigma_3 < 0$) | Brittle | $S_{ut}$ (tensile) or $S_{uc}$ (compressive) |
| 2 | Max Shear Stress (Tresca) | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \leq S_{y}$ (with $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$) | Ductile | $S_y$ (yield strength) |
| 3 | Distortion Energy (von Mises) | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2} \leq S_y$ | Ductile | $S_y$ (yield strength) |
| 4 | Coulomb-Mohr (Modified) | $\dfrac{\sigma_1}{S_{ut}} - \dfrac{\sigma_3}{S_{uc}} = 1$ (with $\sigma_1 \geq 0 \geq \sigma_3$) | Brittle (asymmetric) | $S_{ut}, S_{uc}$ |
| 5 | Modified Mohr (Linearized) | $\dfrac{(S_{uc} - S_{ut})\sigma_1}{S_{ut}\cdot S_{uc}} - \dfrac{\sigma_3}{S_{uc}} = 1$ | Brittle (asymmetric) | $S_{ut}, S_{uc}$ |

**Table 2: Plane Stress Specializations (for $\sigma_3 = 0$)**

| S.No. | Theory | Plane-Stress Design Equation |
|-------|--------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | Rankine | $\sigma_A = \max(\vert \sigma_x \vert, \vert \sigma_y \vert, \vert \sigma_x - \sigma_y \vert)$ (Wait — this is for max in-plane shear; corrected below) |
| 1 (corrected) | Rankine | $\sigma_1 \leq S_{ut}$ where $\sigma_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ |
| 2 | Tresca | $\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \leq \dfrac{S_y}{2}$ |
| 3 | von Mises | $\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2 \leq S_y^2$ |

**Table 3: Standard Symbol Conventions (KTU-Standard)**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical SI Unit |
|--------|---------|------------------|
| $S_{ut}$ | Ultimate tensile strength | MPa |
| $S_{uc}$ | Ultimate compressive strength | MPa |
| $S_y$ | Yield strength | MPa |
| $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ | Principal stresses (max to min) | MPa |
| $\sigma_x, \sigma_y$ | Normal stresses on orthogonal planes | MPa |
| $\tau_{xy}$ | In-plane shear stress | MPa |
| $n$ | Factor of safety (dimensionless) | — |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practical engineering practice, the identification of failure mode and the selection of the corresponding theory of failure govern critical design decisions across industries:

- **Pressure Vessel Design (ASME BPVC):** Uses von Mises criterion for ductile steels and Maximum Normal Stress theory for brittle cast iron components.
- **Bridge and Structural Steel Design:** Uses von Mises (distortion energy) criterion to ensure yielding safety of ductile structural members.
- **Concrete and Masonry Design:** Uses Coulomb-Mohr or Modified Mohr to account for vastly different tensile and compressive strengths.
- **Rotating Machinery (Shafts, Turbines):** Combines von Mises for static safety with fatigue criteria (Goodman, Soderberg) for cyclic loading.
- **Aerospace and Defense:** Employs both distortion energy and fatigue criteria rigorously because cyclic loading dominates service life.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises Criterion)

This derivation traces the thermodynamic origin of the most widely used ductile failure theory, which is **a high-priority KTU examination topic**.

**Step 1: Decompose Strain Energy Density**

The total strain energy density $u$ in a linear elastic material under principal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ is:

$$
u = \frac{1}{2E}\left(\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2\right) - \frac{\nu}{E}\left(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1\right) + \frac{3(1-2\nu)}{2E}\cdot\frac{(\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3)^2}{3}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the standard linear-elastic strain energy for triaxial stress, with the last term representing the **volumetric (dilatational)** contribution and the first two terms representing the **distortional (shape-changing)** contribution.

**Step 2: Define Hydrostatic and Deviatoric Stress Tensors**

The mean (hydrostatic) stress is:

$$
\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}
$$

The deviatoric (shape-changing) stresses are:

$$
\sigma_1' = \sigma_1 - \sigma_m, \quad \sigma_2' = \sigma_2 - \sigma_m, \quad \sigma_3' = \sigma_3 - \sigma_m
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Hydrostatic stress changes *volume* without changing *shape*; the deviatoric component changes *shape* without changing volume. Yielding in ductile metals is a *shape-changing* phenomenon.

**Step 3: Distortional Strain Energy Density**

The distortional energy density is:

$$
u_d = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]
$$

**Step 4: Calibrate Using Uniaxial Tension Test**

In a simple tension test, $\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$ at the onset of yielding. Substituting:

$$
u_{d,y} = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_y - 0)^2 + (0 - 0)^2 + (0 - \sigma_y)^2\right] = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}(2\sigma_y^2) = \frac{1+\nu}{3E}\sigma_y^2
$$

**Step 5: Equate Distortional Energies for Failure**

The failure hypothesis states: $u_d = u_{d,y}$

$$
\frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right] = \frac{1+\nu}{3E}\sigma_y^2
$$

Cancelling the common factor $\frac{1+\nu}{6E}$ from both sides:

$$
(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2\sigma_y^2
$$

Rearranging into the canonical von Mises form:

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2} = \sigma_y
$$

**Step 6: Equivalent von Mises Stress**

The **von Mises equivalent stress** is therefore defined as:

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}
$$

The design inequality is $\sigma_{VM} \leq S_y$ (or with a factor of safety: $\sigma_{VM} \leq S_y / n$).

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca) in Plane Stress

**Step 1: Principal Stresses in Plane Stress**

For plane stress with $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$, the principal stresses are:

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Tresca Hypothesis**

In plane stress, $\sigma_3 = 0$. Ordering the principal stresses $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$ (with possible sign conventions), the maximum shear stress is:

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}
$$

**Step 3: Calibrate Using Uniaxial Tension**

In a uniaxial tension test at yield: $\sigma_1 = S_y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$, so $\tau_{max,y} = S_y / 2$.

**Step 4: Failure Criterion**

Equating: $\tau_{max} = \tau_{max,y}$ gives:

$$
\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \frac{S_y}{2}
$$

This is the plane-stress Tresca design equation.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Logic for Theory Selection

The following decision matrix guides the KTU student to select the appropriate theory of failure based on the *identified failure mode*:

| Identified Failure Mode | Material Behavior | Recommended Theory | Governing Equation Form |
|--------------------------|--------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Yielding (static, ductile) | Ductile, symmetric $S_{ut} \approx S_{uc}$ | von Mises (Distortion Energy) | $\sigma_{VM} \leq S_y / n$ |
| Yielding (static, ductile) | Ductile, conservative design | Tresca (Max Shear) | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \leq S_y / n$ |
| Brittle fracture (tensile) | Brittle, $S_{ut} \neq S_{uc}$ | Rankine (Max Normal Stress) | $\sigma_1 \leq S_{ut} / n$ |
| Brittle fracture (asymmetric) | Brittle, $S_{ut} \ll S_{uc}$ | Coulomb-Mohr | $\dfrac{\sigma_1}{S_{ut}} - \dfrac{\sigma_3}{S_{uc}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ |
| Buckling (slender column) | Elastic instability | Euler column theory (NOT a stress theory) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}$ |
| Fatigue (cyclic) | Ductile, $S_e < S_y$ | Goodman / Soderberg / Gerber | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ |
| Creep (elevated $T$) | Time-dependent | Larson-Miller parameter | $T(C + \log t) = $ const. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Notice that buckling and creep are *not* in the classical "theories of failure" table because they involve *instability* and *time-dependence* respectively, not simple stress exceeding strength. KTU Module 1 establishes the *classical* static theories; Modules 2-4 extend to dynamic, thermal, and environmental modes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Failure Identification and Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Service Failure Event] --> B[Service History Reconstruction]
    B --> C[Macroscopic Examination]
    C --> D[Microscopic Examination]
    D --> E[Failure Mode Identification]
    E --> F{Is it Ductile?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Apply von Mises / Tresca]
    F -->|No| H{Is it Brittle?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Apply Rankine / Coulomb-Mohr]
    H -->|No| J{Is it Cyclic?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Apply Fatigue Criteria]
    J -->|No| L{Is it Time-Temperature?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Apply Creep Models]
    L -->|No| N[Re-examine using advanced methods]
    G --> O[Design Recommendation]
    I --> O
    K --> O
    M --> O
    O --> P[Redesign / Material Change / Process Control]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Classification of Failure Modes and Their Signatures

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Failure Modes] --> B[Ductile Fracture]
    A --> C[Brittle Fracture]
    A --> D[Fatigue]
    A --> E[Creep]
    A --> F[Wear]
    A --> G[Corrosion]
    A --> H[Buckling]
    B --> B1[Microvoid Coalescence - Dimples]
    B --> B2[Necking + Cup-and-Cone]
    C --> C1[Cleavage - River Patterns]
    C --> C2[Little to No Plasticity]
    D --> D1[Beach Marks + Striations]
    D --> D2[Sub-Critical Crack Growth]
    E --> E1[Grain Boundary Cavitation]
    E --> E2[Rupture at Elevated T]
    F --> F1[Abrasive, Adhesive, Erosive]
    G --> G1[SCC, Pitting, Intergranular]
    H --> H1[Euler Critical Load]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Theories of Failure Mapping to Stress State

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Multiaxial Stress State sigma1, sigma2, sigma3] --> B{Material Type}
    B -->|Ductile Symmetric| C[von Mises - Ellipsoidal Surface]
    B -->|Ductile Conservative| D[Tresca - Hexagonal Surface]
    B -->|Brittle Asymmetric| E[Coulomb-Mohr - Asymmetric Cone]
    B -->|Brittle Simple| F[Rankine - Cubic Surface]
    C --> G[Failure Surface in Principal Stress Space]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Safe Design Region inside Surface]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Failure Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Failure Event] --> B[Surface Evidence Collection]
    B --> B1[Visual Inspection]
    B --> B2[Dimensional Measurement]
    B --> B3[NDE - Dye Penetrant, MPI, UT]
    B1 --> C[Fractography]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Macrofractography]
    C --> C2[Microfractography - SEM]
    C1 --> D[Identify Mode]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Map to Theory of Failure]
    E --> F[Select Design Countermeasure]
    F --> G[Validate with Factor of Safety]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that identifying the failure mode is a *prerequisite* to selecting the correct theory of failure. The block diagrams above visually encode this workflow: a brittle failure mode MUST NOT be analyzed using a ductile criterion, and vice versa.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
> **Differentiate between *failure mode*, *failure mechanism*, and *failure cause*. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**
A **failure mode** is the *observable manner* in which a component ceases to function (e.g., a fractured shaft, a worn gear tooth, a buckled column). A **failure mechanism** is the underlying *microstructural or physical process* that produces the mode (e.g., microvoid coalescence in ductile fracture, cleavage in brittle fracture, pitting in corrosion). A **failure cause** is the *root reason* (design, material, manufacturing, or service) that initiated the mechanism (e.g., stress concentration, wrong material grade, overload, inadequate lubrication).

*Example:* A crankshaft failure exhibits the mode of *torsional fatigue fracture*, the mechanism of *transgranular crack propagation with striations*, and the cause of *misalignment during installation*.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for each definition + 1 mark for example linkage)*

---

> **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
> **State the Maximum Normal Stress Theory and Maximum Shear Stress Theory. Indicate the type of material for which each is applicable.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine's Theory)** states that failure occurs when the maximum principal tensile stress reaches the ultimate tensile strength, or when the maximum principal compressive stress reaches the ultimate compressive strength. It is applicable to **brittle materials** such as cast iron, concrete, and ceramics.

The **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca Criterion)** states that failure occurs when the maximum shear stress in the complex stress state equals the maximum shear stress at yielding in a simple tension test, i.e., $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = S_y$. It is applicable to **ductile materials** that yield predominantly by shear.

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks for each theory statement + material identification)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Set A]
>
> **(a)** Explain the Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises Criterion) of failure. Derive the expression for the von Mises equivalent stress in terms of the principal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$. State clearly the material applicability and the failure hypothesis. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A ductile steel component is subjected to a biaxial stress state with $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = -40$ MPa, and $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa. The yield strength of the material is $S_y = 250$ MPa. Using **both** the Maximum Shear Stress Theory and the Distortion Energy Theory, determine the factor of safety. Comment on which theory is more conservative. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1: Hypothesis Statement**
The Distortion Energy Theory postulates that failure (yielding in ductile materials) occurs when the **distortion (shape-changing) strain energy per unit volume** in a complex stress state equals the distortion strain energy per unit volume at yielding in a simple tension test.

**Step 2: Total Strain Energy Density**
The total elastic strain energy density in a triaxial principal stress state is:

$$
u = \frac{1}{2E}\left(\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2\right) - \frac{\nu}{E}\left(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1\right)
$$

**Step 3: Hydrostatic-Deviatoric Decomposition**
Define mean stress $\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3}{3}$ and deviatoric stresses $\sigma_i' = \sigma_i - \sigma_m$.

**Step 4: Distortional Energy**
After algebraic manipulation, the distortional energy density is:

$$
u_d = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]
$$

**Step 5: Uniaxial Calibration**
At yielding in simple tension: $\sigma_1 = S_y, \sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$, giving $u_{d,y} = \frac{1+\nu}{3E}S_y^2$.

**Step 6: Equate and Simplify**
Setting $u_d = u_{d,y}$ and simplifying:

$$
(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2S_y^2
$$

**Step 7: Final von Mises Equation**

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2} \leq S_y
$$

**Material Applicability:** Ductile, isotropic materials with $S_{ut} \approx S_{uc}$ (most steels, aluminium alloys, copper alloys).

*(7 marks: 1 mark for hypothesis, 2 marks for derivation steps 1-4, 2 marks for calibration and simplification, 1 mark for final equation, 1 mark for material applicability)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Compute Principal Stresses**
Using $\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x+\sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$:

$$
\frac{\sigma_x+\sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + (-40)}{2} = 20 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{80-(-40)}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{60^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 900} = \sqrt{4500} \approx 67.08 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_1 = 20 + 67.08 = 87.08 \text{ MPa}
$$
$$
\sigma_2 = 20 - 67.08 = -47.08 \text{ MPa}
$$
$$
\sigma_3 = 0 \text{ (plane stress)}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)**

Maximum shear stress:

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{87.08 - (-47.08)}{2} = \frac{134.16}{2} = 67.08 \text{ MPa}
$$

Allowable shear at yield: $S_y/2 = 250/2 = 125$ MPa.

Factor of safety:

$$
n_{Tresca} = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3} = \frac{250}{134.16} \approx 1.864
$$

**Step 3: Apply Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises)**

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(87.08-(-47.08))^2 + ((-47.08)-0)^2 + (0-87.08)^2}
$$

Computing each term:

$$
(87.08+47.08)^2 = (134.16)^2 \approx 17998.9
$$
$$
(-47.08)^2 \approx 2216.5
$$
$$
(87.08)^2 \approx 7582.9
$$
$$
\text{Sum} = 17998.9 + 2216.5 + 7582.9 = 27798.3
$$

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{27798.3} = \frac{166.79}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 117.94 \text{ MPa}
$$

Factor of safety:

$$
n_{VM} = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_{VM}} = \frac{250}{117.94} \approx 2.120
$$

**Step 4: Comparison**

- Tresca gives $n \approx 1.86$ (more conservative)
- von Mises gives $n \approx 2.12$ (less conservative)

The **Maximum Shear Stress Theory is more conservative** (predicts failure earlier) and is recommended for use where safety is paramount. The von Mises theory is experimentally more accurate and gives a more realistic (less conservative) prediction.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for principal stress calculation, 2 marks for Tresca FoS, 2 marks for von Mises FoS, 1 mark for comparison comment)*

---

> **Question B (Alternative Choice).** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Set B]
>
> **(a)** Classify the various modes of failure encountered in engineering components. Discuss the macroscopic and microscopic features of ductile and brittle fractures with the aid of neat sketches. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A short column is made of a ductile material with yield strength $S_y = 320$ MPa and Young's modulus $E = 200$ GPa. The column has a circular cross-section of diameter $d = 50$ mm, an effective length $L_e = 2.0$ m, and is pinned at both ends. Determine the critical buckling load. If an axial compressive load of $P = 80$ kN is applied, calculate the factor of safety against **buckling** and against **yielding** (compression). Comment on which failure mode governs the design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1: Classification of Failure Modes**

Engineering failures are classified into:
- **Ductile fracture** (with significant plastic deformation)
- **Brittle fracture** (with negligible plastic deformation)
- **Fatigue failure** (under cyclic loading)
- **Creep failure** (at elevated temperatures)
- **Stress corrosion cracking** (combined mechanical-chemical)
- **Wear failure** (adhesive, abrasive, erosive, fatigue wear)
- **Buckling/Instability** (geometric)
- **Thermal failure** (thermal shock, fatigue)

**Step 2: Macroscopic Features of Ductile Fracture**
- Significant *necking* precedes fracture
- Fracture surface is *matte and fibrous* in the central region
- Outer region shows *shear lips* at 45° to the load axis
- Typical *cup-and-cone* morphology in cylindrical specimens
- Large energy absorption; warning signs visible

**Step 3: Microscopic Features of Ductile Fracture**
- Surface is covered with **microvoids** (dimples)
- Mechanism: nucleation → growth → coalescence of microvoids
- Dimples are equiaxed in the central region and elongated (parabolic) in the shear lip zone

**Step 4: Macroscopic Features of Brittle Fracture**
- Little or no plastic deformation; fracture is sudden
- Fracture surface is *bright and granular* (crystalline appearance)
- Fracture is typically perpendicular to the tensile load (flat surface)
- Little to no necking

**Step 5: Microscopic Features of Brittle Fracture**
- **Cleavage** fracture along specific crystallographic planes
- **River patterns** formed by the joining of multiple cleavage steps
- Flat faceted topography
- Possibly **intergranular** fracture (along grain boundaries) in some materials

```
Ductile Cup-and-Cone (Side View)        Brittle Fracture (Side View)
        ___                                      |
       /   \  <- shear lip                      /|\  <- flat, perpendicular
      |     | <- fibrous central                | |    to load axis
      |     |                                  / | \
       \___/                                 /  |  \
                                              |  |  |
                                              ^^^^^^^^
                                              no neck
```

*(7 marks: 2 marks classification, 2 marks ductile features, 2 marks brittle features, 1 mark sketch)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Geometric Properties**

Cross-sectional area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (50)^2}{4} = 1963.5 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Moment of inertia for a solid circular cross-section:

$$
I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (50)^4}{64} = 3.068 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^4
$$

Radius of gyration:

$$
r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.068 \times 10^5}{1963.5}} = \sqrt{156.25} = 12.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Slenderness Ratio**

For a pinned-pinned column, $L_e = L$:

$$
\frac{L_e}{r} = \frac{2000}{12.5} = 160
$$

**Step 3: Critical (Euler) Buckling Load**

Since the slenderness ratio is very high, Euler's formula is applicable:

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 (200 \times 10^3) (3.068 \times 10^5)}{(2000)^2}
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 6.0 \times 10^5 \times 3.068 \times 10^5}{4.0 \times 10^6}
$$

Computing the numerator: $9.8696 \times 6.0 \times 10^5 \times 3.068 \times 10^5 \approx 1.816 \times 10^{12}$

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{1.816 \times 10^{12}}{4.0 \times 10^6} = 4.54 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 454 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4: Factor of Safety Against Buckling**

$$
n_{buckling} = \frac{P_{cr}}{P} = \frac{454 \text{ kN}}{80 \text{ kN}} \approx 5.68
$$

**Step 5: Factor of Safety Against Yielding (Compression)**

Compressive stress:

$$
\sigma_c = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{80 \times 10^3}{1963.5} = 40.74 \text{ MPa}
$$

Factor of safety:

$$
n_{yield} = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_c} = \frac{320}{40.74} \approx 7.85
$$

**Step 6: Governing Failure Mode**

Since $n_{buckling} = 5.68 < n_{yield} = 7.85$, **buckling governs the design** for this slender column. This is the expected result because slenderness promotes instability failure before material yielding.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often compute the **yielding** factor of safety but forget that for *slender* columns, **Euler buckling** is the *governing* failure mode. Always check the slenderness ratio first.
> 
> **Pitfall #2:** The effective length $L_e$ depends on end conditions: pinned-pinned ($L_e = L$), fixed-fixed ($L_e = 0.5L$), fixed-pinned ($L_e = 0.7L$), fixed-free ($L_e = 2L$). KTU exams frequently test this.
> 
> **Pitfall #3:** Euler's formula is valid only when $\sigma_{cr} = P_{cr}/A \leq S_y$. Always verify this intermediate step.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for geometric/slenderness, 2 marks for Euler load, 1.5 marks each for the two FoS calculations, 1.5 marks for governing mode identification with justification)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1)**

- [x] **Failure Mode** = *manner* of failure; **Failure Mechanism** = *microstructural process*; **Failure Cause** = *root reason* (design/material/service).
- [x] The dominant failure modes are: **ductile, brittle, fatigue, creep, wear, corrosion, buckling, thermal**.
- [x] **Ductile** materials → use **von Mises (Distortion Energy)** or **Tresca (Max Shear Stress)**.
- [x] **Brittle** materials → use **Rankine (Max Normal Stress)** or **Coulomb-Mohr / Modified Mohr**.
- [x] **von Mises** equation: $\sigma_{VM} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2} \leq S_y$
- [x] **Tresca** equation: $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \leq S_y$ (with $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$).
- [x] **von Mises is the most accurate** ductile criterion; **Tresca is more conservative** (always gives a lower FoS).
- [x] **Slender columns** fail by **Euler buckling**, not yielding; the slenderness ratio $L_e/r$ dictates the failure mode.
- [x] The **factor of safety** $n = S_y / \sigma_{VM}$ (or equivalent) must be > 1 for safe design; typical design values are 1.5–4.0.
- [x] **Plane stress** simplification ($\sigma_3 = 0$) is valid for thin components loaded in-plane.
- [x] The plane-stress von Mises equation: $\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2 \leq S_y^2$.
- [x] Always identify the failure mode **first**, then select the appropriate theory — *the order matters*.
- [x] Asymmetric brittle materials (cast iron, concrete) require **Coulomb-Mohr** or **Modified Mohr**, not Rankine alone.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a question says "ductile material" without further specification, **default to von Mises (Distortion Energy)**. When it says "brittle material" without further specification, **default to Rankine (Max Normal Stress)** unless tensile and compressive strengths are given separately, in which case use **Coulomb-Mohr**. Always state the hypothesis *before* applying the equation — this is where most students lose easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combined Stresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Combined Stresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Combined Stresses

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Combined Stresses** refer to the state of stress developed at a point in a machine element when it is simultaneously subjected to two or more fundamental types of mechanical loading — namely, **axial loading** (tension or compression), **bending**, **torsion**, and **transverse shear**.

In the design of machine components (shafts, axles, levers, brackets, gears), a cross-section rarely experiences only a single type of stress. Real components must be evaluated under a **superposition** of stresses to predict failure accurately using theories such as Maximum Shear Stress (MSS), Distortion Energy (DE), or Maximum Normal Stress (MNS) theories.

The general total stresses at any point on a 2D element under combined loading are represented by:

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_{axial} + \sigma_{bending}$$

$$\tau_{xy} = \tau_{torsion} + \tau_{transverse}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Pedalling Bicycle Pedal Shaft**
>
> Imagine the pedal shaft of a bicycle while you ride uphill. Your foot pushes **down** (creating **bending**) while your leg simultaneously twists the crank forward (creating **torsion**). The shaft is neither in pure bending nor pure torsion — it experiences **both at the same time**. The total stress state at any cross-section is the algebraic sum of bending stress + torsional shear stress.
>
> Now imagine you also stand up while pedalling — you stretch your leg, applying a small **axial pull**. This adds a third stress component. The shaft now faces a **tri-axial combined stress state**.

### 1.3 Common Loading Scenarios in Machine Design

> [!NOTE]
> - **Axial + Bending:** Long columns with eccentric loads, crane hooks, piston rods.
> - **Bending + Torsion:** Transmission shafts, crankshafts, propeller shafts.
> - **Axial + Bending + Torsion:** Helical springs, gear shafts with thrust loads, connecting rods.
> - **Bending + Transverse Shear:** Short beams, riveted/bolted joints under off-center loads.

The fundamental principle used in all combined stress analyses is the **Principle of Superposition** (valid for linearly elastic materials within the elastic limit).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Loading Combinations

When a structural member is loaded by a combination of forces and moments, the stress components at any point on the cross-section are computed independently and then superposed.

### Case 1: Axial Loading + Bending

A member subjected to an axial force $P$ and a bending moment $M$ produces normal stresses at distance $y$ from the neutral axis:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

- $\frac{P}{A}$ → uniform normal stress from axial load.
- $\frac{M \cdot y}{I}$ → linearly varying bending stress.
- $I$ → second moment of area about the neutral axis.

### Case 2: Bending + Torsion

A circular shaft under bending moment $M$ and torque $T$ generates:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3} \quad \text{(bending stress at extreme fiber)}$$

$$\tau = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} \quad \text{(torsional shear stress at surface)}$$

### Case 3: Axial + Bending + Torsion

The critical point (typically the extreme fiber) experiences combined normal and shear stresses:

$$\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$$

$$\tau_{xy} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$

## 2.2 Equivalent Bending Moment and Equivalent Torque

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Designers use **equivalent moment ($M_e$)** and **equivalent torque ($T_e$)** to apply simple beam/shaft formulas under combined loading. These reduce a complex stress state into a single bending and torsional equivalent.

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2}\left(M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}\right)$$

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

These are derived by **rotating the principal stress element by 45°** so that the maximum shear stress aligns with the axis, making the state equivalent to pure torsion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Use this table as your one-stop reference for Module 1 exam problems.**

| Loading Combination | Normal Stress $\sigma_x$ | Shear Stress $\tau_{xy}$ | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure Axial | $\frac{P}{A}$ | $0$ | Tie rods, columns |
| Pure Bending | $\frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$ | $0$ | Beams, crank webs |
| Pure Torsion | $0$ | $\frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$ | Shafts under twist |
| Axial + Bending | $\frac{P}{A} + \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$ | $0$ | Eccentrically loaded columns |
| Bending + Torsion | $\frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$ | $\frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$ | Transmission shafts |
| Axial + Bending + Torsion | $\frac{P}{A} + \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$ | $\frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$ | Crankshafts, gear shafts |

**Principal Stresses (2D State):**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Equivalent Stresses (Failure Theories):**

| Theory | Equivalent Stress Formula |
|---|---|
| Maximum Normal Stress (MNS) | $\sigma_{1}$ (or $\sigma_{2}$ if tensile) |
| Maximum Shear Stress (MSS / Tresca) | $\sigma_{1} - \sigma_{2}$ |
| Distortion Energy (DE / von Mises) | $\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + 3 \tau_{xy}^2}$ |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this matters in industry:**
> - **Automotive Crankshafts:** Experience combined bending, torsion, and axial thrust from piston combustion loads.
> - **Aerospace Landing Gear:** Subjected to bending during taxiing + torsion during braking + axial compression on impact.
> - **Pressure Vessels + Pipe Supports:** Combine membrane stresses with bending from supports.
> - **Wind Turbine Shafts:** Pure torsional + bending due to aerodynamic loads on the rotor blades.

Accurate combined stress analysis prevents **catastrophic fatigue failure** which is the #1 cause of rotating machinery breakdown in industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent Bending Moment and Equivalent Torque

**Starting Point:** Consider a shaft under bending moment $M$ and torque $T$. At the extreme fiber, the element is subjected to normal stress $\sigma = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$ and shear stress $\tau = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$.

The element experiences **pure shear + uniaxial tension**. We need to find the **maximum shear stress** in the element, which determines yielding.

**Step 1:** Identify principal stresses of the 2D state.

Using standard principal stress formula for $\sigma_x = \sigma$ and $\sigma_y = 0$:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\sigma_x = \sigma$, $\sigma_y = 0$, $\tau_{xy} = \tau$:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

**Step 3:** Compute maximum shear stress.

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute bending and torsion stress expressions.

\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{\left(\frac{16 M}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2} \\
&= \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}
\end{aligned}

**Step 5:** Compare with pure torsion formula $\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T_e}{\pi d^3}$.

For the combined state to be **equivalent to a pure torsion of magnitude $T_e$**:

$$\frac{16 T_e}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

$$\boxed{T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}$$

**Step 6:** Now substitute back into the principal stress formula. The principal stresses are:

\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \frac{\sigma}{2} + \tau_{max} = \frac{16 M}{\pi d^3} + \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \\
&= \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\left(M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}\right)
\end{aligned}

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{\sigma}{2} - \tau_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\left(M - \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Recognize that this principal stress is generated by a **pure bending moment** $M_e$:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{32 M_e}{\pi d^3}$$

\begin{aligned}
\frac{32 M_e}{\pi d^3} &= \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\left(M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}\right) \\
M_e &= \frac{1}{2}\left(M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}\right)
\end{aligned}

$$\boxed{M_e = \frac{1}{2}\left(M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}\right)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: von Mises Equivalent Stress for Combined Bending + Torsion

The Distortion Energy theory gives the equivalent normal stress:

$$\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + 3 \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $\sigma_x = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$ and $\tau_{xy} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$:

\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{vm} &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + 3\left(\frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1024 M^2}{\pi^2 d^6} + \frac{768 T^2}{\pi^2 d^6}} \\
&= \frac{1}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{1024 M^2 + 768 T^2} \\
&= \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{4 M^2 + 3 T^2}
\end{aligned}

$$\boxed{\sigma_{vm} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{4 M^2 + 3 T^2}}$$

This is the **most-commonly-used** combined loading formula in shaft design under KTU syllabus and matches the ASME shaft design standards.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Numerical Application

> **Problem:** A shaft of diameter $50$ mm transmits a torque of $2$ kN·m while simultaneously subjected to a bending moment of $1.5$ kN·m. Determine the equivalent torque and equivalent bending moment using MSS theory.

**Given:** $d = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m, $T = 2$ kN·m $= 2000$ N·m, $M = 1.5$ kN·m $= 1500$ N·m.

**Step 1:** Compute Equivalent Torque.

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{1500^2 + 2000^2} = \sqrt{2{,}250{,}000 + 4{,}000{,}000} = \sqrt{6{,}250{,}000}$$

$$T_e = 2500 \text{ N·m} = 2.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute Equivalent Bending Moment.

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2}\left(1500 + 2500\right) = \frac{4000}{2} = 2000 \text{ N·m} = 2.0 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3:** Verify with principal stress approach.

$$\sigma_x = \frac{32 \times 1500}{\pi \times (0.05)^3} = \frac{48{,}000}{\pi \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}} = 122.23 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_{xy} = \frac{16 \times 2000}{\pi \times (0.05)^3} = \frac{32{,}000}{\pi \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}} = 81.49 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{122.23}{2}\right)^2 + (81.49)^2} = \sqrt{3735.3 + 6640.7} = \sqrt{10{,}376} = 101.86 \text{ MPa}$$

$$T_e = \frac{\tau_{max} \cdot \pi d^3}{16} = \frac{101.86 \times \pi \times (0.05)^3}{16} = 2500 \text{ N·m} \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Combined Stress Loading Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following block diagram illustrates how multiple loads are decomposed and combined into equivalent stresses for failure analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Load1[Axial Force P] --> Super[Superposition Principle]
    Load2[Bending Moment M] --> Super
    Load3[Torque T] --> Super
    Load4[Transverse Shear V] --> Super

    Super --> Norm[Normal Stress Sigma X]
    Super --> Shear[Shear Stress Tau XY]

    Norm --> Crit[Critical Point Identification]
    Shear --> Crit

    Crit --> Prin[Principal Stress Calculation]
    Prin --> S1[Sigma 1 Maximum]
    Prin --> S2[Sigma 2 Minimum]
    Prin --> Tmax[Tau Max Maximum Shear]

    S1 --> Fail1[MNS Failure Theory]
    Tmax --> Fail2[MSS Tresca Theory]
    S1 --> Fail3[DE von Mises Theory]
    S2 --> Fail3

    Fail1 --> Out1[Compare with Allowable Sigma]
    Fail2 --> Out2[Compare with Allowable Tau]
    Fail3 --> Out3[Compare with Allowable Sigma VM]

    Out1 --> Safe[Design Safe or Redesign]
    Out2 --> Safe
    Out3 --> Safe
```

## 4.2 Shaft Loading State Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shaft Subjected to M and T] --> B[Surface Element at Extreme Fiber]
    B --> C[Normal Stress Sigma B from Bending]
    B --> D[Shear Stress Tau from Torsion]
    C --> E[Combined 2D Stress Element]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Rotate 45 Degrees]
    F --> G[Pure Shear Equivalent Element]
    G --> H[Equivalent Torque T Equation]
    G --> I[Equivalent Moment M Equation]
```

## 4.3 Real-World Machine Element Mapping

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph RealWorld[Real World Machine Components]
        R1[Transmission Shaft]
        R2[Crankshaft]
        R3[Helical Spring]
        R4[Piston Rod]
        R5[Crane Hook]
    end

    subgraph Loading[Loading Combination Type]
        L1[Bending + Torsion]
        L2[Bending + Torsion + Axial]
        L3[Axial + Torsion]
        L4[Axial + Bending]
        L5[Axial + Bending]
    end

    subgraph Failure[Failure Analysis Output]
        F1[Principal Stress]
        F2[von Mises Stress]
        F3[Factor of Safety]
    end

    R1 --> L1
    R2 --> L2
    R3 --> L3
    R4 --> L4
    R5 --> L5

    L1 --> F1
    L2 --> F2
    L3 --> F2
    L4 --> F1
    L5 --> F3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define equivalent bending moment and equivalent torque. State the formulas.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent Bending Moment ($M_e$):** It is the fictitious pure bending moment which, when acting alone on a shaft, produces the **same maximum normal stress** as the combined state of bending moment $M$ and torque $T$.
>
> **Equivalent Torque ($T_e$):** It is the fictitious pure torque which, when acting alone on a shaft, produces the **same maximum shear stress** as the combined state.

Formulas:

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2}\left(M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}\right)$$

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List any three theories of failure applicable to combined loading.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Maximum Normal Stress Theory (MNS):** Failure occurs when $\sigma_1$ exceeds the ultimate tensile stress. Suitable for brittle materials.
> 2. **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (MSS / Tresca):** Failure occurs when maximum shear stress $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)/2$ exceeds the shear yield stress. Conservative for ductile materials.
> 3. **Distortion Energy Theory (DE / von Mises):** Failure occurs when the von Mises stress $\sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1 \sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}$ exceeds the yield strength. Most accurate for ductile materials.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

A solid circular shaft of diameter $60$ mm is subjected to a bending moment of $3$ kN·m and a torque of $4$ kN·m.

**(a)** Determine the maximum normal and shear stresses at the surface of the shaft. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using both MSS and DE theories, calculate the equivalent torque. Which theory gives a more conservative design? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Maximum Stresses:**

Given: $d = 60$ mm $= 0.06$ m, $M = 3$ kN·m $= 3000$ N·m, $T = 4$ kN·m $= 4000$ N·m.

Bending (normal) stress:

$$\sigma_x = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 3000}{\pi \times (0.06)^3} = \frac{96{,}000}{\pi \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\sigma_x = 141.47 \text{ MPa}$$

Torsional (shear) stress:

$$\tau_{xy} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 4000}{\pi \times (0.06)^3} = \frac{64{,}000}{\pi \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\tau_{xy} = 94.31 \text{ MPa}$$

**(b) Equivalent Torque:**

**Using MSS Theory:**

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{(70.735)^2 + (94.31)^2}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{5003.4 + 8894.4} = \sqrt{13{,}897.8} = 117.89 \text{ MPa}$$

$$T_e^{MSS} = \frac{\tau_{max} \cdot \pi d^3}{16} = \frac{117.89 \times \pi \times (0.06)^3}{16} = \frac{117.89 \times \pi \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4}}{16}$$

$$T_e^{MSS} = 5000 \text{ N·m} = 5.0 \text{ kN·m}$$

This matches the direct formula:

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{3000^2 + 4000^2} = \sqrt{9{,}000{,}000 + 16{,}000{,}000} = \sqrt{25{,}000{,}000} = 5000 \text{ N·m} \checkmark$$

**Using DE Theory (von Mises):**

$$\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + 3 \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{(141.47)^2 + 3 \times (94.31)^2}$$

$$\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{20{,}013.7 + 26{,}683.2} = \sqrt{46{,}696.9} = 216.09 \text{ MPa}$$

Equivalent torque from von Mises:

$$T_e^{DE} = \frac{\sigma_{vm} \cdot \pi d^3}{16} = \frac{216.09 \times \pi \times (0.06)^3}{16} = \frac{216.09 \times \pi \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4}}{16}$$

$$T_e^{DE} = 9165 \text{ N·m} \approx 9.17 \text{ kN·m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **forget to convert kN·m to N·m** before substitution → entire numerical answer becomes wrong.
> - Many students **omit the $\pi$ in the denominator** of bending/torsion formulas (32 vs 16 confusion).
> - The MSS theory gives a **lower equivalent torque** (5 kN·m) than DE (9.17 kN·m) — this is consistent with MSS being the **more conservative** theory. Clearly state this comparison in the conclusion for **full marks**.
> - Always **write units** in the final boxed answer — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for unit omission.

**Comparison:** DE theory gives a **higher** equivalent torque because it predicts yielding at a higher combined stress. MSS theory is **more conservative** (predicts failure earlier) and is preferred for ductile materials under combined loading for safety-critical applications like aerospace and automotive shafts.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

A short column of rectangular cross-section $100$ mm $\times 60$ mm carries an axial compressive load of $50$ kN with an eccentricity of $20$ mm along the major axis. The column length is $1.5$ m.

**(a)** Calculate the maximum and minimum stresses at the base of the column. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If additionally a horizontal shear force of $10$ kN acts at the top, determine the maximum shear stress at the neutral axis. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Combined Axial + Bending Stress:**

Given: $A = 100 \times 60 = 6000$ mm² $= 6 \times 10^{-3}$ m², $P = 50$ kN (compressive), $e = 20$ mm $= 0.02$ m.

Bending moment due to eccentricity:

$$M = P \cdot e = 50 \times 10^3 \times 0.02 = 1000 \text{ N·m}$$

Section modulus about major axis:

$$Z = \frac{b \cdot d^2}{6} = \frac{60 \times (100)^2}{6} = \frac{60 \times 10000}{6} = 100{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

Direct compressive stress:

$$\sigma_d = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{50{,}000}{6 \times 10^{-3}} = 8.33 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

Bending stress at extreme fiber:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{1000}{10^{-4}} = 10 \text{ MPa}$$

Maximum stress (compression + bending on same side):

$$\sigma_{max} = 8.33 + 10 = 18.33 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

Minimum stress (opposite side):

$$\sigma_{min} = -8.33 + 10 = 1.67 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

**(b) Maximum Shear Stress at Neutral Axis:**

Transverse shear stress at neutral axis:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{3 V}{2 A} = \frac{3 \times 10{,}000}{2 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{30{,}000}{0.012} = 2.5 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students use the **circular cross-section formula** for rectangular sections. The correct shear stress distribution for rectangular sections is **parabolic**, and the maximum is $\frac{3V}{2A}$ at the neutral axis — **not** $V/A$.
> - The eccentricity is along the **major axis** — make sure bending is computed about the correct axis ($I_{xx}$ vs $I_{yy}$). For $100 \times 60$ with eccentricity on the $100$ mm side, bending is about the axis perpendicular to it.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Combined Stresses:**

- ✅ Combined stresses occur whenever **two or more fundamental loads** act simultaneously.
- ✅ Use **Principle of Superposition** (only valid in linear elastic range).
- ✅ Identify the **critical point** (usually extreme fiber on top/bottom of cross-section).
- ✅ Three main loading combinations: Axial+Bending, Bending+Torsion, Axial+Bending+Torsion.
- ✅ Bending stress formula for circular shaft: $\sigma = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$.
- ✅ Torsional shear stress formula for circular shaft: $\tau = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$.
- ✅ Equivalent torque: $T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ (used with MSS theory).
- ✅ Equivalent moment: $M_e = \frac{1}{2}\left(M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}\right)$ (used with MSS theory).
- ✅ von Mises equivalent stress for shaft: $\sigma_{vm} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{4 M^2 + 3 T^2}$.
- ✅ Principal stresses: $\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$.
- ✅ For **rectangular cross-sections**, max shear stress at neutral axis $= \frac{3V}{2A}$ (parabolic distribution).
- ✅ For **circular cross-sections**, max shear stress $= \frac{4V}{3A}$.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** Tensile stresses positive, compressive negative (use consistently).
- ✅ Always convert units to **N, m, MPa** before numerical substitution.
- ✅ **MSS theory** is more conservative than DE — safer but less economical.
- ✅ DE (von Mises) is **most widely used** for ductile materials in modern design codes (ASME, IS).
- ✅ For axial + bending: check both **maximum compression** and **maximum tension** sides separately.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximum Stress Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Maximum Stress Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Maximum Stress Theory (MST)**, historically attributed to **W.J.M. Rankine (1858)**, is the oldest and most intuitive of all the classical failure theories. It is also known as the **Normal Stress Theory** or **Rankine's Theory**.

> [!IMPORTANT] Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)
> *Failure is assumed to occur when the **maximum principal stress** ($\sigma_1$) in a complex three-dimensional stress system reaches a value equal to the **ultimate tensile strength ($S_{ut}$)** of the material in simple uniaxial tension. Similarly, the minimum principal stress ($\sigma_3$) in compression must not exceed the **ultimate compressive strength ($S_{uc}$)**.*

In equation form, the failure criteria are:

$$\sigma_1 \geq S_{ut} \quad \text{(Tensile Failure Criterion)}$$

$$\sigma_3 \leq -S_{uc} \quad \text{(Compressive Failure Criterion)}$$

Where $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$ are the **principal stresses** sorted in algebraic descending order.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are pulling a brittle piece of **chalk** (or a cast-iron specimen) along two perpendicular directions at the same time with unequal forces. The chalk does not care about the "average" pull or the "twist" (shear) — it will simply crack along the plane that is being stretched **the MOST** in any direction.

> [!NOTE] The "Strongest Pull Wins" Analogy
> Think of three ropes tied to a single knot, each pulling in a different direction with different forces. The knot will slip the moment **any one** of those ropes is pulled hard enough to snap. The Maximum Stress Theory says exactly the same thing about a material element: **the moment any principal stress reaches the breaking limit, failure occurs** — regardless of what the other stresses are doing.

This theory is therefore most accurate for **brittle materials** (cast iron, concrete, ceramics, glass) that fail by sudden fracture along the plane of maximum tensile stress, with little or no plastic deformation.

## 1.3 Standard Material Constants (KTU Convention)

The theory requires two experimentally measured material limits:

- **$S_{ut}$ — Ultimate Tensile Strength** : Units: **MPa** or **N/mm²**
- **$S_{uc}$ — Ultimate Compressive Strength** : Units: **MPa** or **N/mm²**
- For ductile materials (mild steel), $S_{ut} \approx S_{uc}$, and the theory reduces to a single criterion.
- For brittle materials (cast iron), $S_{uc} > S_{ut}$, often by a factor of **3 to 4**.

> [!TIP] KTU Examiner's Heuristic
> When a question says *"A brittle shaft is subjected to…"*, your first instinct should be the **Maximum Stress Theory**. When it says *"A ductile shaft…"*, look toward **Distortion Energy Theory** or **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** instead.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Logic — Step by Step

1. **Step 1 — Compute the Stress Tensor**: Determine the state of stress at the critical point of the machine element (typically $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_z$, $\tau_{xy}$, $\tau_{yz}$, $\tau_{xz}$).
2. **Step 2 — Rotate the Coordinate System**: Find the **principal stresses** $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$. These are the normal stresses acting on planes where shear stress is zero.
3. **Step 3 — Rank the Stresses**: Order them algebraically: $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$. Tensile stresses are positive, compressive stresses are negative.
4. **Step 4 — Compare with Strength**: The largest tensile value $\sigma_1$ is compared against $S_{ut}$. The largest compressive magnitude $\vert\sigma_3\vert$ is compared against $S_{uc}$.
5. **Step 5 — Declare Failure or Safety**: If either criterion is violated, the component is predicted to fail.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Theory

The Maximum Stress Theory assumes that the **strength of a material is isotropic in tension and compression independently**. It completely **ignores the interaction** between the principal stresses (i.e., it does not care that $\sigma_2$ exists). This is why it works well for brittle materials (where failure is governed by crack propagation along the maximum tensile plane) but works poorly for ductile materials (where yielding is a shear-driven, interactive phenomenon).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] Master this table — these equations appear in 90% of KTU university questions on this topic.

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Notes / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Principal stresses (2D) | $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Used when one principal stress is zero (plane stress) |
| Principal stresses (3D) | Roots of $\sigma^3 - I_1\sigma^2 + I_2\sigma - I_3 = 0$ | Where $I_1, I_2, I_3$ are stress invariants |
| **Tensile Failure Criterion** | $\sigma_1 = S_{ut}$ | Failure when max tensile principal stress equals ultimate tensile strength |
| **Compressive Failure Criterion** | $\vert\sigma_3\vert = S_{uc}$ | Failure when max compressive principal stress magnitude equals ultimate compressive strength |
| **Factor of Safety (Tensile)** | $n = \dfrac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_1}$ | Valid when $\sigma_1 > 0$ |
| **Factor of Safety (Compressive)** | $n = \dfrac{S_{uc}}{\vert\sigma_3\vert}$ | Valid when $\sigma_3 < 0$ |
| **Design Equation (Tension)** | $\sigma_1 \leq \dfrac{S_{ut}}{N}$ | $N$ is the design factor of safety (given in problem) |
| **Design Equation (Compression)** | $\vert\sigma_3\vert \leq \dfrac{S_{uc}}{N}$ | Used for brittle materials in compression |
| Equivalent Stress (MST) | $\sigma_{eq} = \max(\sigma_1, \vert\sigma_3\vert)$ | Used for plotting on $\sigma_1$–$\sigma_2$ failure envelope |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of cast iron gears, pulleys, and brackets** — all brittle components where fracture is the failure mode.
- **Concrete and masonry structural design** — columns, beams, dams.
- **Ceramic component design** — cutting tools, insulators, turbine blades (early-stage design).
- **Pressure vessel design** — preliminary brittle fracture check (before switching to fracture mechanics).
- **Quality control in foundry and forging** — predicting fracture initiation in cast products.

## 2.5 Graphical Failure Envelope in the $\sigma_1$–$\sigma_2$ Plane

For a 2D principal stress state, the MST envelope is a simple **square** (not a hexagon like Tresca and not an ellipse like von Mises). The square has vertices at $(S_{ut}, 0)$, $(0, S_{ut})$, $(-S_{uc}, 0)$, and $(0, -S_{uc})$. The interior of this square represents the **safe zone**.

$$\boxed{\text{Safe Zone (MST)}: -S_{uc} \leq \sigma_1 \leq S_{ut} \quad \text{AND} \quad -S_{uc} \leq \sigma_2 \leq S_{ut}}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Principal Stresses from a 2D Stress Element

Consider a 2D element subjected to normal stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$ and shear stress $\tau_{xy}$. We want to find the angle $\theta$ at which the shear stress vanishes, leaving only normal stresses (the principal stresses).

**Starting Point — Stress Transformation Equations:**

$$\sigma_{\theta} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

$$\tau_{\theta} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$$

**Step 1 — Apply the principal stress condition.** At a principal plane, shear stress is zero: $\tau_{\theta} = 0$.

$$-\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta_p) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta_p) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate the tangent.**

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back to obtain the principal stress magnitudes.** Substituting $\tan(2\theta_p)$ into the expression for $\sigma_{\theta}$ and simplifying using trigonometric identities:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Königsberger / Mohr's circle** derivation of the principal stresses — a mandatory formula for KTU numericals.

## 3.2 Derivation: Factor of Safety under MST

Suppose the principal stresses at the critical point are $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$.

**Tensile Case ($\sigma_1 > 0$):**
Failure occurs when $\sigma_1$ reaches $S_{ut}$. Since force scales linearly with stress (for a fixed cross-section), the Factor of Safety is the ratio of ultimate load to applied load, which equals the ratio of ultimate stress to applied stress.

$$n_t = \frac{\sigma_{1,\text{failure}}}{\sigma_{1,\text{applied}}} = \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_1}$$

**Compressive Case ($\sigma_3 < 0$):**
The magnitude of the compressive principal stress at failure is $S_{uc}$. Therefore:

$$n_c = \frac{S_{uc}}{\vert\sigma_3\vert}$$

**Governing Factor of Safety:**

$$n = \min(n_t, n_c)$$

The minimum governs because whichever mode (tension or compression) reaches its ultimate strength first will dictate failure.

## 3.3 Derivation: Equivalent Stress for Design Equations

The design equation requires that the actual stress be less than or equal to the allowable stress, scaled by the design safety factor $N$:

$$\sigma_{\text{actual}} \leq \sigma_{\text{allowable}} = \frac{S_{ut}}{N}$$

Rewriting:

$$N \cdot \sigma_1 \leq S_{ut}$$

Dividing both sides by $S_{ut}$:

$$\frac{N \cdot \sigma_1}{S_{ut}} \leq 1$$

This can be written in the canonical "equivalent stress" form:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 \leq \frac{S_{ut}}{N}$$

So the **equivalent stress in MST is simply the maximum principal stress** $\sigma_1$. The factor of safety is then:

$$n = \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_{eq}} = \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_1}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem Statement:** A brittle cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter $d = 200$ mm and wall thickness $t = 10$ mm. The internal pressure is $p = 4$ MPa. The material is cast iron with $S_{ut} = 150$ MPa and $S_{uc} = 600$ MPa. Calculate the factor of safety using the Maximum Stress Theory.

**Step 1 — Calculate the principal stresses.**

For a thin-walled cylinder, the hoop and axial stresses are:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot d}{2t} = \frac{4 \times 200}{2 \times 10} = 40 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

$$\sigma_a = \frac{p \cdot d}{4t} = \frac{4 \times 200}{4 \times 10} = 20 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

The radial stress is negligible (thin-wall assumption): $\sigma_r \approx 0$.

**Step 2 — Identify the three principal stresses.**

$$\sigma_1 = 40 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 20 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_3 \approx 0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Apply MST and compute the safety factor.**

Since $\sigma_3 \geq 0$, only tensile criterion applies:

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_1} \\
&= \frac{150}{40} \\
&= 3.75
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $n = 3.75$. The vessel is safe by MST, but the student should compare with other theories (von Mises, Tresca) for the same problem.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Where MST Succeeds vs Fails

| **Condition** | **MST Prediction** | **Experimental Reality** | **Verdict** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uniaxial tension (brittle) | Accurate | Brittle fracture at $S_{ut}$ | ✅ Excellent |
| Uniaxial compression (brittle) | Accurate | Crushes at $S_{uc}$ | ✅ Excellent |
| Biaxial tension ($\sigma_1 = \sigma_2$) | Conservative | Fails at higher combined stress | ⚠️ Conservative |
| Biaxial equal tension + compression | Inaccurate | Fails by shear (ductile) | ❌ Fails for ductile |
| Pure shear (ductile) | $\sigma_1 = \tau$, predicts $n = S_{ut}/\tau$ | Yields at $\tau = S_{sy}/2$ | ❌ Underestimates by factor of 2 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Maximum Stress Theory Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Stress State at Critical Point] --> B[Input sigmax, sigmay, tauxy]
    B --> C[Compute Principal Stresses sigma1, sigma2, sigma3]
    C --> D[Sort Algebraically: sigma1 ge sigma2 ge sigma3]
    D --> E{Is sigma1 ge Sut?}
    E -- Yes --> F[FAILURE: Tensile Fracture]
    E -- No --> G{Is sigma3 le -Suc?}
    G -- Yes --> H[FAILURE: Compressive Crushing]
    G -- No --> I[Component is SAFE by MST]
    F --> J[Compute n = Sut / sigma1]
    H --> K[Compute n = Suc / abs sigma3]
    I --> L[Report n = min Sut/sigma1, Suc/abs sigma3]
    J --> M[Output: Factor of Safety]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Mohr's Circle Representation of MST

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Mohr_Circle [Mohrs Circle Construction]
        P1[Point A: sigma1 on X-axis] --- P2[Center C: sigmax + sigmay over 2]
        P2 --- P3[Point B: sigma3 on X-axis]
        P4[Radius R equals sqrt of half-difference squared plus tauxy squared] -.-> P2
    end
    P1 --> Q[sigma1 is max tensile principal stress]
    P3 --> R[sigma3 is max compressive principal stress]
    Q --> S[Compare sigma1 to Sut]
    R --> T[Compare abs sigma3 to Suc]
```

## 4.3 Failure Envelope — $\sigma_1$ vs $\sigma_2$ Plane (MST Square)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Envelope [MST Failure Envelope]
        V1[Vertex: Sut, 0 - Tensile Limit on sigma1] --- V2[Vertex: 0, Sut - Tensile Limit on sigma2]
        V2 --- V3[Vertex: -Suc, 0 - Compressive Limit on sigma1]
        V3 --- V4[Vertex: 0, -Suc - Compressive Limit on sigma2]
        V4 --- V1
    end
    Inside[Inside the Square = SAFE ZONE] -.-> V1
    Outside[Outside the Square = FAILURE PREDICTED] -.-> V2
```

## 4.4 Logical Comparison Architecture — MST vs Other Theories

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given Stress State] --> Q{Material Type?}
    Q -- Brittle --> MST[Apply Maximum Stress Theory]
    Q -- Ductile --> Q2{Dominant Failure Mode?}
    Q2 -- Yielding --> VM[Von Mises / DE Theory]
    Q2 -- Shear Driven --> MSS[Maximum Shear Stress Theory]
    MST --> Out1[Output: sigma_eq = sigma1, compare to Sut]
    VM --> Out2[Output: sigma_eq = vm, compare to Sy]
    MSS --> Out3[Output: sigma_eq = sigma1 - sigma3, compare to Sy]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`
**State the Maximum Stress Theory of failure and mention the materials for which it is best suited.**

**Model Answer:**

The Maximum Stress Theory (also called Rankine's Theory) states that a machine component fails when the **maximum principal stress ($\sigma_1$)** in a complex stress system reaches the **ultimate tensile strength ($S_{ut}$)** of the material, or when the **minimum principal stress ($\sigma_3$)** reaches the **ultimate compressive strength ($S_{uc}$)**.

The mathematical criteria are:

$$\sigma_1 = S_{ut} \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_3 = -S_{uc}$$

It is best suited for **brittle materials** such as cast iron, concrete, and ceramics, which fail by sudden fracture along the plane of maximum tensile stress without significant plastic deformation.

**[Marks Distribution: Definition – 1.5, Equation – 1.0, Material – 0.5]**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`
**Write the failure envelope equation for Maximum Stress Theory in the $\sigma_1$–$\sigma_2$ principal stress plane.**

**Model Answer:**

The MST failure envelope in the $\sigma_1$–$\sigma_2$ plane is a square bounded by four lines:

$$\sigma_1 = S_{ut}, \quad \sigma_1 = -S_{uc}, \quad \sigma_2 = S_{ut}, \quad \sigma_2 = -S_{uc}$$

Any stress state lying **inside** this square is safe; any stress state lying on or outside is considered failed. The corners of the square are at $(S_{ut}, 0)$, $(0, S_{ut})$, $(-S_{uc}, 0)$, and $(0, -S_{uc})$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE] KTU Pattern: Part B questions offer an **internal choice**. Choose ONE of the two alternatives below. Each part (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A brittle shaft of diameter 50 mm is subjected to an axial tensile load of 50 kN and a torque of 1.5 kN·m simultaneously. The material properties are $S_{ut} = 200$ MPa and $S_{uc} = 800$ MPa.**

**(a)** Determine the principal stresses at a critical point on the surface of the shaft. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Using the Maximum Stress Theory, calculate the factor of safety and state whether the design is safe. **[7 Marks]**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Principal Stresses Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Compute axial stress $\sigma_x$.**

$$\sigma_x = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{\frac{\pi}{4}(50)^2} = \frac{50000}{1963.5} = 25.46 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute shear stress due to torque $\tau_{xy}$.**

$$\tau_{xy} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 1500 \times 10^3}{\pi \times (50)^3} = \frac{24 \times 10^6}{392699} = 61.12 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Identify stress components.** $\sigma_y = 0$, $\sigma_x = 25.46$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 61.12$ MPa.

**Step 4 — Apply the principal stress formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \\
&= \frac{25.46 + 0}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{25.46}{2}\right)^2 + (61.12)^2} \\
&= 12.73 \pm \sqrt{162.08 + 3735.65} \\
&= 12.73 \pm \sqrt{3897.73} \\
&= 12.73 \pm 62.43
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Principal Stresses:**

$$\sigma_1 = 75.16 \text{ MPa (tensile)}, \quad \sigma_2 = -39.70 \text{ MPa (compressive)}, \quad \sigma_3 \approx 0 \text{ (free surface, plane stress)}$$

---

**(b) Factor of Safety Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Check tensile criterion.**

$$\sigma_1 = 75.16 \text{ MPa} < S_{ut} = 200 \text{ MPa} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_t = \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_1} = \frac{200}{75.16} = 2.66$$

**Step 2 — Check compressive criterion.**

$$\vert\sigma_2\vert = 39.70 \text{ MPa} < S_{uc} = 800 \text{ MPa} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_c = \frac{S_{uc}}{\vert\sigma_2\vert} = \frac{800}{39.70} = 20.15$$

**Step 3 — Governing factor of safety.**

$$n = \min(n_t, n_c) = \min(2.66, 20.15) = 2.66$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

Since $n = 2.66 > 1$, the design is **SAFE** by the Maximum Stress Theory. The tensile mode governs failure.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of 300 mm and a wall thickness of 15 mm. It is subjected to an internal pressure of 6 MPa. The vessel is made of cast iron with $S_{ut} = 180$ MPa and $S_{uc} = 700$ MPa.**

**(a)** Calculate the hoop and longitudinal stresses in the vessel wall and identify the principal stresses. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Using the Maximum Stress Theory, determine whether the vessel will fail. If not safe, suggest a corrective measure. **[7 Marks]**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Stress Computation:**

**Step 1 — Hoop stress.**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{pd}{2t} = \frac{6 \times 300}{2 \times 15} = \frac{1800}{30} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Longitudinal stress.**

$$\sigma_L = \frac{pd}{4t} = \frac{6 \times 300}{4 \times 15} = \frac{1800}{60} = 30 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Principal stresses (radial stress neglected in thin-wall).**

$$\sigma_1 = 60 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 30 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_3 \approx 0 \text{ MPa}$$

---

**(b) MST Safety Assessment:**

**Step 1 — Tensile check.**

$$\sigma_1 = 60 \text{ MPa} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_t = \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_1} = \frac{180}{60} = 3.0$$

**Step 2 — Compressive check.** Not applicable since $\sigma_3 \geq 0$.

**Step 3 — Conclusion.** Since $n_t = 3.0 > 1$, the vessel is **SAFE** by MST. If a design factor of safety $N = 4$ were required, the vessel would be **unsafe**, and a corrective measure would be to **increase the wall thickness**. The required thickness would be:

$$t_{\text{required}} = \frac{pd \cdot N}{2 S_{ut}} = \frac{6 \times 300 \times 4}{2 \times 180} = 20 \text{ mm}$$

---

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks
> 1. **Forgetting to sort the principal stresses**: Students often plug $\sigma_x$ directly into $S_{ut}$, missing the rotation step. The **maximum** principal stress must be identified first.
> 2. **Mixing up $S_{ut}$ and $S_{uc}$**: Brittle materials have very different tensile and compressive strengths. Always check both criteria separately.
> 3. **Ignoring the compressive criterion**: When both $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$ have large magnitudes (e.g., hydrostatic pressure + bending), the **minimum** $n$ governs. Students often compute only the tensile $n$.
> 4. **Sign convention errors**: Compressive stresses are negative in KTU convention. Always verify $\sigma_1 > 0$ and $\sigma_3 < 0$ before applying criteria.
> 5. **Using MST for ductile materials**: This is a common conceptual error. MST predicts failure for ductile materials at half the actual yield stress in pure shear. Always match the theory to the material.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **MST is also called Rankine's Theory** — be prepared for both names in the exam.
- The theory applies to **brittle materials** (cast iron, concrete, ceramics) — not ductile ones.
- **Two failure criteria** must be checked: tensile ($\sigma_1 = S_{ut}$) and compressive ($\sigma_3 = -S_{uc}$).
- The **factor of safety** is $n = \min\left(\dfrac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_1}, \dfrac{S_{uc}}{\vert\sigma_3\vert}\right)$.
- The **equivalent stress** in MST is simply $\sigma_{eq} = \max(\sigma_1, \vert\sigma_3\vert)$.
- The MST failure envelope in the $\sigma_1$–$\sigma_2$ plane is a **square**, not an ellipse (von Mises) or hexagon (Tresca).
- **Principal stresses** must be computed first from the given stress state using Mohr's circle formulas — MST cannot be applied to raw $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$.
- MST is **conservative for biaxial tension** but **dangerously non-conservative for pure shear in ductile materials** — never apply it to shafts carrying only torque.
- For 2D problems, the principal stress formula $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ is **mandatory** and frequently tested.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximum Strain Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Maximum Strain Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Strain Theory (Saint-Venant's Theory):** A failure hypothesis that states that a ductile or brittle material element under any combination of principal stresses ($\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$) is considered to have **failed** when the **maximum principal strain** ($\varepsilon_1$) in the complex state of stress reaches a value equal to the strain at the **yield point** obtained from a uniaxial tension test on the same material.

Mathematically, the failure criterion is expressed as:

$$\varepsilon_{1} \geq \varepsilon_{y} = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{E}$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_1$ = Maximum principal strain in the complex stress state
- $\varepsilon_y$ = Yield strain from uniaxial tensile test
- $\sigma_y$ = Yield strength of the material
- $E$ = Young's Modulus (modulus of elasticity)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Rubber Band Example):** Imagine you stretch a rubber band with a small pull — it stretches a little.
