<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q1. Define catastrophic failure. List any four strategies employed in engineering design to prevent it.**

**Model Answer:**
> Catastrophic failure is the sudden, total, and unplanned loss of structural integrity that prevents the component from sustaining any further service load, often with endangerment of life.
> Four prevention strategies:
> (i) Material selection with adequate fracture toughness $K_{Ic}$ above the service stress intensity.
> (ii) Application of calibrated Factor of Safety ($FoS \ge 1.5$ for static, $\ge 2$ for dynamic).
> (iii) Damage-tolerant design with defined allowable crack length $a_{allow}$.
> (iv) In-service non-destructive testing at scheduled intervals based on Paris Law integration.
> (v) Fail-safe design with redundant load paths.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Distinguish between *safe-life* and *damage-tolerant* design philosophies. In which type of structure is each preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Safe-Life | Damage-Tolerant |
|---|---|---|
| Philosophy | Replace before crack initiates | Allow crack; inspect; repair; replace |
| Crack assumption | No crack tolerated | Pre-existing crack assumed |
| Inspection | Not required | Required at scheduled intervals |
| Used in | Civil bridges, railway axles, landing gears (older) | Aircraft primary structure (FAR 25.571), pressure vessels, nuclear |
| Failure consequence | Sudden if inspection missed | Graceful, repaired before $a_c$ |
| Cost | High replacement frequency | Lower, but high inspection cost |

Safe-life is preferred for **non-inspectable** components; damage-tolerant is preferred for **inspectable critical** components.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**
> **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A steel plate of width $W = 0.150\,\text{m}$ and thickness $B = 0.020\,\text{m}$ contains a central through-thickness crack of length $2a = 0.012\,\text{m}$. It is subjected to a uniform tensile stress of $\sigma = 220\,\text{MPa}$. The material has $K_{Ic} = 55\,\text{MPa}\sqrt{m}$. Determine:
(i) Whether the plate will fail by brittle fracture.
(ii) The critical crack length $a_c$ at the same stress.
(iii) The factor of safety on $K_{Ic}$ for the current crack size. Take $Y = 1.0$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to the same material, if the loading becomes cyclic with $\Delta\sigma = 180\,\text{MPa}$ and $R = 0$, and the material constants are $C = 6.0 \times 10^{-12}$ (SI units) and $m = 3.0$, compute the number of cycles $N$ required for the crack to grow from $a_i = 0.002\,\text{m}$ to $a_c$. Assume $Y = 1.0$.

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Brittle Fracture Analysis

**Step 1 — Compute present stress intensity factor**

$$K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a} = 1.0 \times 220 \times \sqrt{\pi \times 0.006}$$

$$= 220 \times \sqrt{0.01885} = 220 \times 0.1373 = 30.21\,\text{MPa}\sqrt{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with $K_{Ic}$**

$$K_I = 30.21\,\text{MPa}\sqrt{m} \quad \text{vs} \quad K_{Ic} = 55\,\text{MPa}\sqrt{m}$$

Since $K_I < K_{Ic}$, **the plate will NOT fail by brittle fracture under the current load.**

**Step 3 — Critical crack length**

$$a_c = \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{K_{Ic}}{Y\sigma}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{55}{220}\right)^2 = \frac{(0.25)^2}{\pi} = \frac{0.0625}{\pi} = 0.01989\,\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{a_c \approx 19.89\,\text{mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Factor of safety on $K_{Ic}$**

$$FoS_{LEFM} = \frac{K_{Ic}}{K_I} = \frac{55}{30.21} = 1.82$$

This is **below** the recommended value of 2 for damage-tolerant design, so an inspection regime is mandatory.

#### Part (b) — Crack Growth Cycles

**Step 1 — Re-derive integrated Paris Law for $m = 3$**

$$\int_{a_i}^{a_c} a^{-3/2}\,da = C\,(Y\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi})^m \int_0^N dN$$

$$\left[-2 a^{-1/2}\right]_{a_i}^{a_c} = C\,(Y\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi})^3 \cdot N$$

$$2\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{a_i}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{a_c}}\right) = C\,(Y\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi})^3 \cdot N$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$a_i = 0.002\,\text{m}$, $a_c = 0.01989\,\text{m}$:

$$\sqrt{a_i} = 0.04472\,\sqrt{m}, \quad \sqrt{a_c} = 0.14103\,\sqrt{m}$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{a_i}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{a_c}} = 22.36 - 7.09 = 15.27\,\frac{1}{\sqrt{m}}$$

$$2 \times 15.27 = 30.54\,\frac{1}{\sqrt{m}}$$

$$Y\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi} = 1.0 \times 180 \times 1.7725 = 319.05$$

$$(319.05)^3 = 3.247 \times 10^7$$

$$N = \frac{30.54}{6.0 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.247 \times 10^7} = \frac{30.54}{1.948 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.567 \times 10^{5}\,\text{cycles}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 1.57 \times 10^{5}\,\text{cycles}}$$

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**
> **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A rotating shaft of alloy steel (S-N curve given by $\sigma_a = 800\,(N)^{-0.1}$ MPa, for $N \le 10^6$ cycles) operates at a mean stress $\sigma_m = 200\,\text{MPa}$ and alternating stress $\sigma_a = 300\,\text{MPa}$ at a critical section with a shoulder fillet of radius $\rho = 5\,\text{mm}$. The notch sensitivity is $q = 0.85$ and theoretical $K_t = 2.4$. Compute the fatigue factor of safety using the **Soderberg criterion**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the **damage-tolerant design philosophy** as applied to an aircraft wing. List any four NDT methods used during scheduled inspections.

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Soderberg Fatigue FoS

**Step 1 — Apply Neuber's correction for notch**

$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.85(2.4 - 1) = 1 + 1.19 = 2.19$$

**Step 2 — Effective stresses accounting for notch**

$$\sigma_a^{eff} = K_f \sigma_a = 2.19 \times 300 = 657\,\text{MPa}$$
$$\sigma_m^{eff} = K_f \sigma_m = 2.19 \times 200 = 438\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Endurance limit at $N$**

For $N = 10^6$ cycles: $\sigma_e = 800 \times (10^6)^{-0.1} = 800 \times 0.398 = 318.4\,\text{MPa}$

**Step 4 — Soderberg equation**

$$\frac{\sigma_a^{eff}}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m^{eff}}{\sigma_y} = \frac{1}{FoS}$$

Assuming $\sigma_y = 600\,\text{MPa}$:

$$\frac{657}{318.4} + \frac{438}{600} = 2.064 + 0.730 = 2.794$$

$$FoS = \frac{1}{2.794} = 0.358$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

$$FoS = 0.358 < 1$$

The shaft will fail in fatigue at the notch. The design is **unacceptable**. Remedial actions:
- Increase fillet radius $\rho$ to reduce $K_t$.
- Use surface treatments (shot peening, nitriding) to introduce compressive residual stress.
- Reduce the cyclic load or increase shaft diameter.

#### Part (b) — Damage-Tolerant Design of an Aircraft Wing (Model Sketch in Words)

> A damage-tolerant wing is designed assuming an **initial pre-existing crack** at the most critical (highest stress) location — typically the **fastener holes of the lower wing skin** and the **spar cap run-outs**.
> The structure is divided into **discrepancy zones** (Critical Structural Areas, CSA). Each CSA has:
> (i) A **defined inspection threshold** $a_{th}$ — the smallest crack that can be reliably detected.
> (ii) A **maximum allowable crack** $a_{allow}$ — computed from $K_{Ic}/FoS$ (typically 2 on $K_{Ic}$).
> (iii) A **disposition limit** $a_{disp}$ — when the crack reaches this size, the aircraft is grounded until repair.
>
> The wing has **multiple spars** (front, rear, auxiliary) so that failure of one does not cause wing separation — this is the **fail-safe** feature. **Crack arresters** (stiffeners, integral stringers) are designed to slow crack growth.
>
> **Four NDT methods used:**
> 1. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — for subsurface cracks in spars and skin.
> 2. **Eddy Current Testing (ECT)** — for surface cracks at fastener holes.
> 3. **Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI)** — for surface cracks in ferromagnetic parts.
> 4. **Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT)** — for surface cracks in non-ferrous alloys (aluminium wing skin).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU University Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit inconsistency in $K_{Ic}$ problems** — mixing MPa$\sqrt{m}$ and MPa$\sqrt{mm}$. Convert $a$ to metres when $K_{Ic}$ is in MPa$\sqrt{m}$, OR convert $K_{Ic}$ to MPa$\sqrt{mm}$ first. (1–2 mark deduction each instance.)
> 2. **Forgetting the $Y$ factor** — in central cracks $Y = 1.0$, but for edge cracks $Y = 1.12$, and for double-edge cracks $Y = 1.0$. The examiner will give a different $Y$ in the question — read it carefully.
> 3. **Using $\sigma_y$ instead of $\sigma_{UTS}$ for brittle materials** — for cast iron, ceramics, and concrete, use maximum-normal-stress theory with $\sigma_{UTS}$.
> 4. **Sign error in Soderberg/Goodman line** — both $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m$ must be **positive** (use absolute values).
> 5. **Not integrating Paris Law from $a_i$ to $a_{allow}$** — board answer keys expect the **damage tolerance approach** (use $a_{allow}$, not $a_c$). Using $a_c$ is technically correct but loses marks because it ignores the safety factor.
> 6. **Confusing $\sigma_a$ (alternating) with $\sigma_m$ (mean)** in the Soderberg equation. The alternating term uses the endurance limit; the mean term uses the yield.
> 7. **Forgetting to convert $C$ to consistent units** — Paris Law constants $C$ and $m$ are unit-sensitive. Always state the unit system at the top of the solution.
> 8. **Writing "factor of safety" without showing the substitution** — KTU examiners award marks for the *substitution step*, not just the final answer.
> 9. **Skipping the step "Is it safe to operate?"** — always conclude with an engineering interpretation, not just a number.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PREVENTING CATASTROPHIC FAILURES — RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**
>
> **Core Concepts:**
> - [ ] Catastrophic failure = sudden, total loss of load-bearing capacity; not gradual.
> - [ ] Six prevention levels: Material → FoS → DT → Fail-Safe → NDT → Operations.
> - [ ] Safe-life (no crack) vs Damage-tolerant (assumed crack) — DT is modern for aerospace/nuclear.
>
> **Critical Equations (must memorise):**
> - [ ] $K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ (LEFM)
> - [ ] $a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{Ic}}{Y\sigma}\right)^2$
> - [ ] $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$ (Paris Law)
> - [ ] $N_f = \dfrac{a_c - a_i}{C(Y\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi})^m \cdot a_i^{m/2}}$ (approximate)
> - [ ] $FoS_{Soderberg} = \dfrac{\sigma_y \sigma_e}{\sigma_a \sigma_y + \sigma_m \sigma_e}$
> - [ ] $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ (Neuber's notch correction)
> - [ ] $\dot{\varepsilon}_{min} = A\sigma^n e^{-Q/RT}$ (Norton-Bailey creep)
>
> **Mean-Stress Correction Rules:**
> - [ ] Use **Goodman** for conservative design with brittle materials.
> - [ ] Use **Gerber** for ductile materials.
> - [ ] Use **Soderberg** for elastic design with yield as the limiting mean stress.
> - [ ] Use **Morrow** when $\sigma_f' < \sigma_{UTS}$ (high-strength steels).
>
> **Damage Tolerance Workflow (must reproduce in exams):**
> 1. Define loading spectrum and material.
> 2. Assume initial crack size $a_i$ (from NDT capability).
> 3. Compute $a_c$ from $K_{Ic}$.
> 4. Compute $a_{allow} = a_c$ at $K_{Ic}/FoS$.
> 5. Integrate Paris Law to get $N$ cycles from $a_i$ to $a_{allow}$.
> 6. Set inspection interval = $N/2$ or $N/3$ (with safety factor).
>
> **Prevention Strategy Selection:**
> - [ ] Static + ductile → Yield FoS.
> - [ ] Static + brittle → UTS FoS + $K_{Ic}$ check.
> - [ ] Cyclic HCF → Endurance limit + mean correction.
> - [ ] Cyclic LCF → Strain-life (Coffin-Manson).
> - [ ] Aggressive environment → $K_{ISCC}$ + corrosion allowance.
> - [ ] High T sustained → Larson-Miller / Norton-Bailey.
> - [ ] Cyclic + creep → Ductility exhaustion.
> - [ ] Compression member → Euler buckling + slenderness limit.
>
> **Unit Conversions (Kerala Board favourites):**
> - [ ] $1\,\text{MPa}\sqrt{m} = 0.0316\,\text{MPa}\sqrt{mm}$
> - [ ] $1\,\text{ksi}\sqrt{\text{in}} = 1.0988\,\text{MPa}\sqrt{m}$
> - [ ] $1\,\text{psi}\sqrt{\text{in}} = 1.099 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{MPa}\sqrt{m}$
> - [ ] $R = 8.314\,\text{J/mol·K}$ (gas constant for creep).
>
> **Exam Day Tactical Reminders:**
> - [ ] Always **draw a free-body diagram** of the cracked body.
> - [ ] Always **state the governing equation** before substituting.
> - [ ] Always **state units** in the final answer.
> - [ ] Always **interpret** the numerical result (Safe? Unsafe? Redesign needed?).
> - [ ] If $FoS < 1$ → propose **two remedies** (e.g., increase fillet radius, shot peening, derate, replace material).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fatigue Loading and Life Prediction: Fatigue Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fatigue Loading and Life Prediction: Fatigue Loading ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fatigue Loading** is defined as the application of **time-varying, cyclic, or fluctuating stresses and strains** at a point or critical location in a structural component, where the magnitude is sufficiently high (often below the static yield strength of the material) to initiate and propagate cracks, ultimately leading to sudden and catastrophic fracture after a sufficient number of cycles. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, fatigue is treated as a **progressive, localized structural damage phenomenon** caused by **cyclic loading** at stress levels that would not cause failure under a single static application. The phenomenon is governed by the discipline of **fatigue life prediction**, which estimates the number of cycles to failure ($N_f$) under specified service loading conditions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Paper Clip Test:** > Take a standard metal paper clip and bend it back and forth repeatedly. You will notice two things: > 1. The clip **does not snap on the first bend**, even though you applied enough force to permanently deform it. > 2. After **15–20 bends**, the metal becomes warm at the bend point and **suddenly fractures** without warning. > > This is exactly what happens in machine shafts, aircraft wings, bridge cables, railway axles, and turbine blades. The structure **fails catastrophically** under repeated, sub-yield stress cycles. The material does not "tire out" — instead, **microscopic cracks nucleate, grow stably, and finally propagate unstably**. The key insight for a KTU student is that **fatigue is not a static strength problem** — it is a **damage accumulation problem** over time/cycles. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Endurance Limit (Steels) | $S_e$ or $\sigma_e$ | $0.4S_{ut}$ to $0.6S_{ut}$ | MPa | | Fatigue Strength Coefficient | $\sigma'_f$ | $\approx S_{ut}$ for steels | MPa | | Fatigue Strength Exponent | $b$ | $-0.05$ to $-0.12$ | dimensionless | | Fatigue Ductility Coefficient | $\varepsilon'_f$ | $0.01$ to $1.0$ | dimensionless | | Fatigue Ductility Exponent | $c$ | $-0.5$ to $-0.7$ | dimensionless | | Cycles to Endurance Limit | — | $10^6$ (steels), $5\times10^8$ (Al) | cycles | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Standard Highlight:** The universal benchmark for **endurance limit** in ferrous alloys (steels) is **$10^6$ cycles**, while for non-ferrous alloys (aluminum, copper) there is **no true endurance limit** — designers instead use a **fatigue strength at a specified life** (often $5\times10^8$ or $10^8$ cycles). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fundamental Cyclic Stress Parameters A fatigue loading cycle is characterized by the following core variables. Consider a stress-vs-time history: - **Maximum Stress ($\sigma_{max}$):** The highest algebraic (tensile positive, compressive negative) stress in the cycle. - **Minimum Stress ($\sigma_{min}$):** The lowest algebraic stress in the cycle. - **Mean Stress ($\sigma_m$):** The average of the maximum and minimum stresses. - **Stress Amplitude ($\sigma_a$):** Half of the algebraic range. - **Stress Range ($\sigma_r$):** The algebraic difference between maximum and minimum stress. ### 2.2 Key Cyclic Loading Ratios Two ratios completely describe the *shape* of a fatigue cycle: 1. **Stress Ratio ($R$):** $$R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$$ 2. **Amplitude Ratio ($A$):** $$A = \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_m} = \frac{1-R}{1+R}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Memory Aid:** The stress ratio $R$ alone defines the cycle type. Memorize the four classical cases: > - **Fully Reversed:** $R = -1$ (most damaging for same $S_{ut}$) > - **Zero-to-Tension (Pulsating Tension):** $R = 0$ > - **Zero-to-Compression (Pulsating Compression):** $R = \infty$ > - **Static Load:** $R = +1$ ### 2.3 Classification of Fatigue Loading Fatigue loading is classified into two broad categories: **A) Deterministic (Constant Amplitude) Loading:** - **Fully Reversed Loading:** $\sigma_m = 0$, $\sigma_a = \sigma_{max}$. Example: rotating shaft under bending. - **Pulsating (Fluctuating) Loading:** $\sigma_a > 0$, $\sigma_m > 0$. Example: pressure vessel under internal pressure cycling between $\Delta P$ values. - **Repeated Loading:** $\sigma_a > 0$, $\sigma_m > 0$ but $\sigma_{min} > 0$. **B) Non-Deterministic (Variable Amplitude / Random / Spectrum) Loading:** - **Block Loading:** Stepwise constant amplitude blocks. - **Spectrum Loading:** Continuous probability distribution (e.g., Gaussian). - **Proportional / Non-Proportional Multiaxial Loading:** Principal stress axes rotate. ### 2.4 Engineering Significance of Fatigue Loading > [!NOTE] > **Why is this a KTU Hot Topic?** > - **80–90% of all mechanical failures** in service are attributed to fatigue. > - Fatigue failures occur **without prior warning** (brittle in appearance even for ductile materials). > - Design against fatigue is governed by **damage-tolerant** or **safe-life** philosophies in aeronautical, automotive, civil, and pressure vessel codes. > - KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Classify and define fatigue loading parameters"* and *"Differentiate between deterministic and spectrum loading."* ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Equation | Formula | Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Mean Stress | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | MPa | Algebraic (sign-aware) | | 2 | Stress Amplitude | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | MPa | Always positive | | 3 | Stress Range | $\sigma_r = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$ | MPa | $\sigma_r = 2\sigma_a$ | | 4 | Stress Ratio | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ | — | $-1 \le R \le +1$ for tensile-dominant | | 5 | Amplitude Ratio | $A = \dfrac{1-R}{1+R}$ | — | Zero when $R=1$ | | 6 | Basquin's Law (High Cycle) | $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N)^b$ | MPa | $N \ge 10^3$ cycles | | 7 | Coffin-Manson (Low Cycle) | $\dfrac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f (2N)^c$ | — | $N \le 10^3$ cycles | | 8 | Miner's Cumulative Damage | $D = \sum \dfrac{n_i}{N_i}$ | — | Failure when $D \ge 1$ | | 9 | Goodman Line | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$ | — | Most conservative | | 10 | Gerber Parabola | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1$ | — | Less conservative | | 11 | Soderberg Line | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = 1$ | — | Uses yield, not UTS | | 12 | Modified Goodman (with $\sigma'_f$) | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma'_f} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$ | — | Best for finite life | ### 2.6 The Two-Regime View: LCF vs. HCF | Regime | Symbol | Cycle Range | Dominant Damage | Governing Equation | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Low Cycle Fatigue (LCF)** | LCF | $< 10^3$ to $10^4$ cycles | Plastic strain | Coffin-Manson | | **High Cycle Fatigue (HCF)** | HCF | $> 10^4$ to $10^6$+ cycles | Elastic strain | Basquin / S-N | | **Very High Cycle Fatigue (VHCF)** | VHCF | $> 10^7$ cycles | Sub-surface inclusion | Modified Basquin / $\sqrt{\text{area}}$ | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Cyclic Stress Parameters from Time-Domain Signal Let the applied stress history be a **sinusoidal function of time** $t$, with angular frequency $\omega$: $$\sigma(t) = \sigma_m + \sigma_a \sin(\omega t)$$ We define: - $\sigma_{max}$ occurs when $\sin(\omega t) = +1$, so $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a$. - $\sigma_{min}$ occurs when $\sin(\omega t) = -1$, so $\sigma_{min} = \sigma_m - \sigma_a$. **Step 1 — Add the two extremes:** $$\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min} = (\sigma_m + \sigma_a) + (\sigma_m - \sigma_a) = 2\sigma_m$$ Solving for the mean: $$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Subtract the two extremes:** $$\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min} = (\sigma_m + \sigma_a) - (\sigma_m - \sigma_a) = 2\sigma_a$$ Solving for the amplitude: $$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Derive the Stress Ratio $R$:** $$R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{\sigma_m - \sigma_a}{\sigma_m + \sigma_a}$$ Dividing numerator and denominator by $\sigma_m$: $$\boxed{R = \frac{1 - A}{1 + A}}$$ where $A = \sigma_a / \sigma_m$ is the amplitude ratio. **Step 4 — Derive Amplitude Ratio $A$ from $R$:** $$\boxed{A = \frac{1 - R}{1 + R}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic Check:** For fully reversed loading, $R = -1 \Rightarrow A = \dfrac{1-(-1)}{1+(-1)} = \dfrac{2}{0} \Rightarrow$ undefined. This is **mathematically consistent** because in fully reversed loading $\sigma_m = 0$, so $A = \sigma_a / 0 = \infty$. ### 3.2 Derivation of Basquin's Equation (High Cycle Fatigue) Basquin observed that, on a **log-log plot**, the S-N curve for steels is approximately linear in the HCF regime. **Starting empirical form:** $$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N)^b$$ **Step 1 — Take logarithm of both sides:** $$\log(\sigma_a) = \log(\sigma'_f) + b \log(2N)$$ This confirms that a plot of $\log(\sigma_a)$ vs. $\log(2N)$ is a straight line with: - **Y-intercept:** $\log(\sigma'_f)$ - **Slope:** $b$ (negative, typically $-0.05$ to $-0.12$) **Step 2 — Determine $b$ from two known fatigue data points $(N_1, \sigma_{a1})$ and $(N_2, \sigma_{a2})$:** $$\frac{\sigma_{a1}}{\sigma_{a2}} = \left(\frac{2N_1}{2N_2}\right)^b = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^b$$ Taking natural log: $$\ln\left(\frac{\sigma_{a1}}{\sigma_{a2}}\right) = b \cdot \ln\left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)$$ Solving for $b$: $$\boxed{b = \frac{\ln(\sigma_{a1}/\sigma_{a2})}{\ln(N_1/N_2)}}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation of Miner's Cumulative Damage Rule Consider a component subjected to $k$ distinct stress blocks. Block $i$ has: - $n_i$ = number of applied cycles at amplitude $\sigma_{ai}$ - $N_i$ = number of cycles to failure at that amplitude (from S-N curve) **Assumption (Palmgren-Miner Hypothesis):** The fraction of life consumed per block is $n_i / N_i$, and these fractions are linearly additive. **Step 1 — Total damage $D$:** $$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$ **Step 2 — Failure Criterion:** $$D = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Predicted Failure}$$ $$D < 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Component Survives (with residual life)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Miner's rule does **not** account for load sequence effects. The same total damage $D=1$ can be reached by many combinations, but real fatigue is sensitive to the **order** (high-to-low or low-to-high). KTU examiners often mark students down for claiming Miner's rule is "exact." ### 3.4 Derivation of the Goodman Mean-Stress Correction For a fluctuating cycle ($\sigma_a, \sigma_m$), we wish to find an equivalent fully-reversed amplitude $\sigma_{ar}$ that causes failure in the same number of cycles. **Step 1 — Empirical fit (Goodman, 1899):** The locus of equal-life points on the $\sigma_a$ vs. $\sigma_m$ diagram is a straight line passing through: - $(S_e, 0)$ — i.e., endurance limit under fully reversed - $(0, S_{ut})$ — i.e., ultimate strength under static **Step 2 — Two-point line equation:** $$\frac{\sigma_a - 0}{S_e - 0} = \frac{\sigma_m - 0}{0 - S_{ut}}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $\sigma_a$ when $\sigma_m \neq 0$:** $$\sigma_a = S_e \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)$$ ### 3.5 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical) > [!NOTE] > **Sample Problem:** A steel shaft is subjected to a fluctuating torsional load where the maximum shear stress is $\tau_{max} = 220$ MPa and the minimum is $\tau_{min} = -60$ MPa. For the steel, $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa, $S_y = 380$ MPa, $S_e = 280$ MPa. Find (a) mean, amplitude, range, $R$, and $A$. (b) Factor of safety using Goodman. (c) Factor of safety using Soderberg. **Step (a) — Cyclic Parameters:** $$\tau_m = \frac{220 + (-60)}{2} = 80 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\tau_a = \frac{220 - (-60)}{2} = 140 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\tau_r = 220 - (-60) = 280 \text{ MPa}$$ $$R = \frac{-60}{220} = -0.2727$$ $$A = \frac{1 - (-0.2727)}{1 + (-0.2727)} = \frac{1.2727}{0.7273} = 1.75$$ **Step (b) — Goodman Factor of Safety ($n_G$):** The Goodman relation with safety factor $n$: $$\frac{n\tau_a}{S_e} + \frac{n\tau_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$$ Solving: $$n_G = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\tau_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\tau_m}{S_{ut}}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{140}{280} + \dfrac{80}{600}} = \frac{1}{0.500 + 0.1333} = 1.577$$ **Step (c) — Soderberg Factor of Safety ($n_S$):** $$n_S = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\tau_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\tau_m}{S_y}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{140}{280} + \dfrac{80}{380}} = \frac{1}{0.500 + 0.2105} = 1.406$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result Interpretation:** Goodman is more conservative ($n_G = 1.577$) than Soderberg would be *if both used $S_y$*, but **Soderberg gives a lower $n$** because $S_y < S_{ut}$. Therefore **Soderberg is the more conservative criterion** for ductile materials. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fatigue Loading Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Service Loading History] --> B{Loading Type?} B -- Constant Amplitude --> C[Deterministic Parameters] B -- Variable Amplitude --> D[Spectrum / Random] C --> C1[Max Stress sigma_max] C --> C2[Min Stress sigma_min] C --> C3[Mean Stress sigma_m] C --> C4[Stress Amplitude sigma_a] C --> C5[Stress Ratio R] C1 --> E[Cyclic Stress State Definition] C2 --> E C3 --> E C4 --> E C5 --> E E --> F[Mean Stress Correction] F --> F1[Goodman Line] F --> F2[Gerber Parabola] F --> F3[Soderberg Line] F1 --> G[Equivalent Fully Reversed Amplitude] F2 --> G F3 --> G G --> H[Material S-N Curve] H --> I[Basquin Law sigma_a equals sigma_prime_f times 2N to b] H --> J[Endurance Limit S_e] I --> K[Predicted Fatigue Life N_f] J --> K D --> L[Miner Cumulative Damage Rule] L --> K K --> M{Design Adequate?} M -- Yes --> N[Safe Life Design] M -- No --> O[Redesign or Material Change] ``` ### 4.2 Classification Flow Diagram of Cyclic Loading Types ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Fatigue Loading] --> Q{Stress Ratio R} Q -- R equals minus 1 --> R1[Fully Reversed
sigma_m equals 0
Example: Rotating Shaft] Q -- R equals 0 --> R2[Zero to Tension
Pulsating Tension
Example: Pressure Cycle] Q -- R equals infinity --> R3[Zero to Compression
Pulsating Compression] Q -- R equals plus 1 --> R4[Static Load
No Fatigue] Q -- Negative and less than 0 --> R5[Reversed Partially
Compressive Mean] Q -- Positive and less than 1 --> R6[Repeated Tension
Tensile Mean] ``` ### 4.3 Schematic Representation of Stress-vs-Time Cycles ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph CycleTypes [Five Canonical Fatigue Cycles] direction LR S1[Type 1: Fully Reversed
sigma_max equals plus S
sigma_min equals minus S
R equals minus 1] S2[Type 2: Pulsating Tension
sigma_max equals S
sigma_min equals 0
R equals 0] S3[Type 3: Pulsating Compression
sigma_max equals 0
sigma_min equals minus S
R equals infinity] S4[Type 4: Repeated Tension
sigma_max equals 0.8S
sigma_min equals 0.2S
R equals 0.25] S5[Type 5: Asymmetric Reversed
sigma_max equals 0.6S
sigma_min equals minus 0.3S
R equals minus 0.5] end ``` ### 4.4 Interaction Between Mean Stress Correction Methods ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Given: sigma_a and sigma_m] --> B{Choose Method} B -- Conservative --> C[Goodman Line] B -- Less Conservative --> D[Gerber Parabola] B -- Most Conservative --> E[Soderberg Line] C --> F[Compute Equivalent sigma_ar] D --> F E --> F F --> G[Compare sigma_ar with S_e] G -- sigma_ar less than S_e --> H[Infinite Life] G -- sigma_ar greater than S_e --> I[Finite Life N from S-N] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > **Q1.** Define the term *fatigue loading*. List any four cyclic stress parameters used to characterize it. > > **Model Answer:** > *Fatigue loading* is the application of **time-varying, cyclic stresses** at a point in a component, of magnitude sufficient to cause progressive and localized structural damage and eventual fracture after a sufficient number of cycles, even though the peak stress is below the static yield strength. > > Four cyclic stress parameters are: > 1. Maximum stress $\sigma_{max}$ > 2. Minimum stress $\sigma_{min}$ > 3. Mean stress $\sigma_m$ > 4. Stress amplitude $\sigma_a$ (or stress range $\sigma_r$) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > **Q2.** Distinguish between *fully reversed loading* and *pulsating loading* in fatigue. Write the values of mean stress and stress ratio for each case. > > **Model Answer:** > > | Feature | Fully Reversed | Pulsating (Tension) | > |---|---|---| > | Definition | Stress alternates between equal +ve and -ve values | Stress varies from zero to a maximum tensile value | > | Mean Stress $\sigma_m$ | $0$ | $\sigma_{max}/2$ | > | Stress Ratio $R$ | $-1$ | $0$ | > | Example | Rotating shaft under bending | Pressure vessel under cycling internal pressure | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | 14 Marks]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expressions for mean stress, stress amplitude, stress range, and stress ratio starting from a generic cyclic stress signal $\sigma(t) = \sigma_m + \sigma_a \sin(\omega t)$. Explain the meaning of each parameter with a neat labelled sketch. **(b) [7 Marks)** A steel component is subjected to a fluctuating axial load such that $\sigma_{max} = 350$ MPa and $\sigma_{min} = -150$ MPa. The material properties are: $S_{ut} = 700$ MPa, $S_e = 350$ MPa. Using the **Goodman mean-stress criterion**, determine the factor of safety. Comment on whether the design is safe for infinite life. --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** Given $\sigma(t) = \sigma_m + \sigma_a \sin(\omega t)$. 1. $\sigma_{max}$ occurs at $\sin(\omega t) = +1$: $\quad \sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a$ 2. $\sigma_{min}$ occurs at $\sin(\omega t) = -1$: $\quad \sigma_{min} = \sigma_m - \sigma_a$ 3. Adding: $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$ 4. Subtracting: $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$ 5. Stress range: $\sigma_r = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min} = 2\sigma_a$ 6. Stress ratio: $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ A neat sketch would show a sinusoid oscillating about a horizontal mean line $\sigma_m$, with peak excursions $\pm \sigma_a$, vertical axis labeled $\sigma$ and horizontal axis labeled $t$ or $N$. **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1 — Compute cyclic parameters:** $$\sigma_m = \frac{350 + (-150)}{2} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\sigma_a = \frac{350 - (-150)}{2} = 250 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Goodman with safety factor $n$:** $$\frac{n\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{n\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$$ $$n\left(\frac{250}{350} + \frac{100}{700}\right) = 1$$ $$n(0.7143 + 0.1429) = 1$$ $$n(0.8571) = 1$$ $$\boxed{n = 1.167}$$ **Step 3 — Equivalent fully-reversed stress (verify safety for infinite life):** $$\sigma_{ar} = \frac{\sigma_a}{1 - \sigma_m/S_{ut}} = \frac{250}{1 - 100/700} = \frac{250}{0.8571} = 291.7 \text{ MPa}$$ Since $\sigma_{ar} = 291.7 \text{ MPa} < S_e = 350 \text{ MPa}$, the component has **infinite life**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students mistakenly use $S_y$ instead of $S_{ut}$ in Goodman's relation. **Always use $S_{ut}$ for Goodman**, and **only use $S_y$ for Soderberg**. Mixing these up leads to a 2-mark deduction. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Palmgren-Miner linear damage rule** for variable amplitude fatigue loading. State its assumptions, limitations, and the failure criterion. Derive the cumulative damage index expression. **(b) [7 Marks]** A steel shaft is subjected to a two-block loading spectrum: - Block 1: $\sigma_{a1} = 300$ MPa for $n_1 = 5000$ cycles. - Block 2: $\sigma_{a2} = 200$ MPa for $n_2 = 25000$ cycles. The material S-N curve (in the HCF regime) is given by $\sigma_a = 900 (N)^{-0.1}$ MPa. Using Miner's rule, determine the cumulative damage and comment on the design. --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** The **Palmgren-Miner rule** (also called **Miner's linear damage hypothesis**) states that fatigue damage accumulates linearly with cycles. **Assumptions:** 1. Damage per cycle is linearly proportional to $1/N_i$ (where $N_i$ is cycles to failure at that amplitude). 2. Order of loading does not matter. 3. Failure occurs when total damage equals unity. **Derivation of cumulative damage $D$:** For $k$ distinct loading blocks: $$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$ **Failure Criterion:** - $D < 1$: Safe (residual life remains). - $D = 1$: Predicted failure. - $D > 1$: Overload / under-design. **Limitations:** 1. Ignores **load sequence** effects (high-low vs low-high gives different real lives). 2. Does not account for **mean stress** variations between blocks. 3. Inaccurate for **low-cycle fatigue** (plasticity-dominated regime). 4. Empirically, observed $D$ at failure ranges from 0.7 to 2.0, not exactly 1.0. **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1 — From the S-N equation $\sigma_a = 900 N^{-0.1}$, find $N_i$:** For Block 1: $300 = 900 \, N_1^{-0.1}$ $$N_1^{-0.1} = 0.3333 \Rightarrow N_1 = (0.3333)^{-10} = 59049 \text{ cycles}$$ For Block 2: $200 = 900 \, N_2^{-0.1}$ $$N_2^{-0.1} = 0.2222 \Rightarrow N_2 = (0.2222)^{-10} = 2.82 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Miner's rule:** $$D = \frac{n_1}{N_1} + \frac{n_2}{N_2} = \frac{5000}{59049} + \frac{25000}{2.82\times10^6}$$ $$D = 0.0847 + 0.00887 = 0.0936$$ **Step 3 — Conclusion:** $$\boxed{D = 0.0936 \ll 1}$$ The component has consumed only **~9.4%** of its fatigue life. The design is **safe**, with substantial residual capacity. The designer could either accept the design or consider increasing loads for better material utilization. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to **convert the S-N formula's exponent** correctly. If the equation is $\sigma_a = C \cdot N^b$ with $b$ in decimal, you must use $\log_{10}$ and raise to $1/b$. A common mistake is writing $N_1 = (300/900)^{10}$ which **gives the wrong number**. Correct is $N_1 = (900/300)^{10}$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (For Last-Minute KTU Prep)** - [x] Fatigue = **cyclic loading below yield** that causes progressive damage and sudden fracture. - [x] Always remember the **four pillars**: $\sigma_m, \sigma_a, \sigma_r, R$. - [x] Formulas (memorize verbatim): - $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$ - $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$ - $\sigma_r = 2\sigma_a$ - $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$ - $A = (1-R)/(1+R)$ - [x] **Fully reversed** ⇒ $R = -1$, $\sigma_m = 0$, infinite life if $\sigma_a < S_e$. - [x] **Endurance limit** $S_e$ applies only to **ferrous (steel) alloys** at $10^6$ cycles. - [x] **Basquin's Law** governs **HCF** ($\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N)^b$), **Coffin-Manson** governs **LCF**. - [x] **Goodman** = conservative linear (uses $S_{ut}$); **Gerber** = less conservative parabolic; **Soderberg** = most conservative (uses $S_y$). - [x] **Miner's rule**: $D = \sum n_i/N_i = 1$ at failure. - [x] 80–90% of all mechanical failures in service are **fatigue-related**. - [x] Fatigue cracks **initiate at surfaces** — surface finish and residual stress matter enormously. - [x] Mean stress is **harmful** if tensile — increases fatigue damage. - [x] Compressive mean stress is **beneficial** — shot peening uses this principle. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Tip:** Whenever a numerical problem on fatigue loading is asked, **always start by computing $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$ first** — almost every subsequent step depends on them. Draw a clear stress-vs-time sketch and label it. Examiners reward **clarity and method** as much as the final answer. ---

topics.title: High Cycle Fatigue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** High Cycle Fatigue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Cycle Fatigue (HCF)** is a regime of fatigue failure characterized by a **large number of stress cycles (typically $N_f > 10^4$ to $10^5$)** before fracture occurs, under relatively **low cyclic stress amplitudes** where the deformation behavior remains predominantly **elastic** (i.e., the cyclic stress is below the macroscopic yield strength of the material).

In the HCF regime, the fatigue life is governed primarily by the **initiation and slow propagation** of a single dominant crack, with negligible plastic strain accumulation per cycle. This is in contrast to **Low Cycle Fatigue (LCF)**, where strains are macroscopic-plastic and lives are short ($N_f < 10^4$).

The most fundamental representation of HCF behavior is the **Stress-Life (S-N) Curve**, also called the **Wöhler Curve**, which plots the cyclic stress amplitude $\sigma_a$ (or $\sigma_{max}$) against the number of cycles to failure $N_f$ on a semi-logarithmic scale.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Paper Clip Analogy:** Imagine repeatedly bending a paper clip back and forth by a *very small angle*. The clip never visibly deforms — it springs back elastically each time. Yet after perhaps 30–50 bends (well, that's actually LCF for paper clip steel!), imagine bending a *much stiffer* spring-steel wire by an *almost imperceptible* amount, thousands of times per minute. The wire remains perfectly straight to the eye, behaving elastically, but microscopic damage accumulates invisibly until — without warning — it snaps. This is **High Cycle Fatigue**. The dangerous aspect is the *invisibility*: failure occurs at stresses far below the material's yield strength, often at 30–50% of the ultimate tensile strength, governed by atomic-scale slip reversals, not by visible plastic flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Significance:** Aircraft wings, turbine blades, rotating shafts, bridge cables, railway axles, and pressure vessel walls all operate in the HCF regime. The famous **De Havilland Comet disasters (1954)** and the **Aloha Airlines Flight 243 (1988)** are textbook cases of HCF-driven catastrophic failure due to cyclic pressurization.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|--------|--------|----------------------|
| Fatigue Strength Coefficient | $\sigma'_f$ | **MPa** |
| Fatigue Strength Exponent | $b$ | $\approx -0.05$ to $-0.12$ (dimensionless) |
| Fatigue Strength at $2\times10^6$ cycles | $S'_f$ | **MPa** |
| Endurance Limit (Ferrous) | $S_e$ or $\sigma_e$ | **MPa** ($\approx 0.5\,S_{ut}$ for steels) |
| Fatigue Ratio | $S_e / S_{ut}$ | **0.25 – 0.6** (typically **0.5**) |
| Cycle Count Threshold (HCF/LCF boundary) | $N_t$ | **$10^4$ – $10^5$ cycles** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The S-N (Wöhler) Curve — Conceptual Breakdown

The S-N curve is constructed by subjecting polished, standardized specimens to rotating-beam or axial fatigue tests at constant amplitude ($R = -1$, fully reversed loading). For each stress amplitude $\sigma_a$, the number of cycles to failure $N_f$ is recorded.

**Step-by-step logic of curve interpretation:**

1. **Steep slope (low $N_f$):** At high stress amplitudes near $S_{ut}$, the curve drops steeply — small increases in stress cause large reductions in life.
2. **Linear region on semi-log plot (HCF):** For $N_f \approx 10^3$ to $10^6$, the curve is approximately linear when $\sigma_a$ is plotted against $\log N_f$ — this is the **Basquin regime**.
3. **Knee/Endurance limit (for steels only):** For ferrous metals and certain titanium alloys, the curve flattens at a stress called the **endurance limit $S_e$**, below which theoretically infinite life is expected.
4. **No true endurance limit (for non-ferrous):** Aluminum, copper, and most non-ferrous alloys show a continuously descending curve; designers specify a finite life (e.g., $10^8$ cycles) and call that the **fatigue strength at $N$ cycles**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why steels show an endurance limit but aluminum does not:** Steels contain interstitial carbon/nitrogen atoms that pin dislocations after cyclic "shakedown," effectively arrest crack initiation below a threshold stress. Non-ferrous alloys lack this mechanism; cracks continue to nucleate at decreasing stress levels indefinitely.

## 2.2 Basquin's Law (High-Cycle Stress-Life Relation)

The HCF regime is mathematically described by **Basquin's Law** (1910):

$$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f \, (2N_f)^b$$

Where:
- $\sigma_a$ = stress amplitude (alternating stress) in MPa
- $\sigma'_f$ = fatigue strength coefficient, approximately equal to the true fracture strength $\sigma_f \approx S_{ut} + 345$ MPa (for SI units)
- $b$ = fatigue strength exponent (Basquin's exponent), negative, typically $-0.05$ to $-0.12$
- $N_f$ = cycles to failure

In linear (semi-log) form:

$$\log \sigma_a = \log \sigma'_f + b \log (2N_f)$$

## 2.3 Mean Stress Corrections (Critical for HCF Design!)

In real applications, most cyclic loads are **not fully reversed** ($R \neq -1$). A **mean stress $\sigma_m$** shifts the S-N curve. Three classical correction theories are universally tested in KTU:

### (a) **Soderberg Criterion** (Most Conservative)
$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \frac{1}{n}$$

### (b) **Goodman Criterion** (Most Widely Used in Design)
$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

### (c) **Gerber Criterion** (Least Conservative — Best Fit for Ductile Materials)
$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{n}$$

Where $n$ is the **factor of safety** against fatigue failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goodman vs Gerber (Exam Favorite!):** For tensile mean stresses, **Gerber predicts higher allowable alternating stress** (less conservative). For compressive mean stresses, **Goodman is preferred** (Gerber can give unphysical predictions). Designers commonly use Goodman for safety; Gerber is used when material behavior validates parabolic fit (ductile metals with experimental data).

## 2.4 Modified Goodman with Factors of Safety

For a rotating shaft (combined steady + bending), the standard KTU equation is:

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\left[\frac{1}{S_e}\sqrt{4(K_f M_a)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_a)^2} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right]$$

## 2.5 Marin Equation for Modified Endurance Limit (Critical KTU Topic!)

The theoretical *smooth, ideal* endurance limit $S'_e = 0.5\,S_{ut}$ is reduced by a series of **modification factors**:

$$S_e = k_a \, k_b \, k_c \, k_d \, k_e \, k_f \, S'_e = 0.5 \, S_{ut} \cdot k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f$$

| Factor | Symbol | Accounts for |
|--------|--------|--------------|
| Surface factor | $k_a$ | Surface finish (machining, grinding, polishing) |
| Size factor | $k_b$ | Component diameter/size effect |
| Loading factor | $k_c$ | Type of loading (bending, axial, torsion) |
| Temperature factor | $k_d$ | Operating temperature |
| Reliability factor | $k_e$ | Statistical reliability (50%, 90%, 99%, 99.9%) |
| Miscellaneous factor | $k_f$ | Stress concentration, corrosion, fretting |

### Surface Factor (Shigley / Faires):
$$k_a = a \, S_{ut}^b$$

Where $(a, b)$ depend on the surface finish:
- **Ground:** $a = 1.58$, $b = -0.085$ (for $S_{ut}$ in kpsi; convert for MPa)
- **Machined or Cold-drawn:** $a = 2.70$, $b = -0.265$ (kpsi)
- **Hot-rolled:** $a = 14.4$, $b = -0.718$ (kpsi)
- **As-forged:** $a = 39.9$, $b = -0.995$ (kpsi)

### Size Factor (for $2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm):
$$k_b = 0.879 \, d^{-0.107}$$

For $d \le 2.79$ mm: $k_b = 1.0$. For $d > 51$ mm to 254 mm: $k_b = 0.91 \, d^{-0.157}$.

### Loading Factor:
- **Pure bending / Reversed bending:** $k_c = 1.0$
- **Pure axial (push-pull):** $k_c = 0.85$
- **Pure torsion:** $k_c = 0.59$ (for ductile metals)

### Reliability Factor (one-sided normal distribution):
| Reliability % | $k_e$ |
|---------------|-------|
| 50 | 1.000 |
| 90 | 0.897 |
| 95 | 0.868 |
| 99 | 0.814 |
| 99.9 | 0.753 |
| 99.99 | 0.702 |

### Temperature Factor:
$$k_d = \frac{S_{ut,T}}{S_{ut,RT}}$$

(Ratio of UTS at operating to room temperature.)

## 2.6 Stress Concentration and Notch Sensitivity

A stress raiser (hole, keyway, fillet) creates a local **fatigue stress concentration factor $K_f$**:

$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$$

Where:
- $K_t$ = theoretical (geometric) stress concentration factor (from charts)
- $q$ = **notch sensitivity** ($0 \le q \le 1$)

For $q = 1$: $K_f = K_t$ (fully sensitive notch)
For $q = 0$: $K_f = 1$ (no notch effect)

An approximate empirical expression for $q$ (Neuber's constant method):

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a / r}}$$

Where $a$ is Neuber's constant (material-dependent, in mm) and $r$ is the notch radius.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$ | Basquin's law (HCF life) |
| 2 | $S'_e = 0.5 S_{ut}$ | Ideal endurance limit (steel) |
| 3 | $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S'_e$ | Modified endurance limit (Marin) |
| 4 | $k_a = a S_{ut}^b$ | Surface finish factor |
| 5 | $k_b = 0.879 d^{-0.107}$ | Size factor ($2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm) |
| 6 | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | Fatigue stress concentration |
| 7 | $q = 1/(1 + \sqrt{a/r})$ | Notch sensitivity (Neuber) |
| 8 | $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1/n$ | Goodman line |
| 9 | $\sigma_a/S_e + (\sigma_m/S_{ut})^2 = 1/n$ | Gerber parabola |
| 10 | $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_y = 1/n$ | Soderberg (most conservative) |
| 11 | $\sigma_a = S'_e (2N_f)^b$ | Stress-life with Marin $S_e$ |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

HCF analysis is the **backbone of infinite-life design philosophy** in aerospace, automotive, and rotating machinery. Almost every KTU numerical will use a combination of Marin factors + Goodman/Gerber + stress concentration. Critical industrial applications:

- **Aeroengine LP/HP turbine discs** — HCF at $10^9$ cycles is the limit.
- **Helicopter rotor masts** — HCF dominates certification (FAR 29.571).
- **Rolling contact bearings** — Subsurface HCF (classical "race fatigue").
- **Bone implants (hip stems)** — $10^7$–$10^8$ cycles at low stress.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Basquin's Law from Empirical S-N Data

The S-N curve in HCF region is linear when plotted as $\sigma_a$ vs $\log N_f$. Let us derive the closed-form.

**Step 1:** Consider two known points on the S-N curve (typically $N_1 = 1$, $\sigma_{a1} = \sigma'_f$ and an arbitrary point $N_2 = N_f$, $\sigma_{a2} = \sigma_a$). The linear relation on semi-log axes is:

$$\log \sigma_a = \log \sigma'_f + b \log N_f$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $\sigma_a$ at $N_f = 1/2$ cycle equals the fatigue strength coefficient $\sigma'_f$ (intercept). Slope on log-log axes is the fatigue strength exponent $b$.

**Step 3:** Rewrite as:

$$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f \, N_f^b$$

**Step 4:** Account for the convention that failure is often defined at half-cycle in fully reversed loading ($N_f$ cycles = $2N_f$ reversals). Substitute $N_f \rightarrow 2N_f$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_a = \sigma'_f \, (2N_f)^b}$$

**Step 5:** Verify dimensions: $b$ is dimensionless, $\sigma'_f$ has units of MPa — consistent.

## 3.2 Derivation of Goodman's Fatigue Strength Equation (for given $N_f$)

When mean stress $\sigma_m > 0$ and alternating stress $\sigma_a$ is unknown, but life $N_f$ is specified:

**Step 1:** Compute the modified endurance limit $S_e$ using Marin factors.

**Step 2:** Apply Basquin's law at the specified $N_f$ to get the zero-mean-stress fatigue strength:

$$\sigma_{ar} = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b \quad \text{(fully reversed equivalent)}$$

**Step 3:** For arbitrary $R$ ratio, the equivalent fully reversed stress amplitude is:

$$\sigma_{ar} = \frac{\sigma_a}{1 - (\sigma_m / \sigma'_f)}$$

**Step 4:** Set this equal to the modified endurance limit $S_e$ at the design life:

$$\sigma_a = S_e \left[1 - \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma'_f}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5:** In design form (with factor of safety $n$):

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma'_f} = \frac{1}{n}$$

This is the **Goodman relation with Basquin correction** — frequently tested in KTU.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Marin Factors + Goodman

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A steel shaft ($S_{ut} = 700$ MPa, $S_y = 490$ MPa) is 50 mm diameter, machined surface, subjected to bending. Operating temperature 50°C, reliability 99%, with a shoulder fillet producing $K_t = 2.0$, notch radius $r = 3$ mm, and Neuber's $a = 0.5$ mm. Bending stress: $\sigma_a = 200$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 150$ MPa. Find factor of safety using Goodman.

**Solution — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1:** Compute ideal endurance limit:

$$S'_e = 0.5 \times 700 = 350 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Surface factor (machined, $S_{ut}$ in kpsi):
- Convert: $S_{ut} = 700$ MPa $= 101.5$ kpsi
- $k_a = 2.70 \times (101.5)^{-0.265} = 2.70 \times 0.3308 = 0.893$

**Step 3:** Size factor ($d = 50$ mm, between 2.79 and 51 mm):

$$k_b = 0.879 \times (50)^{-0.107} = 0.879 \times 0.6411 = 0.563$$

**Step 4:** Loading factor (bending): $k_c = 1.0$

**Step 5:** Temperature factor (50°C ≈ room temp for steel): $k_d \approx 1.0$

**Step 6:** Reliability factor (99%): $k_e = 0.814$

**Step 7:** Notch sensitivity:

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{0.5/3}} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{0.1667}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.4082} = 0.710$$

**Step 8:** Fatigue stress concentration:

$$K_f = 1 + 0.710 \times (2.0 - 1) = 1.710$$

**Step 9:** Miscellaneous factor (including $K_f$):

$$k_f = \frac{1}{K_f} = \frac{1}{1.710} = 0.585$$

**Step 10:** Modified endurance limit:

$$S_e = 0.893 \times 0.563 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.814 \times 0.585 \times 350$$

$$S_e = 350 \times 0.2506 = 87.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 11:** Goodman factor of safety:

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{200}{87.7} + \frac{150}{700} = 2.280 + 0.214 = 2.494$$

$$n = \frac{1}{2.494} = 0.401$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $n = 0.40 < 1$. The shaft **fails** in fatigue under Goodman criterion. Engineer must increase diameter, improve surface finish, or reduce stress. This negative result is intentional — it teaches the student to identify unsafe designs.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for HCF Problem

| Given | Use This Method |
|-------|-----------------|
| $\sigma_a$, $\sigma_m$, $S_{ut}$, surface, size, etc. | **Goodman/Gerber** with Marin $S_e$ |
| $\sigma_a$, $N_f$, $\sigma_m = 0$, $S_{ut}$ | **Basquin's law** + Marin |
| $S_e$, $N_f$, design stress | $S_f = a N_f^b$ then Goodman |
| Variable amplitude | **Miner's rule** (cumulative damage) |
| Finite life desired | Use $\sigma_a = S_f$ at $N$ cycles |
| Infinite life desired | Use $S_e$ (with Marin factors) |
| Rotating shaft (bending + torsion) | **von Mises equivalent** $\sigma'_a = \sqrt{\sigma_a^2 + 3\tau_a^2}$ |

## 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation — Notch Sensitivity (Neuber)

**Step 1:** Neuber's experimental observation: the **fatigue notch factor $K_f$** lies between 1 (no notch effect) and $K_t$ (fully sharp).

**Step 2:** Empirical relation:

$$K_f = 1 + \frac{K_t - 1}{1 + \sqrt{a/r}}$$

**Step 3:** Define notch sensitivity:

$$q = \frac{K_f - 1}{K_t - 1}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting:

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a/r}}$$

**Step 5:** For very large notch radius ($r \to \infty$): $q \to 0$, $K_f \to 1$ (no notch effect — expected).

**Step 6:** For very sharp notch ($r \to 0$): $q \to 1$, $K_f \to K_t$ (fully sensitive — expected).

**Step 7:** Typical Neuber constant values:
- Steel: $a \approx 0.25$ to $1.0$ mm (depends on UTS)
- Aluminum: $a \approx 0.5$ to 2.0 mm

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High Cycle Fatigue Design Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Loading and Material] --> B{Ductile or Brittle?}
    B --> C[Determine Sut, Sy, Surface, Geometry]
    C --> D[Compute Ideal Endurance Limit Se_prime = 0.5 Sut]
    D --> E[Apply Marin Modification Factors]
    E --> E1[Surface Factor ka]
    E --> E2[Size Factor kb]
    E --> E3[Loading Factor kc]
    E --> E4[Temperature Factor kd]
    E --> E5[Reliability Factor ke]
    E --> E6[Miscellaneous kf including Kf]
    E1 --> F[Compute Modified Se]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    E6 --> F
    F --> G{Mean Stress sigma_m Known?}
    G -- No --> H[Apply Goodman or Gerber Criterion]
    G -- Yes --> I[Determine Stress Amplitude sigma_a]
    I --> H
    H --> J[Compute Factor of Safety n]
    J --> K{n Greater Than or Equal to 1?}
    K -- Yes --> L[SAFE: Design Acceptable]
    K -- No --> M[UNSAFE: Redesign Required]
    M --> N[Increase Size or Improve Finish]
    N --> C
```

## 4.2 S-N Curve Schematic (Wohler Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Stress sigma_a near Sut] -->|Few cycles 10 to 1000| B[Nf: Low Cycle Fatigue Region]
    B -->|Basquin Linear Region 10 cubed to 10 to the 6| C[High Cycle Fatigue Region]
    C -->|Knee at 10 to the 6 to 10 to the 7| D[Endurance Limit Se for Steel]
    D -->|Infinite Life| E[Horizontal Line: No Failure]
    F[Aluminum Non Ferrous] -->|Continuous Decline| G[No True Endurance Limit]
    G -->|10 to the 8 cycles| H[Design Fatigue Strength at N]
```

## 4.3 Mean Stress Correction Diagram (Haigh Diagram Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vertical Axis: sigma_a Alternating Stress] --> B[Origin]
    B --> C[Horizontal Axis: sigma_m Mean Stress]
    C --> D[Yield Sy on Right End]
    D --> E[UTS Sut to the Right of Sy]
    E --> F[Ultimate Tensile Strength Sut on Right]
    F --> G[Soderberg Line through Sy and Se]
    G --> H[Goodman Line through Sut and Se]
    H --> I[Gerber Parabola through Sut and Se]
    I --> J[Soderberg Most Conservative]
    J --> K[Goodman Most Used in Design]
    K --> L[Gerber Least Conservative for Ductile]
```

## 4.4 Marin Factor Reduction Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[S prime e equals 0.5 Sut] --> B[Multiply by ka: Surface Finish]
    B --> C[Multiply by kb: Size]
    C --> D[Multiply by kc: Loading Type]
    D --> E[Multiply by kd: Temperature]
    E --> F[Multiply by ke: Reliability]
    F --> G[Multiply by kf: Miscellaneous including Stress Concentration]
    G --> H[Final Modified Endurance Limit Se]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Failure Analysis Hierarchy in HCF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fatigue Failure Analysis] --> B[Identify Loading Type]
    B --> B1[Reversed Bending R minus 1]
    B --> B2[Axial Push Pull]
    B --> B3[Rotating Bending]
    B --> B4[Torsion]
    A --> C[Identify Stress States]
    C --> C1[Mean Stress]
    C --> C2[Alternating Stress]
    C --> C3[Stress Concentration Sites]
    A --> D[Apply Failure Criterion]
    D --> D1[Soderberg]

    D --> D3[Gerber]
    D --> D4[Morrow]

```
