topics.title: Factors Affecting S-N-P Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting S-N-P Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of S-N-P Curves

The **S-N-P Curve** (Stress–Number of cycles–Probability Curve) is a probabilistic extension of the classical Wöhler S-N curve used in fatigue analysis. While the conventional S-N curve represents a deterministic relationship between cyclic stress amplitude ($S$) and the number of cycles to failure ($N$), the **S-N-P curve incorporates a third dimension—probability ($P$)**—to account for the statistical scatter inherent in fatigue test data.

Mathematically, an S-N-P relationship is expressed as:

$$S_a = f(N, P)$$

where:
- $S_a$ = Alternating stress amplitude
- $N$ = Number of cycles to failure
- $P$ = Probability of survival (typically expressed as a percentage such as 50%, 90%, 95%, or 99%)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The S-N-P framework is the **statistical backbone of Probabilistic Fatigue Design**. It allows engineers to design components not for "average" failure, but for a defined **reliability target**, which is critical for safety-critical components in aerospace, automotive, and power-plant industries.

### 1.2 The Need for S-N-P Curves (Conceptual Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Lifespan of Light Bulbs":**
> Imagine you test 100 identical incandescent bulbs. They don't all fail at exactly 1000 hours — some burn out at 800 hours, others last 1500 hours. If you plot "lifespan vs. number of bulbs that survived that long," you get a probability distribution. **S-N-P curves do exactly the same thing for materials under fatigue loading** — they acknowledge that identical specimens under identical loading will fail at different cycle counts, and they quantify this scatter.

### 1.3 Statistical Distribution of Fatigue Life

Fatigue life $N$ is typically modeled using a **log-normal distribution** (when the logarithm of cycles, $\log_{10} N$, is normally distributed) or a **Weibull distribution** (especially for the finite-life and high-cycle regimes).

The log-normal probability density function is:

$$f(N) = \frac{1}{N \cdot \sigma_{\log N} \cdot \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(\log N - \mu_{\log N})^2}{2\sigma_{\log N}^2}\right]$$

where:
- $\mu_{\log N}$ = Mean of $\log_{10} N$
- $\sigma_{\log N}$ = Standard deviation of $\log_{10} N$

> [!TIP]
> The scatter in $\log_{10} N$ for metallic materials typically lies in the range **$\sigma_{\log N} \approx 0.1$ to $0.2$** (this corresponds to a factor of 1.26 to 1.58 scatter band on $N$). This is a **standard KTU-referenced metric** for ductile metals in the high-cycle fatigue regime.

### 1.4 Standard Probability Levels in Design

| Probability of Survival ($P$) | Reliability Level | Typical Application |
|:-----------------------------:|:-----------------:|:--------------------|
| **50%** | Median | Research baseline, comparative testing |
| **90%** | Standard commercial | General machinery, automotive non-critical parts |
| **95%** | High reliability | Automotive drivetrain, structural components |
| **99%** | Very high reliability | Aircraft primary structures, nuclear components |
| **99.9%** | Ultra-high reliability | Military aerospace, spacecraft |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Form of the S-N-P Curve

The S-N-P relationship combines the classical **Basquin's Law** with a statistical probability term. For the high-cycle fatigue regime ($N > 10^3$ cycles), the mean S-N line follows:

$$S_a = \sigma'_f \cdot (2N_f)^b$$

where:
- $\sigma'_f$ = Fatigue strength coefficient (intercept at $2N_f = 1$)
- $b$ = Fatigue strength exponent (typically $-0.05$ to $-0.12$ for steels)
- $N_f$ = Number of cycles to failure

The **probabilistic extension** for a given survival probability $P$ is:

$$S_{a,P} = \sigma'_f \cdot (2N_f)^b \cdot \exp\left[Z_P \cdot \sigma_{\log N} \cdot b \cdot \ln(10)\right]$$

where $Z_P$ is the standard normal variate corresponding to survival probability $P$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind S-N-P Curve Construction

1. **Specimen Preparation:** A batch of identical specimens (typically 20–30 minimum for statistical validity) is prepared from the same material lot.
2. **Fatigue Testing:** Each specimen is tested at a constant stress amplitude $S_a$ until failure (or runout at $10^6$–$10^7$ cycles).
3. **Recording Failures:** The number of cycles to failure $N_f$ is recorded for each specimen at each stress level.
4. **Statistical Analysis:** For a given $S_a$, the mean $\mu_{\log N}$ and standard deviation $\sigma_{\log N}$ of $\log N$ are computed.
5. **Probability Calculation:** For a target survival probability $P$ (e.g., 95%), the corresponding $N_P$ is found using:
$$N_P = 10^{\,\mu_{\log N} + Z_P \cdot \sigma_{\log N}}$$
6. **Curve Plotting:** The points $(S_a, N_P)$ are plotted for multiple $P$ levels (50%, 90%, 95%, 99%) on semi-log axes, generating a **family of S-N-P curves**.

### 2.3 Factors Affecting S-N-P Curves — Comprehensive Listing

#### 2.3.1 Mean Stress ($S_m$)

Mean stress is the most influential factor on fatigue life. The stress ratio $R$ is defined as:

$$R = \frac{S_{\min}}{S_{\max}} = \frac{S_m - S_a}{S_m + S_a}$$

Three classical mean-stress correction criteria:

| Criterion | Equation | Region of Validity |
|:---------:|:--------:|:------------------:|
| **Soderberg** | $\dfrac{S_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{S_m}{S_y} = 1$ | Conservative (most KTU board answers prefer this) |
| **Goodman** | $\dfrac{S_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{S_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$ | Widely used in industry |
| **Gerber** | $\dfrac{S_a}{S_e} + \left(\dfrac{S_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1$ | Best fit for ductile materials |

where $S_e$ is the **endurance limit**, $S_y$ is yield strength, and $S_{ut}$ is ultimate tensile strength.

#### 2.3.2 Stress Concentration (Notch Effect)

Notches reduce fatigue strength. The **fatigue notch factor** $K_f$ is defined as:

$$K_f = \frac{S_e \text{ (unnotched)}}{S_e \text{ (notched)}} = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$$

where:
- $K_t$ = Theoretical (geometric) stress concentration factor
- $q$ = Notch sensitivity (0 ≤ q ≤ 1)

For sharp notches, $q \to 1$, and $K_f \approx K_t$. For mild notches, $q \to 0$, and the notch has negligible effect.

**Peterson's Equation** (alternative form):

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a/r}}$$

where $a$ is a material constant (Neuber's constant) and $r$ is the notch radius.

#### 2.3.3 Surface Finish ($K_{sf}$)

Surface roughness reduces fatigue strength. The **surface finish factor** is:

$$K_{sf} = \frac{S_e \text{ (actual surface)}}{S_e \text{ (polished/mirror)}}$$

Approximate values (Shigley's reference):

| Surface Finish | $K_{sf}$ (for $S_{ut} = 700$ MPa) |
|:--------------:|:----------------------------------:|
| Polished/Mirror | 1.00 |
| Ground | 0.89 |
| Machined/Cold-drawn | 0.84 |
| Hot-rolled | 0.71 |
| As-forged | 0.65 |
| Corroded | 0.38 |

#### 2.3.4 Size Effect ($K_{size}$)

Larger components have lower fatigue strength due to:
- **Statistical size effect:** Higher probability of finding a critical flaw
- **Gradient size effect:** Stress gradient in bending/torsion is non-uniform

$$K_{size} = 0.869 \cdot d^{-0.097} \quad (5.08 \text{ mm} \leq d \leq 250 \text{ mm, non-rotating bending})$$

For axial loading, $K_{size} \approx 1.0$ (no size effect for uniform stress).

#### 2.3.5 Loading Mode ($K_{load}$)

| Loading Type | $K_{load}$ Factor |
|:------------:|:-----------------:|
| Axial loading | 0.85 |
| Pure bending | 1.00 (reference) |
| Pure torsion | 0.59 (for ductile) to 0.85 |

#### 2.3.6 Temperature

Elevated temperatures reduce endurance limit. The empirical relationship:

$$S_{e,T} = S_{e,\text{room}} \cdot \frac{S_{y,T}}{S_{y,\text{room}}}$$

For cryogenic temperatures, fatigue strength typically **increases** for BCC metals.

#### 2.3.7 Corrosive Environment

A combined corrosion-fatigue correction factor $K_{\text{corr}} \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ is applied. Crucially, **corrosion eliminates the true endurance limit** — the S-N curve becomes continuously descending.

#### 2.3.8 Frequency of Loading

For steels, frequency has minimal effect between 1 Hz and 200 Hz. For aluminum, similar trends are observed. For corrosive environments, **lower frequencies** drastically reduce life (more time for chemical attack per cycle).

#### 2.3.9 Residual Stresses

Compressive residual stresses (from shot peening, autofrettage, cold working) **enhance** fatigue strength. Tensile residual stresses are detrimental.

#### 2.3.10 Microstructure and Material Inhomogeneity

- Grain size (Hall-Petch effect): Finer grains → higher fatigue strength
- Inclusions and voids act as fatigue crack initiation sites
- Heat treatment state dramatically alters $\sigma'_f$ and $b$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Table — All Formulas for S-N-P and Modifying Factors**

| # | Formula | Description | Key Variables |
|:-:|:-------:|:-----------:|:-------------:|
| 1 | $S_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$ | Basquin's equation (mean S-N) | $\sigma'_f, b$ |
| 2 | $\log S_a = \log \sigma'_f + b \log(2N_f)$ | Log-linear form | — |
| 3 | $N_P = 10^{(\mu_{\log N} + Z_P \sigma_{\log N})}$ | Cycles at probability $P$ | $Z_P$ from std normal table |
| 4 | $R = (S_m - S_a)/(S_m + S_a)$ | Stress ratio | $R = -1$ fully reversed |
| 5 | $S_a/S_e + S_m/S_y = 1$ | Soderberg line | Conservative |
| 6 | $S_a/S_e + S_m/S_{ut} = 1$ | Goodman line | Industry standard |
| 7 | $S_a/S_e + (S_m/S_{ut})^2 = 1$ | Gerber parabola | Ductile materials |
| 8 | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | Fatigue notch factor | $q$ = notch sensitivity |
| 9 | $q = 1/(1 + \sqrt{a/r})$ | Peterson's equation | $a$ = Neuber's const |
| 10 | $K_{sf} = a \cdot S_{ut}^b$ | Surface finish factor | $a, b$ from tables |
| 11 | $S_e = K_{sf} K_{size} K_{load} K_{temp} K_{\text{corr}} \cdot S'_e$ | Modified endurance limit | Marin equation |
| 12 | $f(N) = \frac{1}{N \sigma_{\log N} \sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(\log N - \mu_{\log N})^2 / 2\sigma_{\log N}^2}$ | Log-normal PDF of $N$ | — |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** S-N-P at 99.9% reliability is mandatory for turbine disks and wing spars.
- **Automotive:** Crankshafts designed at 90–95% reliability to optimize weight vs. warranty cost.
- **Bridges & Offshore Structures:** S-N-P curves are used with Miner-Palmgren damage accumulation for variable amplitude loading.
- **Medical Implants (Hip stems, dental implants):** Require ultra-high reliability (99.99%) using S-N-P data at physiological loading frequencies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Basquin's Law to the S-N-P Equation

We begin with the deterministic Basquin equation and incorporate the statistical scatter in $N$ to obtain the full S-N-P formulation.

### Step 1: Start with Basquin's Power Law

The classical high-cycle fatigue relationship is:

$$S_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$$

### Step 2: Solve for Number of Cycles

Taking logarithm (base 10) of both sides:

$$\log S_a = \log \sigma'_f + b \log(2N_f)$$

Expanding $\log(2N_f) = \log 2 + \log N_f$:

$$\log S_a = \log \sigma'_f + b \log 2 + b \log N_f$$

Solving for $\log N_f$:

$$\log N_f = \frac{1}{b}\left[\log S_a - \log \sigma'_f - b \log 2\right]$$

$$\log N_f = \frac{1}{b}\log\left(\frac{S_a}{\sigma'_f}\right) - \log 2$$

### Step 3: Introduce Statistical Variability

Now, recognize that $\log N_f$ is a **random variable** with:
- Mean: $\mu_{\log N_f} = \frac{1}{b}\log(S_a / \sigma'_f) - \log 2$
- Standard deviation: $\sigma_{\log N_f}$

### Step 4: Standardize to the Normal Variable

For a survival probability $P$, the standard normal variate $Z_P$ satisfies:

$$P = \Phi\left(\frac{\log N_P - \mu_{\log N_f}}{\sigma_{\log N_f}}\right)$$

where $\Phi$ is the standard normal cumulative distribution function.

Solving for $\log N_P$:

$$\log N_P = \mu_{\log N_f} + Z_P \cdot \sigma_{\log N_f}$$

### Step 5: Substitute the Mean Expression

$$\log N_P = \frac{1}{b}\log\left(\frac{S_a}{\sigma'_f}\right) - \log 2 + Z_P \cdot \sigma_{\log N_f}$$

### Step 6: Invert to Get the S-N-P Form (Stress as a Function of N and P)

$$\frac{1}{b}\log\left(\frac{S_{a,P}}{\sigma'_f}\right) = \log N_P + \log 2 - Z_P \sigma_{\log N_f}$$

$$\log\left(\frac{S_{a,P}}{\sigma'_f}\right) = b\left[\log(2N_P) - Z_P \sigma_{\log N_f}\right]$$

$$\boxed{S_{a,P} = \sigma'_f \cdot (2N_P)^b \cdot 10^{-b \cdot Z_P \cdot \sigma_{\log N_f}}}$$

This is the **complete S-N-P equation** — the stress amplitude corresponding to a specific number of cycles $N_P$ and survival probability $P$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full Analytical Working)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A 2024-T3 aluminum alloy has the following fatigue properties: $\sigma'_f = 900$ MPa, $b = -0.11$, and the standard deviation of $\log N$ is $\sigma_{\log N} = 0.15$. A component is subjected to a stress amplitude of $S_a = 200$ MPa. Calculate the number of cycles to failure for survival probabilities of 50%, 90%, 95%, and 99%.

### Given Data
- $\sigma'_f = 900$ MPa
- $b = -0.11$
- $\sigma_{\log N_f} = 0.15$
- $S_a = 200$ MPa

### Step 1: Compute the Mean $\log N_f$

$$\mu_{\log N_f} = \frac{1}{b}\log_{10}\left(\frac{S_a}{\sigma'_f}\right) - \log_{10}(2)$$

Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{S_a}{\sigma'_f} = \frac{200}{900} = 0.2222$$

Compute $\log_{10}(0.2222) = -0.6532$

Now:

$$\mu_{\log N_f} = \frac{1}{-0.11} \times (-0.6532) - 0.3010$$

$$\mu_{\log N_f} = 5.938 - 0.3010 = 5.637$$

So the **mean (50%) cycles to failure**:

$$N_{50} = 10^{5.637} = 4.33 \times 10^5 \text{ cycles}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Probability Correction

For each $P$, we need $Z_P$ from the standard normal table:

| $P$ (Survival) | $Z_P$ |
|:--------------:|:-----:|
| 50% | 0.000 |
| 90% | 1.282 |
| 95% | 1.645 |
| 99% | 2.326 |

The general formula:

$$\log N_P = \mu_{\log N_f} + Z_P \cdot \sigma_{\log N_f}$$

### Case (a): P = 50%

$$\log N_{50} = 5.637 + (0)(0.15) = 5.637$$

$$N_{50} = 10^{5.637} = 4.33 \times 10^5 \text{ cycles}$$

### Case (b): P = 90%

$$\log N_{90} = 5.637 + (1.282)(0.15) = 5.637 + 0.1923 = 5.829$$

$$N_{90} = 10^{5.829} = 6.75 \times 10^5 \text{ cycles}$$

### Case (c): P = 95%

$$\log N_{95} = 5.637 + (1.645)(0.15) = 5.637 + 0.2468 = 5.884$$

$$N_{95} = 10^{5.884} = 7.66 \times 10^5 \text{ cycles}$$

### Case (d): P = 99%

$$\log N_{99} = 5.637 + (2.326)(0.15) = 5.637 + 0.3489 = 5.986$$

$$N_{99} = 10^{5.986} = 9.68 \times 10^5 \text{ cycles}$$

### Final Tabulated Answer

| Survival Probability $P$ | $Z_P$ | $\log N_P$ | $N_P$ (cycles) |
|:------------------------:|:-----:|:----------:|:--------------:|
| 50% | 0.000 | 5.637 | $4.33 \times 10^5$ |
| 90% | 1.282 | 5.829 | $6.75 \times 10^5$ |
| 95% | 1.645 | 5.884 | $7.66 \times 10^5$ |
| 99% | 2.326 | 5.986 | $9.68 \times 10^5$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Observation:** Increasing reliability from 50% to 99% **more than doubles the predicted fatigue life** at the same stress level. This is why probability-based design is essential for safety-critical components.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Combined Effect of Multiple Factors

When several modifying factors act simultaneously, the modified endurance limit is computed using the **Marin equation**:

$$S_e = K_{sf} \cdot K_{size} \cdot K_{load} \cdot K_{temp} \cdot K_{\text{corr}} \cdot K_{\text{reliability}} \cdot S'_e$$

The **reliability factor** $K_{\text{reliability}}$ is derived directly from the S-N-P framework:

| Reliability $R$ | $K_{\text{reliability}}$ |
|:---------------:|:-----------------------:|
| 50% | 1.000 |
| 90% | 0.897 |
| 95% | 0.868 |
| 99% | 0.814 |
| 99.9% | 0.753 |
| 99.99% | 0.702 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU board exams, when an S-N-P problem is given with multiple modifying factors, use:**
> 1. Calculate $S_e$ (modified) from Marin equation with $K_{\text{reliability}}$ included.
> 2. Apply mean stress correction (Goodman or Soderberg) to find allowable $S_a$.
> 3. Use the S-N-P equation to find $N$ at the target probability.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 S-N-P Curve Family — Schematic Representation

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the family of S-N curves at different survival probability levels, along with the influencing factors.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fatigue Test Data] --> B[Statistical Analysis]
    B --> C[Compute mu_log_N and sigma_log_N]
    C --> D{Choose Survival Probability P}
    D --> E1[Z = 0.000 for P = 50%]
    D --> E2[Z = 1.282 for P = 90%]
    D --> E3[Z = 1.645 for P = 95%]
    D --> E4[Z = 2.326 for P = 99%]
    E1 --> F1[S-N-P Curve at 50%]
    E2 --> F2[S-N-P Curve at 90%]
    E3 --> F3[S-N-P Curve at 95%]
    E4 --> F4[S-N-P Curve at 99%]
    F1 --> G[Design Selection]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Target Reliability Component]
```

## 4.2 Factors Influencing S-N-P Curves — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph InputFactors[Influencing Factors]
        M[Mean Stress Sm]
        N[Notch Effect Kt]
        SF[Surface Finish Ksf]
        SZ[Size Effect Ksize]
        L[Loading Mode Kload]
        T[Temperature]
        C[Corrosion Environment]
        RS[Residual Stresses]
    end
    subgraph CoreEngine[S-N-P Core Engine]
        BL[Basquin Equation]
        ST[Statistical Scatter sigma_log_N]
        PR[Probability Z Transform]
    end
    subgraph OutputDomain[Output Curves]
        S50[S-N-P at 50%]
        S90[S-N-P at 90%]
        S95[S-N-P at 95%]
        S99[S-N-P at 99%]
    end
    M --> BL
    N --> BL
    SF --> BL
    SZ --> BL
    L --> BL
    T --> BL
    C --> BL
    RS --> BL
    ST --> BL
    BL --> PR
    PR --> S50
    PR --> S90
    PR --> S95
    PR --> S99
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Flow of Marin Equation Combined with S-N-P

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Uncorrected Endurance Limit Se] --> B[Ksf: Surface Finish]
    B --> C[Ksize: Size Effect]
    C --> D[Kload: Loading Mode]
    D --> E[Ktemp: Temperature]
    E --> F[Kcorr: Corrosion]
    F --> G[Kreliability: Probability]
    G --> H[Modified Endurance Limit Se modified]
    H --> I[Apply Mean Stress Correction Goodman Soderberg Gerber]
    I --> J[Allowable Alternating Stress Sa allow]
    J --> K[Use S-N-P to find N at target P]
    K --> L[Final Design Life Prediction]
```

## 4.4 Visual Description of the S-N-P Curve Family

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Picture (as described in textbooks):**
> On semi-log axes (stress on linear y-axis, cycles on log x-axis), the S-N-P curve family appears as **parallel descending lines**, with the **P = 50% curve in the middle** and progressively more conservative curves (P = 90%, 95%, 99%) shifting **downward and to the right**. The vertical distance between any two curves is constant on a log-N scale and is governed by $\sigma_{\log N}$ and $Z_P$. This is the visual fingerprint of the S-N-P framework.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: What is an S-N-P curve? How does it differ from a conventional S-N curve?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An S-N-P curve is a graphical representation of the relationship between cyclic stress amplitude ($S$), number of cycles to failure ($N$), and the probability of survival ($P$). It is a probabilistic extension of the conventional S-N (Wöhler) curve, which is deterministic in nature.
- **Conventional S-N curve:** Plots $S$ vs. $N$ for a single (usually 50%) probability level; ignores scatter.
- **S-N-P curve:** Plots a family of curves for different survival probabilities (50%, 90%, 95%, 99%), accounting for the statistical scatter in fatigue life using the standard deviation $\sigma_{\log N}$ of $\log N$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write that S-N-P curves are "more accurate" S-N curves. The correct statement is that they are **statistical/probabilistic** representations, not merely more accurate ones.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Q: List any six factors that affect S-N-P curves.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Mean stress ($S_m$)
2. Stress concentration / notch effect ($K_t$)
3. Surface finish ($K_{sf}$)
4. Size effect ($K_{size}$)
5. Loading mode (axial, bending, torsion) ($K_{load}$)
6. Temperature and corrosive environment

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry 14 marks and typically have two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Two full alternative questions are provided below.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: A machine component is made of AISI 4140 steel with the following properties:**
- $S_{ut} = 750$ MPa, $S_y = 550$ MPa
- $\sigma'_f = 1100$ MPa, $b = -0.09$
- $\sigma_{\log N} = 0.18$
- Surface finish: machined, Size: $d = 50$ mm
- Loading: reversed bending
- Operating temperature: room temperature
- Reliability required: **95%**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the modified endurance limit using the Marin equation and the mean stress if any. Assume fully reversed loading ($R = -1$).

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Determine the number of cycles to failure at the stress amplitude of $S_a = 300$ MPa for survival probabilities of 50%, 90%, and 99%. Plot or tabulate the S-N-P values.

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1:** Uncorrected endurance limit (for $S_{ut} < 1400$ MPa):

$$S'_e = 0.5 \cdot S_{ut} = 0.5 \times 750 = 375 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Surface finish factor (machined, $S_{ut} = 750$ MPa): From standard tables, $K_{sf} \approx 0.82$ (using $K_{sf} = 4.51 \cdot S_{ut}^{-0.265} = 4.51 \times 750^{-0.265} \approx 0.82$).

**Step 3:** Size factor (bending, $d = 50$ mm, non-rotating):

$$K_{size} = 0.869 \cdot d^{-0.097} = 0.869 \times 50^{-0.097} = 0.869 \times 0.748 = 0.650$$

**Step 4:** Loading factor (reversed bending, reference): $K_{load} = 1.00$

**Step 5:** Temperature factor: $K_{temp} = 1.00$

**Step 6:** Corrosion factor: $K_{corr} = 1.00$

**Step 7:** Reliability factor (95%): $K_{reliability} = 0.868$

**Step 8:** Modified endurance limit:

$$S_e = 0.82 \times 0.650 \times 1.00 \times 1.00 \times 1.00 \times 0.868 \times 375$$

$$S_e = 0.82 \times 0.650 \times 0.868 \times 375$$

$$S_e = 0.4627 \times 375 = 173.5 \text{ MPa}$$

Since loading is fully reversed, $S_m = 0$, and no mean stress correction is needed.

> **Part (a) Answer:** $S_e = 173.5$ MPa

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given:** $S_a = 300$ MPa, $\sigma'_f = 1100$ MPa, $b = -0.09$, $\sigma_{\log N} = 0.18$

**Step 1:** Mean log cycles:

$$\mu_{\log N} = \frac{1}{b}\log_{10}\left(\frac{S_a}{\sigma'_f}\right) - \log_{10}(2)$$

$$\frac{S_a}{\sigma'_f} = \frac{300}{1100} = 0.2727$$

$$\log_{10}(0.2727) = -0.5642$$

$$\mu_{\log N} = \frac{-0.5642}{-0.09} - 0.3010 = 6.269 - 0.301 = 5.968$$

**Step 2:** Apply probability correction for each $P$:

$$\log N_P = 5.968 + Z_P \times 0.18$$

| $P$ | $Z_P$ | $\log N_P$ | $N_P$ (cycles) |
|:---:|:-----:|:----------:|:--------------:|
| 50% | 0.000 | 5.968 | $9.29 \times 10^5$ |
| 90% | 1.282 | 6.199 | $1.58 \times 10^6$ |
| 99% | 2.326 | 6.387 | $2.44 \times 10^6$ |

**Part (b) Answer:** The S-N-P values are tabulated above.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Discuss in detail the various factors that affect the S-N-P curves. Explain how mean stress, stress concentration, surface finish, and size effect modify the endurance limit. Use appropriate equations.**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) [7 marks]: Mean Stress and Stress Concentration Effects**

The S-N-P curve is highly sensitive to the mean stress component. For non-zero mean stress, the **Goodman criterion** is commonly applied:

$$\frac{S_a}{S_e} + \frac{S_m}{S_{ut}} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_a = S_e \left(1 - \frac{S_m}{S_{ut}}\right)$$

For **stress concentration** (notch effect):

$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$$

Using Peterson's equation for notch sensitivity $q$:

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a/r}}$$

The effective endurance limit at a notch is reduced to $S_e / K_f$, which shifts the entire S-N-P curve family downward.

**Part (b) [7 marks]: Surface Finish and Size Effect**

The **surface finish factor** $K_{sf}$ accounts for microscopic irregularities:

$$K_{sf} = a \cdot S_{ut}^{\,b}$$

Typical values range from 0.38 (corroded) to 1.00 (polished).

The **size factor** $K_{size}$ arises from:
1. **Stress gradient effect** (in bending, surface stress is highest)
2. **Statistical effect** (larger volume → higher probability of critical flaws)

For non-rotating bending:

$$K_{size} = 0.869 \cdot d^{-0.097} \quad (5.08 \leq d \leq 250 \text{ mm})$$

**Combined Marin Equation** (must be presented in the exam):

$$S_e = K_{sf} \cdot K_{size} \cdot K_{load} \cdot K_{temp} \cdot K_{corr} \cdot K_{rel} \cdot S'_e$$

This single equation encapsulates the influence of all the major factors that shift the S-N-P curve vertically on the S-N plot.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Don't forget the reliability factor $K_{rel}$** when the question mentions a specific probability level. Forgetting this is the #1 cause of losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Use the correct $Z_P$ values** from the standard normal table. Common errors: using $Z = 1.96$ for 95% (correct: 1.645).
> 3. **Marin equation order doesn't matter**, but you must show all six factors explicitly.
> 4. For mean stress, **state which criterion you are using** (Soderberg/Goodman/Gerber). Default to Goodman unless otherwise specified.
> 5. **Always convert $\log N$ to natural log** if the question uses $\ln$ (i.e., replace $\log_{10}$ with $\ln$ and use $\sigma_{\ln N} = 2.303 \cdot \sigma_{\log_{10} N}$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — S-N-P Curves and Their Factors**

- **S-N-P curve** = Stress (S) – Number of cycles (N) – Probability of survival (P) — a probabilistic fatigue diagram.
- **Basquin's equation** governs the mean S-N line: $S_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$.
- **Log-normal distribution** is the standard model for $\log N_f$ scatter.
- **Standard normal variate** $Z_P$ converts survival probability to cycles:
  - 50% → $Z = 0.000$
  - 90% → $Z = 1.282$
  - 95% → $Z = 1.645$
  - 99% → $Z = 2.326$
- **Master S-N-P formula:** $S_{a,P} = \sigma'_f (2N_P)^b \cdot 10^{-b Z_P \sigma_{\log N}}$
- **Mean stress corrections:** Soderberg (conservative), Goodman (industry default), Gerber (ductile).
- **Marin equation factors:** $K_{sf}, K_{size}, K_{load}, K_{temp}, K_{corr}, K_{rel}$.
- **Notch effect:** $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$; for sharp notches, $K_f \to K_t$.
- **Size effect:** Only significant for non-uniform stress fields (bending, torsion).
- **Surface finish:** $K_{sf} < 1$ for all non-polished surfaces; corrosion drops it to ~0.38.
- **Temperature:** Reduces $S_e$ at elevated $T$; cryogenic $T$ may increase $S_e$.
- **Residual compressive stresses** (shot peening) **enhance** fatigue life; tensile residual stresses degrade it.
- **For S-N-P problems in KTU exams:** Always show the Marin equation step, then apply mean stress correction, then compute $N$ at the requested $P$ using $Z_P$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Endurance Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Endurance Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Endurance Diagram (Constant Life Diagram):** A graphical representation that correlates the alternating stress amplitude ($\sigma_a$) and the mean stress ($\sigma_m$) for a given number of cycles to failure ($N$), used to predict the fatigue life of a component under combined steady and fluctuating loads.

The diagram is plotted in the **$\sigma_m$–$\sigma_a$** plane (or equivalently $\sigma_{max}$–$\sigma_{min}$ plane), and the locus of all safe (infinite life) stress combinations forms a **fatigue strength envelope**. Any point *inside* the envelope indicates safe operation; any point *on* or *outside* it indicates potential failure.

The most widely used forms in KTU-evaluated problems are:

| Diagram Type | Geometric Shape | Governing Strength Parameter |
|--------------|----------------|------------------------------|
| **Goodman Line** | Straight line | Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{ut}$) |
| **Gerber Parabola** | Parabola | Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{ut}$) |
| **Soderberg Line** | Straight line | Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$) |
| **Modified Goodman** | Straight line | $\sigma_{ut}$ (most conservative, industry standard) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket of Fatigue"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Fuel Tank of a Truck**
> Imagine a truck's fuel tank represents the *fatigue strength* of a material.
> - The **alternating stress** ($\sigma_a$) is like *bumpy, potholed roads* that shake the truck constantly — every pothole chips away small amounts of structural integrity (micro-crack initiation).
> - The **mean stress** ($\sigma_m$) is like a *constant heavy load* the truck always carries — it pre-stresses the chassis, leaving less reserve capacity.
> - The **endurance limit** ($S_e$) is the fuel-tank's safe operating volume.
> - As the truck carries a heavier constant load ($\sigma_m$ increases), the allowable bumpiness ($\sigma_a$) it can survive *decreases*. This trade-off is precisely what the endurance diagram visualizes.

When the constant load equals the ultimate strength ($\sigma_m = \sigma_{ut}$), the material cannot tolerate *any* alternating stress — the tank is full of cargo, no room left for bumps. Conversely, when the mean stress is zero (purely reversed loading), the alternating stress can reach its maximum of $S_e$.

---

## 1.3 Key Stress Components — Foundational Variables

For any fluctuating stress cycle, three derived quantities are essential:

$$\sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a$$

$$\sigma_{min} = \sigma_m - \sigma_a$$

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}, \quad \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

- **Stress Ratio (R):** $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$
- **Amplitude Ratio (A):** $A = \dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_m}$

These dimensionless ratios allow engineers to classify the loading spectrum (fully reversed, pulsating, fluctuating, etc.).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Memorize the three characteristic points on the $\sigma_m$ axis of any endurance diagram:
> 1. $(0, S_e)$ — Fully reversed loading
> 2. $(S_{ut}, 0)$ — Static fracture (mean stress at ultimate)
> 3. $(S_y, 0)$ — Static yield (used only in Soderberg)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation — Why Does a Linear/Parabolic Relation Exist?

Empirically (from Wöhler/S-N curves), at a fixed life $N$:
- As $\sigma_m$ increases, permissible $\sigma_a$ decreases.
- At $\sigma_m = S_{ut}$ (or $S_y$), $\sigma_a$ must equal **zero** (no cyclic capacity remains).
- At $\sigma_m = 0$, $\sigma_a$ can be as high as $S_e$ (endurance limit at reversed loading).

The challenge is to **mathematically connect** the point $(0, S_e)$ to the point $(S_{ut}, 0)$ in the most physically accurate way.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Logic Chain:**
> 1. A purely empirical (Wöhler) curve gives fatigue strength at $R = -1$.
> 2. Static tests give $S_{ut}$ (fracture) and $S_y$ (yield).
> 3. We need a **mean stress correction theory** to interpolate between these known points.
> 4. Different theories (Goodman, Gerber, Soderberg) differ in *how* they connect them.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **all canonical equations** a KTU 2024 Scheme student must memorize for the topic *Endurance Diagrams*. Units are universally **MPa** or **N/mm²** unless otherwise specified.

| # | Diagram / Quantity | Equation | Domain / Boundary |
|---|--------------------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Mean Stress** | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | Always positive for design check |
| 2 | **Alternating Stress** | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | $\sigma_a \geq 0$ |
| 3 | **Stress Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ | $-1 \leq R \leq 1$ for fatigue regime |
| 4 | **Goodman Line** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$ | Conservative, linear |
| 5 | **Gerber Parabola** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^{2} = 1$ | Liberal, parabolic |
| 6 | **Soderberg Line** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = 1$ | Most conservative, uses yield |
| 7 | **Modified Goodman** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | With factor of safety $n$ |
| 8 | **Factor of Safety (Goodman)** | $n = \dfrac{\sigma_a \cdot S_{ut}}{S_e(S_{ut} - \sigma_m)}$ | Combined loading |
| 9 | **Endurance Limit (Steel, $S_{ut} \leq 1400$ MPa)** | $S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ | Uncorrected baseline |
| 10 | **Modified Endurance Limit** | $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e \cdot S_e'$ | With Marin factors |
| 11 | **Maximum Allowable $\sigma_a$ (Goodman)** | $\sigma_a = S_e \left(1 - \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)$ | Direct design formula |
| 12 | **Static Yield Check (Soderberg)** | $n_y = \dfrac{S_y}{\sigma_m + \sigma_a}$ | Used in Soderberg analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notational Distinction:**
> - $S_e'$ = *uncorrected* (theoretical) endurance limit from $S_{ut}$.
> - $S_e$ = *corrected* (actual) endurance limit after applying Marin factors ($k_a, k_b, k_c, k_d$).
> - The prime is **not** optional in KTU valuation — losing it is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Endurance diagrams are foundational in:

- **Automotive Crankshafts** — Subjected to combined bending (alternating) and torque-induced shear (mean). Goodman criteria validate infinite-life design.
- **Aircraft Wing Spars** — Cabin pressurization creates mean tensile stress; gust loads provide alternating stresses. Soderberg is preferred due to the safety-critical nature.
- **Pressure Vessels** — Cyclic pressure + pre-stress from bolted flanges.
- **Bridge Cables** — Mean stress from self-weight, alternating from wind/traffic.
- **Turbine Blades** — High-cycle fatigue under centrifugal (mean) + vibration (alternating) loads.

> [!TIP]
> In **KTU-style design problems**, you will almost always be asked: *"Check for infinite life using Goodman criterion and find the factor of safety."* This is the bread-and-butter 7-mark question in Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Goodman Line

**Given:**
- Point A: $(0, S_e)$ — Purely reversed loading, no mean stress.
- Point B: $(S_{ut}, 0)$ — Pure static tension at ultimate strength.
- The two-point form of a straight line is required.

**Step 1:** Write the two-point form in the $\sigma_m$–$\sigma_a$ plane.

$$\frac{\sigma_m - 0}{S_{ut} - 0} + \frac{\sigma_a - S_e}{0 - S_e} = 1$$

This is the standard "intercept form" with x-intercept $S_{ut}$ and y-intercept $S_e$.

**Step 2:** Simplify the second fraction.

$$\frac{\sigma_a - S_e}{-S_e} = -\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + 1$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back.

$$\frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} + 1 - \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} = 1$$

**Step 4:** Cancel the $+1$ on both sides.

$$\boxed{\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1}$$

This is the **Goodman line equation**. Note that it is *linear* in $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Gerber Parabola

**Premise:** Gerber (1874) observed that experimental data falls on a *parabola*, not a straight line, when connecting $(0, S_e)$ to $(S_{ut}, 0)$.

**Step 1:** Assume a second-degree relationship of the form:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^{2} = 1$$

**Step 2:** Verify boundary conditions.
- At $\sigma_m = 0$: $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} = 1 \Rightarrow \sigma_a = S_e$ ✓
- At $\sigma_a = 0$: $\left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1 \Rightarrow \sigma_m = S_{ut}$ ✓

**Step 3:** Verify that the curve lies *above* the Goodman line (Gerber is more liberal).

For any $0 < \sigma_m < S_{ut}$, the Goodman line gives:

$$\sigma_a^{Goodman} = S_e\left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)$$

The Gerber parabola gives:

$$\sigma_a^{Gerber} = S_e\left[1 - \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^{2}\right]$$

Since $\left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^{2} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}$ for $0 \leq \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} \leq 1$, we conclude:

$$\sigma_a^{Gerber} \geq \sigma_a^{Goodman}$$

**Conclusion:** The Gerber parabola is **less conservative** than the Goodman line. The Goodman criterion always lies inside (below) the Gerber curve.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Soderberg Line

**Premise:** Soderberg proposed that yielding (not fracture) should be the limiting static criterion. Replace $S_{ut}$ with $S_y$.

$$\boxed{\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = 1}$$

This is the most conservative of the three because $S_y < S_{ut}$ for ductile materials. The Soderberg line is the preferred criterion for **ductile materials with no well-defined endurance limit** (e.g., aluminum alloys) and for **safety-critical applications**.

---

## 3.4 Factor of Safety Derivations (KTU-Standard Form)

### 3.4.1 Goodman Factor of Safety (Combined Loading)

Starting from the Goodman line:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

Multiply through by $n$:

$$\frac{n \cdot \sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{n \cdot \sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$$

Solving explicitly for $n$:

$$n \left(\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right) = 1$$

$$\boxed{n_{Goodman} = \frac{S_e \cdot S_{ut}}{S_{ut} \cdot \sigma_a + S_e \cdot \sigma_m}}$$

### 3.4.2 Soderberg Factor of Safety

By identical algebraic procedure:

$$\boxed{n_{Soderberg} = \frac{S_e \cdot S_y}{S_y \cdot \sigma_a + S_e \cdot \sigma_m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** When the problem says "find the factor of safety using Goodman," it means use **Equation 3.4.1 above**. Do not use $n = S_e / \sigma_a$ alone — that is for purely reversed loading only. Forgetting to include $\sigma_m$ costs 3–4 marks.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example (Standard KTU 7-Mark Pattern)

> **Problem:** A steel shaft has $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa and $S_e = 280$ MPa. It is subjected to a fluctuating bending stress that varies from $+200$ MPa to $-100$ MPa. Using the **Goodman criterion**, find the factor of safety.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute mean and alternating stresses.

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{200 + (-100)}{2} = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{200 - (-100)}{2} = \frac{300}{2} = 150 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Goodman safety factor formula (Eq. 3.4.1).

$$n = \frac{S_e \cdot S_{ut}}{S_{ut} \cdot \sigma_a + S_e \cdot \sigma_m}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$n = \frac{280 \times 600}{600 \times 150 + 280 \times 50}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator.

$$600 \times 150 = 90{,}000$$

$$280 \times 50 = 14{,}000$$

$$\text{Sum} = 104{,}000$$

**Step 5:** Compute numerator.

$$280 \times 600 = 168{,}000$$

**Step 6:** Divide.

$$n = \frac{168{,}000}{104{,}000} = 1.615$$

**Result:** $\boxed{n \approx 1.62}$

> [!TIP]
> **Check:** A factor of safety between 1 and 2 is typical for fatigue design. Since $n > 1$, the shaft has *infinite life* under Goodman criteria. If $n < 1$, infinite life is **not** achieved.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Endurance Diagram Geometry (σ_m vs σ_a Plane)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["ORIGIN sigma_m = 0, sigma_a = 0"] --> B["Purely Reversed Loading: sigma_m = 0, sigma_a = S_e"]
    A --> C["Static Loading: sigma_m = S_ut, sigma_a = 0"]
    A --> D["Yield Limit: sigma_m = S_y, sigma_a = 0"]
    B --> E["GOODMAN LINE - Straight line from B to C"]
    B --> F["SODERBERG LINE - Straight line from B to D"]
    B --> G["GERBER PARABOLA - Curved line from B to C, lies above Goodman"]
    E --> H["SAFE REGION - Below Goodman line"]
    F --> I["SAFER REGION - Below Soderberg line, most conservative"]
    G --> J["LIBERAL REGION - Below Gerber parabola, least conservative"]
    H --> K["INFINITE LIFE ZONE: n greater than or equal to 1"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Vertical axis = $\sigma_a$ (alternating), horizontal axis = $\sigma_m$ (mean). The "safe zone" is the region **enclosed by** the origin and the curve.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Design Decision Flow for Choosing an Endurance Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["START: Material Properties Known S_ut, S_y, S_e"] --> B{"Material has clear endurance limit?"}
    B -- YES, e.g. Steel --> C["Use GOODMAN Modified for general design"]
    B -- NO, e.g. Aluminum --> D["Use SODERBERG or use finite life S-N curve"]
    C --> E{"Application safety critical?"}
    E -- YES, Aircraft, Nuclear --> F["Use SODERBERG Most conservative"]
    E -- NO, General machine --> G["Use GOODMAN Standard industrial practice"]
    D --> H["No endurance limit available, use S-N at specified N"]
    F --> I["Compute n and verify n greater than or equal to design n"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{"n acceptable?"}
    J -- YES --> K["DESIGN ACCEPTED Infinite life achieved"]
    J -- NO --> L["REDESIGN: Reduce stress or change material"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Stress Cycle Classification

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Fluctuating Stress Cycle"] --> B["Compute R = sigma_min / sigma_max"]
    B --> C{"R value"}
    C -- R equals -1 --> D["Fully Reversed, pure alternating, sigma_m = 0"]
    C -- R equals 0 --> E["Pulsating Zero to Max, sigma_min = 0"]
    C -- R equals 1 --> F["Static, sigma_a = 0"]
    C -- Negative R --> G["Reversed with mean tension shift"]
    C -- Positive R less than 1 --> H["Fluctuating with same sign"]
    C -- R greater than 1 --> I["Invalid, not standard fatigue regime"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Note:** Every KTU problem implicitly uses one of these $R$ values. The endurance diagram's y-intercept corresponds to $R = -1$ (fully reversed), and the x-intercept corresponds to $R = 1$ (static).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Distinguish between the Goodman line and the Gerber parabola as mean stress correction theories in fatigue design.**

**Model Answer:**

Both the Goodman and Gerber theories relate alternating stress ($\sigma_a$) to mean stress ($\sigma_m$) for a given fatigue life.

| Feature | Goodman | Gerber |
|---------|---------|--------|
| **Geometric Form** | Straight line | Parabola |
| **Equation** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$ | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1$ |
| **Nature** | Conservative (lies below Gerber) | Liberal (lies above Goodman) |
| **Static Reference** | Uses $S_{ut}$ | Uses $S_{ut}$ |
| **Empirical Match** | Approximately matches data at low $\sigma_m$ | Better match to data over full range |

The **Goodman line** is preferred in industrial design for its simplicity and conservatism. The **Gerber parabola** is used when a more accurate (less conservative) prediction is needed, particularly at moderate mean stresses.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**A component is subjected to a stress cycle with $\sigma_{max} = 250$ MPa and $\sigma_{min} = -50$ MPa. Compute the mean stress, alternating stress, and stress ratio $R$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Mean Stress**

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{250 + (-50)}{2} = \frac{200}{2} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Alternating Stress**

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{250 - (-50)}{2} = \frac{300}{2} = 150 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Stress Ratio**

$$R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{-50}{250} = -0.2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since $R = -0.2$ (negative, but not $-1$), the cycle is *partially reversed* with a tensile mean stress bias.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

A steel shaft is subjected to a fluctuating torque that varies from $T_{max} = 1200$ N·m to $T_{min} = 200$ N·m. The shaft diameter is 50 mm. The material has $S_{ut} = 550$ MPa, $S_y = 380$ MPa, and uncorrected endurance limit $S_e' = 0.5 S_{ut}$. Assume all Marin factors are unity (i.e., $S_e = S_e'$).

**(a)** Determine the mean and alternating shear stresses, and plot the operating point on the Goodman diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the factor of safety against fatigue failure using the **Goodman criterion**, and verify against the **Soderberg criterion** for yielding. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Stress Computation:**

**Step 1: Compute mean and alternating torque.**

$$T_m = \frac{T_{max} + T_{min}}{2} = \frac{1200 + 200}{2} = 700 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T_a = \frac{T_{max} - T_{min}}{2} = \frac{1200 - 200}{2} = 500 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2: Compute polar section modulus.**

$$Z_p = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{16} = \frac{\pi \times (0.050)^{3}}{16}$$

$$Z_p = \frac{\pi \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}}{16} = 2.454 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 3: Compute mean and alternating shear stresses.**

$$\tau_m = \frac{T_m}{Z_p} = \frac{700}{2.454 \times 10^{-5}} = 28.52 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 28.52 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_a = \frac{T_a}{Z_p} = \frac{500}{2.454 \times 10^{-5}} = 20.37 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 20.37 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Endurance limit (shear).**

For torsional loading, the endurance limit in shear is approximately $S_{se} \approx 0.577 S_e'$ (von Mises equivalent).

$$S_e' = 0.5 \times 550 = 275 \text{ MPa}$$

$$S_{se} = 0.577 \times 275 = 158.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Operating point.**

$$\boxed{(\tau_m, \tau_a) = (28.52 \text{ MPa}, 20.37 \text{ MPa})}$$

**Plotting on Goodman Diagram (Shear):**

The Goodman line for shear: $\dfrac{\tau_a}{S_{se}} + \dfrac{\tau_m}{S_{su}} = 1$ where $S_{su} \approx 0.577 S_{ut} = 317.4$ MPa.

The operating point is well **inside** the safe zone (origin to (317.4, 158.7) line), indicating infinite life is plausible.

---

**Part (b) — Factor of Safety:**

**Step 6: Goodman factor of safety (torsional).**

$$n_{Goodman} = \frac{S_{se} \cdot S_{su}}{S_{su} \cdot \tau_a + S_{se} \cdot \tau_m}$$

$$n_{Goodman} = \frac{158.7 \times 317.4}{317.4 \times 20.37 + 158.7 \times 28.52}$$

Numerator:

$$158.7 \times 317.4 = 50{,}371$$

Denominator:

$$317.4 \times 20.37 = 6465.4$$

$$158.7 \times 28.52 = 4526.1$$

$$\text{Sum} = 10{,}991.5$$

$$n_{Goodman} = \frac{50{,}371}{10{,}991.5} = 4.58$$

**Step 7: Soderberg factor of safety (using $S_{sy} = 0.577 S_y$).**

$$S_{sy} = 0.577 \times 380 = 219.3 \text{ MPa}$$

$$n_{Soderberg} = \frac{S_{se} \cdot S_{sy}}{S_{sy} \cdot \tau_a + S_{se} \cdot \tau_m}$$

$$n_{Soderberg} = \frac{158.7 \times 219.3}{219.3 \times 20.37 + 158.7 \times 28.52}$$

Numerator:

$$158.7 \times 219.3 = 34{,}803$$

Denominator:

$$219.3 \times 20.37 = 4467.1$$

$$158.7 \times 28.52 = 4526.1$$

$$\text{Sum} = 8993.2$$

$$n_{Soderberg} = \frac{34{,}803}{8993.2} = 3.87$$

**Step 8: Conclusion.**

$$\boxed{n_{Goodman} = 4.58, \quad n_{Soderberg} = 3.87}$$

Both values are well above 1, confirming **infinite life** with a high safety margin. The Soderberg factor is lower (more conservative) than Goodman, as expected.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

A machine component is subjected to an axial fluctuating load varying from $P_{max} = 50$ kN (tension) to $P_{min} = 10$ kN (tension). The cross-sectional area is $400$ mm². The material has $S_{ut} = 700$ MPa, $S_y = 490$ MPa, and $S_e = 315$ MPa.

**(a)** Compute the mean and alternating stresses, and identify the **stress ratio** $R$. Plot the point on a Gerber parabola. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Gerber criterion**, find the factor of safety. Compare with the **Goodman factor of safety** and comment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Stress Computation:**

**Step 1: Compute mean and alternating stresses.**

$$\sigma_m = \frac{P_{max} + P_{min}}{2A} = \frac{50{,}000 + 10{,}000}{2 \times 400 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{60{,}000}{8 \times 10^{-4}} = 75 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_a = \frac{P_{max} - P_{min}}{2A} = \frac{50{,}000 - 10{,}000}{8 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{40{,}000}{8 \times 10^{-4}} = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Stress ratio.**

$$R = \frac{P_{min}}{P_{max}} = \frac{10}{50} = 0.2$$

Since $R = 0.2 > 0$, the cycle is *tension-tension* (no reversal).

**Step 3: Plotting on Gerber parabola.**

The Gerber parabola equation:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^{2} = 1$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{50}{315} + \left(\frac{75}{700}\right)^{2} = 0.1587 + 0.01148 = 0.1702$$

Since $0.1702 < 1$, the operating point lies **well inside** the Gerber envelope, confirming infinite life.

---

**Part (b) — Factor of Safety Comparison:**

**Step 4: Goodman factor of safety.**

$$n_{Goodman} = \frac{S_e \cdot S_{ut}}{S_{ut} \cdot \sigma_a + S_e \cdot \sigma_m}$$

$$n_{Goodman} = \frac{315 \times 700}{700 \times 50 + 315 \times 75}$$

Numerator:

$$315 \times 700 = 220{,}500$$

Denominator:

$$700 \times 50 = 35{,}000$$

$$315 \times 75 = 23{,}625$$

$$\text{Sum} = 58{,}625$$

$$n_{Goodman} = \frac{220{,}500}{58{,}625} = 3.76$$

**Step 5: Gerber factor of safety.**

Start from:

$$\frac{n \sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\frac{n \sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^{2} = 1$$

Let $x = n$. Substituting numerical values:

$$\frac{50 x}{315} + \left(\frac{75 x}{700}\right)^{2} = 1$$

$$0.1587 x + 0.01148 x^{2} = 1$$

$$0.01148 x^{2} + 0.1587 x - 1 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula $x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2} - 4ac}}{2a}$:

$$x = \frac{-0.1587 \pm \sqrt{(0.1587)^{2} - 4(0.01148)(-1)}}{2(0.01148)}$$

$$x = \frac{-0.1587 \pm \sqrt{0.02519 + 0.04592}}{0.02296}$$

$$x = \frac{-0.1587 \pm \sqrt{0.07111}}{0.02296}$$

$$x = \frac{-0.1587 \pm 0.2667}{0.02296}$$

Taking the positive root:

$$x = \frac{-0.1587 + 0.2667}{0.02296} = \frac{0.1080}{0.02296} = 4.70$$

$$\boxed{n_{Gerber} = 4.70, \quad n_{Goodman} = 3.76}$$

**Step 6: Comment.**

The Gerber factor of safety ($4.70$) is **greater** than the Goodman factor of safety ($3.76$). This confirms that the Gerber parabola is *less conservative* (more permissive) than the Goodman line, because for the same operating point, Gerber allows a higher safety margin to be claimed. The **Goodman line** is preferred for safer designs (e.g., in KTU industry-aligned problems), while the **Gerber parabola** is preferred when better empirical accuracy is needed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme Examinations:**
> 1. **Confusing $S_e$ with $S_e'$:** A 1-mark deduction each time you mix up the corrected and uncorrected endurance limits. Always state explicitly which is in use.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $S_{ut}$ and $S_y$ to shear equivalents** for torsional loading problems: $S_{su} \approx 0.577 S_{ut}$, $S_{sy} \approx 0.577 S_y$. Not doing so is a 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Using $S_{ut}$ in Soderberg's equation:** Soderberg uses $S_y$, not $S_{ut}$. Marks lost: 2.
> 4. **Sign errors in $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$:** Always use $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$ as **actual algebraic values** (including signs). E.g., $\sigma_{min} = -100$ MPa, not $100$ MPa.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention "infinite life"** when $n > 1$, or "finite life" when $n < 1$: A standard 1-mark deduction in the conclusion.
> 6. **Not drawing the operating point** on the diagram when asked: in KTU's OBE pattern, visualization carries 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Endurance Diagrams — Must-Know Bullet Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**
> - An **endurance diagram** plots $\sigma_a$ (vertical) vs $\sigma_m$ (horizontal) for a constant life $N$.
> - **Goodman line** is **linear** and most commonly used: $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$.
> - **Gerber parabola** is **parabolic** and more accurate: $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^{2} = 1$.
> - **Soderberg line** uses **yield strength** $S_y$ instead of $S_{ut}$: $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = 1$.
> - Order of conservatism: **Soderberg > Goodman > Gerber** (Soderberg is most conservative).
> - **Endurance limit baseline (steel):** $S_e' = 0.5 S_{ut}$ for $S_{ut} \leq 1400$ MPa.
> - **Modified $S_e$:** Apply Marin factors $k_a, k_b, k_c, k_d$ for surface, size, reliability, temperature, etc.
> - **Mean and alternating stresses** are the fundamental building blocks; always compute them first.
> - **Stress ratio** $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$ classifies the loading type.
> - **Factor of safety** under Goodman: $n = \dfrac{S_e S_{ut}}{S_{ut} \sigma_a + S_e \sigma_m}$.
> - For **torsional** problems, multiply $S_{ut}, S_y, S_e$ by $\approx 0.577$ to obtain shear equivalents.
> - **Infinite life condition:** Operating point inside the diagram, i.e., $n \geq 1$.
> - **Design check steps:** (1) Compute $\sigma_m, \sigma_a$ → (2) Apply chosen criterion → (3) Compute $n$ → (4) Compare with design $n$ → (5) Conclude infinite/finite life.
> - **Modified Goodman** is the industry default for general machine design.
> - **Gerber** is preferred when high accuracy is needed and the design margin is not safety-critical.
> - **Soderberg** is reserved for ductile materials without clear $S_e$ or for aerospace/nuclear-grade safety.
> - Always **plot the operating point** on the diagram if the question asks for visualization.
> - For aluminum alloys (no $S_e$), use **S-N curve at specified $N$** rather than endurance diagrams.
> - **Memorize the three boundary points** on any diagram: $(0, S_e)$, $(S_{ut}, 0)$, and for Soderberg: $(S_y, 0)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cumulative Damage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cumulative Damage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Cumulative Damage** refers to the progressive accumulation of fatigue damage in a structural component subjected to fluctuating or variable-amplitude loading, where each individual stress cycle contributes a fractional amount to the total damage until the component eventually fails.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, cumulative damage is defined as the **irreversible, monotonic accumulation of microstructural deterioration** (dislocation pile-ups, micro-void formation, persistent slip band extrusion/intrusion) at the most critical location of a component, when it is subjected to a spectrum of stress amplitudes over its service life. The total damage is mathematically expressed as the **sum of fractional damages contributed by each stress cycle** in the loading spectrum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):**
> Cumulative damage theory forms the foundational bridge between **constant-amplitude fatigue test data** (S-N curves from Wöhler experiments) and **real-world variable-amplitude service loading** (spectra encountered in aircraft, bridges, vehicles, and rotating machinery). Mastery of Palmgren-Miner's linear damage rule is **mandatory** for both ESE and continuous assessment evaluations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Water Bucket" Intuition

Imagine you are carrying a **leaky water bucket** that can hold exactly 10 liters before it cracks and breaks. Every time you lift the bucket, a small amount of micro-damage (water leakage through stress) occurs, gradually weakening the bucket. If you lift a 10 kg load, you lose 1 liter. If you lift a 5 kg load, you lose 0.25 liters. Each loading event is a "stress cycle," and the bucket can only endure a finite total amount of damage.

- **Lifting a heavy load once** = equivalent stress above yield → high damage contribution
- **Lifting a light load many times** = sub-yield stress → low damage per cycle but accumulates
- **Bucket cracks when total damage = 10 liters** → this is the failure criterion D = 1

This is exactly what happens inside a steel shaft, an aircraft wing spar, or a turbine blade: damage from each stress cycle is added up, and fracture occurs when the cumulative damage reaches a critical value.

## 1.3 Physical Damage Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition – Fatigue Damage at the Microstructural Level:**
> At the atomic scale, each stress cycle produces one or more of the following irreversible changes:
> 1. **Persistent Slip Band (PSB) formation** on free surfaces
> 2. **Micro-crack nucleation** along slip bands or inclusions
> 3. **Stage I crack growth** along crystallographic planes
> 4. **Stage II crack propagation** perpendicular to maximum tensile stress
>
> The sum of these micro-events constitutes the **accumulated damage D**, which increases monotonically with the number of applied cycles.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following bolded parameters are **standard KTU-referenced quantities** in cumulative damage calculations:

- **S-N Curve Parameters:** Basquin's equation constants **σ'f** (fatigue strength coefficient) and **b** (fatigue strength exponent)
- **Damage per cycle:** **D_i = 1/N_i**, dimensionless
- **Total cumulative damage:** **D_total = Σ(n_i / N_i)**, dimensionless
- **Critical damage at failure:** **D_c = 1.0** (Palmgren-Miner hypothesis)
- **Cycle ratio:** **n_i / N_i**, dimensionless
- **Load sequence parameter:** **n** (block index), integer
- **Stress amplitude range:** **Δσ = σ_max − σ_min** in **MPa**
- **Mean stress correction:** **σ_a,eq = σ_a · K_f**, where **K_f** is the fatigue stress concentration factor

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Building Blocks of Cumulative Damage

Cumulative damage theory is built upon three foundational premises that must be understood before tackling the mathematics:

### Premise 1 – The Linear Damage Hypothesis (Palmgren, 1924; Miner, 1945)
The damage produced by a stress cycle at amplitude **σ_a1** is a fixed fraction (**1/N_1**) of the total life **N_1** that would cause failure under constant-amplitude loading at that stress. **Damage contributions are additive and sequence-independent.**

### Premise 2 – Failure Criterion
The component fails (macro-crack initiation leading to fracture) when the cumulative damage reaches unity:

$$D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} = 1$$

### Premise 3 – Cycle Counting Decomposition
A real-world random load-time history is first decomposed into discrete stress blocks (each with constant amplitude **σ_ai** and cycle count **n_i**) using a cycle-counting algorithm such as the **rainflow counting method** (ASTM E1049).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

1. **Identify the loading spectrum** – The service load history is obtained from strain gauges, FEM analysis, or standard load spectra (e.g., TWIST, Mini-TWIST, GAG, NOWT).
2. **Count cycles** – The complex spectrum is reduced into a discrete set of (σ_ai, n_i) pairs using rainflow counting.
3. **Determine N_i for each stress level** – Using the material's S-N curve: $N_i = (\sigma'f / \sigma_{ai})^{1/b}$
4. **Calculate fractional damage per block** – $D_i = n_i / N_i$
5. **Sum all fractional damages** – $D = \sum D_i$
6. **Apply failure criterion** – If D ≥ 1, failure is predicted.

## 2.3 Key Theoretical Models

### 2.3.1 Palmgren-Miner Linear Damage Rule (PM-LDR)
The classical approach, valid when:
- Loading is **random or variable amplitude**
- No mean stress variation between blocks
- No load interaction effects (cycle-by-cycle memory loss)
- The material exhibits **no abrupt endurance limit**

### 2.3.2 Modified Miner's Rule
Accounts for the observed phenomenon that ductile materials often fail at **D < 1** (typically 0.3 – 0.7) due to damage acceleration under high-low block sequences:

$$D_c = a$$

where **a ≈ 0.3** for ductile metals and **a ≈ 1.0** for brittle materials.

### 2.3.3 Corten-Dolan Damage Rule
A non-linear model that accounts for the development and propagation of multiple micro-cracks:

$$D = N \cdot \left( \frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_f} \right)^d$$

where **d** is the damage exponent determined experimentally (typically 3 ≤ d ≤ 8).

### 2.3.4 Manson-Hirschberg Method
A double linear damage rule that recognizes two distinct regimes:
- **Crack initiation phase** (N_i cycles to first observable crack)
- **Crack propagation phase** (N_p cycles to final fracture)

The total life is partitioned: **N_total = N_i + N_p**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{n_i}{N_i}$ | Palmgren-Miner Cumulative Damage Index | dimensionless |
| 2 | $D_c = 1.0$ | Failure criterion under linear damage rule | dimensionless |
| 3 | $D_c \approx 0.3$ | Modified Miner's critical damage (ductile metal) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $N_i = \left( \dfrac{\sigma'_f}{\sigma_{ai}} \right)^{1/b}$ | Basquin's S-N relation (cycles to failure at $\sigma_{ai}$) | cycles |
| 5 | $\sigma'_f = \sigma'_f \cdot (2N_f)^b$ | Basquin's equation (alternate form) | MPa |
| 6 | $D_i = \dfrac{n_i \cdot \sigma_{ai}^b}{\sigma'_f \cdot (2N_i)^b \cdot n_i}$ | Fractional damage per block | dimensionless |
| 7 | $n_i = N_f \cdot \left( \dfrac{\sigma'_f}{\sigma_{ai}} \right)^{1/b}$ | Permissible cycles at stress level $\sigma_{ai}$ | cycles |
| 8 | $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N)^b$ | Stress-life equation (high-cycle fatigue) | MPa |
| 9 | $D = N \cdot (\sigma_1 / \sigma_f)^d$ | Corten-Dolan damage model | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\sigma_{ar} = \sigma_a \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon'_f}{\sigma'_f} \cdot (2N_f)^{b-c}$ | Strain-life equivalent (Manson-Coffin) | MPa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Notation Convention:** In all KTU 2024 scheme examinations, students are expected to use **σ_a** (stress amplitude), **σ_m** (mean stress), **σ_max** (maximum stress), **σ_min** (minimum stress), and clearly state the **stress ratio R = σ_min / σ_max** whenever a variable-amplitude problem is presented.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cumulative damage theory is the **backbone of damage-tolerant design and fatigue life prediction** in the following engineering sectors:

- **Aerospace:** Aircraft wing spars, fuselage skin panels, helicopter rotor masts
- **Civil Infrastructure:** Highway bridges, offshore platforms, railway axles
- **Power Generation:** Steam turbine blades, wind turbine gearboxes, generator shafts
- **Automotive:** Crankshafts, suspension springs, transmission gears
- **Marine:** Ship hull plating, propeller shafts, drilling risers
- **Pressure Equipment:** Boiler tubes, reactor pressure vessels, gas storage cylinders

In each application, accurate cumulative damage prediction directly translates into **optimized inspection intervals, retirement-for-cause programs, and structural health monitoring** protocols, saving millions in maintenance costs while preventing catastrophic failures.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Palmgren-Miner's Linear Damage Rule

### Starting Assumption – Single Cycle Damage Fraction

Consider a specimen subjected to constant-amplitude cycling at stress amplitude **σ_a1**. Let the total number of cycles to failure be **N_1**. We postulate that each cycle contributes an **equal and infinitesimal amount of damage** to the specimen.

The damage per cycle is therefore:

$$dD_1 = \frac{1}{N_1}$$

**Reasoning:** If the specimen survives **N_1** cycles before failure, and damage accumulates linearly, then each cycle contributes exactly **1/N_1** of the total damage budget.

### Extension to Multiple Stress Levels (Block Loading)

Now consider **k** distinct stress blocks. In block **i**, the specimen is subjected to **n_i** cycles at stress amplitude **σ_ai**, with **N_i** cycles-to-failure available at that stress.

The damage contributed by block **i** is:

$$D_i = n_i \cdot dD_i = \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

By the **additivity hypothesis** (linear damage rule), the total damage after **k** blocks is:

$$D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} D_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

### Failure Criterion

Failure is predicted when the cumulative damage reaches the critical value:

$$D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} = 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** The elegance of this derivation lies in the fact that the **order (sequence) of stress blocks does not appear** in the equation. This is both the strength (simplicity) and the weakness (load-sequence insensitivity) of Miner's rule.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example – KTU Board Standard

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
A steel shaft is subjected to a 2-block variable amplitude loading spectrum:
- **Block 1:** σ_a1 = 350 MPa for n_1 = 10,000 cycles
- **Block 2:** σ_a2 = 280 MPa for n_2 = 50,000 cycles

The material's S-N curve (in the finite life regime) is given by:

$$\sigma_a = 1200 \cdot (N)^{-0.12} \quad \text{[MPa]}$$

**Determine the cumulative damage index and predict whether the shaft will fail.**

### Step 1 – Calculate N_1 (Cycles to failure at σ_a1 = 350 MPa)

Rearranging the S-N equation to solve for N:

$$N_1 = \left( \frac{1200}{350} \right)^{1/0.12}$$

$$N_1 = (3.4286)^{8.3333}$$

Let us compute step-by-step:

$$\ln(3.4286) = 1.2321$$

$$1.2321 \times 8.3333 = 10.2675$$

$$N_1 = e^{10.2675} = 28,600 \text{ cycles}$$

### Step 2 – Calculate N_2 (Cycles to failure at σ_a2 = 280 MPa)

$$N_2 = \left( \frac{1200}{280} \right)^{1/0.12}$$

$$N_2 = (4.2857)^{8.3333}$$

$$\ln(4.2857) = 1.4553$$

$$1.4553 \times 8.3333 = 12.1276$$

$$N_2 = e^{12.1276} = 185,000 \text{ cycles}$$

### Step 3 – Compute the Fractional Damage from Each Block

Damage from Block 1:

$$D_1 = \frac{n_1}{N_1} = \frac{10,000}{28,600} = 0.3497$$

Damage from Block 2:

$$D_2 = \frac{n_2}{N_2} = \frac{50,000}{185,000} = 0.2703$$

### Step 4 – Total Cumulative Damage (Miner's Sum)

$$D_{total} = D_1 + D_2 = 0.3497 + 0.2703 = 0.6200$$

### Step 5 – Failure Prediction

Since **D_total = 0.6200 < 1.0**, the shaft **will NOT fail** under the given spectrum.

The **damage margin** is:

$$\text{Margin} = 1 - D_{total} = 0.38 \quad (38\% \text{ life remaining})$$

### Step 6 – Determine Allowable Additional Cycles at Block 2 Stress

Remaining damage budget:

$$D_{remaining} = 1 - D_1 = 0.6503$$

Allowable additional cycles at σ_a2 = 280 MPa:

$$n_{2,allow} = D_{remaining} \cdot N_2 = 0.6503 \times 185,000 = 120,300 \text{ cycles}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Comparison of Damage Models

| **Model** | **Mathematical Form** | **Sequence Dependent?** | **Mean Stress Capable?** | **KTU Examination Frequency** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Palmgren-Miner (LDR)** | $D = \sum n_i/N_i = 1$ | ❌ No | ❌ No (separate correction needed) | **★★★★★ Very High** |
| **Modified Miner** | $D = a$, a ≈ 0.3 | ❌ No | ❌ No | ★★★★ High |
| **Corten-Dolan** | $D = N(\sigma_1/\sigma_f)^d$ | ✅ Yes (implicitly) | ✅ Yes | ★★★ Moderate |
| **Manson-Hirschberg (Double LDR)** | $D_i + D_p = 1$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ★★★ Moderate |
| **Henry / Fatigue Damage Curve** | Tabular $(\sigma_1/\sigma_2, n_1/N_1)$ | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ★★ Low |
| **Full Numerical Integration (NASALIFE, AFGROW)** | Cycle-by-cycle $\Delta K$ tracking | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ★ Low (advanced) |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation – Basquin's S-N Equation

The Basquin relation is the power-law backbone of the S-N curve. Its derivation begins with the empirical observation that the S-N curve becomes linear on **log-log axes**.

Starting from the linear log-log form:

$$\log \sigma_a = \log \sigma'_f + b \cdot \log(2N)$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f \cdot (2N)^b$$

Solving for N (cycles to failure at a given σ_a):

$$N = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma'_f} \right)^{1/b}$$

The fatigue strength at **2 × 10^6 cycles** (the typical KTU "endurance limit" reference) is:

$$\sigma_e = \sigma'_f \cdot (2 \times 10^6)^b$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU 2024:** Remember the two Basquin constants:
> - **σ'_f** = y-intercept of log σ_a vs log (2N) at 1/4 cycle (extrapolation)
> - **b** = slope of the log-log S-N line (always negative for metals: −0.05 to −0.15)
>
> For **steels:** b ≈ −0.10
> For **aluminum alloys:** b ≈ −0.11

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State and explain Palmgren-Miner's linear damage rule. Mention its key limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

Palmgren-Miner's linear damage rule states that the total fatigue damage in a component subjected to variable-amplitude loading is the linear sum of fractional damages contributed by each stress block:

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} = 1 \quad \text{at failure}$$

where **n_i** is the number of applied cycles at stress amplitude **σ_ai** and **N_i** is the cycles-to-failure at the same stress level obtained from the S-N curve.

**Key Limitation:** The rule is **load-sequence independent**. In reality, when a high-stress block precedes a low-stress block (H-L sequence), Miner's rule overestimates life. Conversely, L-H sequences cause damage acceleration, and Miner's rule underestimates life. The rule also neglects **mean stress variations** and **load interaction effects**.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define cumulative damage and explain the significance of the cycle ratio (n/N) in fatigue analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

Cumulative damage is the progressive accumulation of irreversible microstructural deterioration in a material subjected to repeated cyclic loading. The **cycle ratio (n_i / N_i)** represents the fraction of life consumed by **n_i** cycles of applied loading at stress level **σ_ai**, where **N_i** is the total life available at that stress.

The significance lies in its **additivity** — the total damage equals the sum of all cycle ratios in the loading spectrum. This allows engineers to **translate complex service load histories into a single scalar damage index** for life prediction, inspection scheduling, and retirement-for-cause decisions.

---

## Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) – Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

A steel component is subjected to a three-block variable-amplitude loading spectrum. The loading details are:

| **Block** | **Stress Amplitude σ_ai (MPa)** | **Applied Cycles n_i** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 400 | 8,000 |
| 2 | 320 | 20,000 |
| 3 | 260 | 60,000 |

The material S-N curve (Basquin relation) is:

$$\sigma_a = 1500 \cdot (N)^{-0.10} \quad \text{[MPa]}$$

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the cycles-to-failure N_i for each stress level.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the cumulative damage using Palmgren-Miner's rule and predict the component's remaining fatigue life.

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) – Calculation of N_i**

Using the rearranged Basquin equation:

$$N_i = \left( \frac{1500}{\sigma_{ai}} \right)^{1/0.10} = \left( \frac{1500}{\sigma_{ai}} \right)^{10}$$

**For Block 1 (σ_a1 = 400 MPa):**

$$N_1 = \left( \frac{1500}{400} \right)^{10} = (3.75)^{10}$$

$$\ln(3.75) = 1.3218$$

$$10 \times 1.3218 = 13.218$$

$$N_1 = e^{13.218} = 555,000 \text{ cycles}$$

**For Block 2 (σ_a2 = 320 MPa):**

$$N_2 = \left( \frac{1500}{320} \right)^{10} = (4.6875)^{10}$$

$$\ln(4.6875) = 1.5454$$

$$10 \times 1.5454 = 15.454$$

$$N_2 = e^{15.454} = 5,250,000 \text{ cycles}$$

**For Block 3 (σ_a3 = 260 MPa):**

$$N_3 = \left( \frac{1500}{260} \right)^{10} = (5.7692)^{10}$$

$$\ln(5.7692) = 1.7519$$

$$10 \times 1.7519 = 17.519$$

$$N_3 = e^{17.519} = 4.05 \times 10^7 \text{ cycles}$$

**Part (b) – Cumulative Damage and Remaining Life**

Fractional damages:

$$D_1 = \frac{n_1}{N_1} = \frac{8,000}{555,000} = 0.01441$$

$$D_2 = \frac{n_2}{N_2} = \frac{20,000}{5,250,000} = 0.00381$$

$$D_3 = \frac{n_3}{N_3} = \frac{60,000}{4.05 \times 10^7} = 0.00148$$

**Miner's Sum:**

$$D_{total} = 0.01441 + 0.00381 + 0.00148 = 0.01970$$

**Conclusion:** Since **D_total = 0.0197 << 1.0**, the component has accumulated only **~2% of its total fatigue life**. The remaining life is dominated by Block 1 (the highest stress level).

**Remaining cycles at Block 1 stress:**

$$D_{remaining} = 1 - D_{total} = 0.9803$$

$$n_{1,additional} = D_{remaining} \cdot N_1 = 0.9803 \times 555,000 = 544,100 \text{ cycles}$$

**Final Result:** The component is safe under the applied spectrum, with approximately **544,100 additional Block-1 cycles** available before predicted failure.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

A pressure vessel steel exhibits the following S-N data in the finite-life regime:

| **Stress Amplitude σ_a (MPa)** | **Cycles to Failure N** |
|---|---|
| 500 | 1.0 × 10⁴ |
| 400 | 5.0 × 10⁴ |
| 350 | 1.5 × 10⁵ |
| 300 | 5.0 × 10⁵ |

The component experiences a service spectrum of 3 blocks: 3,000 cycles at 500 MPa, 15,000 cycles at 400 MPa, and 50,000 cycles at 300 MPa.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Basquin equation constants σ'f and b for this material.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Apply Palmgren-Miner's rule to determine the cumulative damage index and the safe number of additional cycles at 400 MPa.

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) – Basquin Constants**

Using two extreme data points: (500 MPa, 10,000 cycles) and (300 MPa, 500,000 cycles).

The log-log form:

$$\log \sigma_a = \log \sigma'_f + b \cdot \log(2N)$$

For **σ_a = 500 MPa, 2N = 20,000:**

$$\log(500) = \log \sigma'_f + b \cdot \log(20,000)$$

$$2.6990 = \log \sigma'_f + 4.3010 \cdot b$$

For **σ_a = 300 MPa, 2N = 1,000,000:**

$$\log(300) = \log \sigma'_f + b \cdot \log(1,000,000)$$

$$2.4771 = \log \sigma'_f + 6.0 \cdot b$$

**Subtracting** (2) from (1):

$$2.6990 - 2.4771 = (4.3010 - 6.0) \cdot b$$

$$0.2219 = -1.6990 \cdot b$$

$$b = -0.1306$$

Substituting back into equation (1):

$$2.6990 = \log \sigma'_f + 4.3010 \times (-0.1306)$$

$$2.6990 = \log \sigma'_f - 0.5617$$

$$\log \sigma'_f = 3.2607$$

$$\sigma'_f = 1822 \text{ MPa}$$

**Basquin equation:**

$$\sigma_a = 1822 \cdot (2N)^{-0.1306} \quad \text{[MPa]}$$

**Part (b) – Cumulative Damage Analysis**

First, find N_i for each stress level using Basquin equation:

For **σ_a = 500 MPa, n_1 = 3,000:**

$$2N_1 = \left( \frac{1822}{500} \right)^{1/0.1306} = (3.644)^{7.658}$$

$$\ln(3.644) = 1.2931$$

$$1.2931 \times 7.658 = 9.9030$$

$$2N_1 = e^{9.9030} = 20,170$$

$$N_1 = 10,085 \text{ cycles}$$

For **σ_a = 400 MPa, n_2 = 15,000:**

$$2N_2 = \left( \frac{1822}{400} \right)^{7.658} = (4.555)^{7.658}$$

$$\ln(4.555) = 1.5165$$

$$1.5165 \times 7.658 = 11.6129$$

$$2N_2 = e^{11.6129} = 110,500$$

$$N_2 = 55,250 \text{ cycles}$$

For **σ_a = 300 MPa, n_3 = 50,000:**

$$2N_3 = \left( \frac{1822}{300} \right)^{7.658} = (6.0733)^{7.658}$$

$$\ln(6.0733) = 1.8042$$

$$1.8042 \times 7.658 = 13.8166$$

$$2N_3 = e^{13.8166} = 1,011,000$$

$$N_3 = 505,500 \text{ cycles}$$

**Fractional damages:**

$$D_1 = \frac{3,000}{10,085} = 0.2975$$

$$D_2 = \frac{15,000}{55,250} = 0.2715$$

$$D_3 = \frac{50,000}{505,500} = 0.0989$$

**Total cumulative damage:**

$$D_{total} = 0.2975 + 0.2715 + 0.0989 = 0.6679$$

**Remaining damage budget:**

$$D_{remaining} = 1 - D_{total} = 0.3321$$

**Safe additional cycles at 400 MPa:**

$$n_{2,allow} = D_{remaining} \cdot N_2 = 0.3321 \times 55,250 = 18,349 \text{ cycles}$$

**Final Result:** The component has accumulated **66.79% of its fatigue life** and can safely withstand approximately **18,349 additional cycles** at 400 MPa stress amplitude before failure is predicted.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in Cumulative Damage Problems (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Mixing S-N notation:** Students frequently confuse σ_a (stress amplitude) with σ_max (maximum stress). The S-N curve always uses **σ_a = (σ_max − σ_min)/2**, not σ_max.
>
> 2. **Wrong N value in Basquin equation:** Some problems use 2N in the S-N equation (R = −1 fully reversed) and N in others. **Always check** whether the given S-N relation uses N or 2N.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the critical value:** Students compute D but forget to compare with 1.0. A full 14-mark answer **must** include the failure comparison statement.
>
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Expressing N_i in scientific notation is acceptable, but **never mix units** (e.g., some stresses in MPa, others in GPa) within the same problem.
>
> 5. **Skipping the interpretation:** The final line of every cumulative damage problem **must** state: "Since D < 1, the component is safe" or "Since D > 1, the component has failed." Examiners allocate up to 2 marks for the interpretation alone.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Palmgren-Miner's rule:** $D = \sum n_i/N_i = 1$ at failure — the **most important formula** in cumulative damage analysis
- ✅ **Basquin's equation:** $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f \cdot (2N)^b$ — relates stress amplitude to fatigue life
- ✅ **Cycle ratio (n_i/N_i)** = fraction of life consumed at stress level i
- ✅ **Failure criterion:** D ≥ 1 indicates failure; D < 1 indicates safe operation
- ✅ **Modified Miner's rule:** uses critical damage D_c ≈ 0.3 for ductile metals
- ✅ **Load sequence** is neglected in linear damage rule — a key limitation
- ✅ **Rainflow counting** is the standard method to reduce a complex spectrum into discrete blocks
- ✅ **Corten-Dolan** model accounts for multi-crack initiation (non-linear damage)
- ✅ **Manson-Hirschberg** double linear damage rule separates crack initiation and propagation
- ✅ **Mean stress corrections** (Goodman, Gerber, Soderberg) are applied **before** the damage sum is computed
- ✅ Always **convert to σ_a** (stress amplitude) before applying the S-N curve
- ✅ **Ductile materials:** D_c ≈ 0.3; **Brittle materials:** D_c ≈ 1.0
- ✅ Final interpretation statement is **mandatory** for full marks
- ✅ The order of block loading **does not** affect the result in Palmgren-Miner's rule
- ✅ Enduring components under variable amplitude: total damage < 1.0 is acceptable

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Life Prediction
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Life Prediction

---

# Life Prediction in Failure Analysis and Design

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned):**
> **Life Prediction** is the engineering methodology that quantitatively estimates the operational duration (in cycles, hours, or revolutions) a structural component or material can sustain under defined service loading and environmental conditions before a critical failure criterion is reached.
