It synthesises material behaviour (fatigue, creep, wear, corrosion), stress analysis, and statistical fracture mechanics to produce a defensible estimate of **remaining useful life (RUL)** or **total fatigue life ($N_f$)**.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Odometer of Damage"

Imagine you drive a car every day. Each kilometre you travel slowly wears the engine, tyres and brakes. You cannot see the wear directly, but it accumulates invisibly. **Life prediction is exactly this — an "odometer" of hidden damage.**
- If the total damage reaches a threshold of **1.0**, the component "dies."
- Two cars driven gently for 10,000 km may accumulate less damage than one car driven aggressively for 5,000 km.
- Likewise, in engineering, **alternating stress cycles at various amplitudes** each contribute fractional damage; the component fails when this accumulated damage reaches a critical value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - **Stress Range, $\Delta\sigma$ (MPa):** Difference between maximum and minimum cyclic stress.
> - **Number of cycles to failure, $N_f$ (dimensionless):** Endurance under constant amplitude loading.
> - **Stress amplitude, $\sigma_a$ (MPa):** Half of the stress range.
> - **Mean stress, $\sigma_m$ (MPa):** Mid-point of the cyclic stress envelope.
> - **Stress Intensity Factor Range, $\Delta K$ (MPa$\sqrt{m}$):** Driving force for crack growth.
> - **Fatigue Life Ratio, $D$ (dimensionless):** Used in Miner's Rule.

### 1.2 Two Principal Philosophical Approaches

| Approach | Philosophy | Best Used When |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Total Life Approach (S-N / Strain-Life)** | Assumes the component is initially crack-free; life is the number of cycles to initiate and propagate a crack to fracture. | Smooth, ductile components; high-cycle fatigue regimes. |
| **Defect-Tolerant Approach (Fracture Mechanics / Paris Law)** | Assumes an **initial flaw of size $a_i$** already exists; life is the number of cycles to grow this flaw to a critical size $a_c$. | Welded structures, castings, components where NDE is mandatory. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Life Prediction Matters in KTU Context:**
> Examiners frequently test whether a student can choose the *correct* approach for a given service scenario. Pitting the **S-N curve** against the **Paris Law** is a hallmark 14-mark KTU module question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Life Prediction

1. **Loading Spectrum Characterisation** — Identify the variable amplitude stress history $\sigma(t)$. In practice, this is condensed into a **Rainflow-counted stress histogram**.
2. **Material Response Function** — Obtain the constitutive law relating stress (or strain) to cycles to failure: the **S-N curve**, **$\varepsilon$-N curve**, or **da/dN–ΔK curve**.
3. **Damage Accumulation Rule** — A mathematical model (e.g., **Miner's Linear Damage Rule**) that integrates fractional damage across load blocks.
4. **Failure Criterion** — A clearly defined endpoint: fracture, a critical crack length, excessive deformation, or a probability of survival.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are the **canonical set** for KTU Module 2 — Life Prediction. Memorise these and you will solve 90 % of paper questions.**

| # | Equation Name | Governing Formula | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Basquin's Law (High-Cycle Fatigue) | $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f \, (2N_f)^{b}$ | $\sigma'_f$ fatigue strength coefficient (MPa), $b$ fatigue strength exponent (typically $-0.05$ to $-0.12$) |
| 2 | Coffin–Manson Relation (Low-Cycle Fatigue) | $\dfrac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f \, (2N_f)^{c}$ | $\varepsilon'_f$ fatigue ductility coefficient, $c$ fatigue ductility exponent ($\approx -0.5$ to $-0.7$) |
| 3 | Total Strain-Life (Ramberg-Osgood + Coffin-Manson) | $\dfrac{\Delta\varepsilon}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma'_f}{E}(2N_f)^{b} + \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^{c}$ | $E$ Young's modulus (MPa) |
| 4 | Miner's Linear Damage Rule | $D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{n_i}{N_i} \leq 1$ | $n_i$ applied cycles at stress level $i$, $N_i$ cycles to failure at that level |
| 5 | Modified Goodman (Mean Stress Correction) | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} = 1$ | $\sigma_e$ endurance limit, $\sigma_u$ ultimate tensile strength |
| 6 | Soderberg (Conservative Correction) | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = 1$ | $\sigma_y$ yield strength |
| 7 | Paris Law (Crack Growth) | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^{m}$ | $C, m$ material constants; $a$ crack length (m); $\Delta K$ stress intensity range (MPa$\sqrt{m}$) |
| 8 | Stress Intensity Factor Range | $\Delta K = Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | $Y$ geometry factor |
| 9 | Crack Growth Life Integration | $N_f = \dfrac{a_c^{\,1-m/2} - a_i^{\,1-m/2}}{C \, (Y\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi})^{m} \, (1 - m/2)}$ | $a_i$ initial crack, $a_c$ critical crack |
| 10 | Endurance Limit (Steels) | $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \, \sigma_u$ for $N \geq 10^{6}$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule for KTU Scripts:**
> When writing $\vert \sigma_a \vert$ or any absolute value inside a markdown table, **always use** `\vert \sigma_a \vert` or `\lvert \sigma_a \rvert`. The bare vertical pipe character `|` will break the table renderer in many KTU internal evaluation portals.

### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Formula

- **Basquin's Law (Eq. 1)** exists because, in the *high-cycle regime*, macroscopic deformation is purely elastic, so the damage-driving parameter is **stress amplitude**, not plastic strain.
- **Coffin–Manson (Eq. 2)** emerges from *low-cycle fatigue*, where each cycle produces measurable plastic strain $\Delta\varepsilon_p$. The cyclic plastic strain is the *true* driver of crack nucleation.
- **Eq. 3 combines Eq. 1 + Eq. 2**, giving the *unified strain-life curve* widely used in modern fatigue codes (e.g., NASALIFE, AFLS).
- **Miner's Rule (Eq. 4)** is empirical — it assumes damage is *linear* and *commutative*, meaning the order of load blocks does not matter. Real materials often show $D_{critical}$ between **0.7 and 2.0**, with **1.0** being the design assumption.
- **Paris Law (Eq. 7)** governs the *stable crack propagation regime II* of the da/dN vs $\Delta K$ curve, bounded by the threshold $\Delta K_{th}$ and fracture toughness $K_{IC}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Aerospace:** Total life prediction using $\varepsilon$-N curves for turbine disc lives of $10^{4}$ cycles.
- **Bridges / Pressure Vessels:** Defect-tolerant Paris-Erdogan integration, with periodic NDE re-inspection intervals.
- **Automotive:** Miner's Rule summation over road-load spectra to certify chassis endurance.
- **Power Plants:** Creep-fatigue interaction life prediction for boiler tubes operating at $T > 0.4 T_m$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Crack Propagation Life (Paris Law Integration)

We seek the number of cycles $N_f$ required for a crack to grow from an initial size $a_i$ to a critical size $a_c$.

**Step 1 — Begin with Paris Law:**

$$
\dfrac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the standard expression for $\Delta K$:**

$$
\Delta K = Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dfrac{da}{dN} = C \left( Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a} \right)^{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables to prepare for integration:**

$$
dN = \dfrac{da}{C \left( Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a} \right)^{m}}
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the denominator for clarity:**

$$
dN = \dfrac{da}{C \, (Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi})^{m} \, a^{m/2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate both sides from $a = a_i$ to $a = a_c$, and $N = 0$ to $N = N_f$:**

$$
\int_{0}^{N_f} dN = \int_{a_i}^{a_c} \dfrac{da}{C \, (Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi})^{m} \, a^{m/2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Pull constants out of the integral (they do not depend on $a$):**

$$
N_f = \dfrac{1}{C \, (Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi})^{m}} \int_{a_i}^{a_c} a^{-m/2} \, da
$$

**Step 7 — Apply the power rule of integration $\int a^{n} \, da = \dfrac{a^{n+1}}{n+1}$:**

$$
\int a^{-m/2} \, da = \dfrac{a^{1 - m/2}}{1 - m/2}
$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate the definite integral:**

$$
N_f = \dfrac{1}{C \, (Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi})^{m}} \cdot \dfrac{a_c^{\,1-m/2} - a_i^{\,1-m/2}}{1 - m/2}
$$

**Step 9 — Final closed-form expression:**

$$
N_f = \dfrac{a_c^{\,1-m/2} - a_i^{\,1-m/2}}{C \, (Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi})^{m} \, (1 - m/2)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Limitation:** This equation is valid only when $m \neq 2$. For $m = 2$, the integral reduces to a logarithmic form. Always verify $m$ against the material's Paris regime constants before numerical substitution.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Miner's Rule Damage Accumulation

A steel shaft experiences the following duty cycle in one service block:

| Block | Stress Amplitude $\sigma_{a,i}$ (MPa) | Cycles Applied $n_i$ | Cycles to Failure $N_i$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 300 | 5,000 | 40,000 |
| 2 | 250 | 20,000 | 90,000 |
| 3 | 200 | 80,000 | 300,000 |

**Question:** Will the shaft fail in one duty block? If not, how many identical blocks until predicted failure?

**Step 1 — Apply Miner's Rule:**

$$
D = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \dfrac{n_i}{N_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the tabulated values:**

$$
D = \dfrac{5000}{40000} + \dfrac{20000}{90000} + \dfrac{80000}{300000}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each fraction:**

$$
\dfrac{5000}{40000} = 0.125
$$

$$
\dfrac{20000}{90000} = 0.2222
$$

$$
\dfrac{80000}{300000} = 0.2667
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the damage contributions:**

$$
D = 0.125 + 0.2222 + 0.2667 = 0.6139
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the failure criterion $D \leq 1$:**

$$
0.6139 < 1.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{The shaft survives one block.}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the number of blocks to failure:**

$$
N_{blocks} = \dfrac{1}{D} = \dfrac{1}{0.6139} \approx 1.629
$$

**Conclusion:** The shaft will fail during the **2nd duty block** at approximately **40 %** completion of the second block (i.e., after roughly 24,560 additional equivalent damage-weighted cycles).

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Paris Law Crack Growth Life

A 7075-T6 aluminium alloy wing-rib fitting has:
- Initial flaw: $a_i = 1.0 \, \text{mm} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$
- Critical flaw: $a_c = 5.0 \, \text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$
- Applied stress range: $\Delta\sigma = 120 \, \text{MPa}$
- Geometry factor: $Y = 1.0$
- Material constants: $C = 2 \times 10^{-11}$, $m = 3.0$ (SI units)

**Step 1 — Identify the exponent $1 - m/2$:**

$$
1 - \dfrac{m}{2} = 1 - \dfrac{3}{2} = -\dfrac{1}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator $(a_c^{-1/2} - a_i^{-1/2})$:**

$$
a_c^{-1/2} = (5 \times 10^{-3})^{-0.5} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5 \times 10^{-3}}} = \dfrac{1}{0.07071} = 14.142
$$

$$
a_i^{-1/2} = (1 \times 10^{-3})^{-0.5} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 \times 10^{-3}}} = \dfrac{1}{0.03162} = 31.623
$$

$$
a_c^{-1/2} - a_i^{-1/2} = 14.142 - 31.623 = -17.481
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator $(1 - m/2)$ explicitly:**

$$
1 - \dfrac{m}{2} = -0.5
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the $(Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi})^{m}$ term:**

$$
Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi} = (1.0)(120)\sqrt{\pi} = 120 \times 1.7725 = 212.69 \, \text{MPa}\sqrt{m}
$$

$$
(212.69)^{3} = 9.622 \times 10^{6} \, (\text{MPa}\sqrt{m})^{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble $N_f$:**

$$
N_f = \dfrac{-17.481}{(2 \times 10^{-11})(9.622 \times 10^{6})(-0.5)}
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the denominator:**

$$
(2 \times 10^{-11})(9.622 \times 10^{6})(-0.5) = -9.622 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 7 — Final calculation:**

$$
N_f = \dfrac{-17.481}{-9.622 \times 10^{-5}} = 181{,}656 \, \text{cycles}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The negative signs cancel because we are dividing $-17.481$ by $-9.622 \times 10^{-5}$.** A frequent student error is to retain a negative answer, indicating they inverted $a_c^{-1/2}$ and $a_i^{-1/2}$. Always check your sign convention.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Methodology Flow — Life Prediction Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Service Conditions and Duty Spectrum] --> B0[Load Spectrum Characterisation]
    B0 --> C0{Choose Approach}
    C0 -->|Smooth component, initiation critical| D0[S-N or Strain Life Method]
    C0 -->|Cracked or defect critical| E0[Fracture Mechanics Paris Law]
    D0 --> F0[Apply Miner's Rule for Variable Amplitude]
    E0 --> G0[Integrate da per dN over a_i to a_c]
    F0 --> H0[Compute Total Damage D]
    G0 --> H1[Compute Crack Growth Life N_f]
    H0 --> I0{D greater than or equal 1}
    H1 --> I1{Compare with Inspection Interval}
    I0 -->|Yes| J0[Predict Failure and Redesign]
    I0 -->|No| K0[Remaining Life equals 1 minus D block period]
    I1 -->|N_f less than interval| J0
    I1 -->|N_f greater than interval| K0
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Damage Accumulation via Miner's Rule

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Stress Block 1 sigma a1] --> B1[Damage fraction n1 over N1]
    A2[Stress Block 2 sigma a2] --> C1[Damage fraction n2 over N2]
    A3[Stress Block k sigma ak] --> D1[Damage fraction nk over Nk]
    B1 --> E1[Summation Block]
    C1 --> E1
    D1 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Total Damage D]
    F1 --> G1{D greater than or equal 1}
    G1 -->|Yes| H1[Failure Predicted]
    G1 -->|No| I1[Safe Continue Service]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Crack Propagation Regime Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Region I Near Threshold da per dN Approaches Zero] --> B2[Region II Paris Stable Growth]
    B2 --> C2[Region III Unstable Near K_IC]
    A2 -->|Delta K less than Delta K_th| D2[No Crack Growth]
    B2 -->|Integrate Paris Law| E2[Crack Life N_f]
    C2 -->|Fast Fracture Risk| F2[Immediate Failure]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block in these flow diagrams corresponds directly to a step in the **Derivation** and **Worked Numerical** sections. The decision nodes (`{...}`) represent the most common places where students lose partial credit on KTU valuation — *always show the threshold comparison and its numerical outcome*.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Define life prediction. Distinguish between *total life approach* and *defect-tolerant approach* with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Life prediction is the quantitative estimation of the number of stress cycles, hours, or revolutions a component can endure before reaching a defined failure criterion under prescribed service conditions.

- **Total Life Approach:** Assumes an initially defect-free component. Life is the cycles required to nucleate a crack and propagate it to fracture. *Example: S-N curve-based prediction for a polished turbine shaft.*
- **Defect-Tolerant Approach:** Assumes an initial flaw $a_i$ is already present. Life is the cycles required to propagate this flaw to a critical size $a_c$. *Example: Fracture-mechanics life prediction of a welded pressure vessel using ASME Section XI.*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **State Miner's Linear Damage Rule. Mention one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Miner's Linear Damage Rule states that failure occurs when the cumulative damage $D$ from a sequence of load blocks equals unity:

$$
D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{n_i}{N_i} = 1
$$

**Limitation (1 mark):** The rule ignores **load-interaction effects** (e.g., overload-induced retardation or acceleration) and is purely linear; in reality, the sequence of loading influences the damage accumulation, and $D_{critical}$ can deviate significantly from 1.0 (often between 0.3 and 3.0).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for crack propagation life $N_f$ using the Paris Law $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^{m}$, clearly stating the underlying assumptions and the closed-form result.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A 2024-T3 aluminium plate contains an initial edge crack of length $a_i = 1.5 \, \text{mm}$. The applied stress range is $\Delta\sigma = 90 \, \text{MPa}$. The critical crack length is $a_c = 7.0 \, \text{mm}$. Given $Y = 1.12$, $C = 1.5 \times 10^{-11}$, $m = 3.2$ (SI units), compute the fatigue crack propagation life $N_f$.

**Model Solution (Part a, 7 marks):**

The derivation proceeds as follows:

$$
\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^{m} = C (Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a})^{m}
$$

Separating variables and integrating:

$$
\int_{0}^{N_f} dN = \int_{a_i}^{a_c} \dfrac{da}{C (Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi})^{m} \, a^{m/2}}
$$

Evaluating the integral using the power rule:

$$
N_f = \dfrac{1}{C (Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi})^{m}} \cdot \dfrac{a_c^{\,1-m/2} - a_i^{\,1-m/2}}{1 - m/2}
$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Constant amplitude loading (no overload retardation).
2. Geometry factor $Y$ is independent of crack length.
3. The crack is in Paris Region II ($\Delta K_{th} \ll \Delta K \ll K_{IC}$).

**Model Solution (Part b, 7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $1 - m/2$:**

$$
1 - \dfrac{3.2}{2} = 1 - 1.6 = -0.6
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $a_c^{-0.6}$ and $a_i^{-0.6}$:**

$$
a_c^{-0.6} = (7.0 \times 10^{-3})^{-0.6}
$$

$$
a_i^{-0.6} = (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^{-0.6}
$$

Using $\ln$:

$$
\ln(a_c^{-0.6}) = -0.6 \ln(7.0 \times 10^{-3}) = -0.6 \times (-4.962) = 2.977
$$

$$
\ln(a_i^{-0.6}) = -0.6 \ln(1.5 \times 10^{-3}) = -0.6 \times (-6.502) = 3.901
$$

Exponentiating:

$$
a_c^{-0.6} = e^{2.977} = 19.65
$$

$$
a_i^{-0.6} = e^{3.901} = 49.40
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$
a_c^{-0.6} - a_i^{-0.6} = 19.65 - 49.40 = -29.75
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator term $(Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi})^{m}$:**

$$
Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi} = 1.12 \times 90 \times 1.7725 = 178.68
$$

$$
(178.68)^{3.2}
$$

Using logs:

$$
\ln(178.68) = 5.186
$$

$$
3.2 \times 5.186 = 16.595
$$

$$
(178.68)^{3.2} = e^{16.595} = 1.624 \times 10^{7}
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble $N_f$:**

$$
N_f = \dfrac{-29.75}{(1.5 \times 10^{-11})(1.624 \times 10^{7})(-0.6)}
$$

**Step 6 — Denominator value:**

$$
(1.5 \times 10^{-11})(1.624 \times 10^{7}) = 2.436 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
(2.436 \times 10^{-4})(-0.6) = -1.462 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 7 — Final:**

$$
N_f = \dfrac{-29.75}{-1.462 \times 10^{-4}} = 203{,}490 \, \text{cycles}
$$

> **Examiner Note:** Always present the negative sign cancellation explicitly — students who submit a negative answer lose **2 marks**.

---

> **Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted — INTERNAL CHOICE]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the strain-life ($\varepsilon$-N) approach for low-cycle fatigue life prediction. Derive the unified total strain-life equation starting from Basquin's Law and Coffin-Manson relation.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A pressure vessel steel undergoes fully reversed cyclic loading ($\sigma_m = 0$). The cyclic stress amplitude is $\sigma_a = 320 \, \text{MPa}$. Material data: $\sigma'_f = 900 \, \text{MPa}$, $b = -0.08$, $\varepsilon'_f = 0.4$, $c = -0.6$, $E = 210 \, \text{GPa}$. Compute the number of cycles to failure.

**Model Solution (Part a, 7 marks):**

The strain-life approach is used in the *low-cycle fatigue* regime (typically $N_f < 10^{4}$ to $10^{5}$ cycles), where macroscopic plastic strain is significant. The total strain amplitude is partitioned into elastic and plastic components:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta\varepsilon}{2} = \dfrac{\Delta\varepsilon_e}{2} + \dfrac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2}
$$

**Basquin's contribution (elastic strain):**

$$
\dfrac{\Delta\varepsilon_e}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma'_f}{E}(2N_f)^{b}
$$

**Coffin-Manson contribution (plastic strain):**

$$
\dfrac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^{c}
$$

**Unified strain-life equation:**

$$
\dfrac{\Delta\varepsilon}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma'_f}{E}(2N_f)^{b} + \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^{c}
$$

This is the canonical *Morrow–Manson–Coffin* form.

**Model Solution (Part b, 7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta\varepsilon / 2$ from elastic strain (Hooke's Law):**

$$
\dfrac{\Delta\varepsilon_e}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_a}{E} = \dfrac{320}{210 \times 10^{3}} = 1.524 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Basquin's Law to find elastic-life cycles $N_{e}$:**

$$
\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_e)^{b}
$$

$$
320 = 900 (2N_e)^{-0.08}
$$

$$
\dfrac{320}{900} = (2N_e)^{-0.08}
$$

$$
0.3556 = (2N_e)^{-0.08}
$$

Taking the $-1/0.08$ power:

$$
2N_e = (0.3556)^{-1/0.08} = (0.3556)^{-12.5}
$$

$$
\ln(2N_e) = -12.5 \ln(0.3556) = -12.5 \times (-1.034) = 12.928
$$

$$
2N_e = e^{12.928} = 4.097 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
N_e = 2.048 \times 10^{5} \, \text{cycles}
$$

**Step 3 — Since the problem provides the elastic amplitude (no plastic data is required here because total $\Delta\varepsilon$ is dominated by elastic strain at this stress level), the answer is:**

$$
N_f \approx N_e \approx 2.05 \times 10^{5} \, \text{cycles}
$$

> **Examiner Note:** KTU expects students to recognise that when $\sigma_a \ll \sigma_y$ (i.e., $320 \, \text{MPa} \ll 900 \, \text{MPa}$ yield-like strength), plastic strain is **negligible**. For full credit, students should briefly justify why the Coffin-Manson term is dropped.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in Life Prediction Questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign Errors in Paris Integration** — A negative $N_f$ means you inverted $a_c$ and $a_i$. Always verify: $a_c > a_i$ should yield **negative exponent** $a_c^{1-m/2} - a_i^{1-m/2}$ when $m > 2$, and the *negative × negative* denominator resolves to positive $N_f$.
> 2. **Forgetting the Geometry Factor $Y$** — KTU question banks include $Y$ values from 0.9 to 1.5. Dropping $Y$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Stress Amplitude with Stress Range** — $\sigma_a = \Delta\sigma / 2$. Use $\sigma_a$ in Basquin and $\Delta\sigma$ in Paris.
> 4. **Wrong Units in Paris Constant $C$** — If $C$ is given in inch-cycle units, conversion to SI is mandatory before calculation.
> 5. **Miner's Rule Without a Threshold** — Always state that $D = 1$ is the design failure criterion; do not write $D = 0$.
> 6. **No Justification of Assumptions** — Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for omitting assumptions (constant amplitude, validity in Region II, etc.).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Life prediction** estimates cycles/hours before failure under defined service conditions.
- **Two philosophies:** Total life (S-N / $\varepsilon$-N) for crack initiation; defect-tolerant (Paris Law) for crack propagation.
- **Basquin's Law:** $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^{b}$ — high-cycle, elastic regime.
- **Coffin-Manson:** $\Delta\varepsilon_p / 2 = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^{c}$ — low-cycle, plastic regime.
- **Unified strain-life:** Elastic + Plastic terms added.
- **Miner's Rule:** $D = \sum n_i / N_i = 1$ at failure; order-independent, linear, no interaction effects.
- **Paris Law:** $da/dN = C (\Delta K)^{m}$; integrates to the closed-form $N_f$ expression.
- **Mean Stress Corrections:** Modified Goodman (most common in KTU), Soderberg (most conservative), Gerber (least conservative).
- **Decision Tree:** Choose Total-Life for smooth ductile parts; choose Defect-Tolerant for welded/cast components with assumed initial flaws.
- **Units Discipline:** $a$ in metres, $\Delta K$ in MPa$\sqrt{m}$, $\Delta\sigma$ in MPa.
- **Always quote the validity range** of Paris Region II and justify assumptions to earn full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fracture Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fracture Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fracture Control** is defined as the systematic engineering discipline that integrates fracture mechanics principles, material selection, structural design philosophy, manufacturing quality assurance, and in-service inspection methodologies to prevent catastrophic brittle fracture and to ensure structural integrity, reliability, and predetermined service life of engineering components and structures.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context for **PEMET744 – Failure Analysis and Design**, fracture control is treated as the *proactive countermeasure framework* to fracture, complementing the *post-failure diagnostic* approach of failure analysis. It is governed by three pillars:

1. **Prevention** – Designing out the conditions that initiate fracture.
2. **Prediction** – Quantifying safe operating life using crack-growth laws.
3. **Protection** – Implementing inspection and fail-safe mechanisms to arrest or tolerate cracks before they reach critical size.

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Leaking Water Tank**
>
> Imagine a pressurized overhead water tank made of mild steel. Even a tiny hairline crack (say 2 mm) may not cause immediate failure because the steel is ductile. However, a brittle tank of the same size, with the same crack, can rupture explosively. **Fracture Control** is the engineering equivalent of:
> - Choosing the right *material grade* (like using a tougher tank).
> - Designing *welded joints away from high-stress zones* (smart geometry).
> - *Inspecting periodically* for new cracks (like annual maintenance).
> - Installing a *crack arrester* (a thick reinforcement ring) to stop propagation.
>
> In short, **fracture control is the "insurance policy" that ensures a structure never experiences a catastrophic, unstable fracture during its service life.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are central to fracture control and are universally adopted in KTU reference materials:

- **Stress Intensity Factor, K** (units: **MPa·√m** or **ksi·√in**)
- **Plane-Strain Fracture Toughness, K_IC** (units: **MPa·√m**)
- **Fracture Energy, G_C** (units: **J/m²** or **N/m**)
- **Paris Law Constant, C** and **Exponent, m**
- **Critical Crack Length, a_c** (units: **mm** or **m**)
- **Threshold Stress Intensity Range, ΔK_th** (units: **MPa·√m**)
- **Geometry (Correction) Factor, Y** (dimensionless, typically 0.5 to 2.0)

## 1.4 Key Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fracture control is non-negotiable in **aerospace**, **nuclear**, **offshore**, **pressure vessel**, and **bridge** applications — industries where fracture leads to loss of life, environmental catastrophe, or strategic failure. The **Damage Tolerance Design philosophy** mandated by **FAA, US Navy (MIL-A-83444)**, and **ASME Section XI** for nuclear piping is a direct application of fracture control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fracture Control Triad — A Structured Theoretical Framework

Fracture control rests on three tightly coupled engineering decisions:

### 2.1.1 Pillar I — Material Selection for Fracture Resistance

- **High K_IC** (fracture toughness) is desired, especially at low service temperatures.
- Avoid materials exhibiting **ductile-to-brittle transition** within the operating temperature window.
- Prefer **fine-grained microstructures**; coarser grains lower toughness.
- Use **Charpy V-Notch (CVN) impact energy ≥ 20 J at minimum service temperature** as a practical specification (per ASME and many KTU reference standards).
- Account for **strain-rate sensitivity**: higher loading rates shift DBTT upward.

### 2.1.2 Pillar II — Design Philosophy (Choose One)

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes three classical philosophies, frequently appearing as **14-mark analytical questions**:

| # | Philosophy | Core Principle | When Used |
|---|------------|----------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Safe-Life Design** | Component is retired after a calculated service life *before* any crack reaches critical size. *No cracks allowed in service.* | Aircraft wings, rotating components |
| 2 | **Fail-Safe Design** | Structure contains redundant load paths so that a single fracture does not cause total loss. Cracks are detected and managed. | Aircraft fuselages, bridges |
| 3 | **Damage Tolerant Design** | Pre-existing flaws (assumed minimum detectable) are tolerated. Inspection schedule ensures cracks are found *well before* reaching critical size. | Aircraft, nuclear, offshore |

### 2.1.3 Pillar III — Inspection & Crack-Growth Management

- **Initial Crack Size Assumption (a_i):** Typically taken as the **minimum detectable crack size (a_d)** of the chosen Non-Destructive Evaluation (NDE) method.
- **Final Crack Size (a_f):** Critical crack length **a_c = (K_IC/(Yσ))² / π**.
- **Inspection Interval (T):** Calculated so that a crack grows from a_i to a_f over at least one inspection period plus a safety margin.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **most-tested set of equations** for KTU Part-B (14-mark) questions on Fracture Control.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | Stress Intensity Factor (Mode I) | $K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ | $\sigma$ = applied stress; $a$ = crack length; $Y$ = geometry factor |
| 2 | Critical Stress for Fracture | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sqrt{\pi a}}$ | Stress at which unstable fracture initiates |
| 3 | Critical Crack Length | $a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sigma}\right)^{2}$ | Maximum allowable crack length |
| 4 | Energy Release Rate (Irwin) | $G = \dfrac{K_I^{2}}{E'}$ | $E' = E$ for plane stress; $E' = E/(1-\nu^{2})$ for plane strain |
| 5 | Fracture Criterion | $K_I \geq K_{IC}$ | Brittle fracture condition |
| 6 | Paris Law (Crack Growth) | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^{m}$ | $C$, $m$ are material constants; $\Delta K = K_{max}-K_{min}$ |
| 7 | Crack Growth Life (m ≠ 2) | $N_f = \dfrac{a_f^{\,1-m/2} - a_i^{\,1-m/2}}{C(\Delta\sigma)^{m} Y^{m} \pi^{m/2}\,(1-m/2)}$ | General integration of Paris Law |
| 8 | Crack Growth Life (m = 2) | $N_f = \dfrac{\ln(a_f/a_i)}{C(\Delta\sigma)^{2} Y^{2} \pi}$ | Special case for m = 2 |
| 9 | Threshold Condition | $\Delta K \leq \Delta K_{th}$ | Below this, crack does not propagate |
| 10 | Damage Tolerance Inspection Period | $T = \dfrac{N_f}{f}$ | $f$ = cycles/year; conservative: use factor of 2–4 |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Fracture Control

- **Aerospace:** Determines inspection intervals for aircraft wings (Damage Tolerance).
- **Nuclear Power:** ASME Section XI mandates fracture control in piping welds.
- **Offshore Structures:** Governs platform fatigue life in marine environments.
- **Pressure Vessels & Boilers:** Prevents catastrophic rupture, often governed by **PB Code** and **ASME Sec VIII**.
- **Civil Bridges:** Fracture-critical members are inspected using **NDE techniques** (Dye Penetrant, Magnetic Particle, Eddy Current, Ultrasonic).

## 2.4 Conceptual Flow of Fracture Control Engineering

> **Design** → **Material Selection** → **Stress Analysis** → **Detect Flaw (a_i)** → **Predict Growth (Paris Law)** → **Critical Crack (a_c)** → **Set Inspection Interval** → **Replace / Repair**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Crack Length from Fracture Criterion

**Given:** Brittle fracture condition $K_I = K_{IC}$ and the general SIF expression $K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$.

**Step 1 — Equate the SIF to the material toughness** (this is the fracture criterion for unstable crack growth):

$$
K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}
$$

$$
K_{IC} = Y\sigma_c\sqrt{\pi a_c}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve algebraically for the critical crack length $a_c$.**

Square both sides to remove the square root:

$$
K_{IC}^{\,2} = Y^{2}\,\sigma_c^{2}\,\pi a_c
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $a_c$:**

$$
a_c = \dfrac{K_{IC}^{\,2}}{Y^{2}\,\pi\,\sigma_c^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Standard textbook form (commonly tested):**

$$
\boxed{\,a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sigma_c}\right)^{2}\,}
$$

> This derivation is the foundation of every fracture-control problem. **Memorize the boxed form** for KTU examinations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Crack Growth Life from Paris Law (General Case, m ≠ 2)

**Given:** Paris Law $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m$ with $\Delta K = Y\,\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$.

**Step 1 — Substitute the SIF range into Paris Law:**

$$
\dfrac{da}{dN} = C\left(Y\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}\right)^{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the power-law expansion** (since $m$ is usually 2, 3, or 4 in practice):

$$
\dfrac{da}{dN} = C\,Y^{m}\,(\Delta\sigma)^{m}\,\pi^{m/2}\,a^{m/2}
$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables** (separable ODE):

$$
a^{-m/2}\,da = C\,Y^{m}\,(\Delta\sigma)^{m}\,\pi^{m/2}\,dN
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from initial crack size $a_i$ to final crack size $a_f$:**

$$
\int_{a_i}^{a_f} a^{-m/2}\,da = C\,Y^{m}\,(\Delta\sigma)^{m}\,\pi^{m/2}\,\int_{0}^{N_f} dN
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the left integral** (using $\int a^n da = \dfrac{a^{n+1}}{n+1}$ with $n = -m/2$):

$$
\left[\dfrac{a^{\,1-m/2}}{1-m/2}\right]_{a_i}^{a_f} = C\,Y^{m}\,(\Delta\sigma)^{m}\,\pi^{m/2}\,N_f
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $N_f$:**

$$
\dfrac{a_f^{\,1-m/2} - a_i^{\,1-m/2}}{1-m/2} = C\,Y^{m}\,(\Delta\sigma)^{m}\,\pi^{m/2}\,N_f
$$

$$
\boxed{\,N_f = \dfrac{a_f^{\,1-m/2} - a_i^{\,1-m/2}}{C\,Y^{m}\,(\Delta\sigma)^{m}\,\pi^{m/2}\,(1-m/2)}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula is valid only when $m \neq 2$. When $m = 2$, the left-hand integral becomes $\int a^{-1}\,da = \ln a$, leading to the logarithmic form given in row 8 of the formula sheet.

---

## 3.3 Special Case Derivation: Crack Growth Life when m = 2

**Step 1 — Substitute m = 2 in Paris Law:**

$$
\dfrac{da}{dN} = C\,Y^{2}\,(\Delta\sigma)^{2}\,\pi\,a
$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**

$$
\dfrac{da}{a} = C\,Y^{2}\,(\Delta\sigma)^{2}\,\pi\,dN
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate:**

$$
\int_{a_i}^{a_f} \dfrac{da}{a} = C\,Y^{2}\,(\Delta\sigma)^{2}\,\pi\int_{0}^{N_f}dN
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{a_f}{a_i}\right) = C\,Y^{2}\,(\Delta\sigma)^{2}\,\pi\,N_f
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N_f$:**

$$
\boxed{\,N_f = \dfrac{\ln(a_f/a_i)}{C\,Y^{2}\,(\Delta\sigma)^{2}\,\pi}\,}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Damage Tolerance Inspection Interval

**Given:** $N_f$ = total cycles to reach critical crack; $f$ = loading frequency (cycles per unit time).

**Step 1 — Convert cycles to time:**

$$
t_f = \dfrac{N_f}{f}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply safety factor (typical KTU convention: divide by 2 to 4):**

$$
T = \dfrac{t_f}{n} = \dfrac{N_f}{n\,f}, \quad n \in [2,4]
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** Inspection must occur at intervals ≤ $T$ to guarantee detection *before* the crack reaches $a_c$.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Matrix-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **KTU-style numerical** that may appear as a 7-mark sub-question.

**Problem:**
A steel plate contains an edge crack. Given $Y = 1.12$, $K_{IC} = 55\,\text{MPa}\sqrt{m}$, applied stress $\sigma = 200\,\text{MPa}$, initial crack $a_i = 1\,\text{mm}$, and Paris Law constants $C = 1.2\times10^{-11}$, $m = 3$, $\Delta\sigma = 180\,\text{MPa}$. Calculate (a) critical crack length and (b) cycles to failure.

### Solution

**(a) Critical crack length:**

$$
a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{55}{1.12 \times 200}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{55}{224}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\,(0.2455)^{2} = \dfrac{0.06028}{\pi}
$$

$$
\boxed{a_c = 0.01919\,\text{m} = 19.19\,\text{mm}}
$$

**(b) Cycles to failure (m = 3, hence $m/2 = 1.5$, $1 - m/2 = -0.5$):**

$$
N_f = \dfrac{a_f^{-0.5} - a_i^{-0.5}}{C\,Y^{m}\,(\Delta\sigma)^{m}\,\pi^{m/2}\,(-0.5)}
$$

Plugging in (use SI units, $a_f = 0.01919$, $a_i = 0.001$):

$$
a_f^{-0.5} = (0.01919)^{-0.5} = 7.218
$$

$$
a_i^{-0.5} = (0.001)^{-0.5} = 31.623
$$

$$
a_f^{-0.5} - a_i^{-0.5} = 7.218 - 31.623 = -24.405
$$

Denominator:

$$
C\,Y^{3}\,(\Delta\sigma)^{3}\,\pi^{1.5}\,(-0.5)
$$

$$
= 1.2\times10^{-11} \times (1.12)^3 \times (180)^3 \times (5.568) \times (-0.5)
$$

Compute stepwise:

- $(1.12)^3 = 1.4049$
- $(180)^3 = 5.832\times10^{6}$
- Product: $1.4049 \times 5.832\times10^{6} = 8.196\times10^{6}$
- Multiply by $\pi^{1.5} = 5.568$: $8.196\times10^{6} \times 5.568 = 4.564\times10^{7}$
- Multiply by $C = 1.2\times10^{-11}$: $4.564\times10^{7} \times 1.2\times10^{-11} = 5.477\times10^{-4}$
- Multiply by $(-0.5)$: $-2.738\times10^{-4}$

Therefore:

$$
N_f = \dfrac{-24.405}{-2.738\times10^{-4}} = 89{,}128\,\text{cycles}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_f \approx 8.91 \times 10^{4}\,\text{cycles}}
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Design Philosophy

| Condition / Requirement | Recommended Philosophy | Justification |
|--------------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Single critical member, no redundancy | **Safe-Life** | No fail-safe option |
| Highly inspectable, redundant load paths | **Fail-Safe** | Crack detectable, alternate path carries load |
| Frequent inspection impractical, critical structure | **Damage Tolerant** | Conservative life based on assumed initial flaw |
| Rotating component (high-cycle fatigue) | **Safe-Life** | Crack initiation life controls retirement |
| Aircraft primary structure (per FAA) | **Damage Tolerant** | Mandatory by regulation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fracture Control Program

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Design Requirements] --> nodeB[Material Selection]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Stress Analysis & SIF Calculation]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Detect or Assume Initial Flaw a_i]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Compute Critical Crack a_c]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Apply Paris Law da/dN]
    F1[Choose C and m from database] --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Predict Total Life N_f]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Set Inspection Interval T]
    nodeH --> nodeI[In-Service NDE Monitoring]
    nodeI --> nodeJ{Crack detected?}
    nodeJ -- Yes --> nodeK[Repair / Replace / Re-route Load]
    nodeJ -- No --> nodeL[Continue Service]
    nodeK --> nodeM[Failure Analysis Loopback]
    nodeL --> nodeH
```

> [!NOTE]
> This flowchart shows the **closed-loop nature** of fracture control. The system is not static; inspection data continuously updates the operational decisions, mirroring the **damage tolerance philosophy**.

## 4.2 Decision Tree: Choosing the Design Philosophy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[New Structural Component] --> nodeB{Can the structure be inspected?}
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC{Is redundancy available?}
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeD[Fail-Safe Design]
    nodeC -- No --> nodeE[Damage Tolerant Design]
    nodeB -- No --> nodeF{Can crack be prevented entirely?}
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG[Safe-Life Design]
    nodeF -- No --> nodeH[Re-evaluate material or geometry]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of the Three Design Philosophies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Safe-Life] --> nodeA1[No cracks tolerated]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2[Retire before a reaches a_c]
    nodeB[Fail-Safe] --> nodeB1[Cracks tolerated if load is rerouted]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2[Multiple load paths]
    nodeC[Damage Tolerant] --> nodeC1[Cracks assumed at a_i = detectable size]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2[Inspect before a reaches a_c]
```

## 4.4 Crack-Growth Curve (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Region I: da/dN approaches zero below Delta K th] --> nodeB[Region II: Paris Law regime da/dN = C Delta K to m]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Region III: Rapid unstable growth toward a_c]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Catastrophic Fracture K_I reaches K_IC]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Region II (Paris regime)** is the basis for life prediction in fracture control. **Region III** is *never* allowed during service — fracture control ensures the crack never reaches this stage.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define fracture control and list its three primary objectives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Fracture Control** is the engineering discipline that uses fracture mechanics principles to prevent catastrophic brittle fracture and to ensure safe, reliable operation of structural components.
>
> **Three primary objectives:**
> 1. **Prevention** — Eliminate the conditions that lead to fracture initiation.
> 2. **Prediction** — Quantify remaining useful life via crack-growth laws such as Paris Law.
> 3. **Protection** — Schedule inspection and design fail-safe features so cracks are detected and arrested before they reach critical size.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for prevention/prediction/protection mention + 1 mark for any one explained example.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Safe-Life Design and Damage Tolerant Design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Safe-Life | Damage Tolerant |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| Crack Assumption | No crack allowed in service | Initial crack of detectable size assumed |
| Retirement | At calculated initiation life | When crack reaches critical size during inspection |
| Inspection | Not primary reliance | Mandatory periodic NDE |
| Best Use | Rotating components | Aircraft primary structure |

*(Allocation: 1 mark per distinguishing row, or 1.5 marks for principle + 1.5 marks for example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse initial flaw $a_i$ with detectable flaw $a_d$.** Always state your assumption explicitly.
> 2. **Geometry factor Y ≠ 1.0 in most problems.** Students lose 2 marks by writing Y = 1 always.
> 3. **Use consistent SI units.** Mixing mm with MPa·√m is a common error losing 1–2 marks.
> 4. **For Damage Tolerance questions, always mention inspection interval logic**, not just $N_f$.
> 5. **Critical crack length is in metres** when $\sigma$ is in MPa and $K_{IC}$ is in MPa·√m. Convert mm → m before plugging in.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

A large steel pressure vessel is fabricated from a plate with $K_{IC} = 66\,\text{MPa}\sqrt{m}$. A semi-elliptical surface crack of depth $a_i = 2\,\text{mm}$ is detected by ultrasonic inspection. The geometry factor $Y = 1.0$, applied cyclic stress range $\Delta\sigma = 240\,\text{MPa}$, and Paris Law constants are $C = 6.9\times10^{-12}$ (in SI units with $\Delta K$ in MPa·√m and $a$ in m), $m = 3$.

**(a)** Compute the critical crack length $a_c$ and the number of cycles $N_f$ to reach this critical size. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the vessel undergoes 10 pressure cycles per day, determine the safe inspection interval using a safety factor of 3. Justify whether the design is damage tolerant. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Critical crack length and cycles to failure**

For a surface crack with $Y = 1.0$ and $\sigma = \Delta\sigma/2$ for mean-stress simplicity, the critical crack is computed assuming the maximum tensile stress equals $\sigma_{max} = 240\,\text{MPa}$.

$$
a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sigma_{max}}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{66}{1.0 \times 240}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\,(0.275)^{2}
$$

$$
a_c = \dfrac{0.075625}{\pi} = 0.02407\,\text{m} = 24.07\,\text{mm}
$$

**Cycles to failure using Paris Law (m = 3):**

$$
N_f = \dfrac{a_f^{-0.5} - a_i^{-0.5}}{C\,Y^{3}\,(\Delta\sigma)^{3}\,\pi^{1.5}\,(-0.5)}
$$

Convert crack sizes to metres: $a_f = 0.02407\,\text{m}$, $a_i = 0.002\,\text{m}$.

- $a_f^{-0.5} = (0.02407)^{-0.5} = 6.445$
- $a_i^{-0.5} = (0.002)^{-0.5} = 22.361$
- Numerator: $6.445 - 22.361 = -15.916$

Denominator:

- $Y^{3} = 1.0$
- $(\Delta\sigma)^{3} = (240)^{3} = 1.382\times10^{7}$
- $\pi^{1.5} = 5.568$
- Product: $1.382\times10^{7} \times 5.568 = 7.695\times10^{7}$
- Multiply by $C = 6.9\times10^{-12}$: $7.695\times10^{7} \times 6.9\times10^{-12} = 5.310\times10^{-4}$
- Multiply by $(-0.5)$: $-2.655\times10^{-4}$

$$
N_f = \dfrac{-15.916}{-2.655\times10^{-4}} = 59{,}944\,\text{cycles}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_f \approx 5.99\times10^{4}\,\text{cycles},\quad a_c \approx 24.07\,\text{mm}}
$$

---

**(b) Safe inspection interval and damage tolerance justification**

Time to reach critical crack:

$$
t_f = \dfrac{N_f}{f} = \dfrac{59{,}944}{10\,\text{cycles/day}} = 5{,}994\,\text{days}
$$

Convert to years: $5{,}994/365 = 16.42\,\text{years}$.

Apply safety factor $n = 3$:

$$
T = \dfrac{t_f}{n} = \dfrac{16.42}{3} = 5.47\,\text{years}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 5.5\,\text{years (recommended inspection interval)}}
$$

**Justification (Damage Tolerant):**

The design is damage tolerant because:
1. The initial flaw $a_i = 2\,\text{mm}$ is detectable by Ultrasonic NDE.
2. There are 16.4 years of crack growth before reaching $a_c$.
3. With inspection every 5.5 years, at least 2 inspections occur *before* critical size, providing adequate safety margin.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the three classical design philosophies used in fracture control: Safe-Life, Fail-Safe, and Damage Tolerant Design. Compare them with suitable engineering examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A welded steel structure has $K_{IC} = 50\,\text{MPa}\sqrt{m}$, $Y = 1.12$, and operates under a maximum cyclic stress of $\sigma_{max} = 180\,\text{MPa}$, with $\Delta\sigma = 120\,\text{MPa}$. If the minimum detectable crack is $a_i = 1.5\,\text{mm}$, calculate (i) the critical crack length and (ii) the cycles to reach critical size using $C = 2.0\times10^{-11}$ and $m = 3$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Three design philosophies** *(rubric: ~2.3 marks each)*

**1. Safe-Life Design:**
- Principle: Component is retired *before* any crack can initiate and propagate to critical size.
- No allowance for in-service cracks; relies on calculated fatigue life.
- Example: Aircraft turbine discs, propeller blades — high-cycle fatigue dominated.

**2. Fail-Safe Design:**
- Principle: Structure has redundant load paths so that failure of one member does not cause total collapse.
- Cracks are tolerated *provided* alternate load paths can carry the redistributed load.
- Example: Multi-spar aircraft wings; bridge trusses with multiple girders.

**3. Damage Tolerant Design:**
- Principle: Structure is assumed to contain an initial detectable flaw at the time of manufacture. Periodic inspection ensures the crack is detected before reaching critical size.
- Combines fracture mechanics with NDE scheduling.
- Example: Modern airframes (per FAA damage tolerance regulations), nuclear pressure vessels (ASME Sec XI).

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Safe-Life | Fail-Safe | Damage Tolerant |
|--------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Crack Presence | None | Possible | Assumed |
| Inspection | Not Primary | Useful | Mandatory |
| Load Redundancy | Not Required | Required | Not Required |
| Life Basis | Crack Initiation | Inspection + Redundancy | Crack Growth |
| Industry | Rotating Machinery | Aircraft, Bridges | Aircraft, Nuclear |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**(i) Critical crack length:**

$$
a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sigma_{max}}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{50}{1.12 \times 180}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{50}{201.6}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\,(0.2480)^{2} = \dfrac{0.0615}{\pi}
$$

$$
\boxed{a_c = 0.01958\,\text{m} = 19.58\,\text{mm}}
$$

**(ii) Cycles to critical crack (m = 3):**

Use $a_f = 0.01958\,\text{m}$, $a_i = 0.0015\,\text{m}$.

- $a_f^{-0.5} = (0.01958)^{-0.5} = 7.146$
- $a_i^{-0.5} = (0.0015)^{-0.5} = 25.820$
- Numerator: $7.146 - 25.820 = -18.674$

Denominator:

- $Y^{3} = (1.12)^{3} = 1.4049$
- $(\Delta\sigma)^{3} = (120)^{3} = 1.728\times10^{6}$
- $\pi^{1.5} = 5.568$
- Product: $1.4049 \times 1.728\times10^{6} = 2.428\times10^{6}$
- Multiply by $5.568$: $1.352\times10^{7}$
- Multiply by $C = 2.0\times10^{-11}$: $2.704\times10^{-4}$
- Multiply by $(-0.5)$: $-1.352\times10^{-4}$

$$
N_f = \dfrac{-18.674}{-1.352\times10^{-4}} = 138{,}123\,\text{cycles}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_f \approx 1.38\times10^{5}\,\text{cycles}}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fracture Control**
>
> - **Fracture Control = Prevention + Prediction + Protection.**
> - The **fracture criterion** is $K_I \geq K_{IC}$ (Mode I brittle fracture).
> - **Critical crack length:** $a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sigma}\right)^{2}$. Always convert crack length to **metres** for SI consistency.
> - **Paris Law:** $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^{m}$ governs Region II crack growth.
> - **Crack growth life (m ≠ 2):** $N_f = \dfrac{a_f^{\,1-m/2} - a_i^{\,1-m/2}}{C\,Y^{m}\,(\Delta\sigma)^{m}\,\pi^{m/2}\,(1-m/2)}$.
> - **Crack growth life (m = 2):** $N_f = \dfrac{\ln(a_f/a_i)}{C\,Y^{2}\,(\Delta\sigma)^{2}\,\pi}$.
> - **Inspection interval:** $T = \dfrac{N_f}{n\,f}$ where $n = 2$–$4$ (safety factor) and $f$ = cycles per unit time.
> - **Three philosophies:**
>   - *Safe-Life:* No cracks allowed; retire before initiation.
>   - *Fail-Safe:* Multiple load paths tolerate cracks.
>   - *Damage Tolerant:* Pre-existing flaws assumed; inspect periodically.
> - **Geometry factor Y** depends on crack geometry — never assume Y = 1.0 unless stated.
> - **Threshold ΔK_th** marks the start of Region II crack growth; cracks below this value do not propagate.
> - **Mandatory units:** $K_{IC}$ in MPa·√m, $\sigma$ in MPa, $a$ in **m**, $C$ such that SI consistency holds.
> - **Damage Tolerance applications:** Aerospace (FAA, MIL-A-83444), Nuclear (ASME Sec XI), Offshore, Pressure Vessels.
> - **NDE techniques:** Dye Penetrant, Magnetic Particle, Eddy Current, Ultrasonic — used to detect $a_i$ at the start of service life.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fatigue Design for Combined Stress
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fatigue Design for Combined Stress

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fatigue Design for Combined Stress** (also called **Multiaxial Fatigue** or **Multimode Fatigue**) is the analytical and experimental methodology used to predict the fatigue life of engineering components subjected to simultaneous or phase-shifted loading on more than one axis, where the principal stress directions may be fixed in space (**proportional loading**) or rotate with time (**non-proportional loading**).

In the context of KTU 2024 Module 2, this concept extends the classical uniaxial S-N curve approach (Goodman, Gerber, Soderberg) to components experiencing combined **normal (tensile/compressive) and shear stresses**, or **multiple normal stresses acting on different planes** simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box — Multiaxial Fatigue:**
> "A fatigue loading condition in which the stresses in a critical material element act in more than one principal direction, requiring the use of equivalent stress criteria or critical plane methods to relate the multiaxial stress state to uniaxial fatigue data for life prediction."

### 1.2 Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams

| Term | Formal Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Proportional Loading** | Principal stress directions remain constant; stress components maintain a constant ratio |
| **Non-Proportional Loading** | Principal stress directions rotate during the load cycle |
| **Critical Plane** | The material plane on which fatigue damage is maximum |
| **Equivalent Stress** | A scalar representation of the multiaxial state reduced to an equivalent uniaxial stress |
| **Phase Angle (φ)** | The time-shift between two stress components in a cycle |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **bicycle rider's wrist** during a bumpy ride on a curvy mountain road. The wrist is not just bent up and down (uniaxial) — it is simultaneously being **twisted (torsional shear)** as the handlebar rotates, **bent sideways (lateral bending)** as the rider leans into curves, and **stretched axially (tensile)** as the body weight pushes down. The fatigue damage is not just the sum of these individual stresses — they interact, and the worst plane of damage rotates as the load history changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:** Crankshafts, turbine blades, driveline couplings, railway axles, helicopter rotor hubs, and pressure vessel nozzles all experience **combined stress fatigue**. Designing them using only the largest uniaxial stress is **unsafe** and **non-conservative**, which is why KTU 2024 explicitly tests this topic in Module 2.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (Bolded Constants for KTU Boards)

- **Modulus of Elasticity (E)**: typically **200 GPa** for structural steel
- **Poisson's Ratio (ν)**: **0.3** for most metals
- **Endurance Limit (S<sub>e</sub> or S'<sub>n</sub>)**: For steels, **S'<sub>n</sub> ≈ 0.5 S<sub>ut</sub>** (Rotating beam)
- **Shear Endurance Limit (S<sub>se</sub>)**: **S<sub>se</sub> ≈ 0.577 S'<sub>n</sub>**
- **Fatigue Strength Fraction (f)**: **f = 0.9** (axial loading correction, Shigley/Norton)
- **Stress Concentration Factor (K<sub>t</sub>)** and **Notch Sensitivity (q)** — used with Neuber's equation for combined stress concentration.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Uniaxial Methods Fail Under Combined Stress

Classical **Goodman, Gerber, and Soderberg** relations are derived from **rotating-beam fatigue tests** where the stress state is purely uniaxial tension-compression. When the stress state becomes biaxial or triaxial, three fundamental problems arise:

1. **Direction Problem**: The fatigue crack initiates on a specific **critical plane** that depends on the loading history, not merely on the maximum principal stress.
2. **Phase Problem**: Out-of-phase stress components cause **additional mean stress** on the critical plane, accelerating damage.
3. **Material Anisotropy**: Metals exhibit **different sensitivity to tensile vs. shear** cyclic loading — a fact ignored by simple equivalent-stress rules.

## 2.2 Classification of Multiaxial Fatigue Criteria (KTU Board-Favorite)

### A. **Equivalent Stress (Integral) Criteria**
These methods reduce the multiaxial state to a single scalar (equivalent stress) and apply the uniaxial S-N curve.

#### (i) **Von Mises Equivalent Stress Approach**
Treats the cyclic Von Mises stress as the uniaxial driver.

$$\sigma_{a,eq} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_{1a}-\sigma_{2a})^2 + (\sigma_{2a}-\sigma_{3a})^2 + (\sigma_{3a}-\sigma_{1a})^2}{2}}$$

**Limitation**: Ignores mean stress and phase shifts.

#### (ii) **Sines Criterion (1959)**
Accounts for **mean stress hydrostatic component** on the critical plane.

$$\frac{\sigma_{a,eq}}{S_n} + \frac{\sigma_{H,m}}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{N_f^b}$$

where **σ<sub>H,m</sub>** is the hydrostatic mean stress.

#### (iii) **Crossland Criterion**
Adds the **maximum shear stress amplitude** to account for torsional fatigue.

$$\sqrt{J_{2,a}} + \alpha \cdot \sigma_{H,max} = \beta$$

where **J<sub>2,a</sub>** is the second invariant of the deviatoric stress tensor, **α** and **β** are material constants calibrated from **pure tension and pure torsion fatigue tests**.

### B. **Critical Plane (Local) Criteria**
These search for the plane of maximum damage.

#### (iv) **Findley Criterion (1959) — Most Tested in KTU**

$$\frac{\Delta \tau}{2} + k \cdot \sigma_n = \tau_f' \cdot (2N_f)^b$$

- **Δτ/2** = shear stress amplitude on the plane
- **σ<sub>n</sub>** = maximum normal stress on the plane
- **k, τ'<sub>f</sub>, b** = material fatigue parameters

#### (v) **Dang Van Criterion (1973)**
The crack initiates when, on the **mesoscopic scale**, the elastic shakedown limit is exceeded:

$$\max_{t} \left[ \tau(t) + \alpha \cdot \sigma_H(t) \right] = \beta$$

- **τ(t)** = resolved shear stress
- **σ<sub>H</sub>(t)** = hydrostatic stress
- **α, β** = Dang Van constants from pure tension + pure torsion S-N data

## 2.3 Stress Transformation for Combined Loading

For a 2D stress element with **σ<sub>x</sub>, σ<sub>y</sub>, τ<sub>xy</sub>**, the stress on a plane at angle **θ** is:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin 2\theta$$

$$\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy} \cos 2\theta$$

The **critical plane** is found by maximizing the damage parameter (e.g., Findley's LHS) over **θ ∈ [0°, 180°]**.

## 2.4 Neuber's Rule for Combined Stress Concentration

When the combined loading creates a notch, the **local stresses** at the notch root are:

$$K_f \cdot \sigma_{nominal} = \sqrt{\sigma_{local} \cdot \varepsilon_{local} \cdot E}$$

Neuber's rule is applied **component-by-component** and then combined via Von Mises:

$$\sigma_{eq,local} = \sqrt{\sigma_{x,local}^2 + \sigma_{y,local}^2 - \sigma_{x,local}\sigma_{y,local} + 3\tau_{xy,local}^2}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{a,VM} = \sqrt{3 \cdot J_{2,a}}$ | Von Mises equivalent amplitude |
| 2 | $\sigma_H = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}$ | Hydrostatic stress |
| 3 | $\sigma_{H,m} = \frac{\sigma_{1m} + \sigma_{2m} + \sigma_{3m}}{3}$ | Hydrostatic mean stress |
| 4 | $\sqrt{J_{2,a}} + \alpha \sigma_{H,max} = \beta$ | **Crossland Criterion** |
| 5 | $\max[\tau(t) + \alpha \sigma_H(t)] = \beta$ | **Dang Van Criterion** |
| 6 | $\frac{\Delta\tau}{2} + k \sigma_n = \tau_f' (2N_f)^b$ | **Findley Criterion** |
| 7 | $\frac{\sigma_a}{S_n} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$ | Goodman (uniaxial) |
| 8 | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | Fatigue stress concentration |
| 9 | $S_n = S_n' \cdot k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e$ | Marin factors |
| 10 | $\sigma_\theta, \tau_\theta$ (transformation) | Plane-stress rotation |
| 11 | $\sigma_1, \sigma_2 = \frac{\sigma_x+\sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Principal stresses |
| 12 | $S_{se} = 0.577 \, S_n'$ | Shear endurance limit |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always convert **MPa to ksi** or vice versa consistently. The examiners **deduct marks** for inconsistent units. Use **N and mm** for SI or **lbf and inch** for US customary.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Crankshafts**: Bending + torsion (Findley and Crossland widely used in industry — e.g., **FEA software like nCode, Fe-Safe**).
- **Aerospace Turbine Discs**: Combined centrifugal + thermal + vibratory stresses → **Dang Van criterion** is the standard.
- **Offshore Risers**: Wave-induced bending + axial + internal pressure → Critical plane methods required.
- **Railway Axles**: Rotating bending + press-fit radial stress → **Sines** or **Findley** approach.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Findley Critical Plane Parameter

The **Findley parameter** is the most commonly derived expression in KTU 2024 Module 2 exams. Let us derive it from the **uniaxial fatigue law on an arbitrary plane**.

### Step 1 — Stress on an Inclined Plane
Consider a 2D stress element (σ<sub>x</sub>, σ<sub>y</sub>, τ<sub>xy</sub>). On a plane inclined at angle **θ** to the x-axis:

$$\sigma_n(\theta) = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin 2\theta$$

$$\tau(\theta) = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy} \cos 2\theta$$

### Step 2 — Decompose into Mean and Amplitude
For a **proportional** load history, each component splits into mean (m) and amplitude (a):

$$\sigma_n = \sigma_{n,m} + \sigma_{n,a}, \quad \tau = \tau_m + \tau_a$$

### Step 3 — Form the Findley Damage Function
Findley proposed that the fatigue damage on plane θ is governed by:

$$F(\theta) = \frac{\Delta \tau(\theta)}{2} + k \cdot \sigma_{n,max}(\theta)$$

where **Δτ/2 = τ<sub>a</sub>** is the **shear stress amplitude**, and **σ<sub>n,max</sub> = σ<sub>n,m</sub> + σ<sub>n,a</sub>** is the **maximum normal stress** on the plane during the cycle.

### Step 4 — Identify the Critical Plane
Differentiate and set to zero to find the critical angle **θ<sub>cr</sub>**:

$$\frac{dF(\theta)}{d\theta} = 0 \Rightarrow \theta_{cr} = \frac{1}{2} \arctan\left(\frac{2 \tau_{xy,a}}{\sigma_{x,a} - \sigma_{y,a}}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Apply the S-N Power Law
At the critical plane, the fatigue life is given by:

$$\frac{\Delta \tau_{cr}}{2} + k \cdot \sigma_{n,cr}^{max} = \tau_f' \cdot (2N_f)^b$$

Solving for **N<sub>f</sub>**:

$$N_f = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\frac{\Delta \tau_{cr}}{2} + k \cdot \sigma_{n,cr}^{max}}{\tau_f'}\right]^{1/b}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Dang Van Constants from Standard Fatigue Data

Given:
- Uniaxial fully reversed fatigue: **σ<sub>a,t</sub>** at **N = N<sub>f,t</sub>**
- Pure torsion fully reversed: **τ<sub>a,t</sub>** at **N = N<sub>f,t</sub>**

The Dang Van criterion is:

$$\max_{t}\left[\tau(t) + \alpha \sigma_H(t)\right] = \beta$$

For fully reversed uniaxial tension (σ(t) = σ<sub>a</sub> sin ωt):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_a}{2}, \quad \sigma_{H,max} = \frac{\sigma_a}{3}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{2} + \alpha \frac{\sigma_a}{3} = \beta \Rightarrow \sigma_a\left(\frac{1}{2} + \frac{\alpha}{3}\right) = \beta$$

For fully reversed pure torsion (τ(t) = τ<sub>a</sub> sin ωt):

$$\tau_{max} = \tau_a, \quad \sigma_{H,max} = 0 \Rightarrow \tau_a = \beta$$

Therefore, the **Dang Van constants** are:

$$\beta = \tau_{a,t} \quad \text{(torsion fatigue limit at } N_f \text{ cycles)}$$

$$\alpha = 3\left(\frac{\sigma_{a,t}}{2\tau_{a,t}} - 1\right) = 3\left(\frac{\sigma_{a,t}}{2\beta} - 1\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For ductile steels:** σ<sub>a,t</sub> ≈ 2 τ<sub>a,t</sub>, giving **α ≈ 0**. This means hydrostatic stress has negligible effect — a known result for **hydrostatic-pressure insensitivity** of fully plastic fatigue in ductile metals.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A steel shaft is subjected to a **synchronous bending moment M = 600 N·m** and a **torsion T = 450 N·m**, both fluctuating between 0 and their maximum value. The shaft diameter is **d = 40 mm**. Material: **S<sub>ut</sub> = 600 MPa**, **S<sub>sy</sub> = 380 MPa**, **S'<sub>n</sub> = 280 MPa**, fatigue strength exponent **b = −0.1**, fatigue ductility coefficient **σ'<sub>f</sub> = 950 MPa**, **ε'<sub>f</sub> = 0.13**, **E = 207 GPa**. Determine the factor of safety using the **Sines Criterion** and **Von Mises equivalent stress**.

### Step 1 — Section Modulus and Stresses
Bending section modulus:

$$Z = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} = \frac{\pi (40)^3}{32} = 6283.2 \text{ mm}^3$$

Polar section modulus:

$$Z_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{16} = 12566.4 \text{ mm}^3$$

Bending stress (0 to max):

$$\sigma_{x,max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{600 \times 10^3}{6283.2} = 95.5 \text{ MPa}$$

Torsional shear stress:

$$\tau_{xy,max} = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{450 \times 10^3}{12566.4} = 35.8 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 2 — Mean and Amplitude Components
Fluctuating 0 → max gives **σ<sub>m</sub> = σ<sub>a</sub> = σ<sub>max</sub>/2**:

$$\sigma_{x,m} = \sigma_{x,a} = 47.75 \text{ MPa}, \quad \tau_{xy,m} = \tau_{xy,a} = 17.9 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 3 — Von Mises Equivalent Stress

$$\sigma_{eq,a} = \sqrt{\sigma_{x,a}^2 + 3\tau_{xy,a}^2} = \sqrt{(47.75)^2 + 3(17.9)^2}$$

$$\sigma_{eq,a} = \sqrt{2280 + 961} = \sqrt{3241} = 56.93 \text{ MPa}$$

Similarly for mean:

$$\sigma_{eq,m} = 56.93 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Goodman Criterion (Uniaxial Equivalent)

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_{eq,a}}{S_n} + \frac{\sigma_{eq,m}}{S_{ut}} = \frac{56.93}{280} + \frac{56.93}{600}$$

$$\frac{1}{n} = 0.2033 + 0.0949 = 0.2982$$

$$\boxed{n = 3.35}$$

### Step 5 — Cross-Check with Sines Criterion
For proportional loading, **σ<sub>H,m</sub> = σ<sub>eq,m</sub>/3 = 18.98 MPa**:

$$\frac{\sigma_{eq,a}}{S_n} + \frac{\sigma_{H,m}}{S_{ut}} = 0.2033 + \frac{18.98}{600} = 0.2033 + 0.0316 = 0.2349$$

$$\boxed{n_{Sines} = 4.26}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The Sines criterion gives a **less conservative (higher) factor of safety** because it only penalizes the *hydrostatic* (volumetric) component of mean stress, whereas the Goodman-based Von Mises approach penalizes the **full equivalent mean stress**. The two converge when **ν = 0.5** (incompressible plastic flow).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Multiaxial Fatigue Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component Geometry] --> B[Load History Definition]
    B --> C{Biaxial or Triaxial?}
    C -- Yes --> D[FE Stress Analysis]
    C -- No --> E[Use Uniaxial S-N]
    D --> F[Critical Location ID]
    F --> G[Stress Tensor Extraction]
    G --> H[Mean and Amplitude Split]
    H --> I[Choose Fatigue Criterion]
    I --> I1[Equivalent Stress]
    I --> I2[Critical Plane]
    I1 --> J1[Von Mises]
    I1 --> J2[Sines]
    I1 --> J3[Crossland]
    I2 --> K1[Findley]
    I2 --> K2[Dang Van]
    J1 --> L[Compute Equivalent Uniaxial]
    J2 --> L
    J3 --> L
    K1 --> M[Search All Planes 0 to 180 deg]
    K2 --> M
    L --> N[Apply S-N Curve with Mean Stress Correction]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Calculate Cycles to Failure]
    O --> P[Compare with Design Life]
    P -- Safe --> Q[Acceptable]
    P -- Not Safe --> R[Modify Geometry or Material]
    R --> A
```

## 4.2 Critical Plane Search Algorithm Flow (Findley Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Initialize theta = 0 deg] --> P1[Compute sigma_n and tau at theta]
    P1 --> P2[Findley Parameter F = tau_a + k sigma_n max]
    P2 --> P3[Store F and theta]
    P3 --> P4[Increment theta by 5 deg]
    P4 --> P5{theta less than 180 deg?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P1
    P5 -- No --> P6[Identify theta_cr at Maximum F]
    P6 --> P7[Apply Findley S-N Power Law]
    P7 --> P8[Output N_f]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Criterion to Apply?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Loading Type?} --> Q1A[Proportional]
    Q1 --> Q1B[Non-Proportional]
    Q1A --> Q2{Material Behavior?}
    Q2 -- Ductile --> Q2A[Von Mises or Sines]
    Q2 -- Brittle --> Q2B[Findley or Crossland]
    Q1B --> Q3{High Cycle Regime?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q3A[Dang Van]
    Q3 -- No --> Q3B[Critical Plane Methods]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Note:** For KTU board exams, when a **Mermaid diagram is not permitted**, redraw the above as a **labelled block diagram** on the answer sheet, clearly marking: (i) Inputs, (ii) Decision nodes, (iii) Output quantities. Examiners award **1–2 marks** for neat, well-labelled schematics.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Distinguish between proportional and non-proportional loading in multiaxial fatigue. State one criterion suitable for each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Proportional loading:** The principal stress directions remain fixed in space throughout the load cycle, and the ratios of stress components (e.g., σ<sub>x</sub>/σ<sub>y</sub>) stay constant. The Mohr's circle merely expands and contracts; it does not rotate. **Suitable criterion:** Von Mises equivalent stress with Goodman mean stress correction (Sines, Crossland, or Findley all apply).

**Non-proportional loading:** The principal stress axes **rotate with time** during the cycle. The Mohr's circle changes both size and orientation. This produces **additional cyclic hardening** in ductile materials and cannot be captured by simple equivalent-stress rules. **Suitable criterion:** **Dang Van criterion** or **Brown-Miller critical plane** method.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "non-proportional = out of phase" without explaining the **rotation of principal axes**. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this incomplete definition.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"What is the Findley parameter? How is the critical plane identified?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Findley parameter** is a fatigue damage indicator defined on an arbitrary material plane inclined at angle θ to the loading axis:

$$F(\theta) = \frac{\Delta \tau(\theta)}{2} + k \cdot \sigma_{n,max}(\theta)$$

where **Δτ/2** is the resolved shear stress amplitude and **σ<sub>n,max</sub>** is the maximum normal stress on the plane.

**Identification of critical plane:** The plane angle **θ<sub>cr</sub>** is found by maximizing **F(θ)** over **θ ∈ [0°, 180°]** (or by setting **dF/dθ = 0**):

$$\frac{dF(\theta)}{d\theta} = 0 \Rightarrow \theta_{cr} = \frac{1}{2} \arctan\left(\frac{2\tau_{xy,a}}{\sigma_{x,a} - \sigma_{y,a}}\right)$$

The fatigue life is then predicted by equating **F(θ<sub>cr</sub>)** to the material's shear-based S-N power law.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** Derive the **Crossland fatigue criterion** starting from the second invariant of the deviatoric stress tensor. Clearly state the assumptions and identify the two material constants. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shaft of diameter 50 mm is subjected to a fully reversed **bending stress σ<sub>a</sub> = 200 MPa** and a **torsional stress τ<sub>a</sub> = 130 MPa** in phase. The material has **S<sub>ut</sub> = 800 MPa**, **S<sub>sy</sub> = 500 MPa**, endurance limit **S'<sub>n</sub> = 400 MPa**, and **surface factor k<sub>a</sub> = 0.8**, size factor **k<sub>b</sub> = 0.85**. Compute the factor of safety using:
    (i) Von Mises + Goodman, and
    (ii) Crossland criterion (α = 0.3 MPa/MPa, β = 220 MPa). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### (a) Derivation of the Crossland Criterion (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Decompose the stress tensor (1 mark):**
The stress tensor at any instant is decomposed into hydrostatic (volumetric) and deviatoric (shape-changing) parts:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_H \delta_{ij} + S_{ij}$$

where **σ<sub>H</sub> = σ<sub>kk</sub>/3** and **S<sub>ij</sub> = σ<sub>ij</sub> − σ<sub>H</sub>δ<sub>ij</sub>**.

**Step 2 — Second invariant of the deviatoric stress tensor (1 mark):**

$$J_2 = \frac{1}{2} S_{ij} S_{ij} = \frac{1}{6}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right]$$

**Step 3 — Crossland's hypothesis (1 mark):**
Crossland postulated that fatigue failure occurs when the combination of **deviatoric amplitude** and **maximum hydrostatic stress** reaches a critical envelope:

$$\sqrt{J_{2,a}} + \alpha \cdot \sigma_{H,max} = \beta$$

**Step 4 — Decomposition into mean and amplitude (1 mark):**
For proportional loading:

$$J_{2,a} = \frac{1}{6}\left[(\sigma_{1a}-\sigma_{2a})^2 + (\sigma_{2a}-\sigma_{3a})^2 + (\sigma_{3a}-\sigma_{1a})^2\right]$$

$$\sigma_{H,max} = \frac{1}{3}\left(\sigma_{1,max} + \sigma_{2,max} + \sigma_{3,max}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Calibration of material constants (2 marks):**
- **Constant β** is obtained from **fully reversed pure torsion** (σ<sub>H,max</sub> = 0):

$$\beta = \tau_{a,fatigue\,limit}$$

- **Constant α** is obtained from **fully reversed uniaxial tension**:

$$\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{a,t}^2}{3}} + \alpha \cdot \frac{\sigma_{a,t}}{3} = \beta \Rightarrow \alpha = 3\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}\,\beta}{\sigma_{a,t}} - 1\right)$$

**Assumptions:** (i) Proportional loading, (ii) Isotropic homogeneous material, (iii) High-cycle fatigue regime where elastic shakedown governs.

---

### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Endurance limit correction (1 mark):**

$$S_n = S_n' \cdot k_a \cdot k_b = 400 \times 0.8 \times 0.85 = 272 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Von Mises equivalent amplitude (1 mark):**

$$\sigma_{a,VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_a^2 + 3\tau_a^2} = \sqrt{(200)^2 + 3(130)^2}$$

$$\sigma_{a,VM} = \sqrt{40000 + 50700} = \sqrt{90700} = 301.2 \text{ MPa}$$

Since loading is **fully reversed**, **σ<sub>m</sub> = 0**, so only the amplitude term appears in Goodman:

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_{a,VM}}{S_n} = \frac{301.2}{272} = 1.107$$

$$\boxed{n_{VM} = 0.90 \text{ — NOT SAFE}}$$

**Step 3 — Crossland criterion (3 marks):**

$$\sqrt{J_{2,a}} = \frac{\sigma_{a,VM}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{301.2}{1.732} = 173.9 \text{ MPa}$$

For uniaxial + shear (σ<sub>2</sub> = σ<sub>3</sub> = 0):

$$\sigma_{H,max} = \frac{\sigma_{a}}{3} = \frac{200}{3} = 66.67 \text{ MPa}$$

Apply Crossland:

$$LHS = 173.9 + 0.3 \times 66.67 = 173.9 + 20.0 = 193.9 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\frac{1}{n_{Crossland}} = \frac{193.9}{\beta} = \frac{193.9}{220} = 0.881$$

$$\boxed{n_{Crossland} = 1.13}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (2 marks):**
- Von Mises-Goodman gives **n = 0.90** → unsafe.
- Crossland gives **n = 1.13** → marginally safe.
- The **8% discrepancy** arises because the Von Mises approach uses the **full tensile amplitude** while Crossland uses **only the hydrostatic mean stress contribution**, which is **smaller for uniaxial loading**.
- **Recommendation:** The shaft should be redesigned — either increase diameter to **d ≈ 55 mm** or use a higher-strength steel with **S<sub>ut</sub> > 1000 MPa**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, explain the **concept of the critical plane** in multiaxial fatigue. How does the Findley criterion use this concept to predict fatigue life? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pressure vessel nozzle is subjected to:
    • **Hoop stress** σ<sub>θ</sub> = 100 ± 60 MPa,
    • **Axial stress** σ<sub>z</sub> = 60 ± 40 MPa,
    • **Shear stress** τ = 30 ± 20 MPa.
Material properties: **S<sub>ut</sub> = 550 MPa**, **S<sub>sy</sub> = 380 MPa**, **S'<sub>n</sub> = 260 MPa**, **b = −0.085**, **σ'<sub>f</sub> = 800 MPa**, **k = 0.30** (Findley), **τ'<sub>f</sub> = 480 MPa**, Marin factor product **k<sub>tot</sub> = 0.72**.
Calculate the fatigue life using the **Findley criterion**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### (a) Concept of Critical Plane (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **critical plane** is the material plane on which the **combined action of shear and normal stress** produces maximum fatigue damage. For multiaxial loading, cracks do not always initiate on the plane of **maximum principal stress**; instead, they grow on planes of **maximum shear** modified by the **normal stress acting on those planes**.

**Sketch Description (2 marks — must be drawn on answer sheet):**
Draw a 2D element with σ<sub>x</sub>, σ<sub>y</sub>, τ<sub>xy</sub>. Indicate a cut plane at angle θ, resolving it into **σ<sub>n</sub>(θ)** and **τ(θ)**. Mark the critical plane θ<sub>cr</sub> where the Findley damage function is maximum.

**Findley Criterion Application (3 marks):**

$$F(\theta) = \frac{\Delta \tau(\theta)}{2} + k \cdot \sigma_{n,max}(\theta) = \tau_f' (2N_f)^b$$

**Procedure:**
1. Compute **σ<sub>n</sub>(θ)** and **τ(θ)** using transformation equations.
2. Compute **F(θ)** for θ = 0°, 5°, 10°, …, 180°.
3. Identify **θ<sub>cr</sub>** at maximum F.
4. Solve for **N<sub>f</sub>**:

$$N_f = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{F(\theta_{cr})}{\tau_f'}\right]^{1/b}$$

---

### (b) Findley Life Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Mean and amplitude split (1 mark):**
- σ<sub>θ</sub>: σ<sub>m</sub> = 100, σ<sub>a</sub> = 60 MPa
- σ<sub>z</sub>: σ<sub>m</sub> = 60, σ<sub>a</sub> = 40 MPa
- τ: τ<sub>m</sub> = 30, τ<sub>a</sub> = 20 MPa

**Step 2 — Stress transformation (2 marks):**
At angle θ:

$$\sigma_{n,a} = \frac{\sigma_{\theta,a} - \sigma_{z,a}}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_a \sin 2\theta$$

$$\sigma_{n,m} = \frac{\sigma_{\theta,m} + \sigma_{z,m}}{2} + \frac{\sigma_{\theta,m} - \sigma_{z,m}}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_m \sin 2\theta$$

$$\tau_a = -\frac{\sigma_{\theta,a} - \sigma_{z,a}}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_a \cos 2\theta$$

**Step 3 — Maximize F(θ) (2 marks):**
Compute F(θ) for a few key angles:

| θ | σ<sub>n,a</sub> | σ<sub>n,max</sub> | τ<sub>a</sub> | F(θ) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0° | 10 | 90 | 0 | 0 + 0.3(90) = 27 |
| 30° | 0 | 80 | 17.3 | 17.3 + 24 = 41.3 |
| 45° | −7.1 | 72.9 | 20.0 | 20 + 21.9 = 41.9 |
| 60° | −17.3 | 62.7 | 17.3 | 17.3 + 18.8 = 36.1 |

Maximum at **θ<sub>cr</sub> ≈ 45°**, **F(θ<sub>cr</sub>) = 41.9 MPa**.

**Step 4 — Solve for N<sub>f</sub> (2 marks):**

$$N_f = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{F(\theta_{cr})}{\tau_f'}\right]^{1/b} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{41.9}{480}\right]^{-1/0.085}$$

$$\frac{41.9}{480} = 0.0873$$

$$\log(0.0873) = -1.059$$

$$\frac{1}{0.085} = 11.76$$

$$-1.059 \times 11.76 = -12.45$$

$$N_f = 0.5 \times 10^{12.45} = 0.5 \times 2.82 \times 10^{12}$$

$$\boxed{N_f \approx 1.41 \times 10^{12} \text{ cycles}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting Marin factors** (k<sub>a</sub>, k<sub>b</sub>, k<sub>c</sub>): −2 marks.
> 2. **Sign error in stress transformation** at θ = 90° vs θ = 0°: −2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing σ<sub>n,mean</sub> with σ<sub>n,amplitude</sub>** in the Findley formula: −3 marks. (Findley uses **σ<sub>n,MAX</sub> = σ<sub>n,m</sub> + σ<sub>n,a</sub>**, NOT just the mean.)
> 4. **Not iterating to find θ<sub>cr</sub>**: −3 marks. The critical plane is **not** always the plane of maximum principal stress.
> 5. **Mixing units** (MPa vs GPa, mm vs m): −1 mark.
> 6. **Ignoring phase shift** in non-proportional loading: −2 marks. Use **time-domain analysis** with full stress history.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Module 2 High-Yield Recall Points:**

- ✅ **Combined (multiaxial) fatigue** occurs when stresses act on more than one principal direction simultaneously.
- ✅ **Three classes** of criteria: **Equivalent Stress** (Von Mises, Sines, Crossland), **Critical Plane** (Findley, Brown-Miller), and **Mesoscopic** (Dang Van).
- ✅ **Sines criterion** is the simplest for **proportional + mean stress** cases; uses hydrostatic mean stress.
- ✅ **Crossland criterion** adds the **maximum hydrostatic stress** to the deviatoric amplitude → suitable for **proportional HCF**.
- ✅ **Findley criterion** searches the plane θ for maximum **τ<sub>a</sub> + k σ<sub>n,max</sub>** → most general, suitable for **proportional and non-proportional**.
- ✅ **Dang Van criterion** uses **elastoplastic shakedown** — applies at the **mesoscopic scale**, suitable for **non-proportional loading**.
- ✅ For **proportional fully-reversed** loading, all criteria reduce to **Von Mises + Goodman**.
- ✅ The **shear endurance limit** ≈ **0.577 × tensile endurance limit**.
- ✅ The **hydrostatic stress σ<sub>H</sub> = (σ<sub>1</sub> + σ<sub>2</sub> + σ<sub>3</sub>)/3** plays a **secondary role** in ductile fatigue.
- ✅ **Always iterate θ from 0° to 180°** in 5°–10° steps to find the critical plane.
- ✅ **Marin factors** (k<sub>a</sub>, k<sub>b</sub>, k<sub>c</sub>, k<sub>d</sub>, k<sub>e</sub>) must be applied **after** computing equivalent stress, **not before**.
- ✅ The **phase angle φ** between stress components dramatically affects damage in non-proportional loading.
- ✅ **Neuber's rule** handles **notches under combined stress** by computing local stresses component-wise and then combining via Von Mises.
- ✅ In KTU 2024 board exams, **always state the assumption** of proportional vs non-proportional loading **before** choosing a criterion.
- ✅ **Goodman** is more conservative than **Gerber** for tensile mean stress; **Soderberg** is the most conservative (uses yield).
- ✅ For ductile steels, Dang Van's **α ≈ 0**, meaning **pure hydrostatic stress has no effect** on fatigue life.
