- ✅ The Findley **k parameter** is typically **0.2–0.4** for steels and is found from **biaxial fatigue tests** at 45°.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "SCD" for KTU Boards:**
> **S**ines (hydrostatic + amplitude), **C**rossland (deviatoric + max hydrostatic), **D**ang Van (mesoscopic shakedown). Remember: **"SCD — Stress Calculated, Damage predicted."**
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low Cycle Fatigue and Fracture Mechanics: Low Cycle Fatigue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Low Cycle Fatigue and Fracture Mechanics: Low Cycle Fatigue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Low Cycle Fatigue (LCF)** is a regime of fatigue failure occurring at relatively **high cyclic stresses** (typically above the yield strength) producing **finite, measurable plastic strain** in each load cycle, with fatigue life usually between **10¹ to 10⁴ cycles** (occasionally extended to ~10⁵). LCF is therefore characterized as a **strain-controlled** phenomenon best described using the **strain-life approach** rather than the stress-life (S-N) approach used in High Cycle Fatigue.

The governing framework for LCF is built upon three pillars:

| Pillar | Concept | Governing Equation Family |
|---|---|---|
| Elastic strain life | Basquin's law | $\varepsilon_e$ vs $N_f$ |
| Plastic strain life | Coffin-Manson law | $\varepsilon_p$ vs $N_f$ |
| Cyclic plasticity | Cyclic stress-strain curve | $\Delta\sigma$ vs $\Delta\varepsilon$ |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Paperclip

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Take a standard metal paperclip and bend it open and closed repeatedly. After only **10–20 cycles**, the wire becomes hot at the bend, then cracks, and finally snaps. This is **Low Cycle Fatigue**. The bending causes visible *plastic deformation* each cycle, and the failure occurs in very few cycles. By contrast, a guitar string vibrating thousands of times per second at stress levels far below yielding represents **High Cycle Fatigue (HCF)** — it never deforms plastically but eventually fractures after millions of cycles.

The key distinguishing feature of LCF is therefore **plasticity in every cycle** — the material yields locally, generating a **hysteresis loop** on the stress-strain diagram whose enclosed area equals the plastic work dissipated per cycle.

### 1.3 HCF vs LCF — A Comparative Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Metrics (Industry Standards):**
> - **Transition Life, $N_T \approx 10^{3}$ to $10^{4}$ cycles** separates LCF from HCF.
> - **Endurance Limit, $\sigma_e \approx 0.5\,\sigma_{UTS}$** for steels applies only to HCF.
> - In LCF there is **no endurance limit** — failure will always occur given enough plastic strain.

| Property | Low Cycle Fatigue (LCF) | High Cycle Fatigue (HCF) |
|---|---|---|
| Cycle count $N_f$ | $10^{1}$ – $10^{4}$ | $>10^{4}$ (up to $10^{7}$+) |
| Stress level | Near / above $\sigma_y$ | Below $\sigma_y$ |
| Strain regime | Elastic + **Plastic** | Elastic only |
| Control variable | **Strain** | **Stress** |
| Governing law | Coffin-Manson + Basquin | Basquin / S-N curve |
| Typical component | Turbine blade root, pressure vessel nozzle | Shaft, spring, gear tooth |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Strain-Control in LCF?

In LCF, the stress may exceed the yield strength, so a prescribed *load* does not give a unique *strain*. Two specimens loaded to the same nominal stress may exhibit different plastic responses depending on prior history. Conversely, prescribing the **strain amplitude** yields a deterministic, repeatable hysteresis loop. Therefore, LCF testing is universally **strain-controlled** (ASTM E606).

### 2.2 Decomposition of Total Strain Amplitude

The total strain amplitude in a stabilized cycle is the sum of elastic and plastic parts:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon}{2} = \frac{\Delta\varepsilon_e}{2} + \frac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2}$$

where:
- $\Delta\varepsilon_e/2$ → recoverable elastic strain amplitude.
- $\Delta\varepsilon_p/2$ → irrecoverable plastic strain amplitude.

This decomposition is the conceptual foundation of the entire strain-life approach.

### 2.3 Basquin's Equation (Elastic Strain-Life)

Originally derived for HCF but valid in LCF for the elastic portion:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_e}{2} = \frac{\sigma'_a}{E}\,(2N_f)^b$$

- $\sigma'_a$ → fatigue strength coefficient (intercept at $2N_f = 1$).
- $b$ → fatigue strength exponent (typically $-0.05$ to $-0.12$).
- $E$ → Young's modulus (**typically 200 GPa for steels**).

### 2.4 Coffin-Manson Equation (Plastic Strain-Life)

The cornerstone of LCF:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f\,(2N_f)^c$$

- $\varepsilon'_f$ → fatigue ductility coefficient (≈ true fracture ductility $\varepsilon_f$).
- $c$ → fatigue ductility exponent (typically $-0.5$ to $-0.7$).

### 2.5 Master Strain-Life Equation

Combining both:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\Delta\varepsilon}{2} = \underbrace{\frac{\sigma'_a}{E}\,(2N_f)^b}_{\text{elastic term}} + \underbrace{\varepsilon'_f\,(2N_f)^c}_{\text{plastic term}}\;}$$

### 2.6 Cyclic Stress-Strain Curve

The locus of tips of stabilized hysteresis loops:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon}{2} = \frac{\Delta\sigma}{2E} + \left(\frac{\Delta\sigma}{2K'}\right)^{1/n'}$$

- $K'$ → cyclic strength coefficient.
- $n'$ → cyclic strain hardening exponent.

### 2.7 Mean Stress Corrections

Morrow's elastic mean-stress correction:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_e}{2} = \frac{\sigma'_a - \sigma_m}{E}\,(2N_f)^b$$

Smith–Watson–Topper (SWT) parameter (energy-based):

$$SWT = \sigma_{max}\,\varepsilon_a = \frac{(\sigma'_f)^2}{E}\,(2N_f)^{2b} + \sigma'_f\,\varepsilon'_f\,(2N_f)^{b+c}$$

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Domain / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Strain decomposition | $\Delta\varepsilon/2 = \Delta\varepsilon_e/2 + \Delta\varepsilon_p/2$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Basquin | $\Delta\varepsilon_e/2 = (\sigma'_a / E)(2N_f)^b$ | LCF + HCF elastic |
| 3 | Coffin-Manson | $\Delta\varepsilon_p/2 = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$ | LCF plastic |
| 4 | Master equation | $\Delta\varepsilon/2 = (\sigma'_a / E)(2N_f)^b + \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$ | full range |
| 5 | Cyclic $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ | $\Delta\varepsilon/2 = \Delta\sigma/(2E) + (\Delta\sigma / 2K')^{1/n'}$ | stabilized loop |
| 6 | Morrow correction | $(\sigma'_a - \sigma_m)/E \cdot (2N_f)^b$ | mean stress |
| 7 | SWT parameter | $\sigma_{max}\varepsilon_a = (\sigma'_f)^2 / E \cdot (2N_f)^{2b} + \sigma'_f\varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^{b+c}$ | mean stress |
| 8 | Manson-Hirschberg method | $\Delta\varepsilon_p/2 = D^{0.6}$ (approx.) | quick estimate |
| 9 | Transition life | $N_T = (E\,\varepsilon'_f / \sigma'_a)^{1/(b-c)}$ | LCF/HCF boundary |
| 10 | True fracture ductility | $\varepsilon_f = \ln(A_0 / A_f)$ | ductility metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used to design against LCF in **nuclear reactor piping**, **aerospace turbine disks**, **automotive connecting rods**, and **offshore pressure vessels** where thermal-mechanical strain cycling dominates.

### 2.9 Physical Interpretation of Exponents

- A more **negative** $b$ → steeper S-N curve → less fatigue-resistant in elastic regime.
- A more **negative** $c$ → steeper Coffin-Manson line → lower LCF life.
- **Bauschinger effect:** cyclic yielding lowers yield strength in reverse direction, producing the open hysteresis loop central to LCF.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Coffin-Manson Relation (from First Principles)

The original empirical observation by Coffin (1954) and Manson (1954) can be rationalized through plastic strain energy per cycle. Consider a hysteresis loop of plastic strain amplitude $\Delta\varepsilon_p/2$ and stress amplitude $\Delta\sigma/2$. The plastic strain energy density per cycle is:

$$W_p = \oint \sigma\,d\varepsilon_p \approx 4 \cdot \frac{\Delta\sigma}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = \Delta\sigma \cdot \Delta\varepsilon_p$$

Assuming the cyclic stress amplitude follows a power-law decay (analogous to a Ramberg-Osgood type):

$$\Delta\sigma = A\,(N_f)^\beta$$

with the plastic strain amplitude remaining the controlling variable, the total plastic work to failure $W_f$ is:

$$W_f = W_p \cdot N_f = \Delta\sigma \cdot \Delta\varepsilon_p \cdot N_f = A\,(N_f)^\beta \cdot \Delta\varepsilon_p \cdot N_f$$

Setting this equal to a material constant $W_{tot}$ (the toughness-like plastic work to failure):

$$A\,(N_f)^{\beta+1} \cdot \Delta\varepsilon_p = W_{tot}$$

Solving for $\Delta\varepsilon_p$:

$$\Delta\varepsilon_p = \frac{W_{tot}}{A}\,(N_f)^{-(\beta+1)}$$

Defining $\varepsilon'_f = W_{tot}/(2A)$ and $c = -(\beta+1)$, and noting the conventional factor of 2 from $N_f$ to reversals $2N_f$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f\,(2N_f)^c\;}$$

This is the Coffin-Manson equation.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Master Strain-Life Equation

Starting from the strain decomposition:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon}{2} = \frac{\Delta\varepsilon_e}{2} + \frac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2}$$

Substituting Basquin (elastic) and Coffin-Manson (plastic):

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon}{2} = \frac{\sigma'_a}{E}\,(2N_f)^b + \varepsilon'_f\,(2N_f)^c$$

Both terms are plotted on log-log axes versus $2N_f$. The elastic line has slope $b$ (negative) and the plastic line has slope $c$ (more negative). They intersect at the **transition life**:

$$2N_T = \left(\frac{E\,\varepsilon'_f}{\sigma'_a}\right)^{1/(b-c)}$$

For $2N_f < 2N_T$ → plastic dominates → **LCF regime**.
For $2N_f > 2N_T$ → elastic dominates → **HCF regime**.

### 3.3 Derivation of Cyclic Stress-Strain from Monotonic + Hysteresis

For a stabilized hysteresis loop, the Masing material model assumes the loading branches follow the monotonic curve scaled by a factor of 2. The Ramberg-Osgood cyclic form is written as:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} + \left(\frac{\sigma}{K'}\right)^{1/n'}$$

At the tip of the loop ($\sigma = \Delta\sigma/2$, $\varepsilon = \Delta\varepsilon/2$):

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon}{2} = \frac{\Delta\sigma}{2E} + \left(\frac{\Delta\sigma}{2K'}\right)^{1/n'}$$

Solving for $\Delta\sigma$ given $\Delta\varepsilon$ yields the **Neuber rule** for LCF:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta\sigma \cdot \Delta\varepsilon = \text{const} \cdot \frac{(K')^{1/n'}}{E^{1/n' - 1}}\;}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Morrow Mean-Stress Correction

Basquin's equation uses $\sigma'_a$ as the *true* stress amplitude with zero mean. When $\sigma_m \neq 0$, the elastic strain amplitude must be reduced because mean tensile stress accelerates crack initiation. Replacing $\sigma'_a$ by $(\sigma'_a - \sigma_m)$ in Basquin:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_e}{2} = \frac{\sigma'_a - \sigma_m}{E}\,(2N_f)^b$$

This is the **Morrow correction**, valid when $\sigma_m$ is not too large compared to $\sigma'_a$.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A steel component has the following properties:
- $E = 200$ GPa, $\sigma'_a = 1000$ MPa, $b = -0.10$
- $\varepsilon'_f = 0.30$, $c = -0.55$
- Applied total strain amplitude $\Delta\varepsilon/2 = 0.01$

**Find:** Number of cycles to failure.

**Solution:**

The master equation is:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon}{2} = \frac{\sigma'_a}{E}\,(2N_f)^b + \varepsilon'_f\,(2N_f)^c$$

Substituting values:

$$0.01 = \frac{1000}{200{,}000}\,(2N_f)^{-0.10} + 0.30\,(2N_f)^{-0.55}$$

$$0.01 = 0.005\,(2N_f)^{-0.10} + 0.30\,(2N_f)^{-0.55}$$

Trial-and-error on $2N_f$:

- Try $2N_f = 100$ → $0.005(100)^{-0.10} + 0.30(100)^{-0.55} = 0.005(0.501) + 0.30(0.0282) = 0.00251 + 0.00846 = 0.01097$ (too high)
- Try $2N_f = 200$ → $0.005(200)^{-0.10} + 0.30(200)^{-0.55} = 0.005(0.467) + 0.30(0.0199) = 0.00234 + 0.00597 = 0.00831$ (too low)
- Try $2N_f = 130$ → $0.005(130)^{-0.10} + 0.30(130)^{-0.55} = 0.005(0.488) + 0.30(0.0245) = 0.00244 + 0.00735 = 0.00979$ (very close)

Therefore $2N_f \approx 130$, giving $N_f \approx 65$ cycles — a clear LCF result.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LCF vs HCF Regimes — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Cyclic Loading Applied to Component"] --> node2{"Total Strain Amplitude?"}
    node2 -- "Below Yield" --> node3["Elastic-Only Cycling"]
    node2 -- "Above Yield" --> node4["Elastic + Plastic Cycling"]
    node3 --> node5["HCF Regime"]
    node4 --> node6["LCF Regime"]
    node5 --> node7["Nf greater than 10^4 cycles"]
    node6 --> node8["Nf between 10^1 and 10^4 cycles"]
    node5 --> node9["Governed by S-N Curve"]
    node6 --> node10["Governed by Strain-Life Curve"]
    node9 --> node11["Basquin Equation"]
    node10 --> node12["Coffin-Manson + Basquin"]
```

### 4.2 Strain-Life Curve Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Total Strain Amplitude Delta Epsilon over 2"] --> nodeB["Sum of Two Contributions"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Elastic Branch - Slope b"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Plastic Branch - Slope c"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Basquin Line"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Coffin-Manson Line"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["HCF Dominant Region"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["LCF Dominant Region"]
    nodeE -- "Intersection" --> nodeI["Transition Life NT"]
    nodeF -- "Intersection" --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["2NT equals E epsilon prime f over sigma prime a raised to 1 over b minus c"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LCF Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1["Input: Cyclic Strain History"] --> nodeS2["Compute Strain Amplitude"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3["Apply Master Strain-Life Equation"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4["Elastic Term Calculation"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS5["Plastic Term Calculation"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS6["Sum Both Terms"]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7{"Compare with Allowable Strain"}
    nodeS7 -- "Exceeds" --> nodeS8["FAILURE Predicted - LCF"]
    nodeS7 -- "Within Limit" --> nodeS9["SAFE - Continue Service"]
    nodeS8 --> nodeS10["Output: Cycles to Failure Nf"]
    nodeS9 --> nodeS10
```

### 4.4 Hysteresis Loop Schematic (Descriptive Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeL1["Compressive Yield Point"] --> nodeL2["Loading Branch - Plastic Flow"]
    nodeL2 --> nodeL3["Tensile Tip - Max Stress"]
    nodeL3 --> nodeL4["Unloading Branch - Elastic then Reverse Yield"]
    nodeL4 --> nodeL5["Compressive Tip - Min Stress"]
    nodeL5 --> nodeL6["Reloading Branch Returns to Start"]
    nodeL6 --> nodeL1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The enclosed area of the loop in this representation equals the **plastic strain energy dissipated per cycle** ($W_p$), which drives damage accumulation in LCF.

### 4.5 Decision Flowchart for Choosing LCF vs HCF Analysis Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeD1["Identify Cyclic Loading on Component"] --> nodeD2["Estimate Local Stress Range"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD3{"Stress Range vs Yield Strength?"}
    nodeD3 -- "Less than Yield" --> nodeD4["Use HCF Method - S-N Curve"]
    nodeD3 -- "Greater than Yield" --> nodeD5["Use LCF Method - Strain-Life"]
    nodeD4 --> nodeD6["Compute Nf from Basquin"]
    nodeD5 --> nodeD7["Compute Nf from Coffin-Manson"]
    nodeD6 --> nodeD8["Compare with Design Life"]
    nodeD7 --> nodeD8
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define Low Cycle Fatigue. Mention its typical cycle range.

**Model Answer:**
> Low Cycle Fatigue (LCF) is a fatigue regime where cyclic loading produces **measurable plastic strain** in each cycle, with failure typically occurring between **$10^{1}$ and $10^{4}$ cycles**. Stress levels are at or above the yield strength, and the analysis is conducted using the **strain-life approach** based on the Coffin-Manson relationship. LCF is encountered in components subjected to thermal cycling, high mechanical overloading, or large strain amplitudes such as turbine blades, pressure vessels, and nuclear piping.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2.** State the Coffin-Manson equation and explain the significance of its constants.

**Model Answer:**
> The Coffin-Manson equation is:
> $$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f\,(2N_f)^c$$
> where $\varepsilon'_f$ is the **fatigue ductility coefficient** (approximately equal to the true fracture ductility), and $c$ is the **fatigue ductility exponent** (typically between $-0.5$ and $-0.7$). The equation relates the plastic strain amplitude per cycle to the number of reversals to failure, and is the fundamental constitutive law for the LCF regime.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper]** — *Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern*

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the Coffin-Manson relationship from energy considerations. Discuss the physical significance of the fatigue ductility coefficient $\varepsilon'_f$ and exponent $c$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A steel specimen has the following cyclic properties: $E = 210$ GPa, $\sigma'_a = 1100$ MPa, $b = -0.09$, $\varepsilon'_f = 0.45$, $c = -0.58$. If it is subjected to a total strain amplitude of $0.008$, determine the number of cycles to failure.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The plastic strain energy per cycle is:

$$W_p \approx \Delta\sigma \cdot \Delta\varepsilon_p$$

Assuming cyclic stress decays as $\Delta\sigma = A(N_f)^\beta$:

$$W_p \cdot N_f = A(N_f)^\beta \cdot \Delta\varepsilon_p \cdot N_f = W_{tot}$$

Solving:

$$\Delta\varepsilon_p = \frac{W_{tot}}{A}\,(N_f)^{-(\beta+1)}$$

Setting $\varepsilon'_f = W_{tot}/(2A)$ and $c = -(\beta+1)$:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f\,(2N_f)^c$$

- $\varepsilon'_f$ represents the **fatigue ductility coefficient** ≈ true fracture strain in monotonic tension; it indicates how much plastic strain the material tolerates in the first half-cycle.
- $c$ indicates the **rate of damage accumulation**; more negative $c$ means faster life degradation per cycle.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Using the master strain-life equation:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon}{2} = \frac{\sigma'_a}{E}\,(2N_f)^b + \varepsilon'_f\,(2N_f)^c$$

$$0.008 = \frac{1100}{210{,}000}\,(2N_f)^{-0.09} + 0.45\,(2N_f)^{-0.58}$$

$$0.008 = 0.005238\,(2N_f)^{-0.09} + 0.45\,(2N_f)^{-0.58}$$

Iterating:

- Try $2N_f = 50$ → $0.005238(50)^{-0.09} + 0.45(50)^{-0.58}$
  $= 0.005238(0.581) + 0.45(0.0753)$
  $= 0.003043 + 0.033885 = 0.036928$ (too high)

- Try $2N_f = 200$ → $0.005238(200)^{-0.09} + 0.45(200)^{-0.58}$
  $= 0.005238(0.482) + 0.45(0.0298)$
  $= 0.002525 + 0.013410 = 0.015935$ (still high)

- Try $2N_f = 600$ → $0.005238(600)^{-0.09} + 0.45(600)^{-0.58}$
  $= 0.005238(0.420) + 0.45(0.0170)$
  $= 0.002200 + 0.007650 = 0.009850$ (slightly high)

- Try $2N_f = 700$ → $0.005238(700)^{-0.09} + 0.45(700)^{-0.58}$
  $= 0.005238(0.412) + 0.45(0.0155)$
  $= 0.002158 + 0.006975 = 0.009133$ (slightly high)

- Try $2N_f = 800$ → $0.005238(800)^{-0.09} + 0.45(800)^{-0.58}$
  $= 0.005238(0.405) + 0.45(0.0143)$
  $= 0.002121 + 0.006435 = 0.008556$ (very close)

Therefore $2N_f \approx 770$, giving **$N_f \approx 385$ cycles** — clearly in the LCF regime.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With neat sketches, explain the difference between **monotonic** and **cyclic** stress-strain response. Define the cyclic stress-strain curve and explain how its parameters $K'$ and $n'$ are determined experimentally.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Morrow** and **Smith-Watson-Topper (SWT)** mean stress correction methods for LCF. Under what conditions is each preferred?

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Monotonic vs Cyclic Response:**

| Feature | Monotonic | Cyclic |
|---|---|---|
| Loading direction | One-direction | Reversed |
| Strain produced | Single value | Hysteresis loop |
| Strain hardening | Initial only | Saturation after ~20–50 cycles |
| Yield strength | $\sigma_y$ (initial) | $\sigma'_y$ (cyclic) — may differ |

**Cyclic Stress-Strain Curve:** The locus of tips of stabilized hysteresis loops from multiple strain-controlled tests at different amplitudes. It is described by:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon}{2} = \frac{\Delta\sigma}{2E} + \left(\frac{\Delta\sigma}{2K'}\right)^{1/n'}$$

**Experimental Determination:** A series of strain-controlled LCF tests is run at various strain amplitudes. After stabilization (~50 cycles), each loop's tip is plotted in $\Delta\varepsilon/2$ vs $\Delta\sigma/2$ space. The data is fitted with the Ramberg-Osgood form above using non-linear regression to extract $K'$ and $n'$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Morrow Correction:**
$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_e}{2} = \frac{\sigma'_a - \sigma_m}{E}\,(2N_f)^b$$

- Replaces $\sigma'_a$ by $\sigma'_a - \sigma_m$ in Basquin's equation.
- Simple to apply; works well for **moderate tensile mean stresses**.
- Preferred when the **elastic strain component dominates** the total life (i.e., near the transition life).

**SWT Correction:**
$$SWT = \sigma_{max}\,\varepsilon_a = \frac{(\sigma'_f)^2}{E}\,(2N_f)^{2b} + \sigma'_f\,\varepsilon'_f\,(2N_f)^{b+c}$$

- Energy-based; multiplies maximum stress by strain amplitude.
- Accounts for both **crack initiation (stress-driven)** and **crack growth (strain-driven)**.
- Preferred for **wide range of mean stresses**, including compressive, and for **ductile materials**.

Both methods collapse data from various $R$-ratios onto a single master curve, extending LCF life prediction to non-symmetric loading.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in LCF Problems:**
> 1. **Unit confusion on $E$:** Always use **E in MPa** when $\sigma$ is in MPa (not GPa), or convert both consistently. This is the **#1 cause of numerical errors**.
> 2. **Mixing up $2N_f$ (reversals) and $N_f$ (cycles):** The Coffin-Manson equation uses **reversals** ($2N_f$), not cycles. Missing the factor of 2 leads to answers off by a factor of ~4–10.
> 3. **Forgetting the strain decomposition:** Many students write only one term (Coffin-Manson or Basquin) instead of the full master equation. KTU examiners specifically award marks for the **complete** equation.
> 4. **Not identifying the regime:** Always state whether the answer lies in LCF ($N_f < 10^4$) or HCF ($N_f > 10^4$) — this earns a discrete "regime identification" mark.
> 5. **Skipping units in mean-stress corrections:** The Morrow term $(\sigma'_a - \sigma_m)$ must be positive; negative values indicate the equation has been misapplied.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LCF regime** = $10^{1} \le N_f \le 10^{4}$ cycles, with **plastic strain in every cycle**.
- **Strain-controlled testing** is mandatory in LCF (ASTM E606).
- **Master strain-life equation** has two parts: **elastic (Basquin)** + **plastic (Coffin-Manson)**.
- **Coffin-Manson:** $\Delta\varepsilon_p/2 = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$, with $c \approx -0.5$ to $-0.7$.
- **Basquin:** $\Delta\varepsilon_e/2 = (\sigma'_a / E)(2N_f)^b$, with $b \approx -0.05$ to $-0.12$.
- **Transition life** $N_T$ marks LCF/HCF boundary: $2N_T = (E\varepsilon'_f/\sigma'_a)^{1/(b-c)}$.
- **Cyclic stress-strain curve** uses $K'$ and $n'$; these differ from monotonic values.
- **Bauschinger effect** produces open hysteresis loops — the area equals plastic work per cycle.
- **Morrow correction** for moderate mean stresses; **SWT** for broad $R$-ratio range.
- **No endurance limit** in LCF — failure is guaranteed given sufficient plastic strain cycles.
- Always state **regime, units, and reversals vs cycles** explicitly in KTU answers.
- Real-world examples: **turbine blades, nuclear piping, pressure vessel nozzles, automotive exhaust manifolds**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cumulative Damage in Low Cycle Fatigue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cumulative Damage in Low Cycle Fatigue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — CUMULATIVE DAMAGE IN LOW CYCLE FATIGUE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cumulative Damage in Low Cycle Fatigue (LCF)** is the progressive, irreversible accumulation of plastic strain-induced deterioration in a ductile metallic component subjected to a **finite number of load cycles** (typically $N_f \le 10^3$ to $10^4$ cycles), where the strain amplitude is high enough to cause measurable plastic deformation in each cycle. The total damage is computed by summing the fractional damage contributions from each discrete load block until the cumulative damage index reaches a critical value that signifies **fatigue crack initiation** and subsequent failure.

Mathematically, it is the integration of the damage-per-cycle over the entire variable-amplitude loading history:

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

where $D$ is the **cumulative damage factor** (Palmgren–Miner index), $n_i$ is the number of cycles applied at stress/strain range level $i$, and $N_i$ is the cycles to failure at that level obtained from the cyclic stress–strain (CSS) and strain–life (ε–N) curves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PEMET744 / M3**
> Low cycle fatigue is governed by **plastic strain-controlled damage**, in contrast to high cycle fatigue (HCF) which is dominated by elastic strain. The damage metric in LCF is therefore derived from the **Manson–Coffin strain–life relationship**, not the S–N (Basquin) curve alone.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bending a Paperclip"

Imagine repeatedly bending a paperclip back and forth by a large angle (say ±90°). Each bend plastically deforms the metal at the bend point. After about 5 to 8 cycles the wire snaps.

Now imagine a more realistic case: an aircraft wing experiences:
- 1 large gust producing a high strain (low-cycle event)
- followed by thousands of small turbulence cycles (high-cycle events)
- followed by another large gust
- … and so on

The paperclip analogy, applied at component level, is **cumulative damage in LCF**: every plastic strain cycle "uses up" a portion of the material's finite fatigue life, and the *order* and *magnitude* of these cycles determine when total fracture occurs.

### 1.3 Distinguishing LCF from HCF

| Feature | Low Cycle Fatigue (LCF) | High Cycle Fatigue (HCF) |
|---|---|---|
| Cycle count to failure $N_f$ | $\le 10^3$ – $10^4$ | $> 10^4$ – $10^7$ |
| Dominant strain | Plastic, $\Delta\varepsilon_p$ | Elastic, $\Delta\varepsilon_e$ |
| Loading mode | Strain-controlled | Stress-controlled |
| Governing law | Manson–Coffin | Basquin / S–N |
| Typical applications | Turbine discs, pressure vessels, earthquake loading | Springs, shafts, aircraft wings |
| Damage metric | Cyclic plastic strain energy / $\sum n_i/N_i$ | Stress amplitude, $\sum n_i/N_i$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in LCF**:
> - **Cyclic strength coefficient** — $K'$ (MPa)
> - **Cyclic strain hardening exponent** — $n'$ (dimensionless)
> - **Fatigue strength coefficient** — $\sigma'_f$ (MPa)
> - **Fatigue strength exponent** — $b$ (typically −0.05 to −0.12)
> - **Fatigue ductility coefficient** — $\varepsilon'_f$ (dimensionless)
> - **Fatigue ductility exponent** — $c$ (typically −0.5 to −0.7)
> - **Modulus of Elasticity** — $E$ (MPa, typically 2×10⁵ MPa for steels)

### 1.4 Why Cumulative Damage?

Real engineering structures never experience a single, constant-amplitude load block. They are subjected to **variable-amplitude, stochastic service histories** (spectrum loading). Predicting life therefore requires a rule to **superpose damage** from all the different amplitudes experienced.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of LCF Damage Mechanics

The cumulative damage framework rests on three foundational pillars that the KTU examiner expects you to interrelate:

### Pillar 1 — Cyclic Stress–Strain Response (CSS Curve)
The material hardens (or softens) under repeated plastic straining until a stable hysteresis loop is reached. The locus of tips of stable hysteresis loops is the CSS curve:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon}{2} = \frac{\Delta\sigma}{2E} + \left(\frac{\Delta\sigma}{2K'}\right)^{\frac{1}{n'}}$$

The Ramberg–Osgood form: $\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} + \left(\frac{\sigma}{K'}\right)^{1/n'}$

### Pillar 2 — Strain–Life Relationship (Manson–Coffin–Basquin)
The total strain amplitude is partitioned into elastic and plastic components:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon}{2} = \underbrace{\frac{\sigma'_a}{E}(2N_f)^b}_{\text{Elastic (Basquin)}} + \underbrace{\varepsilon'_f(2N_f)^c}_{\text{Plastic (Coffin–Manson)}}$$

- **Basquin's law** dominates at *long* lives ($N_f > 10^4$).
- **Coffin–Manson law** dominates at *short* lives ($N_f < 10^3$) — the LCF regime.
- The **transition life** $N_T$ occurs where $\Delta\varepsilon_e = \Delta\varepsilon_p$:

$$N_T = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{E \cdot \varepsilon'_f}{\sigma'_f}\right)^{\frac{1}{b-c}}$$

### Pillar 3 — Damage Summation Rule
A rule must collapse the multi-level spectrum into a single failure criterion. The most common is the **Palmgren–Miner linear damage rule** (LDR), but several modifications exist.

## 2.2 Damage Accumulation Rules — Comparative Analysis

### 2.2.1 Palmgren–Miner Linear Damage Rule (LDR)

$$\boxed{D = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{n_i}{N_i} = 1 \;\;\text{at failure}}$$

**Key logical steps:**
- Each cycle of amplitude $i$ consumes $1/N_i$ of the available life.
- Damage from each block is summed linearly, *regardless of load sequence*.
- Failure predicted when $D = 1$.
- The rule is **load-sequence-independent**.

**Why it works / fails:**
- *Strength:* Mathematically simple; needs only the ε–N curve.
- *Weakness:* Ignores load interaction, overload retardation, and mean-stress effects. Empirically, $D$ at failure ranges from **0.3 to 3.0** in tests.

### 2.2.2 Modified Miner's Rule (Plankett's Approach)

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{n_i}{N_i} = a$$

where $a$ is a material-dependent critical damage sum (often found between 0.7 and 1.4 for steels; for ductile aluminium $a \approx 0.7$).

### 2.2.3 Corten–Dolan Non-Linear Rule

$$\boxed{N_i \cdot D_i^{a} = \text{constant}}$$

where $a$ is the **Corten–Dolan exponent** ($0.6 \le a \le 1.0$).

Failure criterion: $D_1 \cdot N_1^{1/a} + D_2 \cdot N_2^{1/a} + \cdots = 1$

Equivalent form: $D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} \left(\frac{\sigma_{a,\max}}{\sigma_{a,i}}\right)^{d}$

where $d = 1/a - 1$.

### 2.2.4 Manson's Double Linear Damage Rule
Manson observed two distinct crack-initiation phases and split life into:
- $N_1$ = cycles to form an engineering-size crack (~0.25 mm)
- $N_2$ = cycles to propagate that crack to final fracture

$$D = D_1 + D_2 = \frac{n_1}{N_1} + \frac{n_2}{N_2} = 1$$

This rule is sequence-dependent because $N_1$ and $N_2$ depend on the *previous* loading history.

### 2.2.5 Manson–Hirschberg Method
A cycle-by-cycle damage calculation where the current cycle's stress–strain response is influenced by the memory of prior cycles. Used in conjunction with the **Neuber's rule** for notched components:

$$K_t \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \Delta\varepsilon = \frac{(K_f \cdot \Delta S)^2}{E}$$

where $K_f$ is the fatigue stress concentration factor and $\Delta S$ is the nominal stress range.

### 2.2.6 Marco–Starkey Non-Linear Rule

$$\boxed{D = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\left(\frac{n_i}{N_i}\right)^{x_i}}$$

where $x_i$ is a damage exponent for block $i$ (often $x_i = \sigma_i / \sigma_{\max}$ or determined experimentally).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET — Cumulative Damage in LCF (PEMET744 / M3)**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\frac{\Delta\varepsilon}{2} = \frac{\sigma'_a}{E}(2N_f)^b + \varepsilon'_f(2N_f)^c$ | Manson–Coffin–Basquin total strain-life | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f(2N_f)^c$ | Coffin–Manson plastic part (LCF) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_e}{2} = \frac{\sigma'_a}{E}(2N_f)^b$ | Basquin elastic part (HCF) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $N_T = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{E\varepsilon'_f}{\sigma'_f}\right)^{\frac{1}{b-c}}$ | Transition life (elastic = plastic) | cycles |
| 5 | $D = \sum \frac{n_i}{N_i} = 1$ | Palmgren–Miner LDR (failure) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $D = \sum \frac{n_i}{N_i} = a$ | Modified Miner (empirical) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $N_i D_i^{a} = \text{const.}$ | Corten–Dolan | cycles |
| 8 | $D = \sum \left(\frac{n_i}{N_i}\right)^{x_i}$ | Marco–Starkey | dimensionless |
| 9 | $K_t \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \Delta\varepsilon = \frac{(K_f\Delta S)^2}{E}$ | Neuber's rule (notched LCF) | MPa |
| 10 | $\frac{\Delta\varepsilon}{2} = \frac{\Delta\sigma}{2E} + \left(\frac{\Delta\sigma}{2K'}\right)^{1/n'}$ | Cyclic stress–strain (Ramberg–Osgood) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\sigma_a = K'(\varepsilon_p)^{n'}$ | CSS power law | MPa |
| 12 | $\varepsilon_a = \frac{\sigma_a}{E} + \left(\frac{\sigma_a}{K'}\right)^{1/n'}$ | CSS in strain form | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Caution in Markdown Tables:** Throughout the KTU formula sheet, all absolute values and modulus signs are written using $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mathrm{abs}(\cdot)$ syntax, never the raw `|` character, to keep the markdown table renderable.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace turbine discs**: LCF at takeoff, landing, and maneuvering combined with HCF from vibration → cumulative damage must be computed over a flight spectrum.
- **Nuclear reactor pressure vessels**: Thermal striping produces LCF plastic strain cycles; cumulative damage from transients (heat-up, cool-down, SCRAM events) is summed to find the inspection interval.
- **Earthquake engineering**: Aftershock sequences are LCF events whose damage accumulates.
- **Offshore platforms**: Wave loading spectra must be rainflow-counted and summed.
- **Automotive connecting rods**: Plastic strain peaks at start-up/shut-down are summed with cruise HCF cycles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Manson–Coffin Plastic Strain–Life Law (from First Principles of Ductility Exhaustion)

### Statement
The plastic strain amplitude per reversal is related to the number of reversals to failure $2N_f$ by:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$$

### Derivation (Exhaustive, Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Define the Coffin–Manson Hypothesis.**
L.F. Coffin (1954) and S.S. Manson (1953) independently proposed that the **plastic strain per cycle** causes incremental damage, and that failure occurs when the *accumulated plastic strain* equals the material's monotonic ductility (true strain at fracture, $\varepsilon_f$).

**Step 2 — Cumulative Plastic Strain to Failure.**
For a single block of constant-amplitude loading with plastic strain amplitude $\Delta\varepsilon_p/2$, the plastic strain accumulated in $2N_f$ reversals (one reversal = half cycle) is:

$$\varepsilon_{p,\text{cum}} = 2N_f \cdot \frac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = N_f \cdot \Delta\varepsilon_p$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Failure Criterion.**
At failure, the cumulative plastic strain equals the monotonic fracture ductility $\varepsilon_f$:

$$N_f \cdot \Delta\varepsilon_p = \varepsilon_f$$

Solving for $\Delta\varepsilon_p$:

$$\Delta\varepsilon_p = \frac{\varepsilon_f}{N_f}$$

**Step 4 — Express in Terms of Reversals $2N_f$.**
For a fully-reversed cycle, $2N_f$ reversals, so:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = \frac{\varepsilon_f}{2N_f}$$

**Step 5 — Generalize via Power Law (Empirical Refinement).**
Test data show the relation is *not* exactly inverse-linear. Introduce the fatigue ductility coefficient $\varepsilon'_f$ and exponent $c$:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$$

with $c \approx -0.5$ to $-0.7$ for most ductile metals.

**Step 6 — Verify Limiting Cases.**
- At $2N_f = 1$ (one reversal to failure): $\Delta\varepsilon_p/2 = \varepsilon'_f$, which equals the true fracture strain $\varepsilon_f$ in tension-compression. ✓
- At large $2N_f$: $\Delta\varepsilon_p \to 0$ ✓
- Dimensionally consistent: both sides are dimensionless strain. ✓

$$\therefore \;\; \boxed{\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Transition Life $N_T$ Between LCF and HCF

### Statement
The transition life $N_T$ is the cycles-to-failure at which the elastic strain amplitude equals the plastic strain amplitude.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Equate Basquin and Coffin–Manson Terms.**

$$\frac{\sigma'_f}{E}(2N_T)^b = \varepsilon'_f (2N_T)^c$$

**Step 2 — Collect the $(2N_T)$ Terms.**

$$(2N_T)^{b-c} = \frac{\varepsilon'_f E}{\sigma'_f}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $2N_T$.**

$$2N_T = \left(\frac{\varepsilon'_f E}{\sigma'_f}\right)^{\frac{1}{b-c}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N_T$.**

$$N_T = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{E \cdot \varepsilon'_f}{\sigma'_f}\right)^{\frac{1}{b-c}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Verification (Example).**
For a typical steel: $E = 2\times 10^5$ MPa, $\sigma'_f = 1000$ MPa, $\varepsilon'_f = 0.5$, $b = -0.08$, $c = -0.6$.

$$\begin{aligned}
N_T &= \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{2\times 10^5 \times 0.5}{1000}\right)^{\frac{1}{-0.08-(-0.6)}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}(100)^{\frac{1}{0.52}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}(100)^{1.923} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}(100 \times 100^{0.923}) \\
&\approx \frac{1}{2}(100 \times 60.0) \\
&\approx 3000 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}$$

This $N_T \approx 3000$ is the canonical boundary between LCF and HCF regimes. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Palmgren–Miner Linear Damage Rule

### Statement
Under variable-amplitude loading with $k$ distinct stress/strain blocks, the cumulative damage $D$ is:

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

Failure predicted at $D = 1$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Define Damage per Cycle in Block $i$.**
If a constant-amplitude spectrum of $N_i$ cycles causes failure at level $i$, then each cycle consumes a fraction $1/N_i$ of life.

**Step 2 — Damage in Block $i$.**
If $n_i$ cycles are applied at level $i$:

$$D_i = \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

**Step 3 — Assumptions of the Rule.**
- **Linear superposition:** Damage sums additively.
- **No load interaction:** Damage in block $i$ is unaffected by prior blocks.
- **Order independence:** Reordering blocks gives the same $D$.

**Step 4 — Sum Over All Blocks.**

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} D_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

**Step 5 — Failure Criterion.**
Empirically and theoretically, $D = 1$ is the most widely used failure threshold.

**Step 6 — Worked Example (Two-Block Spectrum).**

> *A steel shaft experiences 500 cycles at $\Delta\varepsilon/2 = 0.01$ and 2000 cycles at $\Delta\varepsilon/2 = 0.003$. The strain-life parameters are: $\sigma'_f = 1200$ MPa, $\varepsilon'_f = 0.4$, $b = -0.09$, $c = -0.55$, $E = 2\times 10^5$ MPa. Use Miner's rule to assess life.*

**Step 6a — Compute $N_1$ at $\Delta\varepsilon_1/2 = 0.01$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
0.01 &= \frac{1200}{2\times 10^5}(2N_1)^{-0.09} + 0.4(2N_1)^{-0.55} \\
0.01 &= 0.006(2N_1)^{-0.09} + 0.4(2N_1)^{-0.55}
\end{aligned}$$

Trial-and-error: $2N_1 = 200 \Rightarrow 0.006(200)^{-0.09} + 0.4(200)^{-0.55}$

$$= 0.006(0.612) + 0.4(0.0392) = 0.00367 + 0.0157 = 0.0194 \quad (\text{too high})$$

Try $2N_1 = 100$: $0.006(100)^{-0.09} + 0.4(100)^{-0.55} = 0.006(0.649) + 0.4(0.0282) = 0.00389 + 0.01128 = 0.0152$ (still high)

Try $2N_1 = 60$: $0.006(60)^{-0.09} + 0.4(60)^{-0.55} = 0.006(0.681) + 0.4(0.0382) = 0.00409 + 0.0153 = 0.0194$ (high)

Try $2N_1 = 50$: $0.006(50)^{-0.09} + 0.4(50)^{-0.55} = 0.006(0.692) + 0.4(0.0416) = 0.00415 + 0.0166 = 0.0208$ (high)

Try $2N_1 = 70$: $0.006(70)^{-0.09} + 0.4(70)^{-0.55} = 0.006(0.671) + 0.4(0.0350) = 0.00403 + 0.0140 = 0.0180$ (high)

Try $2N_1 = 80$: $0.006(80)^{-0.09} + 0.4(80)^{-0.55} = 0.006(0.665) + 0.4(0.0326) = 0.00399 + 0.01304 = 0.0170$ (high)

Iterating between 60–80, take $2N_1 \approx 75$: $0.006(75)^{-0.09} + 0.4(75)^{-0.55} \approx 0.006(0.668) + 0.4(0.0338) = 0.00401 + 0.0135 = 0.0175$ — too high.

Accept $2N_1 \approx 65$: $0.006(65)^{-0.09} + 0.4(65)^{-0.55} = 0.006(0.677) + 0.4(0.0366) = 0.00406 + 0.01465 = 0.0187$ — closer.

So $2N_1 \approx 60 \Rightarrow N_1 \approx 30$ cycles to failure at the high amplitude.

**Step 6b — Compute $N_2$ at $\Delta\varepsilon_2/2 = 0.003$.**

By similar procedure (left for student practice), $2N_2 \approx 4000 \Rightarrow N_2 \approx 2000$ cycles.

**Step 6c — Apply Miner's Rule.**

$$D = \frac{n_1}{N_1} + \frac{n_2}{N_2} = \frac{500}{30} + \frac{2000}{2000} = 16.7 + 1.0 = 17.7$$

**Step 6d — Interpretation.**

$$D = 17.7 \gg 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Component fails long before the second block is complete.}$$

In fact, only $n_1^* = N_1 \cdot 1 = 30$ cycles of block 1 are allowed before failure, meaning the second block cannot even begin. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The critical lesson from this example:** A few high-amplitude LCF cycles can dominate a Miner's-rule calculation, because $N_1$ is so small. The "small" LCF events often *control* the life of a spectrum-loaded structure.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Modified Miner's Rule (Critical Damage Sum)

### Statement
For a material with experimentally-determined critical damage sum $a$ (typically $0.5 \le a \le 1.5$ for steels):

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} = a \;\;\text{at failure}$$

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Observe Empirical Scatter.**
Palmgren–Miner predicts $D = 1$ at failure, but tests show:
- Underestimate (low-to-high loading): $D_{f} \approx 0.3$ to $1.0$
- Overestimate (high-to-low): $D_{f} \approx 1.0$ to $3.0$

**Step 2 — Introduce Empirical Constant $a$.**

$$D_f = a$$

**Step 3 — Choose $a$ from S–N data on the material.**
For ductile steels: $a \approx 0.7$. For brittle materials: $a \approx 0.3$–$0.5$. For ductile aluminium: $a \approx 0.7$.

**Step 4 — Predictive Equation.**

$$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{n_i}{N_i} = a}$$

**Step 5 — Consequence.**
If $a < 1$, Miner's rule is *non-conservative* for the specific spectrum — designers should use $a$ for safety.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Corten–Dolan Non-Linear Rule

### Statement
The Corten–Dolan (1956) rule is a non-linear damage summation:

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}\left(\frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_{\max}}\right)^{d}$$

with $d = 1/a - 1$ (where $a$ is the Corten–Dolan exponent, $0.6 \le a \le 1.0$).

### Derivation (Sketch)

**Step 1 — Hypothesis.**
Corten and Dolan assumed that the highest stress level in a spectrum nucleates a fatigue crack, and lower stresses merely *propagate* it. The damage rate at level $i$ is proportional to $\sigma_i^{d}$.

**Step 2 — Differential Form.**
Per cycle at level $i$:

$$\frac{dD}{dn_i} = \frac{1}{N_i}\left(\frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_{\max}}\right)^{d}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Over the Block.**

$$D_i = \frac{n_i}{N_i}\left(\frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_{\max}}\right)^{d}$$

**Step 4 — Sum and Set to Unity.**

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{n_i}{N_i}\left(\frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_{\max}}\right)^{d} = 1$$

**Step 5 — Special Two-Block Case.**
If the spectrum has the maximum at $i=1$:

$$\frac{n_1}{N_1} + \frac{n_2}{N_2}\left(\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\right)^{d} = 1$$

Equivalently, with $N_1 D_1^{a} = N_2 D_2^{a} = \text{const.}$:

$$\frac{n_1}{N_1} + \frac{n_2}{N_2}\left(\frac{N_1}{n_1}\right)^{1-a} = 1 \quad \text{(where } a = 1/(1+d)\text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Corten–Dolan is Better than Miner:** It accounts for the fact that high-amplitude cycles in early blocks *nucleate the crack* and hence weight them more heavily (since $\sigma_i \le \sigma_{\max}$ means $(\sigma_i/\sigma_{\max})^{d} \le 1$, but the cumulative effect of many low cycles can still be significant).

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Manson–Hirschberg (Cycle-by-Cycle, Neuber-Based)

### Statement
A cycle-by-cycle (rainflow counted) damage model uses Neuber's rule to translate nominal stresses into local elastic-plastic strains at a notch root.

### Key Equation (Neuber's Rule)

$$K_t^2 \cdot \Delta\sigma_{\text{nom}} \cdot \Delta\varepsilon_{\text{nom}} = K_t^2 \cdot \frac{(\Delta S)^2}{E} = \Delta\sigma \cdot \Delta\varepsilon$$

where the RHS uses local stress and strain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exam (PEMET744 / M3):** Students are expected to *recognize* the Manson–Hirschberg method, know that it requires (i) the CSS curve, (ii) Neuber's rule, and (iii) the ε–N curve, but full cycle-by-cycle numerical evaluation is rarely asked in 14-mark problems — only the methodology and a representative calculation are required.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Which Rule to Use When?

| Spectrum type | Recommended rule | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Narrow band, low-to-high sequence | Modified Miner ($a \approx 0.7$) | Non-conservative Miner needs safety factor |
| Wide band, random spectrum | Corten–Dolan | Captures crack-nucleation physics |
| Two-block overload + baseline | Double Linear Damage Rule (Manson) | Explicitly handles two-phase crack life |
| Notched components | Manson–Hirschberg (Neuber) | Local plasticity matters |
| High-mean-stress, ductile | SWT or Smith–Watson–Topper | Mean stress critical |
