# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Expected]`

**Define notch sensitivity. Explain Peterson's notch sensitivity index with its range of values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Notch sensitivity is a material property that measures the degree to which a material's fatigue strength is reduced by the presence of a geometric discontinuity such as a notch, hole, or fillet under cyclic loading.

**Peterson's Notch Sensitivity Index (q):**

$$q = \frac{K_f - 1}{K_t - 1}$$

where $K_t$ is the theoretical (geometric) stress concentration factor and $K_f$ is the fatigue stress concentration factor.

**Range of q:**
- $q = 0$: Material is fully **notch insensitive** ($K_f = 1$); the material ignores the notch.
- $0 < q < 1$: Material is **partially notch sensitive**; this is the typical range for most engineering metals.
- $q = 1$: Material is **fully notch sensitive** ($K_f = K_t$); the material responds to the full theoretical severity of the notch.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Distinguish between theoretical stress concentration factor ($K_t$) and fatigue stress concentration factor ($K_f$).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | $K_t$ (Theoretical) | $K_f$ (Fatigue) |
|--------|---------------------|-----------------|
| **Nature** | Geometric property | Material + Geometric property |
| **Depends on** | Shape of the notch and type of loading | Shape, material ductility, strength, and loading |
| **Determined by** | Elasticity theory, photoelasticity, or stress concentration charts | S-N curves, experiments, or Neuber's relation |
| **Range** | $K_t \geq 1$ | $1 \leq K_f \leq K_t$ |
| **Static or Cyclic** | Static (elastic) | Cyclic (fatigue) |
| **Material sensitive?** | No | Yes |

The relationship: $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$, where q is notch sensitivity.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain Neuber's empirical formula for notch sensitivity. How is Neuber's constant $\sqrt{a}$ determined for steels? Discuss the significance of notch sensitivity in the design of machine components subjected to fatigue loading.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Neuber's Formula (3 marks):**

Neuber proposed an empirical relationship between the notch sensitivity index (q), the notch root radius (r), and a material constant called Neuber's constant ($\sqrt{a}$):

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a/r}}$$

This equation indicates that:
- For a given material (fixed $\sqrt{a}$), as the notch radius **r increases**, $\sqrt{a/r}$ decreases, and **q increases** (approaches 1).
- For a given notch geometry, materials with a **larger $\sqrt{a}$** (softer, more ductile steels) have **lower q** (less notch sensitive).

**Determination of $\sqrt{a}$ for Steels (2 marks):**

For steels, $\sqrt{a}$ is correlated with the ultimate tensile strength $S_{ut}$ (in ksi):

$$\sqrt{a} = \sqrt{0.25 \times 10^{-(S_{ut}/20) - 1}} \quad \text{(in)}^{1/2}$$

The constant **0.25 in (6.35 mm)** is the standard reference notch radius used in Peterson's charts. As $S_{ut}$ increases, $\sqrt{a}$ decreases, so q increases — meaning **higher-strength steels are more notch sensitive**.

**Significance in Fatigue Design (2 marks):**

Notch sensitivity is critical in fatigue design because:
1. It quantifies how much of the theoretical stress concentration a material "feels" in cyclic loading.
2. It determines the **fatigue stress concentration factor $K_f$**, which directly reduces the endurance limit: $S_{e,n} = S_e / K_f$.
3. It guides surface treatment decisions (e.g., shot peening to introduce compressive residual stresses that effectively lower q).
4. It explains why high-strength components require generous fillet radii and surface finishing to mitigate fatigue failure.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A steel shaft with ultimate tensile strength $S_{ut} = 600 \, \text{MPa}$ has a shoulder fillet with theoretical stress concentration factor $K_t = 2.2$ and notch root radius $r = 3 \, \text{mm}$. The smooth specimen endurance limit is $S_e = 300 \, \text{MPa}$. Compute the notch sensitivity index (q) using Neuber's formula and the notched endurance limit. Use SI units.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute Neuber's constant $\sqrt{a}$ (SI units).**

$$\sqrt{a} = 0.245 \times 10^{-(S_{ut}/2000)-1} = 0.245 \times 10^{-(600/2000)-1}$$

$$= 0.245 \times 10^{-0.3 - 1} = 0.245 \times 10^{-1.3}$$

$$10^{-1.3} = 0.05012$$

$$\sqrt{a} = 0.245 \times 0.05012 = 0.01228 \, \text{mm}^{1/2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sqrt{a/r}$.**

$$\sqrt{a/r} = \sqrt{\frac{0.01228}{3}} = \sqrt{0.004093} = 0.06398$$

**Step 3 — Compute q.**

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a/r}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.06398} = \frac{1}{1.06398}$$

$$q = 0.9399$$

**Step 4 — Compute $K_f$.**

$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.9399 \times (2.2 - 1) = 1 + 0.9399 \times 1.2$$

$$K_f = 1 + 1.1279 = 2.1279$$

**Step 5 — Compute Notched Endurance Limit $S_{e,n}$.**

$$S_{e,n} = \frac{S_e}{K_f} = \frac{300}{2.1279} = 141.0 \, \text{MPa}$$

### Final Results Table

| Quantity | Computed Value |
|----------|----------------|
| Neuber's constant $\sqrt{a}$ | 0.01228 mm$^{1/2}$ |
| Notch sensitivity index q | **0.9399** |
| Fatigue stress concentration factor $K_f$ | **2.1279** |
| Notched endurance limit $S_{e,n}$ | **141.0 MPa** |

> **Conclusion:** The high q value (≈0.94) indicates that this high-strength steel shaft is **highly notch sensitive** — the material responds to nearly the full theoretical stress concentration. Design improvements should include increasing the fillet radius or applying surface treatments.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Expected Pattern]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Discuss the various factors affecting notch sensitivity in metallic materials. Explain with neat sketches how the S-N curve of a notched specimen differs from that of a smooth specimen for (i) a notch-insensitive material, and (ii) a fully notch-sensitive material.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Factors Affecting Notch Sensitivity (4 marks):**

1. **Ultimate Tensile Strength ($S_{ut}$):** As $S_{ut}$ increases, notch sensitivity (q) increases. High-strength steels are more notch sensitive.

2. **Notch Root Radius (r):** Smaller notch radii produce higher stress concentrations. For a fixed material, a sharper notch causes more local plastic deformation, making q smaller.

3. **Ductility:** Materials with higher % elongation and reduction in area exhibit lower q. Ductile materials relieve local stress through plastic flow.

4. **Temperature:** Increasing temperature reduces yield strength and increases ductility, thereby reducing q.

5. **Residual Stresses:** Compressive residual stresses (from shot peening, nitriding, autofrettage) effectively reduce the fatigue stress concentration, lowering effective q.

6. **Type of Loading:** Torsional, axial, and bending loadings each have different $K_t$ values for the same geometry.

7. **Grain Size & Microstructure:** Finer grain sizes generally improve fatigue strength and can influence q.

**S-N Curve Comparison (3 marks):**

- **(i) Notch-Insensitive Material (q = 0):** The S-N curve of the notched specimen essentially **coincides** with the smooth specimen's curve. The material's fatigue strength is unaffected by the presence of the notch. Example: annealed low-carbon steel with low $S_{ut}$.

- **(ii) Fully Notch-Sensitive Material (q = 1):** The notched specimen's S-N curve is **significantly lower**, with the endurance limit reduced by a factor of $K_t$. The fatigue life drops sharply. Example: quenched and tempered high-strength steel.

For partially notch-sensitive materials (0 < q < 1), the notched curve lies between these two extremes, with the endurance limit reduced by $K_f$ (not $K_t$).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A stepped shaft in a gearbox is made of alloy steel with $S_{ut} = 900 \, \text{MPa}$ and smooth endurance limit $S_e = 450 \, \text{MPa}$. The shoulder fillet has $K_t = 1.6$ and notch root radius $r = 2 \, \text{mm}$. The shaft is subjected to a fluctuating bending stress with mean stress $\sigma_m = 200 \, \text{MPa}$ and amplitude $\sigma_a = 150 \, \text{MPa}$. Calculate the factor of safety using the modified Goodman criterion, considering notch sensitivity.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute Neuber's constant.**

$$\sqrt{a} = 0.245 \times 10^{-(900/2000)-1} = 0.245 \times 10^{-0.45 - 1} = 0.245 \times 10^{-1.45}$$

$$10^{-1.45} = 0.03548$$

$$\sqrt{a} = 0.245 \times 0.03548 = 0.008693 \, \text{mm}^{1/2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sqrt{a/r}$.**

$$\sqrt{a/r} = \sqrt{\frac{0.008693}{2}} = \sqrt{0.004347} = 0.06593$$

**Step 3 — Compute q.**

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + 0.06593} = \frac{1}{1.06593} = 0.9381$$

**Step 4 — Compute $K_f$.**

$$K_f = 1 + 0.9381 \times (1.6 - 1) = 1 + 0.9381 \times 0.6 = 1 + 0.5629 = 1.5629$$

**Step 5 — Compute Notched Endurance Limit.**

$$S_{e,n} = \frac{S_e}{K_f} = \frac{450}{1.5629} = 287.9 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Apply Modified Goodman Criterion with $K_f$.**

The modified Goodman equation for notched components:

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{16 K_f \sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{8 K_f \sigma_m}{S_{ut}}$$

Substituting values:

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{16 \times 1.5629 \times 150}{450} + \frac{8 \times 1.5629 \times 200}{900}$$

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{3750.96}{450} + \frac{2500.64}{900}$$

$$\frac{1}{n} = 8.335 + 2.778 = 11.113 \, \text{(dimensionless stress ratio per 10)}$$

Wait — this gives an unrealistically low n. The typical modified Goodman form for *notched* members is:

$$\frac{K_f \cdot \sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{K_f \cdot \sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{1.5629 \times 150}{450} + \frac{1.5629 \times 200}{900} = \frac{1}{n}$$

$$\frac{234.4}{450} + \frac{312.6}{900} = 0.5209 + 0.3473 = 0.8682$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{1}{n} = 0.8682 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = 1.152$$

### Final Results Table

| Quantity | Computed Value |
|----------|----------------|
| Notch sensitivity index q | 0.9381 |
| Fatigue stress concentration factor $K_f$ | 1.5629 |
| Notched endurance limit $S_{e,n}$ | 287.9 MPa |
| **Factor of Safety (n)** | **1.152** |

> **Conclusion:** The factor of safety of 1.152 is marginally acceptable for a gearbox shaft (typical n ≥ 1.5 for steady fluctuating loads). Design improvement via increased fillet radius is recommended.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Losing Mistakes:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in Neuber's Formula:** Students frequently mix inch and SI versions. Always verify whether $S_{ut}$ is in ksi or MPa and use the **correct** formula: inch formula uses exponent $-(S_{ut}/20)-1$ with $S_{ut}$ in **ksi**; SI formula uses $-(S_{ut}/2000)-1$ with $S_{ut}$ in **MPa**. (2 marks lost if wrong)
> 2. **Forgetting to Apply $K_f$ in Fatigue Strength:** Students often compute $K_f$ correctly but then use $K_t$ instead in the Goodman/Soderberg equation. The fatigue stress concentration factor (not theoretical) must be used for fatigue life. (3 marks lost)
> 3. **q Out of Range:** If computed q > 1 or q < 0, clamp to 1 or 0 respectively. A common mistake is leaving an invalid q value unchecked. (1 mark lost)
> 4. **Confusing $K_t$ with $K_f$ in Definitions:** $K_t$ is theoretical/geometric, $K_f$ is fatigue/material. Marks awarded in Part A definitions depend on this distinction. (2 marks lost)

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### Core Concepts (Must Memorize)
- ✅ Notch sensitivity **q** is a **material property**; $K_t$ is a **geometric property**; $K_f$ is **both**.
- ✅ Formula: $q = (K_f - 1) / (K_t - 1)$; rearranged: $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$.
- ✅ Range: $q = 0$ (insensitive) → $q = 1$ (fully sensitive).
- ✅ $K_f \leq K_t$ always holds.

### Neuber's Empirical Formula
- ✅ $q = 1 / (1 + \sqrt{a/r})$
- ✅ $\sqrt{a}$ (Neuber's constant) is a **material constant**; larger $\sqrt{a}$ → lower q → more ductile.
- ✅ Reference radius is **r = 0.25 in (6.35 mm)** in Peterson's charts.
- ✅ For steels: $\sqrt{a}$ decreases as $S_{ut}$ increases → high-strength steels are **more notch sensitive**.

### Factors Affecting q
- ✅ **Increase q**: higher $S_{ut}$, larger notch radius, more brittle microstructure.
- ✅ **Decrease q**: higher ductility, higher temperature, compressive residual stresses (shot peening), smaller notch radius.

### Key Engineering Insights
- ✅ Notched endurance limit: $S_{e,n} = S_e / K_f$.
- ✅ In fatigue design, use **$K_f$** (not $K_t$) in modified Goodman/Soderberg/GER equations.
- ✅ For high-strength brittle materials, assume **q = 1** as conservative estimate.
- ✅ For low-strength ductile materials, assume **q ≈ 0** as engineering bound.
- ✅ Counter-intuitive fact: **stronger materials are more notch sensitive in fatigue**.

### Common Numerical Problem Pattern (KTU Exam Style)
1. Given: $S_{ut}$, $K_t$, r, $S_e$.
2. Compute $\sqrt{a}$ using correct unit system.
3. Compute $q$ using Neuber's formula.
4. Compute $K_f$ using Peterson's relation.
5. Compute $S_{e,n} = S_e / K_f$.
6. Optionally, apply in modified Goodman with mean and alternating stresses.

### Final Mantra for KTU
> **"q describes how much of the theoretical stress concentration a material accepts in fatigue — high-strength materials accept more, ductile materials accept less."**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Fracture Mechanics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principles of Fracture Mechanics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fracture Mechanics** is the field of applied mechanics that studies the propagation of cracks in materials. It uses analytical, numerical, and experimental methods to predict the conditions under which a pre-existing crack becomes unstable and leads to catastrophic failure, even when the applied nominal stress is well below the material's yield strength.

Fracture Mechanics fundamentally questions the classical failure theories (like yield criteria) by stating that **"failure does not depend on stress alone, but on the combined effect of stress, crack length, and geometry."** The discipline evolved from A.A. Griffith's pioneering work (1921) on brittle fracture of glass and was later extended by G.R. Irwin (1957) into the discipline of **Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Torn Paper Analogy:**
> Imagine trying to tear a fresh sheet of paper. It is very difficult because the paper has no flaws. Now take the same sheet and make a tiny cut (a few mm) at the edge. The paper tears effortlessly along the cut, even though you apply the same pulling force. The tiny cut is the **crack**, and the ease of tearing represents **stress concentration**.
>
> **The Saran Wrap Analogy:**
> If you stretch a small piece of plastic wrap (Saran Wrap) it will deform plastically and not break. If you pierce a tiny hole in it and stretch it, it will tear catastrophically from the hole. The plastic wrap represents a **ductile material** with a **critical crack**, and stretching represents the **applied stress field**.

**The Key Insight:** Even in a perfectly elastic, brittle material, a small crack amplifies the local stress at its tip by a factor of 10 to 1000 times the nominal (average) stress applied to the structure. This is why ships, bridges, and aircraft can fracture at stresses far below their yield strength.

## 1.3 The Three Critical Parameters

Fracture mechanics pivots around three fundamental metrics:

- **Stress Intensity Factor ($K$)** — measured in **MPa·√m** (or **psi·√in**)
- **Energy Release Rate ($G$)** — measured in **N/m** or **J/m²** (energy per unit crack area)
- **Fracture Toughness ($K_C$ or $G_C$)** — a material property representing the critical value at which unstable crack propagation occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the 2024 scheme, students must clearly distinguish between the **Energy Approach (Griffith)** and the **Stress Intensity Approach (Irwin)**, and understand the equivalence between the two through the relationship $G = K^2 / E'$ (for plane stress/strain).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Fracture Mechanics

Fracture mechanics is built upon two complementary theoretical frameworks:

### 2.1.1 Griffith's Energy Balance Approach (1921)

Griffith postulated that a crack will propagate when the **decrease in elastic strain energy** released by the material is at least equal to the **energy required to create the new crack surfaces**.

- **Before crack propagation:** The system stores elastic strain energy.
- **During crack propagation:** Part of this energy is converted into surface energy on the two new crack faces.
- **Thermodynamic criterion:** The crack extends when the energy released per unit area of crack growth exceeds (or equals) the surface energy per unit area.

### 2.1.2 Irwin's Stress Intensity Approach (1957)

Irwin reformulated Griffith's theory by focusing on the **stress field in the immediate vicinity of the crack tip** rather than on global energy. This was the birth of modern **Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM)**.

- A crack in a linear elastic body creates a **singular stress field** near its tip.
- The intensity of this stress field is characterized by a single scalar parameter: the **Stress Intensity Factor ($K$)**.
- The factor $K$ depends on the applied stress, the crack size, and the geometry of the component.

## 2.2 The Three Modes of Crack Loading

> [!NOTE]
> A crack can be loaded in three independent ways. Real-world cracks are often a combination of these, but each can be analyzed separately using the principle of **superposition**.

- **Mode I — Opening Mode (Tensile):** The two crack faces move apart, perpendicular to the plane of the crack. This is the **most common and most dangerous** mode in engineering. Notation: $K_I$ or $K_{IC}$ (plane strain fracture toughness).
- **Mode II — Sliding Mode (In-plane Shear):** The two crack faces slide over each other in the plane of the crack, perpendicular to the leading edge.
- **Mode III — Tearing Mode (Anti-plane Shear):** The two crack faces slide over each other out of the plane of the crack, parallel to the leading edge.

## 2.3 The Concept of Stress Concentration

In an uncracked body under uniform tensile stress $\sigma$, the stress is uniformly distributed. When a crack is introduced:

- The stress cannot be transmitted across the free surfaces of the crack faces.
- This forces the load to "flow around" the crack tip.
- The result is a **localized stress amplification** that diverges as $r \to 0$ (where $r$ is the distance from the crack tip).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is a **high-yield summary** of every formula you must memorize for the KTU University Examination on this topic. Pay close attention to the boundary conditions and the constants.

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description / Condition** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stress Intensity Factor (Central Crack) | $K = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Infinite plate, central crack of length $2a$ | MPa·√m |
| Stress Intensity Factor (Edge Crack) | $K = 1.12 \, \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Semi-infinite plate, single edge crack of length $a$ | MPa·√m |
| General SIF (with Geometry Factor) | $K = Y \, \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | $Y$ = dimensionless geometry/finite-width correction factor | MPa·√m |
| Griffith Energy Criterion | $\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E\gamma_s}{\pi a}}$ | Brittle material, plane stress | MPa |
| Irwin's Modified Griffith | $\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E(\gamma_s + \gamma_p)}{\pi a}}$ | Includes plastic work $\gamma_p$ | MPa |
| Energy Release Rate (Plane Stress) | $G = \dfrac{\pi \sigma^2 a}{E}$ | Through-thickness crack, plane stress | N/m or J/m² |
| Energy Release Rate (Plane Strain) | $G = \dfrac{\pi \sigma^2 a}{E}(1 - \nu^2)$ | Through-thickness crack, plane strain | N/m or J/m² |
| Equivalence (Plane Stress) | $G = \dfrac{K_I^2}{E}$ | Relates $G$ and $K$ under plane stress | J/m² |
| Equivalence (Plane Strain) | $G = \dfrac{K_I^2}{E}(1 - \nu^2)$ | Relates $G$ and $K$ under plane strain | J/m² |
| Fracture Criterion | $K_I \geq K_{IC}$ | Catastrophic failure occurs when applied $K$ reaches toughness | MPa·√m |
| Plastic Zone Size (Plane Stress) | $r_p = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_I}{\sigma_{ys}}\right)^2$ | Dugdale/Irwin estimate for Mode I | m |
| Plastic Zone Size (Plane Strain) | $r_p = \dfrac{1}{6\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_I}{\sigma_{ys}}\right)^2$ | Significantly smaller than plane stress | m |
| Critical Crack Length | $a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sigma}\right)^2$ | Maximum allowable crack length for safe operation | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - $E$ = Young's Modulus
> - $\nu$ = Poisson's Ratio
> - $\gamma_s$ = Surface energy per unit area
> - $\gamma_p$ = Plastic work per unit area
> - $\sigma_{ys}$ = Yield strength
> - $Y$ = Geometry factor (also written as $f(\frac{a}{W})$ or $F$)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM) is used in the damage tolerance design of aircraft fuselages. Inspection intervals are scheduled based on the predicted crack growth rate.
- **Civil Engineering:** Fracture mechanics is used to assess the integrity of large welded structures, bridges, and pressure vessels containing pre-existing weld defects.
- **Pressure Vessels and Pipelines:** The ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code Section XI uses fracture mechanics to determine the maximum allowable flaw size.
- **Automotive and Rail:** Fatigue crack propagation analysis (Paris Law) is directly grounded in the principles of fracture mechanics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Griffith's Energy Release Rate for a Through-Thickness Crack

**Problem Setup:** Consider a large plate of unit thickness containing a central through-thickness crack of total length $2a$. The plate is subjected to a uniform remote tensile stress $\sigma$.

We will use the **energy balance approach**: for the crack to extend by an infinitesimal amount $da$, the change in total potential energy of the system must be negative (energy is released).

**Step 1 — Establish the strain energy of an uncracked plate.**

The elastic strain energy stored in a unit thickness of the uncracked plate per unit length is:

$$U_0 = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E} \cdot (\text{volume per unit length}) = \frac{\sigma^2 W}{2E}$$

where $W$ is the width of the plate. For an infinite plate, we consider the strain energy released from a "damage zone" of width $2a$ around the crack.

**Step 2 — Calculate the strain energy release as the crack extends.**

When a central crack of length $2a$ exists, the stress field near the crack is zero (free surfaces), so the strain energy in the cracked region is zero. The released strain energy (per unit thickness) due to the introduction of the crack is:

$$U_{\text{released}} = \frac{\sigma^2 (2a)}{2E} = \frac{\sigma^2 a}{E}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the released energy with respect to crack length.**

The energy release rate $G$ is defined as the rate of change of potential energy with respect to crack area $A$ (here, area = crack length $da \times 1$ for unit thickness):

$$G = \frac{dU}{dA} = \frac{d}{d(2a)}\left(\frac{\sigma^2 a}{E}\right) \cdot \frac{d(2a)}{dA}$$

Since $dA = 2 \, da$ (two new crack surfaces, each of length $da$ for unit thickness):

$$G = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{d}{da}\left(\frac{\sigma^2 a}{E}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma^2}{E} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E} \cdot 2a \cdot \frac{1}{a}$$

Let us redo this carefully, using the well-known Westergaard solution. The exact result (which we will verify below) is:

$$G = \frac{\pi \sigma^2 a}{E}$$

**Step 4 — Detailed verification using the Westergaard stress function.**

For a central crack of length $2a$ in an infinite plate under remote stress $\sigma$, the change in compliance $C$ of the plate is:

$$C = \frac{2\pi a^2}{EW}$$

The strain energy stored is $U = \frac{1}{2} P^2 C = \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 W \cdot C \cdot 1$ (force $P = \sigma W$ for unit thickness).

The energy release rate per unit crack area is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= \frac{1}{2} P^2 \frac{dC}{dA} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} (\sigma W)^2 \cdot \frac{d}{d(2a)} \left( \frac{2\pi a^2}{EW} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{W} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 W^2 \cdot \frac{4\pi a}{EW} \cdot \frac{1}{2W} \\
&= \frac{\sigma^2 W^2 \cdot 4\pi a}{4 E W^2} \\
G &= \frac{\pi \sigma^2 a}{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the fracture criterion.**

For the crack to propagate, the energy release rate must equal (or exceed) the critical value $G_C$, which represents the energy required to create new crack surface (and any associated plastic work):

$$\frac{\pi \sigma^2 a}{E} = G_C$$

Solving for the critical stress:

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{E G_C}{\pi a}}
$$

If we identify $G_C = 2\gamma_s$ (twice the surface energy, since two new surfaces are created), we recover Griffith's classical result:

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E \gamma_s}{\pi a}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The critical fracture stress $\sigma_c$ is **inversely proportional to the square root of the crack length $a$**. This is the famous "square-root singularity" and is the mathematical reason why halving the crack size reduces the failure stress by only a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$, not by 2.

## 3.2 Derivation: Equivalence Between $G$ and $K$

**Step 1 — Recall the Mode I stress field near a crack tip.**

For Mode I loading, the stresses in the immediate vicinity of the crack tip (in polar coordinates $r, \theta$) are given by the **Williams expansion** (dominant singular term):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{xx} &= \frac{K_I}{\sqrt{2\pi r}} \cos\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\left[1 - \sin\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sin\left(\frac{3\theta}{2}\right)\right] \\
\sigma_{yy} &= \frac{K_I}{\sqrt{2\pi r}} \cos\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\left[1 + \sin\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sin\left(\frac{3\theta}{2}\right)\right] \\
\tau_{xy} &= \frac{K_I}{\sqrt{2\pi r}} \cos\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sin\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\cos\left(\frac{3\theta}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the strain energy density.**

The strain energy density $u$ in a 2D linear elastic body under plane stress is:

$$u = \frac{1}{2E}\left(\sigma_{xx}^2 + \sigma_{yy}^2 - 2\nu \sigma_{xx}\sigma_{yy} + 2(1+\nu)\tau_{xy}^2\right)$$

**Step 3 — Integrate the strain energy density around the crack tip.**

The energy release rate $G$ is the flux of energy into the crack tip, computed by integrating the strain energy density over a closed contour around the tip. For Mode I:

$$G = \lim_{r \to 0} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \left( u \, n_x - \sigma_{ij} n_j u_i \right) r \, d\theta$$

After performing this contour integration (a standard result in elasticity), we obtain:

$$G = \frac{K_I^2}{E} \quad \text{(plane stress)}$$

For plane strain, the factor $(1 - \nu^2)$ appears due to the constraint of zero out-of-plane strain $\varepsilon_{zz} = 0$:

$$G = \frac{K_I^2}{E}(1 - \nu^2) \quad \text{(plane strain)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the contour integration yield a finite result despite the $1/\sqrt{r}$ singularity?** The integrand contains terms like $\sigma^2 r$ from the Jacobian. Since $\sigma^2 \propto 1/r$, the product $\sigma^2 r$ is finite as $r \to 0$. The $1/r$ singularity is integrable (it is a "weak" singularity).

## 3.3 Derivation: Critical Crack Length

Starting from the fracture criterion $K_I = K_{IC}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_I &= K_{IC} \\
Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a_c} &= K_{IC} \\
\sqrt{\pi a_c} &= \frac{K_{IC}}{Y \sigma} \\
\pi a_c &= \left(\frac{K_{IC}}{Y \sigma}\right)^2 \\
a_c &= \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{K_{IC}}{Y \sigma}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This equation is the **design equation for damage-tolerant structures**. Given an applied stress $\sigma$ and a known toughness $K_{IC}$, we can compute the maximum permissible crack length $a_c$. Any crack longer than this will cause catastrophic failure.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three Modes of Crack Loading

This diagram illustrates the three fundamental modes of loading that a crack can experience. In real engineering components, a crack may be subjected to a combination of these modes, but Mode I (opening) is by far the most critical.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Loading] --> B{Mode Selection}
    B --> C[Mode I: Opening]
    B --> D[Mode II: Sliding]
    B --> E[Mode III: Tearing]
    C --> F[Tensile stress perpendicular to crack plane]
    D --> G[In-plane shear stress]
    E --> H[Out-of-plane shear stress]
    F --> I[Most dangerous mode in engineering]
    G --> J[Common in mixed-mode loading]
    H --> K[Rare in thick plates]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fracture Analysis

This schematic maps the procedural flow of a complete fracture mechanics analysis, from problem identification to the final design recommendation. It serves as a decision-tree blueprint that an engineer (or KTU examiner) follows when solving a problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Component and Material] --> B[Determine Applied Stress sigma]
    B --> C[Measure or Assume Crack Length a]
    C --> D[Identify Crack Geometry: Central, Edge, Surface, Embedded]
    D --> E[Select Geometry Factor Y from Standard Tables]
    E --> F[Calculate Stress Intensity Factor K equals Y sigma sqrt of pi a]
    F --> G{Compare K to K_IC}
    G -->|K is less than K_IC| H[SAFE: Component is crack-tolerant]
    G -->|K is greater than or equal to K_IC| I[UNSAFE: Catastrophic failure imminent]
    H --> J[Schedule Periodic Inspection for Crack Growth]
    I --> K[Recommend Repair, Replacement, or Stress Reduction]
    K --> L[Recompute a_c for New Conditions]
    J --> M[Use Paris Law to Predict Inspection Interval]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Final Design Decision]
```

## 4.3 Stress Distribution Around a Crack Tip

This diagram illustrates the qualitative stress amplification phenomenon at the tip of a crack. The nominal stress $\sigma$ is uniform far from the crack, but near the tip, the stress rises dramatically to a theoretically infinite value (the singularity). In reality, the material yields locally and creates a small plastic zone of size $r_p$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Remote Stress sigma is uniform] --> B[Stress field lines detour around the crack]
    B --> C[Lines crowd near the crack tip]
    C --> D[Local stress amplifies by factor of 10 to 1000]
    D --> E[At distance r approaching 0]
    E --> F[Stress approaches infinity in theory]
    F --> G[Plastic zone of radius r_p forms in ductile materials]
    G --> H[For brittle materials: sudden fracture]
    G --> I[For ductile materials: stable crack growth until r_p becomes critical]
```

## 4.4 LEFM vs EPFM Validity Map

The choice of fracture mechanics framework depends on the size of the plastic zone relative to the crack and the component. This flowchart helps decide which theory to apply.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component with Crack] --> B{Plastic Zone Size r_p vs Crack Length a and Thickness B}
    B -->|r_p is much less than a and B| C[LEFM Valid: Use K and G Approach]
    B -->|r_p comparable to a or B| D[EPFM Required: Use J-integral or CTOD]
    B -->|r_p is much greater than a| E[Material yields globally: Classical plasticity applies]
    C --> F[Plane Strain Dominant: K_IC and G_IC]
    C --> G[Plane Stress Dominant: K_C and G_C]
    D --> H[Elastic-Plastic Fracture Mechanics EPFM]
    E --> I[Limit Load Analysis]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common question asks "Is LEFM applicable to this material/component?" The answer hinges on the ratio $r_p / a$. If it is less than 0.02 (or 0.1 for plane stress), LEFM is valid. The student must calculate $r_p$ and compare.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Q: Differentiate between Griffith's energy approach and Irwin's stress intensity approach to fracture mechanics.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Griffith's Energy Approach** | **Irwin's Stress Intensity Approach** |
|---|---|---|
| Year proposed | 1921 | 1957 |
| Governing parameter | Energy release rate $G$ | Stress intensity factor $K$ |
| Primary focus | Global energy balance of the body | Local stress field near the crack tip |
| Material assumption | Perfectly brittle (no plasticity) | Linear elastic with small-scale yielding |
| Fracture criterion | $G = G_C = 2\gamma_s$ | $K = K_C$ (or $K_{IC}$ under plane strain) |
| Equivalence | $G = K^2 / E$ (plane stress) | $G = K^2(1-\nu^2)/E$ (plane strain) |

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q: What is meant by the three modes of crack loading? Which one is most critical in engineering design?**

**Model Answer:**

The three modes of crack loading are:
- **Mode I (Opening Mode):** The crack faces separate symmetrically, perpendicular to the plane of the crack. Driven by remote tensile stress.
- **Mode II (Sliding Mode):** The crack faces slide over each other in the plane of the crack, perpendicular to the leading edge. Driven by in-plane shear.
- **Mode III (Tearing Mode):** The crack faces slide over each other out of the plane of the crack, parallel to the leading edge. Driven by anti-plane shear.

**Mode I is the most critical** in engineering design because it produces the maximum stress amplification at the crack tip, the lowest fracture toughness, and the highest crack-driving force. Most real-world failures initiate in Mode I.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the energy release rate $G$ for a central through-thickness crack of length $2a$ in an infinite plate subjected to a uniform remote tensile stress $\sigma$. Use the energy balance approach.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel plate ($E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_{ys} = 350$ MPa) contains a central through-thickness crack of length $2a = 40$ mm. The plate is loaded in tension with a remote stress $\sigma = 150$ MPa. Calculate:
- (i) The stress intensity factor $K_I$.
- (ii) The energy release rate $G$ (under plane strain).
- (iii) The plastic zone size $r_p$ (under plane strain).
- (iv) Comment on the validity of LEFM if the plate thickness is $B = 25$ mm.

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Step 1 — Concept Setup:**
We use the energy balance method proposed by Griffith. The crack will propagate when the energy released from the elastic strain field equals the energy required to create new crack surfaces.

**Step 2 — Compliance of the cracked plate:**
For a central crack of length $2a$ in an infinite plate of width $W$ under remote stress $\sigma$, the change in elastic compliance is given by the Westergaard solution:

$$\Delta C = \frac{2\pi a^2}{EW}$$

**Step 3 — Energy stored in the cracked plate:**
For unit thickness, the force is $P = \sigma W$. The strain energy stored is:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} P^2 (\Delta C) = \frac{1}{2} (\sigma W)^2 \cdot \frac{2\pi a^2}{EW} = \frac{\pi \sigma^2 a^2 W}{E}$$

Wait — for a remote stress $\sigma$ applied through rigid grips, the **complementary energy** formulation is more direct. The energy release rate is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= \frac{1}{2} P^2 \frac{d(\Delta C)}{dA} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} (\sigma W)^2 \cdot \frac{d}{d(2a)}\left(\frac{2\pi a^2}{EW}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{W \cdot 1} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 W^2 \cdot \frac{4\pi a}{EW} \cdot \frac{1}{2W} \\
&= \frac{\pi \sigma^2 a}{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $dA = 2 \cdot da$ because two new crack surfaces are created. Hence:

$$\boxed{G = \frac{\pi \sigma^2 a}{E}} \quad \text{(Plane Stress)}$$

**Step 4 — Plane strain correction:**
For plane strain (thick plate), the out-of-plane strain is constrained, so the effective modulus becomes $E' = E/(1-\nu^2)$:

$$\boxed{G = \frac{\pi \sigma^2 a}{E}(1 - \nu^2)} \quad \text{(Plane Strain)}$$

**Step 5 — Fracture criterion:**
The crack propagates when $G \geq G_C = 2\gamma_s$ (brittle) or $G_C = 2(\gamma_s + \gamma_p)$ (with plastic work). Substituting:

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E\gamma_s}{\pi a}}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- $E = 200$ GPa $= 200 \times 10^3$ MPa
- $\nu = 0.3$
- $\sigma_{ys} = 350$ MPa
- $2a = 40$ mm, so $a = 20$ mm $= 0.020$ m
- $\sigma = 150$ MPa
- $B = 25$ mm

**(i) Stress Intensity Factor $K_I$:**

For a central through-thickness crack in an infinite plate, $Y = 1.0$:

$$K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a} = 1.0 \times 150 \times \sqrt{\pi \times 0.020}$$

$$K_I = 150 \times \sqrt{0.0628} = 150 \times 0.2507 = 37.6 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$$

**(ii) Energy Release Rate (Plane Strain):**

$$G = \frac{K_I^2}{E}(1 - \nu^2) = \frac{(37.6)^2}{200 \times 10^3}(1 - 0.3^2)$$

$$G = \frac{1413.76}{200000} \times 0.91 = 0.00707 \times 0.91 = 6.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J/m}^2$$

Wait — let me recompute with correct units. $K_I$ is in MPa·√m, so $K_I^2$ is in MPa²·m. To get J/m², we need $E$ in MPa.

$$G = \frac{1413.76 \text{ MPa}^2 \cdot \text{m}}{200000 \text{ MPa}} \times 0.91$$

$$G = 7.07 \times 10^{-3} \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{m} \times 0.91 = 6.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{m}$$

Converting to J/m²: $1 \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{m} = 10^6 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{m} = 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 \cdot \text{m} = 10^6 \text{ N/m} = 10^6 \text{ J/m}^2$.

$$G = 6.43 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^6 = 6430 \text{ J/m}^2$$

**(iii) Plastic Zone Size (Plane Strain):**

$$r_p = \frac{1}{6\pi}\left(\frac{K_I}{\sigma_{ys}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{6\pi}\left(\frac{37.6}{350}\right)^2$$

$$r_p = \frac{1}{18.85} \times (0.1074)^2 = 0.0531 \times 0.01154 = 6.13 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.613 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Validity of LEFM:**

For LEFM (plane strain) to be valid, the following ASTM E399 conditions must hold:

- $B \geq 2.5(K_{IC}/\sigma_{ys})^2$. We don't know $K_{IC}$, but we can check $r_p$ relative to $B$ and $a$.
- $a \geq 2.5(K_{IC}/\sigma_{ys})^2$ (similar condition).
- $r_p \ll a$ and $r_p \ll B$.

Here, $r_p = 0.613$ mm, $a = 20$ mm, $B = 25$ mm.

Ratio $r_p / a = 0.613 / 20 = 0.031$ (which is less than 0.1 — borderline acceptable).
Ratio $r_p / B = 0.613 / 25 = 0.025$ (less than 0.1 — acceptable).

**Conclusion:** LEFM is **conditionally valid** for this geometry, as long as the plate thickness and crack length satisfy ASTM E399 size requirements relative to the toughness. The small plastic zone justifies the linear elastic assumption.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of stress intensity factor. For a central through-thickness crack in a finite-width plate, write the expression for $K_I$ and explain the role of the geometry factor $Y$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An aluminum alloy component ($K_{IC} = 35$ MPa·√m) is subjected to a service stress of $\sigma = 200$ MPa. The component has a central through-thickness crack. Using a geometry factor $Y = 1.1$, determine:
- (i) The critical crack length $a_c$ that would cause catastrophic failure.
- (ii) If the actual crack length is $a = 10$ mm, what is the factor of safety against fracture?
- (iii) If the design requires a factor of safety of 2.0, what is the maximum allowable service stress?

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Definition:**
The Stress Intensity Factor ($K$) is a parameter that characterizes the intensity of the stress field in the immediate vicinity of a crack tip. It is a function of the applied stress, the crack size, and the geometry of the cracked body.

For a central through-thickness crack of length $2a$ in a plate of finite width $W$:

$$K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

where $Y = f(a/W)$ is a dimensionless **geometry factor** (also called a calibration factor) that accounts for:
- Finite width effects (the crack cannot be assumed to be in an infinite plate).
- The location of the crack (central, edge, surface, or embedded).
- The loading configuration (tension, bending, or combined).

**Role of $Y$:**
- $Y = 1.0$ for a central crack in an infinite plate.
- $Y \approx 1.12$ for a single edge crack in a semi-infinite plate.
- $Y$ increases as $a/W \to 1.0$ because the ligament between the crack and the free edge becomes small and the stress concentration intensifies.
- $Y$ values are tabulated in standard references (e.g., Tada, Paris, and Irwin's Stress Analysis of Cracks Handbook) or computed numerically.

**Fracture Criterion:** Failure occurs when $K_I \geq K_{IC}$, where $K_{IC}$ is the **plane strain fracture toughness** of the material.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- $K_{IC} = 35$ MPa·√m
- $\sigma = 200$ MPa
- $Y = 1.1$

**(i) Critical Crack Length $a_c$:**

Using the fracture criterion $K_I = K_{IC}$:

$$Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a_c} = K_{IC}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{\pi a_c} &= \frac{K_{IC}}{Y \sigma} = \frac{35}{1.1 \times 200} = \frac{35}{220} = 0.1591 \text{ m}^{1/2} \\
\pi a_c &= (0.1591)^2 = 0.02532 \text{ m} \\
a_c &= \frac{0.02532}{\pi} = 0.00806 \text{ m} = 8.06 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The critical crack length is $a_c = 8.06$ mm.

**(ii) Factor of Safety (FOS) for $a = 10$ mm:**

First, calculate the actual $K_I$ at $a = 10$ mm:

$$K_{I,\text{actual}} = 1.1 \times 200 \times \sqrt{\pi \times 0.010}$$

$$K_{I,\text{actual}} = 220 \times \sqrt{0.0314} = 220 \times 0.1772 = 38.99 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$$

Factor of Safety:

$$\text{FOS} = \frac{K_{IC}}{K_{I,\text{actual}}} = \frac{35}{38.99} = 0.898$$

**Answer:** FOS = 0.898. Since FOS < 1, the component will **fail** at the given stress because the actual crack (10 mm) exceeds the critical crack (8.06 mm).

**(iii) Maximum Allowable Stress for FOS = 2.0:**

The allowable stress is the stress at which $K_I = K_{IC} / \text{FOS}$:

$$K_{I,\text{allowable}} = \frac{K_{IC}}{\text{FOS}} = \frac{35}{2.0} = 17.5 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$$

Solving for the allowable stress $\sigma_{\text{allow}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{allow}} &= \frac{K_{I,\text{allowable}}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a}} = \frac{17.5}{1.1 \times \sqrt{\pi \times 0.010}} \\
\sigma_{\text{allow}} &= \frac{17.5}{1.1 \times 0.1772} = \frac{17.5}{0.1950} = 89.7 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The maximum allowable service stress is $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 89.7$ MPa.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** A majority of students lose 2-3 marks by not converting mm to m before substituting into $K = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$. If $a$ is in mm, the answer for $K$ will be off by a factor of $\sqrt{1000} \approx 31.6$.
> 2. **Plane Stress vs Plane Strain:** Forgetting to multiply by $(1-\nu^2)$ in plane strain calculations is a frequent mistake. Read the question carefully — the word "plane strain" or "thick plate" triggers the correction factor.
> 3. **Geometry Factor Confusion:** Students often write $K = \sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ for an edge crack and get a wrong answer. The correct form is $K = 1.12\,\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$.
> 4. **Critical Crack Length Definition:** $a_c$ refers to the half-length of a central crack (i.e., $a$ in the equation $K = \sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$), not the full length $2a$. Misinterpreting this can give an answer off by a factor of 2.
> 5. **FOS Definition:** In fracture mechanics, the FOS is defined as the ratio $K_{IC} / K_{I,\text{actual}}$, **not** the ratio of stresses (unless the geometry factor $Y$ is constant).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fracture Mechanics** predicts failure of cracked components under stress levels below the yield strength.
- **Griffith's Energy Criterion:** $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2E\gamma_s / (\pi a)}$ — failure occurs when released elastic energy equals surface energy.
- **Irwin's Stress Intensity Factor:** $K = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ — characterizes the singular stress field at the crack tip.
- **Equivalence (Plane Stress):** $G = K_I^2 / E$.
- **Equivalence (Plane Strain):** $G = K_I^2(1-\nu^2) / E$.
- **Fracture Criterion:** Failure occurs when $K_I \geq K_{IC}$.
- **Three Modes:** Mode I (opening, most critical), Mode II (in-plane shear), Mode III (anti-plane shear).
- **Plastic Zone Size (Plane Stress):** $r_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}(K_I/\sigma_{ys})^2$.
- **Plastic Zone Size (Plane Strain):** $r_p = \frac{1}{6\pi}(K_I/\sigma_{ys})^2$.
- **Critical Crack Length:** $a_c = \frac{1}{\pi}(K_{IC}/Y\sigma)^2$.
- **LEFM Validity:** Requires $r_p \ll a$ and $r_p \ll B$ (small-scale yielding).
- **ASTM E399:** Specifies minimum thickness $B \geq 2.5(K_{IC}/\sigma_{ys})^2$ for valid $K_{IC}$ measurement.
- **Design Equation:** $K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ is the master equation — every problem reduces to computing or comparing $K_I$ and $K_{IC}$.
- **Units:** Always use $a$ in meters (m) and $\sigma$ in MPa to get $K$ in MPa·√m.
- **Geometry Factor $Y$:** Central infinite plate: 1.0; single edge: 1.12; for finite plates, $Y = f(a/W)$ from standard tables.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Crack Initiation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Crack initiation** is the localised stage in the failure process at which a stable discontinuity (a sub-critical microcrack, void, or decohesion event) of critical size $a_0$ is first nucleated within an otherwise intact material volume, driven by a sustained combination of mechanical, thermal, chemical, and microstructural driving forces. It is formally considered complete when the nucleated defect reaches a size at which the local **stress intensity factor** $K$ equals or exceeds the material's intrinsic threshold value $K_{th}$ (or $K_{ISCC}$ in corrosive environments), after which propagation under the same loading becomes energetically favourable.

In the KTU-revised Bloom's taxonomy framework, this topic addresses **CO3 / CO4** outcomes: *“Analyse the mechanisms of crack nucleation and apply fracture-mechanics criteria to predict the onset of failure.”*

Mathematically, the governing inequality for the *instant* of crack initiation is:

$$
K_{local} \;\geq\; K_{th}
$$

where $K_{local}$ is the local (microstructural) stress intensity and $K_{th}$ is the threshold stress intensity factor (in **MPa·m$^{1/2}$**). For ductile materials, this is augmented by a strain criterion expressed through the **critical equivalent plastic strain** $\varepsilon_p^{crit}$, while for brittle systems a **critical stress** $\sigma_c$ criterion (e.g., the **Griffith criterion**, $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2E\gamma_s / \pi a}$) is invoked.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Plain-English Analogy — “The Paper Clip That Snaps”**
> Take an ordinary steel paper clip and bend it back and forth slowly. At first, nothing visible happens — the metal *yields* plastically but no crack is apparent. After perhaps 8–10 cycles you may notice, with a hand lens, a faint line on the outer tensile surface. That line is the **initiated microcrack**. It is invisible to the naked eye (typically $a_0 \approx 1$–$10\;\mu m$ in steels), but it marks the moment the damage state has crossed an irreversible threshold. From that instant onward, the wire *knows* it will eventually break — the rest is just propagation.

The analogy maps onto the four canonical sub-processes of crack initiation:

| Real-world observation | Physical counterpart | Length scale |
|---|---|---|
| Slight darkening of outer surface | Persistent slip band (PSB) formation | $0.1\;\mu m$ |
| Faint line under magnification | Micro-void coalescence / intrusions | $1$–$10\;\mu m$ |
| Visible hair-line crack | Through-thickness Stage I crack | $10$–$100\;\mu m$ |
| Sudden snap | Catastrophic Stage II propagation | $\geq 100\;\mu m$ |

> [!TIP] **Why the length scale matters in KTU exams**
> Examiners often award 1–2 marks simply for correctly identifying the **initiation length scale** ($a_0 \sim 1$–$10\;\mu m$) versus the **propagation length scale** ($a \geq 0.1$–$1\;mm$). Memorise the orders of magnitude — they are heavily tested.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used

- **Young's modulus**, $E$ — typically **200 GPa** for steels, **110 GPa** for copper, **70 GPa** for aluminium.
- **Surface energy**, $\gamma_s$ — typically **1–2 J/m$^2$** for metals, **0.5–10 J/m$^2$** for ceramics and polymers.
- **Burgers vector**, $b$ — typically **0.25–0.3 nm** for BCC/FCC metals.
- **Shear modulus**, $G$ — typically **80 GPa** for steels.
- **Threshold stress intensity factor**, $K_{th}$ — typically **2–9 MPa·m$^{1/2}$** for steels.
- **Poisson's ratio**, $\nu$ — typically **0.3** for most engineering metals.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Mechanisms of Crack Initiation

Mechanistically, crack initiation arises from **one (or a combination) of four fundamental processes**:

### A. Slip-Band Cracking (Stage I Fatigue Initiation)

This is the dominant mechanism in ductile metals subjected to cyclic loading. Under cyclic plastic strain, dislocations organise into **persistent slip bands (PSBs)** — thin sheets (typically $\sim 0.1\;\mu m$ thick) that carry most of the plastic strain. The PSB surface emerges as **extrusions** and **intrusions** (the so-called *Wood's mechanism*, 1958), and the intrusions act as natural stress raisers of effective root radius $\rho \approx b$ (one Burgers vector deep).

> [!NOTE] **Wood's Mechanism — Quick Statement**
> Repeated cyclic slip along the same PSB plane produces irreversible surface steps whose height grows with cycle count. An intrusion of depth $\delta_i \approx N \cdot b$ acts as a notch with stress concentration factor $K_t \approx 2\sqrt{c/\rho}$, where $c$ is the intrusion half-length.

### B. Grain-Boundary Cracking

At elevated temperatures, or under creep / stress-corrosion conditions, vacancies and impurities segregate to grain boundaries, weakening the cohesive strength $\sigma_{gb}$. When the local normal stress $\sigma_n$ exceeds the weakened cohesive strength:

$$
\sigma_n \;\geq\; \sigma_{gb}^{cohesive} - \sigma_{dec}
$$

a wedge crack or cavity opens along the boundary. This is the principal mechanism in **welding HAZ cracking**, **creep cavitation**, and **temper embrittlement**.

### C. Inclusion / Second-Phase Particle Debonding

In commercial alloys (especially steels with MnS inclusions, or Al–Si alloys with Si particles), the matrix–particle interface decoheres when the local interfacial stress exceeds the interfacial bond strength. The decohered particle then acts as a pre-existing crack of size $a_0 \approx d_p$ (particle diameter, typically $1$–$50\;\mu m$). This is the **foundation of the Tanaka–Mura model** discussed in §3.

### D. Cleavage Initiation (Brittle Fracture)

In BCC metals at low temperatures and high strain rates, plastic flow is restricted. A slip band impinging on a grain boundary or carbide (e.g., TiC, NbC in steel) generates a stress pile-up. When the maximum local tensile stress $\sigma_{max}$ at the tip of the pile-up exceeds the theoretical cohesive strength:

$$
\sigma_{max} \;\geq\; \sigma_{coh} \;\approx\; \sqrt{\frac{E \gamma_s}{a_0}}
$$

a cleavage microcrack nucleates. This is captured by the **Stroh dislocation pile-up theory** derived in §3.1.

---

## 2.2 Critical Microstructural & Loading Parameters

The initiation event is governed by an *interplay* of the following parameters, all of which are regularly tested:

- **Grain size** $d$ — finer grains raise the Hall–Petch slip length and raise $\sigma_{coh}$, delaying initiation (but improving toughness only up to a point).
- **Inclusion spacing** $\lambda$ — controlled by cleanliness; smaller $\lambda$ ⇒ earlier initiation.
- **Stress state triaxiality** $T = \sigma_h / \sigma_{vm}$ — high $T$ promotes void nucleation (Rice–Tracey model).
- **Strain rate** $\dot{\varepsilon}$ — higher $\dot{\varepsilon}$ suppresses diffusion-controlled damage but promotes adiabatic shear.
- **Temperature** $T$ — influences slip planarity, creep, and environment–material reactions.
- **Environment** — hydrogen, chlorides, and water vapour all drastically lower $K_{th}$ to $K_{ISCC}$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP] **Master this table — it covers $\sim$70% of numerical marks in Module 3.**

| # | Formula / Criterion | Physical Meaning | Typical Use | Key Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_{local} \geq K_{th}$ | Stress-intensity based initiation threshold | Fatigue, SCC | $K$ in **MPa·m$^{1/2}$**, $K_{th} \approx 2$–$9$ for steels |
| 2 | $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2E\gamma_s / (\pi a)}$ | Griffith's critical stress for pre-existing crack | Brittle fracture | $\sigma_c$ in **MPa**, $a$ in **m** |
| 3 | $\sigma_c \approx \sqrt{E\gamma_s / b}$ | Theoretical cohesive strength | Cleavage | $b$ in **m** ($\sim 0.3$ nm) |
| 4 | $\tau_c = \tau_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ | Hall–Petch slip resistance | Grain-boundary strengthening | $d$ in **m**, $k_y$ in **MPa·m$^{1/2}$** |
| 5 | $\sigma_n^{max} = \sigma_0 \sqrt{d/L}$ | Stroh pile-up stress concentration | Slip-band/grain-boundary crack | $d$ grain size, $L$ slip-band length |
| 6 | $N_i = A \cdot (\Delta\varepsilon_p)^{-2}$ | Coffin–Manson initiation life | Low-cycle fatigue | $\Delta\varepsilon_p$ plastic strain range |
| 7 | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N_f)^b$ | Basquin's high-cycle initiation life | HCF regime | $b \approx -0.05$ to $-0.12$ |
| 8 | $\Delta K_{th} = (1-R)\Delta K_{0} + R K_{th,0}$ | Walker-form threshold | Variable-amplitude fatigue | $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$ |
| 9 | $\varepsilon_p^{crit} = \text{const.}$ | Critical local strain | Ductile initiation | Strain at decohesion |
| 10 | $\Delta K_{eff} = K_{max} - K_{op}$ | Effective stress intensity | Crack closure correction | $K_{op}$ opening SIF |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Crack initiation theory is the engineering basis for the following design practices:

1. **Defect-tolerant design** (FAA FAR 25.571, ASME BPVC Sec. XI): components are accepted with known defects provided $K < K_{th}$ over the service lifetime.
2. **Damage-tolerance inspection intervals**: inspection periods are set as fractions of the predicted crack-initiation life $N_i$ (typically $N_i/3$).
3. **Material selection for high-cycle fatigue**: gear steels, bearing steels, and spring steels are selected to maximise $K_{th}$ via cleanliness control (low inclusion content).
4. **Welding procedure qualification**: preheat and post-weld heat treatment are specified to suppress HAZ reheat cracking (a grain-boundary initiation phenomenon).
5. **Surface engineering**: shot peening, nitriding, and laser peening introduce beneficial **compressive residual stresses** that effectively shift $\sigma_{max}$ below zero, preventing initiation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Stroh's Dislocation Pile-Up Theory (Slip-Band → Cleavage Initiation)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why this derivation matters**
> Stroh (1954) provided the first rigorous analytical model for crack nucleation at a grain boundary due to a slip-band pile-up. It is the foundation of the *Zener–Stroh* cleavage criterion and is directly examinable at KTU ESE.

**Step 0 — Physical setup.** Consider $n$ edge dislocations gliding on a slip plane of length $L$ (approximately equal to the grain size $d$) which terminates at a grain boundary. The leading dislocation is held at the boundary; the trailing dislocations pile up behind it under an applied shear stress $\tau$.

**Step 1 — Dislocation density distribution.** For a continuous pile-up, the dislocation density function $f(x)$ satisfies the equilibrium condition:

$$
\frac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)} \int_{0}^{L} \frac{f(x')\,dx'}{x - x'} \;+\; \tau \;=\; 0
$$

This is a singular integral equation whose classical solution is:

$$
f(x) \;=\; \frac{2(1-\nu)\,n\,\tau}{Gb} \,\cdot\, \frac{x}{\sqrt{L^2 - x^2}} \,\cdot\, \frac{1}{\pi}
$$

where $n$ is the number of dislocations in the pile-up, related to the applied stress by:

$$
n \;=\; \frac{\pi L \tau (1-\nu)}{Gb}
$$

**Step 2 — Stress concentration ahead of the pile-up.** The stress field at a point a distance $r$ ahead of the pile-up tip, oriented normal to the slip plane, is:

$$
\sigma_{n}(r) \;=\; \sigma_0 \,\sqrt{\frac{L}{r}}
$$

where $\sigma_0$ is the applied tensile stress resolved onto the grain-boundary plane. Substituting the equilibrium pile-up condition into the elasticity solution gives:

$$
\sigma_{n}^{max} \;=\; \frac{2}{\sqrt{3\pi}} \,\cdot\, \frac{\tau^2 L}{Gb} \,\cdot\, \left(\frac{1+\nu}{1-\nu}\right)^{1/2}
$$

**Step 3 — Cleavage initiation criterion.** A microcrack of grain-boundary length $a_0 \approx b$ nucleates when $\sigma_n^{max}$ equals the theoretical cohesive strength:

$$
\sigma_{coh} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{E \gamma_s}{b}}
$$

Setting $\sigma_n^{max} = \sigma_{coh}$ yields the critical resolved shear stress for initiation:

$$
\tau_c \;=\; \left[ \frac{\sqrt{3\pi}\, G b \, \sigma_{coh}^{1/2}}{2 L^{1/2}} \,\cdot\, \left(\frac{1-\nu}{1+\nu}\right)^{1/4} \right]^{1/2}
$$

$$
\tau_c \;\approx\; \left(\frac{G b \sigma_{coh}}{2 L}\right)^{1/2}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation.** The required shear stress for crack initiation decreases as the slip-band length $L$ (i.e., grain size $d$) increases. Hence *coarse-grained* metals initiate cracks at lower applied stress — a result in agreement with the experimentally observed **lower fracture toughness of coarse-grained steels at low temperature**.

---

## 3.2 Tanaka–Mura Model for Fatigue Crack Initiation

The **Tanaka–Mura model (1981)** treats Stage I fatigue crack initiation as the result of irreversible dislocation dipoles accumulating within a PSB until the stored elastic energy exceeds the surface energy of a nucleating crack.

**Step 1 — Energy of dislocation dipoles.** Consider a pile-up of $N$ dislocation dipoles of Burgers vector $\pm b$ piled up over a length $L$ within a PSB. The total reversible strain energy stored is:

$$
U_{stored} \;=\; \frac{N^2 b^2 \mu L}{4\pi (1-\nu)} \,\cdot\, \ln\!\left(\frac{L}{b}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Energy of the nucleating crack.** A through-thickness crack of length $2a$ requires surface energy:

$$
U_{surface} \;=\; 2\gamma_s \,\cdot\, (2a) \;=\; 4\gamma_s a
$$

Using Griffith's energy-release relation, the equivalent crack length for the PSB pile-up is $a \approx L/2$.

**Step 3 — Initiation criterion.** The crack initiates when the energy released by collapsing the dipoles equals the surface energy required:

$$
\frac{N^2 b^2 \mu}{4\pi (1-\nu)} \ln\!\left(\frac{L}{b}\right) \;\geq\; 4\gamma_s \cdot \frac{L}{2}
$$

Solving for $N$:

$$
N \;\geq\; \sqrt{\frac{8\pi (1-\nu)\gamma_s L}{\mu b^2 \,\ln(L/b)}}
$$

**Step 4 — Cycle count.** Because each cycle contributes a fixed number $n_{dip}$ of dipoles per PSB (typically $n_{dip} \approx 50$–$100$), the initiation life is:

$$
N_i \;=\; \frac{N_{critical}}{n_{dip}}
$$

Substituting typical values for a low-carbon steel ($\mu = 80\;GPa$, $b = 0.25\;nm$, $\gamma_s = 1.5\;J/m^2$, $L = 10\;\mu m$):

$$
N_{critical} \;\approx\; \sqrt{\frac{8\pi (0.7)(1.5)(10^{-5})}{(80\times10^9)(6.25\times10^{-20})(12.0)}}
$$

$$
N_{critical} \;\approx\; \sqrt{\frac{2.64\times10^{-4}}{6.0\times10^{-11}}}
$$

$$
N_{critical} \;\approx\; \sqrt{4.4\times10^{6}} \;\approx\; 2100\;\text{dipoles}
$$

With $n_{dip} \approx 75$ dipoles/cycle, this yields $N_i \approx 28$ cycles — consistent with low-cycle fatigue initiation observations.

---

## 3.3 Cottrell–Petch Criterion (Pile-Up vs Cohesive Strength)

Cottrell and Petch (1958) recast Stroh's result into the more familiar form relating fracture stress to grain size:

$$
\sigma_f \;=\; \sigma_0 \,+\, k_y d^{-1/2}
$$

The fracture stress is therefore **highest** for fine-grained materials — the empirical basis for tempering martensitic steels to fine grain size for high toughness.

---

## 3.4 Initiation under Mixed Loading — R-Ratio Correction (Walker)

For variable-amplitude or non-zero-mean fatigue, the threshold $\Delta K_{th}$ depends on the load ratio $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$. The **Walker correction** is:

$$
\Delta K_{th}(R) \;=\; \frac{\Delta K_{th}(R=0)}{(1-R)^{\gamma}}
$$

where $\gamma$ is the Walker exponent (typically $\gamma = 0.4$–$0.7$ for steels). For $R > 0$, the threshold drops — i.e., mean tensile stress accelerates initiation. This is why *shot peening* (compressive residual stress, effectively $R < 0$ at the surface) is so effective.

---

## 3.5 Initiation Map — Decision Matrix

The following matrix shows which initiation criterion dominates in which engineering scenario. This is a high-yield KTU question.

| Material class | Loading | Temperature | Dominant mechanism | Governing criterion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ductile steel | Cyclic, HCF | Ambient | PSB intrusion/extrusion | $\Delta K \geq \Delta K_{th}$ |
| Ductile steel | Cyclic, LCF | Ambient | PSB + inclusion decohesion | $\Delta\varepsilon_p$ (Coffin–Manson) |
| Mild steel | Monotonic | Low ($< -50^\circ C$) | Cleavage at carbide | Stroh pile-up |
| Austenitic SS | Static + Cl$^-$ | 60–$200^\circ C$ | SCC at slip-step | $K \geq K_{ISCC}$ |
| Turbine blade | Static, high T | 0.6 $T_m$ | Creep cavity at GB | $\varepsilon_{creep} \geq \varepsilon_{cav}$ |
| Pressurised vessel | Static + H$_2$ | Ambient | Hydrogen decohesion | $K \geq K_{IH}$ |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define crack initiation. How does it differ from crack propagation in terms of length scale and energetics?**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Crack initiation is the process by which a sub-critical microcrack, void, or decohesion event of size $a_0 \approx 1$–$10\;\mu m$ is nucleated within an initially defect-free material volume under the combined action of stress, microstructure, and environment.
- **Length scale (1 mark):** Initiation operates at the **microstructural** scale (slip-band, grain-boundary, inclusion), whereas propagation operates at the **continuum** scale (mm and above), governed by macroscopic $K$ and Paris law.
- **Energetics (1 mark):** Initiation requires overcoming the local *cohesive* energy barrier ($\gamma_s$ or $\sigma_{coh}$); propagation requires the *driving* energy release rate $G$ to exceed $2\gamma_s$ (or $G_c$ in elastic–plastic fracture).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List four distinct mechanisms by which cracks initiate in engineering materials.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):** *(Any four with one-line description — 0.75 mark each)*

1. **Slip-band cracking** (Stage I fatigue): persistent slip bands extrude and intrude the surface, forming notches that nucleate Stage I cracks.
2. **Grain-boundary decohesion**: at high temperature or in embrittled states, segregation weakens GB cohesion and a wedge crack opens under normal stress.
3. **Inclusion/matrix debonding**: brittle second-phase particles (e.g., MnS in steel) decohere from the matrix, acting as pre-existing cracks of size $a_0 \approx d_p$.
4. **Cleavage at slip-band pile-up**: Stroh–Zener mechanism — a slip band impinging on a grain boundary produces a stress concentration sufficient to cleave the boundary.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive Stroh's expression for the critical resolved shear stress required to nucleate a cleavage microcrack at a grain boundary due to a dislocation pile-up. Discuss the dependence of the initiation stress on grain size and explain the physical significance of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A low-carbon BCC steel has the following properties: shear modulus $G = 80\;GPa$, Burgers vector $b = 0.28\;nm$, Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.3$, theoretical cohesive strength $\sigma_{coh} = 12\;GPa$, and grain size $d = 50\;\mu m$. Determine (i) the critical resolved shear stress for crack initiation, and (ii) the number of dislocations in the critical pile-up. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

**Step 1 — Pile-up geometry.** Let $n$ edge dislocations pile up on a slip plane of length $L \approx d$ (the grain size). The leading dislocation is held by the grain boundary.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium of the pile-up.** The number of dislocations in the pile-up under applied shear stress $\tau$ is:

$$
n \;=\; \frac{\pi L \tau (1-\nu)}{Gb}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum normal stress at the pile-up tip.** Stroh's elasticity solution gives the tensile stress normal to the slip plane at a distance $r$ ahead of the tip:

$$
\sigma_n(r) \;=\; \frac{2}{\sqrt{3\pi}} \,\cdot\, \frac{\tau^2 L}{Gb} \,\cdot\, \left(\frac{1+\nu}{1-\nu}\right)^{1/2} \,\cdot\, \sqrt{\frac{d}{r}}
$$

**Step 4 — Initiation criterion.** A microcrack of length $a_0 \approx b$ nucleates when $\sigma_n(r = b)$ reaches the cohesive strength $\sigma_{coh}$:

$$
\sigma_{coh} \;=\; \frac{2}{\sqrt{3\pi}} \,\cdot\, \frac{\tau_c^{\,2} L}{Gb} \,\cdot\, \left(\frac{1+\nu}{1-\nu}\right)^{1/2} \,\cdot\, \sqrt{\frac{d}{b}}
$$

**Step 5 — Solving for the critical shear stress:**

$$
\tau_c \;=\; \left[ \frac{\sqrt{3\pi}\, Gb \,\sigma_{coh}}{2\sqrt{d/b}\,\left(\dfrac{1+\nu}{1-\nu}\right)^{1/2} \,\cdot\, L} \right]^{1/2}
$$

Substituting $L = d$ and simplifying:

$$
\tau_c \;\approx\; \left[ \frac{\sqrt{3\pi}\, Gb \,\sigma_{coh}}{2 \sqrt{d/b}} \right]^{1/2}
$$

**Step 6 — Grain-size dependence.** Inspection of the boxed expression reveals:

$$
\tau_c \;\propto\; d^{-1/4}
$$

i.e., the critical shear stress for initiation **decreases with increasing grain size** — coarser grains nucleate cracks at lower applied stresses. **(3 marks for derivation + 2 marks for physical interpretation + 2 marks for grain-size discussion.)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**(i) Critical resolved shear stress (4 marks):**

Given: $G = 80 \times 10^9\;Pa$, $b = 0.28 \times 10^{-9}\;m$, $\sigma_{coh} = 12 \times 10^9\;Pa$, $d = 50 \times 10^{-6}\;m$, $\nu = 0.3$.

Compute the pile-up geometric factor:

$$
\sqrt{\frac{d}{b}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{50\times10^{-6}}{0.28\times10^{-9}}} \;=\; \sqrt{1.786\times10^{5}} \;=\; 422.6
$$

Poisson correction factor:

$$
\left(\frac{1+\nu}{1-\nu}\right)^{1/2} \;=\; \left(\frac{1.3}{0.7}\right)^{1/2} \;=\; (1.857)^{1/2} \;=\; 1.363
$$

Stroh's constant $\sqrt{3\pi} \approx 3.07$.

Substituting:

$$
\tau_c \;=\; \left[ \frac{3.07 \times (80\times10^9)(0.28\times10^{-9})(12\times10^9)}{2 \times 422.6 \times 1.363 \times (50\times10^{-6})} \right]^{1/2}
$$

Numerator:

$$
3.07 \times 80\times10^9 \times 0.28\times10^{-9} \times 12\times10^9 \;=\; 8.255 \times 10^{11}
$$

Denominator:

$$
2 \times 422.6 \times 1.363 \times 50\times10^{-6} \;=\; 0.0576
$$

Quotient:

$$
\frac{8.255\times10^{11}}{0.0576} \;=\; 1.433 \times 10^{13}
$$

Square root:

$$
\boxed{\tau_c \;\approx\; 3.79 \times 10^{6}\;Pa \;\approx\; 3.8\;MPa}
$$

**(ii) Number of dislocations in the pile-up (3 marks):**

$$
n \;=\; \frac{\pi L \tau_c (1-\nu)}{Gb} \;=\; \frac{\pi (50\times10^{-6})(3.79\times10^{6})(0.7)}{(80\times10^9)(0.28\times10^{-9})}
$$

$$
n \;=\; \frac{0.4167}{22.4} \;\approx\; 18.6
$$

$$
\boxed{n \;\approx\; 19\;\text{dislocations}}
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Tanaka–Mura dislocation-dipole model** for Stage I fatigue crack initiation. Clearly state the energy criterion and the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A copper alloy has $\mu = 48\;GPa$, $b = 0.26\;nm$, $\nu = 0.34$, $\gamma_s = 1.2\;J/m^2$, PSB length $L = 8\;\mu m$, and dipole generation rate $n_{dip} = 60$ dipoles per cycle. Estimate the number of cycles to crack initiation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Stage I fatigue initiation occurs within a PSB treated as a *slip band* of length $L$.
2. Dislocation dipoles of opposite sign $\pm b$ accumulate irreversibly along the PSB; their density grows linearly with cycle count.
3. The dipole array is in static mechanical equilibrium within the PSB.
4. Crack nucleation is energy-driven: when the stored elastic energy of the dipole pile-up equals the surface energy of a crack of length $a \approx L/2$, initiation occurs.

**Energy of dislocation dipoles (2 marks):**

For $N$ dipoles of Burgers vector $\pm b$ piled up over length $L$:

$$
U_{stored} \;=\; \frac{N^2 b^2 \mu}{4\pi (1-\nu)} \,\ln\!\left(\frac{L}{b}\right)
$$

**Surface energy of nucleating crack (1 mark):**

For a crack of length $2a = L$ (i.e., $a = L/2$):

$$
U_{surface} \;=\; 4\gamma_s \cdot \frac{L}{2} \;=\; 2\gamma_s L
$$

**Initiation criterion (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{N^2 b^2 \mu}{4\pi (1-\nu)} \ln\!\left(\frac{L}{b}\right) \;\geq\; 2\gamma_s L
$$

Solving:

$$
N_c \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{8\pi (1-\nu)\gamma_s L}{\mu b^2 \,\ln(L/b)}}
$$

This is the **minimum number of dipoles** that must accumulate before a crack of PSB length nucleates. It is independent of applied stress amplitude to first order — only the rate of dipole accumulation is stress-dependent (which feeds back through the cycle count).

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

Given: $\mu = 48\times10^9\;Pa$, $b = 0.26\times10^{-9}\;m$, $\nu = 0.34$, $\gamma_s = 1.2\;J/m^2$, $L = 8\times10^{-6}\;m$, $n_{dip} = 60$/cycle.

**Step 1 — Compute $\ln(L/b)$:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{L}{b}\right) \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{8\times10^{-6}}{0.26\times10^{-9}}\right) \;=\; \ln(3.077\times10^{4}) \;\approx\; 10.33
$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**

$$
8\pi (1-\nu)\gamma_s L \;=\; 8\pi (0.66)(1.2)(8\times10^{-6}) \;=\; 1.592\times10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3 — Denominator:**

$$
\mu b^2 \ln(L/b) \;=\; (48\times10^9)(0.26\times10^{-9})^2 (10.33) \;=\; (48\times10^9)(6.76\times10^{-20})(10.33)
$$

$$
= \; 3.353 \times 10^{-8}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $N_c$:**

$$
N_c \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1.592\times10^{-4}}{3.353\times10^{-8}}} \;=\; \sqrt{4750} \;\approx\; 68.9
$$

Rounding up to the nearest integer (since it is a count):

$$
N_c \;\approx\; 69\;\text{dipoles}
$$

**Step 5 — Cycle count:**

$$
N_i \;=\; \frac{N_c}{n_{dip}} \;=\; \frac{69}{60} \;\approx\; 1.15
$$

$$
\boxed{N_i \;\approx\; 1\;\text{cycle (initiation essentially immediate)}}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation.** With the assumed dipole generation rate, a crack initiates within the very first cycle — consistent with a **very low-cycle fatigue** scenario at high plastic strain amplitude. For a real copper component at lower $\Delta\varepsilon_p$, $n_{dip}$ would be smaller and $N_i$ correspondingly larger.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Marks-Loss Traps in Crack Initiation Problems**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $\mu m$ to $m$ in the $L/b$ ratio.** A factor-of-$10^6$ error here silently corrupts the answer by 12 orders of magnitude. Always state unit conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing $\gamma_s$ (surface energy per unit area) with $\gamma_s$ (surface energy per unit length).** Use the **per-area** form $J/m^2$.
> 3. **Writing $K_{th}$ without specifying whether it is $\Delta K_{th}$ (cyclic) or $K_{ISCC}$ (SCC) or $K_{IH}$ (hydrogen).** Examiners expect the *qualifier*.
> 4. **Treating $a_0$ as a constant.** It is *microstructure-dependent*: typically $a_0 \approx b$ for cleavage, $a_0 \approx d_p$ for inclusion decohesion, $a_0 \approx L_{PSB}$ for fatigue.
> 5. **Omitting the $K_t$ stress-concentration factor** when describing Wood's mechanism. The intrusion is a notch, not a free surface — its stress-raising effect is what initiates the crack.
> 6. **Mixing up Paris law (propagation) with Coffin–Manson / Basquin (initiation).** KTU questions are very specific about which regime applies. Always state which.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Crack Initiation**

- **Definition:** Crack initiation = nucleation of a micro-defect of size $a_0 \sim 1$–$10\;\mu m$ that satisfies $K_{local} \geq K_{th}$ or $\sigma_{local} \geq \sigma_{coh}$.
- **Four canonical mechanisms:** Slip-band cracking, grain-boundary decohesion, inclusion-matrix debonding, cleavage at pile-up.
- **Wood's mechanism (1958):** Cyclic slip $\rightarrow$ extrusion & intrusion $\rightarrow$ notch $\rightarrow$ Stage I crack.
- **Stroh's pile-up theory:** $\sigma_n^{max} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3\pi}}\dfrac{\tau^2 L}{Gb}\left(\dfrac{1+\nu}{1-\nu}\right)^{1/2}\sqrt{d/r}$; sets $\sigma_n^{max} = \sigma_{coh}$ for initiation.
- **Grain-size effect (Cottrell–Petch):** $\sigma_f \propto d^{-1/2}$ for cleavage; finer grain $\Rightarrow$ higher initiation stress.
- **Tanaka–Mura fatigue model:** initiation when dipole energy $\geq$ surface energy; $N_c = \sqrt{8\pi(1-\nu)\gamma_s L / (\mu b^2 \ln(L/b))}$.
- **Length scales:** Initiation $\sim \mu m$; propagation $\sim$ mm and above.
- **Hall–Petch baseline:** $\tau_c = \tau_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ — slip resistance rises with finer grain.
- **Threshold K:** $K_{th}$ (fatigue, 2–9 MPa·m$^{1/2}$), $K_{ISCC}$ (SCC), $K_{IH}$ (hydrogen).
- **R-ratio correction:** Walker $\Delta K_{th}(R) = \Delta K_{th}(0)/(1-R)^\gamma$; $R > 0$ lowers threshold.
- **Coffin–Manson (LCF):** $N_i = A(\Delta\varepsilon_p)^{-2}$.
- **Basquin (HCF):** $\sigma_a = \sigma_f'(2N_f)^b$, with $b \approx -0.05$ to $-0.12$.
- **Surface engineering:** shot peening introduces compressive $\sigma_R$ $\Rightarrow$ effective $R < 0$ $\Rightarrow$ delay / suppression of initiation.
- **Constant values to memorise:** $G \approx 80$ GPa (steel), $b \approx 0.25$–$0.3$ nm, $\nu \approx 0.3$, $\gamma_s \approx 1$–$2$ J/m$^2$.
- **Engineering applications:** damage-tolerant design (FAA, ASME), defect-tolerant inspection intervals, welding preheat to prevent HAZ reheat cracking.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** mixing initiation (Stroh, Tanaka–Mura, Wood) with propagation (Paris, LEFM $K_{IC}$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crack Propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Crack Propagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Crack Propagation** is the progressive extension of an existing crack or flaw within a structural component under the influence of applied (or residual) stress, environmental conditions, and material microstructure. In the context of Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM), crack propagation is governed by the **Stress Intensity Factor (K)**, which characterizes the magnitude of the stress field at the crack tip. The driving force for crack extension is mathematically expressed through relationships such as **Paris Law**, which quantifies the subcritical fatigue crack growth rate per loading cycle as a function of the cyclic stress intensity range.

The three fundamental modes of crack-tip displacement are:
- **Mode I (Opening Mode):** Tensile stress acts normal to the crack plane; crack faces separate symmetrically.
- **Mode II (Sliding/Shearing Mode):** In-plane shear stress causes the crack faces to slide over each other.
- **Mode III (Tearing/Anti-Plane Shear Mode):** Out-of-plane shear stress produces a tearing action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Mode I is the most commonly encountered and the most critical mode in design against fracture, as it represents the dominant driving force for unstable crack propagation in ductile and brittle materials alike.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Paper-Tear Analogy:** Imagine you have a sheet of paper with a small nick (the initial crack) at its edge. If you gently pull the paper apart (Mode I), the nick slowly grows. The harder you pull, the faster it grows. If you instead rub the paper edges sideways back and forth (fatigue), the nick grows a tiny bit with each back-and-forth motion — even if the pull is small. The "speed" of this growth is exactly what crack propagation describes.
>
> The **Stress Intensity Factor (K)** is like a "magnifying glass" that tells you how concentrated the stress is right at the tip of that nick. A small nick (short crack) under high stress can be just as dangerous as a large nick under moderate stress — because K depends on **both** stress **and** crack length.

**Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in this Module:**
- **Stress Intensity Factor Units:** MPa√m (megapascal root meter)
- **Crack Growth Rate (da/dN):** mm/cycle or m/cycle
- **Threshold Stress Intensity Range (ΔK_th):** typically **0.5 – 10 MPa√m** for metals
- **Fracture Toughness (K_c or K_IC):** typically **20 – 200 MPa√m** for engineering alloys
- **Paris Law Exponent (m):** typically **2 – 7** for metals in Region II (stable crack growth)

## 1.3 The Crack Tip Stress Field

For a sharp crack in an isotropic linear elastic material, the stress field in the immediate vicinity of the crack tip in polar coordinates (r, θ) is given by the **Williams' asymptotic solution**:

$$
\sigma_{ij} = \frac{K}{\sqrt{2\pi r}} \cdot f_{ij}(\theta) + \text{higher order terms}
$$

The factor $1/\sqrt{r}$ produces a **square-root singularity** — meaning stresses become theoretically infinite at r = 0. This singularity is the mathematical justification for using K as a single parameter to characterize crack-tip severity, provided the **small-scale yielding (SSY)** condition holds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** Since stresses diverge at the crack tip, conventional stress-based failure criteria (yield, ultimate) are insufficient. We must use a **fracture-mechanics-based criterion** that accounts for the existing flaw size.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Stages of Fatigue Crack Growth

When a cyclically loaded component contains an existing flaw, the crack growth behavior is divided into **three distinct regions** when plotted on a log-log graph of **crack growth rate (da/dN)** vs. **stress intensity factor range (ΔK)**:

| Region | Name | Behavior | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Region I** | Near-Threshold | da/dN → 0 as ΔK → ΔK_th | Crack grows by shear-sliding along crystallographic planes (Stage I, faceted growth) |
| **Region II** | Paris Regime | Linear log-log relation: da/dN = C(ΔK)^m | Stable, striation-forming transgranular propagation |
| **Region III** | Unstable Growth | Rapid acceleration; da/dN → ∞ as K_max → K_c | Near-critical fracture, ductile/tearing mechanism |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners frequently test the student's ability to identify which region a given ΔK value falls into, and to compute cycle life by integrating Paris Law over the corresponding bounds.

## 2.2 The Paris Law (Core Governing Equation)

**Paul C. Paris** (1961) proposed the empirical relation that defines fatigue crack growth in **Region II**:

$$
\frac{da}{dN} = C \cdot (\Delta K)^m
$$

Where:
- **a** = crack length (m or mm)
