- **N** = number of loading cycles (dimensionless)
- **da/dN** = crack growth rate per cycle (m/cycle)
- **ΔK** = cyclic stress intensity factor range = K_max − K_min (MPa√m)
- **C** = Paris constant (material/environment dependent)
- **m** = Paris exponent (typically 2–7 for metals)

### Derivation of ΔK

For a through-thickness center crack of length 2a in an infinite plate under remote uniform stress σ:

$$
K_I = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}
$$

Under cyclic loading with σ_max and σ_min:

$$
\Delta K = K_{max} - K_{min} = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}) \sqrt{\pi a} = \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a}
$$

For a finite-width plate or other geometry, a **geometry correction factor Y(a/W)** is introduced:

$$
\Delta K = Y(a) \cdot \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ΔK and not K_max?** The **stress range** drives the *incremental* crack advance per cycle. K_max controls *final instability*, but ΔK controls the *rate*. This is a classic KTU Part A question.

## 2.3 Effect of Stress Ratio (R)

The **stress ratio R = σ_min / σ_max** significantly affects crack growth. Higher R (more tensile mean stress) → faster crack growth at a given ΔK. Several modified Paris formulations account for R:

**Forman Equation (Region II + III):**

$$
\frac{da}{dN} = \frac{C(\Delta K)^m}{(1-R)K_c - \Delta K}
$$

**Walker Equation:**

$$
\frac{da}{dN} = C \left[ \frac{\Delta K}{(1-R)^{1-\gamma}} \right]^m
$$

**Nasato Equation:** Generalization covering all three regions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often plug K_max into Paris Law instead of ΔK. Always use ΔK in the basic Paris relation unless the modified (Forman/Walker) form is explicitly given.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| K_I | Mode I stress intensity factor | MPa√m | Opening mode |
| K_II | Mode II stress intensity factor | MPa√m | In-plane shear |
| K_III | Mode III stress intensity factor | MPa√m | Out-of-plane shear |
| K_IC | Plane-strain fracture toughness | MPa√m | Material property at critical |
| \Delta K | Stress intensity range (K_max − K_min) | MPa√m | Drives Paris growth |
| \Delta K_{th} | Threshold \Delta K below which crack does not grow | MPa√m | 1–10 for metals |
| Y(a/W) | Geometry correction factor | dimensionless | Tabulated in textbooks |
| C, m | Paris constants | C in (m/cycle)/(MPa√m)^m; m dimensionless | Empirically determined |
| da/dN | Crack growth rate per cycle | m/cycle or mm/cycle | Slope of a vs N curve |
| R | Stress ratio = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max} | dimensionless | −1 ≤ R ≤ 1 typically |
| \sigma_f | Fatigue limit (endurance limit) | MPa | Distinct from \Delta K_{th} |
| a_i | Initial crack length | m or mm | NDT-measured flaw size |
| a_f | Final (critical) crack length | m or mm | Where K_max = K_c |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Hallucination Reminder:** Per the KTU 2024 syllabus and standard fracture-mechanics texts, the form of Paris Law and ΔK expressions above are mathematically verifiable and reproducible. Geometry factors Y(a/W) for specific configurations (CT, SENB, CCT) must be cited from a standard reference when used in numerical problems.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Crack propagation analysis is essential for:
- **Aerospace:** Damage-tolerant design of airframes (per FAA/JAR/FAR 25.571). Inspection intervals are scheduled based on predicted crack growth between inspections.
- **Pressure vessels & piping:** ASME Section XI (nuclear) and Section VIII (unfired pressure vessels) use flaw-evaluation procedures rooted in Paris Law.
- **Bridges & civil infrastructure:** Fatigue assessment of welded details, riveted joints, and orthotropic decks.
- **Automotive:** Drive shafts, suspension components, and engine components.
- **Offshore & marine:** Subsea pipelines, ship hulls, risers under wave loading.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cycles to Failure from Paris Law

**Problem Setup:** Given initial crack length $a_i$ and final (critical) crack length $a_f$, find the number of cycles $N_f$ required to propagate the crack from $a_i$ to $a_f$ under constant amplitude loading.

**Step 1 — Start with the Paris Law:**

$$
\frac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the general expression for ΔK:**

$$
\frac{da}{dN} = C \left[ Y(a) \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a} \right]^m
$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate:**

$$
dN = \frac{da}{C \left[ Y(a) \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a} \right]^m}
$$

$$
N_f = \int_{a_i}^{a_f} \frac{da}{C \cdot Y^m(a) \cdot \Delta\sigma^m \cdot \pi^{m/2} \cdot a^{m/2}}
$$

**Step 4 — For the simplest case (Y ≈ constant, e.g., Y = 1.0 for a small edge crack in a wide plate):**

$$
N_f = \int_{a_i}^{a_f} \frac{da}{C \cdot \Delta\sigma^m \cdot \pi^{m/2} \cdot a^{m/2}}
$$

$$
N_f = \frac{1}{C \cdot \Delta\sigma^m \cdot \pi^{m/2}} \int_{a_i}^{a_f} a^{-m/2} \, da
$$

**Step 5 — Perform the integration (assuming m ≠ 2):**

$$
N_f = \frac{1}{C \cdot \Delta\sigma^m \cdot \pi^{m/2}} \cdot \left[ \frac{a^{1 - m/2}}{1 - m/2} \right]_{a_i}^{a_f}
$$

$$
N_f = \frac{a_f^{\,1 - m/2} - a_i^{\,1 - m/2}}{C \cdot \Delta\sigma^m \cdot \pi^{m/2} \cdot (1 - m/2)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** This closed-form solution is the foundation of all "inspection-interval" calculations used in damage-tolerant design. The denominator term $(1 - m/2)$ is **negative** for typical $m \in [2, 7]$, which simply means we evaluate $|a_f^{\,1 - m/2} - a_i^{\,1 - m/2}|$ to keep $N_f$ positive.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Crack Length

**Problem Setup:** Given a material with fracture toughness $K_{IC}$ and an applied maximum stress $\sigma_{max}$, find the critical crack length $a_c$ at which catastrophic fracture occurs.

**Step 1 — Apply the failure criterion:** Failure occurs when $K_{max}$ reaches $K_{IC}$:

$$
K_{max} = Y \cdot \sigma_{max} \cdot \sqrt{\pi a_c} = K_{IC}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $a_c$:**

$$
\sqrt{\pi a_c} = \frac{K_{IC}}{Y \cdot \sigma_{max}}
$$

$$
\pi a_c = \left( \frac{K_{IC}}{Y \cdot \sigma_{max}} \right)^2
$$

$$
a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{K_{IC}}{Y \cdot \sigma_{max}} \right)^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** $a_c$ scales with the **square** of the ratio of toughness to stress. Doubling the toughness quadruples the tolerable crack size, while halving the stress also quadruples it. This is why material selection (high $K_{IC}$) and stress reduction are equally powerful design strategies.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A steel component ($K_{IC} = 55$ MPa√m, Paris constants $C = 6.9 \times 10^{-12}$ m/cycle (MPa√m)$^m$, $m = 3.0$, $Y = 1.0$) is subjected to a cyclic stress with $\sigma_{max} = 250$ MPa and $\sigma_{min} = 25$ MPa. The initial crack length detected by NDT is $a_i = 2$ mm. Determine:
- (a) The critical crack length $a_c$
- (b) The number of cycles to failure $N_f$

**Solution (a) — Critical Crack Length:**

$$
a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{K_{IC}}{Y \cdot \sigma_{max}} \right)^2
$$

$$
a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{55}{1.0 \times 250} \right)^2
$$

$$
a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \times (0.22)^2 = \frac{0.0484}{\pi} = 0.0154 \text{ m} = 15.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Solution (b) — Cycles to Failure:**

First, compute $\Delta\sigma$:

$$
\Delta\sigma = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min} = 250 - 25 = 225 \text{ MPa}
$$

Apply the integrated Paris Law formula:

$$
N_f = \frac{a_f^{\,1 - m/2} - a_i^{\,1 - m/2}}{C \cdot \Delta\sigma^m \cdot \pi^{m/2} \cdot (1 - m/2)}
$$

With $m = 3.0$, $1 - m/2 = 1 - 1.5 = -0.5$:

$$
N_f = \frac{a_f^{-0.5} - a_i^{-0.5}}{C \cdot \Delta\sigma^3 \cdot \pi^{1.5} \cdot (-0.5)}
$$

Convert crack lengths to meters: $a_i = 0.002$ m, $a_f = 0.0154$ m.

Compute numerator:

$$
a_f^{-0.5} = (0.0154)^{-0.5} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.0154}} = 8.06
$$

$$
a_i^{-0.5} = (0.002)^{-0.5} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.002}} = 22.36
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 8.06 - 22.36 = -14.30
$$

Compute denominator:

$$
C = 6.9 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m/cycle}
$$

$$
\Delta\sigma^3 = (225)^3 = 1.139 \times 10^7
$$

$$
\pi^{1.5} = \pi \cdot \sqrt{\pi} = 3.1416 \times 1.7725 = 5.568
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 6.9 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.139 \times 10^7 \times 5.568 \times (-0.5)
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 6.9 \times 10^{-12} \times 6.342 \times 10^7 \times (-0.5)
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 4.376 \times 10^{-4} \times (-0.5) = -2.188 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Final result:

$$
N_f = \frac{-14.30}{-2.188 \times 10^{-4}} = 65,357 \text{ cycles}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The component can withstand approximately **65,000 load cycles** before catastrophic fracture, given a 2 mm initial flaw. Inspection/maintenance schedules should target intervals well below this number — typically 25–33% of $N_f$ for safety.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Loading Conditions vs. Crack Propagation Laws

| Loading Type | Governing Equation | Region of Validity | Key Assumption |
|---|---|---|---|
| Static (monotonic) | $K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$; failure at $K_{IC}$ | Single-cycle fracture | LEFM, small-scale yielding |
| Constant-amplitude fatigue (CFA) | $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$ | Region II (Paris) | $R$ effect ignored or folded into C, m |
| Variable-amplitude fatigue (VAF) | Use $\Delta K_{eff} = \Delta K \cdot (1-R)^{0.5}$ in Paris | Region II | Linear-elastic superposition |
| Stress corrosion cracking (SCC) | $da/dt = C_{SCC}(K_I)^n$ | Subcritical, sustained load | Environment-specific |
| R-ratio effect (Forman) | $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m/[(1-R)K_c - \Delta K]$ | Region II + III | Captures K_c-instability |
| Near-threshold (Region I) | $da/dN = A\left[(\Delta K - \Delta K_{th})/(K_{max} - \Delta K)\right]^p$ | Below Paris range | Microstructurally sensitive |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three Modes of Crack-Tip Displacement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Loading] --> B{Mode Type}
    B -- "Tensile \n (perpendicular to crack)" --> C[Mode I Opening]
    B -- "In-plane shear \n (parallel to crack tip)" --> D[Mode II Sliding]
    B -- "Out-of-plane shear \n (tearing)" --> E[Mode III Tearing]
    C --> F[K_I governs]
    D --> G[K_II governs]
    E --> H[K_III governs]
    F --> I[Most critical in design]
    G --> J[Often secondary]
    H --> K[Rare but possible]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** This flowchart represents the block-level functional architecture of crack-mode classification. In actual practice, a real schematic would show physical arrows indicating the direction of crack-face displacement relative to the crack front.

## 4.2 Stress Intensity Field Around a Crack Tip

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Remote Applied Stress sigma] --> B[Stress Concentration at Crack Tip]
    B --> C["Stress Field: sigma_ij = K/sqrt(2*pi*r) * f(theta)"]
    C --> D{Loading Modality}
    D -- "Cyclic \n (sigma_max, sigma_min)" --> E["Delta K = K_max - K_min \n Drives Paris Growth"]
    D -- "Static monotonic" --> F["K_max compared to K_IC \n Drives Brittle Failure"]
    D -- "Sustained load + environment" --> G["K_I drives SCC \n da/dt = C * K_I^n"]
    E --> H[Region I, II, III Analysis]
    F --> I[Single-cycle fracture check]
    G --> J[Time-to-failure prediction]
```

## 4.3 Fatigue Crack Growth Curve (da/dN vs. ΔK)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Delta K increases \n (crack grows)"] --> B[Region I: Near-Threshold]
    B --> C[Region II: Paris Law Linear Region]
    C --> D[Region III: Unstable Tearing]
    D --> E[K_max reaches K_c \n Fast Fracture]
    B --- B1["da/dN approaches 0 \n as Delta K -> Delta K_th"]
    C --- C1["da/dN = C * Delta K^m \n Stable Striation Growth"]
    D --- D1["da/dN accelerates rapidly \n Approach to Criticality"]
```

## 4.4 Damage-Tolerant Design Flow (Inspection Interval Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Detect Initial Flaw a_i via NDT] --> B[Compute Critical Crack Length a_c]
    B --> C[Apply Paris Law Integration]
    C --> D[Compute Total Cycles to Failure N_f]
    D --> E[Set Inspection Interval = N_f / Safety Factor]
    E --> F[Schedule NDT Inspection]
    F --> G{Flaw Detected?}
    G -- "Yes" --> H[Repair or Replace Component]
    G -- "No" --> I[Continue Service]
    I --> J[Next Inspection Cycle]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Parameters Influencing Crack Propagation Rate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crack Growth Rate da/dN] --> B[Mechanical Driving Forces]
    A --> C[Material Resistance]
    A --> D[Environmental Factors]
    B --> B1["Delta K = Y * Delta sigma * sqrt(pi*a)"]
    B --> B2[Stress Ratio R]
    B --> B3[Loading Frequency]
    C --> C1[Paris Constants C and m]
    C --> C2[Microstructure Grain Size]
    C --> C3[Residual Stresses]
    D --> D1[Corrosive Medium]
    D --> D2[Temperature]
    D --> D3[Humidity]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Stress Intensity Factor and explain Mode I, Mode II, and Mode III.

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Similar Pattern)]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Stress Intensity Factor (K)** is a fracture-mechanics parameter that quantifies the magnitude of the stress field at the tip of a sharp crack in a linear elastic material. It is defined for a Mode I crack of length $a$ in an infinite plate under uniform remote stress $\sigma$ as:

$$
K_I = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}
$$

The three modes of crack-tip displacement are:
- **Mode I (Opening):** Tensile stress normal to the crack plane; the crack faces move apart symmetrically. Governed by $K_I$.
- **Mode II (Sliding/In-plane shear):** Shear stress acts parallel to the crack plane and perpendicular to the crack front. Governed by $K_{II}$.
- **Mode III (Tearing/Anti-plane shear):** Shear stress acts parallel to the crack front. Governed by $K_{III}$.

Mode I is the most critical in engineering design because it is the most commonly encountered and produces the highest crack-driving force.

### Question 2: State the Paris Law and explain the significance of the exponent m.

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 (Similar Pattern)]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Paris Law** describes the stable fatigue crack growth rate in Region II as:

$$
\frac{da}{dN} = C \cdot (\Delta K)^m
$$

where $C$ and $m$ are material constants determined experimentally, and $\Delta K$ is the cyclic stress intensity factor range.

The **exponent m** represents the sensitivity of crack growth rate to changes in $\Delta K$. A higher m indicates greater sensitivity to stress range variations. Typical values are:
- **m ≈ 2–4** for ductile metals (e.g., aluminum alloys, mild steel)
- **m ≈ 4–7** for high-strength steels and brittle materials

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students often write Paris Law as $da/dN = C \cdot (K_{max})^m$. This is **incorrect**. The correct argument is $\Delta K$ (the range), not $K_{max}$ or $K_{min}$, because fatigue damage per cycle depends on the cyclic swing, not the absolute peak.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Adapted)]**

(a) **Derive an expression for the number of cycles to failure $N_f$ for a center-cracked plate subjected to constant-amplitude fatigue loading, using Paris Law. State all assumptions clearly.** (7 Marks)

(b) **A structural steel component has the following properties: $K_{IC} = 80$ MPa√m, $C = 1.5 \times 10^{-11}$ m/cycle (MPa√m)$^m$, $m = 3.5$, geometry factor $Y = 1.1$. The component is subjected to cyclic loading with $\sigma_{max} = 300$ MPa and $\sigma_{min} = 0$ MPa. An initial surface crack of length 3 mm is detected. Calculate the safe inspection interval in cycles, using a safety factor of 3 on the calculated fatigue life.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Linear elastic fracture mechanics (LEFM) is valid (small-scale yielding).
2. Geometry factor Y is constant throughout crack growth.
3. Far-field stress is uniform and uniaxial.
4. Crack remains sharp; no crack closure effects considered.
5. Constant-amplitude loading (R constant).

**Derivation:**

Starting with Paris Law:

$$
\frac{da}{dN} = C \cdot (\Delta K)^m
$$

Substituting $\Delta K = Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a}$:

$$
\frac{da}{dN} = C \cdot \left[ Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a} \right]^m = C \cdot Y^m \cdot \Delta\sigma^m \cdot \pi^{m/2} \cdot a^{m/2}
$$

Separating variables:

$$
dN = \frac{da}{C \cdot Y^m \cdot \Delta\sigma^m \cdot \pi^{m/2} \cdot a^{m/2}}
$$

Integrating from $a_i$ to $a_f$:

$$
N_f = \int_{a_i}^{a_f} \frac{da}{C \cdot Y^m \cdot \Delta\sigma^m \cdot \pi^{m/2} \cdot a^{m/2}}
$$

$$
N_f = \frac{1}{C \cdot Y^m \cdot \Delta\sigma^m \cdot \pi^{m/2}} \int_{a_i}^{a_f} a^{-m/2} \, da
$$

Assuming $m \neq 2$:

$$
N_f = \frac{1}{C \cdot Y^m \cdot \Delta\sigma^m \cdot \pi^{m/2}} \cdot \left[ \frac{a^{1 - m/2}}{1 - m/2} \right]_{a_i}^{a_f}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_f = \frac{a_f^{\,1 - m/2} - a_i^{\,1 - m/2}}{C \cdot Y^m \cdot \Delta\sigma^m \cdot \pi^{m/2} \cdot (1 - m/2)}}
$$

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Compute Critical Crack Length**

$$
a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{K_{IC}}{Y \cdot \sigma_{max}} \right)^2 = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{80}{1.1 \times 300} \right)^2
$$

$$
a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \times (0.2424)^2 = \frac{0.05876}{\pi} = 0.01871 \text{ m} = 18.71 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $\Delta\sigma$ and exponent terms**

$$
\Delta\sigma = 300 - 0 = 300 \text{ MPa}, \quad m = 3.5, \quad 1 - m/2 = -0.75
$$

$$
\Delta\sigma^m = (300)^{3.5} = 300^3 \times \sqrt{300} = 2.7 \times 10^7 \times 17.32 = 4.676 \times 10^8
$$

$$
\pi^{m/2} = \pi^{1.75} = 8.258
$$

$$
Y^m = (1.1)^{3.5} = 1.1^3 \times \sqrt{1.1} = 1.331 \times 1.049 = 1.396
$$

**Step 3: Compute numerator**

Convert to meters: $a_i = 0.003$ m, $a_f = a_c = 0.01871$ m.

$$
a_f^{-0.75} = (0.01871)^{-0.75} = 12.49
$$

$$
a_i^{-0.75} = (0.003)^{-0.75} = 78.66
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 12.49 - 78.66 = -66.17
$$

**Step 4: Compute denominator**

$$
\text{Denominator} = 1.5 \times 10^{-11} \times 1.396 \times 4.676 \times 10^8 \times 8.258 \times (-0.75)
$$

$$
= 1.5 \times 10^{-11} \times 5.413 \times 10^9 \times (-0.75)
$$

$$
= 8.120 \times 10^{-2} \times (-0.75) = -6.090 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 5: Compute $N_f$**

$$
N_f = \frac{-66.17}{-0.0609} = 1086 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 6: Apply Safety Factor**

$$
N_{inspection} = \frac{N_f}{3} = \frac{1086}{3} = 362 \text{ cycles}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The component must be inspected after every **362 cycles** to maintain a 3× safety margin. This very short interval reflects the aggressive loading conditions (high stress range, 300 MPa, no compression phase) and the relatively small initial flaw.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**

(a) **Discuss the three regions of fatigue crack growth with the help of a schematic da/dN vs. ΔK plot. Explain the dominant micromechanism in each region.** (7 Marks)

(b) **A turbine blade is made of a nickel superalloy with $K_{IC} = 75$ MPa√m. The blade experiences centrifugal stress $\sigma_{max} = 450$ MPa and $\sigma_{min} = 150$ MPa during each rotation. An initial crack of length 1.5 mm is detected. The Paris Law constants are $C = 2.0 \times 10^{-11}$ m/cycle (MPa√m)$^m$ and $m = 3.0$. Assume $Y = 1.0$. Compute the number of cycles until fracture.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

The fatigue crack growth curve is conventionally plotted as log(da/dN) on the y-axis vs. log(ΔK) on the x-axis, and exhibits three distinct regions:

**Region I — Near-Threshold Growth:**
- Occurs at low ΔK, just above the threshold $\Delta K_{th}$.
- Growth rate is highly sensitive to microstructure, environment, and R-ratio.
- Dominant mechanism: **Stage I (crystallographic) growth**, where the crack advances along slip planes (shear mode) producing faceted, zig-zag fracture surfaces.
- da/dN → 0 as ΔK → $\Delta K_{th}$ (often taken as the practical "no-growth" limit for design).

**Region II — Stable Paris Growth:**
- A wide, log-linear region in the middle of the curve.
- Dominant mechanism: **Stage II (striation) growth**, where the crack advances perpendicular to the maximum principal tensile stress, producing characteristic **fatigue striations** on the fracture surface — one striation per load cycle.
- Paris Law strictly applies: $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$.

**Region III — Unstable Tearing Growth:**
- Occurs at high ΔK, approaching instability.
- Dominant mechanism: **Static fracture modes** (microvoid coalescence, cleavage) blend with fatigue. da/dN accelerates rapidly.
- Failure occurs when $K_{max} = K_{IC}$.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Critical Crack Length**

$$
a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{K_{IC}}{Y \cdot \sigma_{max}} \right)^2 = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{75}{1.0 \times 450} \right)^2
$$

$$
a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \times (0.1667)^2 = \frac{0.02778}{\pi} = 8.842 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 8.842 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Δσ**

$$
\Delta\sigma = 450 - 150 = 300 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Integrated Paris Law**

With $m = 3.0$, $1 - m/2 = -0.5$:

$$
N_f = \frac{a_f^{-0.5} - a_i^{-0.5}}{C \cdot \Delta\sigma^3 \cdot \pi^{1.5} \cdot (-0.5)}
$$

Convert to meters: $a_i = 0.0015$ m, $a_f = 0.008842$ m.

$$
a_f^{-0.5} = (0.008842)^{-0.5} = 10.64
$$

$$
a_i^{-0.5} = (0.0015)^{-0.5} = 25.82
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 10.64 - 25.82 = -15.18
$$

$$
\Delta\sigma^3 = (300)^3 = 2.7 \times 10^7
$$

$$
\pi^{1.5} = 5.568
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 2.0 \times 10^{-11} \times 2.7 \times 10^7 \times 5.568 \times (-0.5)
$$

$$
= 2.0 \times 10^{-11} \times 1.503 \times 10^8 \times (-0.5)
$$

$$
= 3.007 \times 10^{-3} \times (-0.5) = -1.503 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $N_f$**

$$
N_f = \frac{-15.18}{-1.503 \times 10^{-3}} = 10,100 \text{ cycles}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** 
> - Students frequently confuse $\sigma_{max}$ with $\Delta\sigma$ in the Paris Law formula. **Δσ must be used**, not σ_max.
> - Some students forget to convert crack lengths to **meters** when $C$ is given in SI units. A 1000× error in $N_f$ results.
> - Forgetting the geometry factor $Y$ (using $Y = 1.0$ by default) gives a non-conservative estimate if the actual $Y > 1$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Crack Propagation**

- **Stress Intensity Factor (K)** is the single parameter governing LEFM crack-tip severity; depends on stress, crack length, and geometry.
- **Three modes:** I (opening, most critical), II (sliding), III (tearing).
- **Paris Law:** $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$ governs **Region II** stable crack growth.
- **ΔK** (not $K_{max}$) is the proper argument: $\Delta K = Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$.
- **Three regions of growth:** Region I (near-threshold, faceted), Region II (Paris, striations), Region III (unstable, static mode blending).
- **Stress ratio R** affects growth: higher R → faster growth at the same ΔK. Use Forman or Walker equation when R effects matter.
- **Critical crack length:** $a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y \cdot \sigma_{max}}\right)^2$
- **Integrated Paris Law (Y = const, m ≠ 2):** $N_f = \dfrac{a_f^{\,1-m/2} - a_i^{\,1-m/2}}{C \cdot Y^m \cdot \Delta\sigma^m \cdot \pi^{m/2} \cdot (1-m/2)}$
- **Unit consistency is critical:** if $C$ is in SI (m/cycle), all lengths must be in meters and stresses in MPa.
- **Threshold $\Delta K_{th}$** defines the practical "no-growth" boundary for design purposes.
- **Fracture toughness $K_{IC}$** is a true material property; $\Delta K_{th}$ is also a material-environment combination property.
- **Engineering utility:** damage-tolerant design, inspection-interval calculation, retirement-for-cause (RFC) programs, life-extension studies for aging aircraft and nuclear plants.
- **Common KTU marks-losing mistakes:** using $K_{max}$ instead of $\Delta K$; ignoring $Y$; unit mismatch; forgetting to take the absolute value of $(1 - m/2)$ when $m > 2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Crack Propagation.** Continue with neighboring Module 3 topics (e.g., fracture toughness testing, stress-corrosion cracking, creep crack growth) for full KTU 2024 Scheme coverage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Designing Against Fatigue and Fracture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Designing Against Fatigue and Fracture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Designing Against Fatigue and Fracture** is the engineering discipline focused on preventing structural and component failure caused by **cyclic loading** (fatigue) and the propagation of pre-existing or incipient cracks (fracture mechanics). It establishes quantitative procedures to predict safe service life, determine allowable stress amplitudes, and set inspection intervals based on the damage tolerance philosophy.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this topic is anchored on three pillars:

1. **Fatigue Life Prediction** — using stress-life ($S$–$N$) or strain-life ($\varepsilon$–$N$) approaches.
2. **Mean Stress Correction** — modifying the alternating stress using criteria such as **Goodman**, **Soderberg**, or **Gerber**.
3. **Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM)** — predicting crack growth via the **Paris Law** and the **Stress Intensity Factor** $K$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Paperclip Analogy**
> Imagine bending a paperclip back and forth. Initially it resists, but after 8–10 bends the metal *heats up locally*, the bend point **work-hardens**, and a tiny crack initiates. Each successive bend grows this crack until the cross-section cannot carry the load and **sudden, brittle-looking separation occurs** — even though the wire was never loaded beyond its yield point.
>
> This is precisely **fatigue + fracture**: a high-cycle load (well below yield) creates a sub-critical crack; the crack propagates slowly; final separation is sudden.

In a KTU context, this translates directly to:
- **Connecting rods** in IC engines
- **Aircraft wing spars**
- **Pressure vessel nozzle welds**
- **Bridge stay cables** under wind-induced vibration

### 1.3 Key Terminology Standardized for KTU 2024

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|------|--------|------------------|
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | $S_{ut}$ | Maximum stress before necking |
| Yield Strength | $S_y$ | Stress at which plastic deformation begins |
| Endurance Limit | $S_e$ | Stress amplitude below which life is infinite (for ferrous metals) |
| Alternating Stress | $\sigma_a$ | $\dfrac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2}$ |
| Mean Stress | $\sigma_m$ | $\dfrac{\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min}}{2}$ |
| Stress Range | $\Delta\sigma$ | $\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}$ |
| Stress Ratio | $R$ | $\dfrac{\sigma_{\min}}{\sigma_{\max}}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fatigue Design Philosophy — Stepwise Logic

The design philosophy is structured into a **decision flow**:

1. **Identify the loading spectrum** — constant amplitude (CA) vs. variable amplitude (VA).
2. **Compute $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m$** from the service load history.
3. **Apply modification factors** to convert the raw endurance limit $S'_e = 0.5 S_{ut}$ to the corrected endurance limit $S_e$.
4. **Apply a mean stress correction** (Goodman / Soderberg / Gerber) to obtain the allowable alternating stress $\sigma_{ar}$.
5. **Compute the factor of safety** $n$.
6. **If a crack is suspected**, switch to **fracture mechanics** and apply Paris Law to estimate residual life.
7. **Apply Miner's Rule** for cumulative damage under variable amplitude loading.

### 2.2 Endurance Limit Correction (Marin Factors)

The raw endurance limit $S'_e = 0.5 S_{ut}$ (valid for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa) must be corrected as:

$$
S_e = k_a \, k_b \, k_c \, k_d \, k_e \, k_f \, S'_e
$$

where the **Marin factors** are:

| Factor | Symbol | Effect |
|--------|--------|--------|
| Surface | $k_a$ | Accounts for finish (polished $\approx 1$, as-forged $\approx 0.7$) |
| Size | $k_b$ | Accounts for diameter $> 7.62$ mm |
| Loading | $k_c$ | $1.0$ (axial), $0.85$ (bending), $0.59$ (torsion) |
| Temperature | $k_d$ | $= 1$ at room temperature |
| Reliability | $k_e$ | $0.814$ for 99% reliability, $1.000$ for 50% |
| Miscellaneous | $k_f$ | Corrosion, stress concentration residuals |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **standard, textbook-verified (Shigley / Dowling)** and are the only ones the KTU Board examiner will accept.

| # | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---------|----------|
| 1 | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2}$ | Alternating stress |
| 2 | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min}}{2}$ | Mean stress |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | **Goodman Line** (most conservative) |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | **Soderberg Line** (over-conservative for ductile) |
| 5 | $\dfrac{n \sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\dfrac{n \sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1$ | **Gerber Parabola** (least conservative) |
| 6 | $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N)^b$ | **Basquin's Equation** (high-cycle) |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f (2N)^c$ | **Coffin–Manson** (low-cycle) |
| 8 | $\sum \dfrac{n_i}{N_i} = 1$ | **Miner's Cumulative Damage Rule** |
| 9 | $K = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | **Stress Intensity Factor** |
| 10 | $\Delta K = Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Cyclic stress intensity range |
| 11 | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$ | **Paris Law** (Region II crack growth) |
| 12 | $K_{IC} = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a_c}$ | **Fracture Toughness** critical condition |
| 13 | $\sigma = \dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a}}$ | Critical (allowable) stress for a given crack |
| 14 | $N_f = \dfrac{a_c^{1 - m/2} - a_i^{1 - m/2}}{C (1 - m/2) \Delta\sigma^m Y^m \pi^{m/2}}$ | **Crack-initiation cycles** (integrated Paris) |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula table above, all absolute values / conditionals are written using $\vert$ or are implicit. For example, $\vert a_c - a_i \vert$ may appear in expanded form.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in KTU Engineering Streams

| Branch | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| Mechanical | Crankshaft, gear teeth, springs, pressure vessels |
| Civil | Bridge girders, pre-stressed concrete tendons |
| Aerospace | Wing spars, fuselage riveted joints, turbine disks |
| Naval | Welded ship hull plates, offshore risers |
| Automotive | Connecting rod, suspension arm, axle shafts |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Goodman Line

The **Goodman Criterion** assumes a linear relationship between the allowable alternating stress $\sigma_{ar}$ and the mean stress $\sigma_m$. It passes through two anchor points:

- **Point A**: When $\sigma_m = 0$, the allowable alternating stress equals the endurance limit, i.e. $\sigma_{ar} = S_e$.
- **Point B**: When $\sigma_{ar} = 0$, the allowable mean stress equals the ultimate strength, i.e. $\sigma_m = S_{ut}$.

Using the **two-point form of a straight line** in the $(\sigma_m, \sigma_{ar})$ plane:

$$
\frac{\sigma_m - 0}{S_{ut} - 0} + \frac{\sigma_{ar} - S_e}{0 - S_e} = 1
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} - \frac{\sigma_{ar}}{S_e} + 1 = 1
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{\sigma_{ar}}{S_e} = 1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}
$$

Now introducing the factor of safety $n$ on the actual operating stresses $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m$:

$$
\frac{n \sigma_a}{S_e} = 1 - \frac{n \sigma_m}{S_{ut}}
$$

Solving explicitly for $n$ yields the **canonical KTU Board form**:

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}
$$

This is the most frequently tested fatigue relation in KTU PEMET744.

### 3.2 Derivation of Crack Growth Cycles from Paris Law

The **Paris Law** is an empirical differential equation:

$$
\frac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m
$$

Substitute the definition $\Delta K = Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$:

$$
\frac{da}{dN} = C \left( Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a} \right)^m
$$

Separate variables to integrate from initial crack size $a_i$ to critical crack size $a_c$:

$$
\int_{a_i}^{a_c} \frac{da}{a^{m/2}} = \int_0^{N_f} C \left( Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi} \right)^m \, dN
$$

Evaluating the left integral:

$$
\int_{a_i}^{a_c} a^{-m/2} \, da = \left[ \frac{a^{1 - m/2}}{1 - m/2} \right]_{a_i}^{a_c}
$$

Evaluating the right integral (constant amplitude):

$$
C \left( Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi} \right)^m N_f
$$

Equating both sides:

$$
\frac{a_c^{1 - m/2} - a_i^{1 - m/2}}{1 - m/2} = C \left( Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi} \right)^m N_f
$$

Solving for the **number of cycles to failure $N_f$**:

$$
N_f = \frac{a_c^{1 - m/2} - a_i^{1 - m/2}}{C (1 - m/2) \, Y^m \Delta\sigma^m \, \pi^{m/2}}
$$

This expression is the **analytical backbone** of damage-tolerance design and is heavily tested in KTU University Examination Part-B questions.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Fully Solved

**Problem:** A steel shaft has $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa, $S_y = 400$ MPa, and corrected endurance limit $S_e = 250$ MPa. Under service it experiences $\sigma_{\max} = 350$ MPa and $\sigma_{\min} = -150$ MPa. Using the **Goodman criterion**, find the factor of safety $n$.

**Step 1 — Compute alternating and mean stresses:**

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2} = \frac{350 - (-150)}{2} = \frac{500}{2} = 250 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min}}{2} = \frac{350 + (-150)}{2} = \frac{200}{2} = 100 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Goodman:**

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}
$$

$$
\frac{250}{250} + \frac{100}{600} = \frac{1}{n}
$$

$$
1 + 0.1667 = \frac{1}{n}
$$

$$
1.1667 = \frac{1}{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $n$:**

$$
n = \frac{1}{1.1667} = 0.857
$$

Since $n < 1$, the shaft **fails** the Goodman check under these service stresses and must be redesigned (e.g., larger diameter or better surface finish).

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Mean Stress Criteria

| Criterion | Mathematical Form | Conservatism | Best Used When |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------|----------------|
| Soderberg | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | Most conservative | Brittle materials, unknown $S_{ut}$ |
| Goodman | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | Moderately conservative | General ductile design (KTU default) |
| Gerber | $\dfrac{n\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\dfrac{n\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1$ | Least conservative | Ductile materials with $\sigma_m > 0$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fatigue Design Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Service Load] --> B[Compute sigma_max and sigma_min]
    B --> C[Compute sigma_a and sigma_m]
    C --> D{Is Material Ferrous and Sut less than 1400 MPa}
    D -- Yes --> E[Set Se prime equal to 0.5 Sut]
    D -- No --> E2[Use Se from S-N data]
    E --> F[Apply Marin Factors ka, kb, kc, kd, ke, kf]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Compute Corrected Endurance Limit Se]
    G --> H{Choose Mean Stress Criterion}
    H -- Conservative --> I1[Use Soderberg Line]
    H -- Standard --> I2[Use Goodman Line]
    H -- Ductile and optimistic --> I3[Use Gerber Parabola]
    I1 --> J[Compute Factor of Safety n]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K{n greater than or equal to 1}
    K -- Yes --> L[Design is Safe in Fatigue]
    K -- No --> M[Redesign: Increase Section or Reduce Load]
    M --> B
    L --> N{Is Crack Suspected or NDT Flagged}
    N -- Yes --> O[Switch to Fracture Mechanics]
    N -- No --> P[Final Design Approved]
    O --> Q[Apply Paris Law: da over dN equals C times delta K to power m]
    Q --> R[Integrate from a initial to a critical]
    R --> S[Compute Residual Life Nf]
    S --> T[Schedule Inspection Interval equals Nf divided by 2]
```

### 4.2 Paris Crack Growth Schematic (Three Regions)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Region I: Near Threshold] -->|da/dN decreases| R2[Region II: Stable Paris Growth]
    R2 -->|K approaches KIC| R3[Region III: Unstable Fast Fracture]
    R1A[da/dN less than 10 to power minus 7 mm per cycle] --> R1
    R2A[Linear log log plot] --> R2
    R3A[da/dN rises sharply to infinity] --> R3
```

### 4.3 Damage Tolerance Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Detect Crack via NDT] --> B[Measure Initial Crack a_i]
    B --> C[Compute Critical Crack a_c from K equals KIC]
    C --> D[Integrate Paris Law]
    D --> E[Cycles to Failure N_f]
    E --> F[Inspection Interval equals N_f divided by Safety Factor 2]
    F --> G[Repair or Retire Component]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

*Distinguish between high-cycle fatigue (HCF) and low-cycle fatigue (LCF). State the governing equations.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Cycle Fatigue (HCF):** $N > 10^4$ cycles, predominantly **elastic** strain, governed by the **Basquin equation**:
>
> $$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N)^b$$
>
> **Low-Cycle Fatigue (LCF):** $N < 10^4$ cycles, predominantly **plastic** strain, governed by the **Coffin–Manson relation**:
>
> $$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f (2N)^c$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

*Define the Stress Intensity Factor $K$. What is the physical meaning of $K_{IC}$?*

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Stress Intensity Factor** $K = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ characterizes the **magnitude of the stress field at the tip of a sharp crack**. It depends on the applied stress $\sigma$, the crack length $a$, and a dimensionless geometry factor $Y$.
>
> The **Fracture Toughness $K_{IC}$** is the **critical value of $K$** at which a crack propagates catastrophically. It is a material property, measured in MPa·$\sqrt{\text{m}}$, and represents the **material's resistance to unstable crack growth**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Goodman + Marin Factor Combined Problem (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern]**

A steel shaft of diameter $d = 50$ mm is subjected to a fluctuating bending load. The service stresses are $\sigma_{\max} = 280$ MPa and $\sigma_{\min} = 80$ MPa. Material properties: $S_{ut} = 700$ MPa, $S_y = 470$ MPa, surface is ground, operating temperature is 50°C, reliability required is 99%. For the same steel, the fully corrected endurance limit for an axial-loaded, polished, 8 mm-diameter specimen tested at 99% reliability is found to be $S_{e0} = 280$ MPa.

**(a)** Compute the corrected endurance limit $S_e$ for the shaft using all relevant Marin factors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Goodman criterion**, find the factor of safety $n$. Comment on the safety of the design. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Solution A(a) — Marin Factor Computation

**Step 1 — Surface Factor $k_a$ (Ground surface):**

$$
k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^b = 1.58 \cdot (700)^{-0.085} = 1.58 \cdot 0.664 = 1.049 \quad \text{(use } a = 1.58, b = -0.085 \text{ for ground)}
$$

Since $k_a$ must be $\le 1$ by convention, take $k_a \approx 0.94$ (typical textbook value for ground).

**Step 2 — Size Factor $k_b$ (for $d = 50$ mm, $2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm):**

$$
k_b = 1.24 \, d^{-0.107} = 1.24 \cdot (50)^{-0.107} = 1.24 \cdot 0.633 = 0.785
$$

**Step 3 — Loading Factor $k_c$ (Bending):**

$$
k_c = 0.85
$$

**Step 4 — Temperature Factor $k_d$:**

$$
k_d \approx 1.0 \quad \text{(negligible at 50°C)}
$$

**Step 5 — Reliability Factor $k_e$ (99%):**

$$
k_e = 0.814
$$

**Step 6 — Miscellaneous Factor $k_f$:**

$$
k_f = 1.0
$$

**Step 7 — Aggregate Corrected Endurance Limit:**

$$
S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S_{e0}
$$

$$
S_e = 0.94 \cdot 0.785 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.814 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 280
$$

$$
S_e = 0.94 \cdot 0.785 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 0.814 \cdot 280
$$

Compute progressively:

$$
0.94 \cdot 0.785 = 0.7379
$$

$$
0.7379 \cdot 0.85 = 0.6272
$$

$$
0.6272 \cdot 0.814 = 0.5106
$$

$$
S_e = 0.5106 \cdot 280 = 142.97 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_e \approx 143 \text{ MPa}}
$$

---

##### Solution A(b) — Goodman Factor of Safety

**Step 1 — Compute $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m$:**

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2} = \frac{280 - 80}{2} = 100 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min}}{2} = \frac{280 + 80}{2} = 180 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Goodman:**

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}
$$

$$
\frac{100}{143} + \frac{180}{700} = \frac{1}{n}
$$

$$
0.6993 + 0.2571 = \frac{1}{n}
$$

$$
0.9564 = \frac{1}{n}
$$

$$
n = \frac{1}{0.9564} = 1.046
$$

$$
\boxed{n \approx 1.05}
$$

**Comment:** Since $n$ is only marginally above 1, the design is **just barely safe**. For KTU valuation, recommend increasing the shaft diameter by 10–15% or improving the surface finish (e.g., polishing or shot-peening) to boost $k_a$ and consequently $S_e$.

---

#### **Question B — Paris Law Crack Growth Problem (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern]**

A center-cracked plate is made of steel with $K_{IC} = 80$ MPa·$\sqrt{\text{m}}$. The plate is subject to a cyclic tensile stress with $\sigma_{\max} = 200$ MPa and $\sigma_{\min} = 20$ MPa. The geometry factor $Y = 1.0$. Initial crack length $a_i = 1$ mm = $0.001$ m. Material constants: $C = 1.2 \times 10^{-11}$ (units: m/cycle·(MPa·$\sqrt{\text{m}}$)$^{-m}$), $m = 3.0$.

**(a)** Compute the critical crack length $a_c$ at which fast fracture occurs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Paris Law, compute the number of cycles $N_f$ required for the crack to grow from $a_i$ to $a_c$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Solution B(a) — Critical Crack Length

**Step 1 — Stress Intensity Criterion at Fracture:**

$$
K_{IC} = Y \sigma_{\max} \sqrt{\pi a_c}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $a_c$:**

$$
a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{K_{IC}}{Y \sigma_{\max}} \right)^2
$$

$$
a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{80}{1.0 \cdot 200} \right)^2
$$

$$
a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} (0.4)^2 = \frac{0.16}{\pi}
$$

$$
a_c = 0.0509 \text{ m} = 50.93 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{a_c \approx 50.9 \text{ mm}}
$$

---

##### Solution B(b) — Number of Cycles via Paris Law

**Step 1 — Stress Range:**

$$
\Delta\sigma = \sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min} = 200 - 20 = 180 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Integrated Paris Law (with $m = 3$):**

$$
N_f = \frac{a_c^{1 - m/2} - a_i^{1 - m/2}}{C \, (1 - m/2) \, Y^m \, \Delta\sigma^m \, \pi^{m/2}}
$$

For $m = 3$: $\;1 - m/2 = 1 - 1.5 = -0.5$

**Step 3 — Compute Numerator:**

Convert to meters: $a_c = 0.0509$ m, $a_i = 0.001$ m.

$$
a_c^{-0.5} = (0.0509)^{-0.5} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.0509}} = \frac{1}{0.2256} = 4.432
$$

$$
a_i^{-0.5} = (0.001)^{-0.5} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.001}} = \frac{1}{0.03162} = 31.62
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 4.432 - 31.62 = -27.19
$$

Take absolute value: $\vert -27.19 \vert = 27.19$.

**Step 4 — Compute Denominator:**

$$
1 - m/2 = 1 - 1.5 = -0.5
$$

$$
\Delta\sigma^m = (180)^3 = 5{,}832{,}000 \text{ MPa}^3
$$

$$
\pi^{m/2} = \pi^{1.5} = \pi \sqrt{\pi} = 3.1416 \cdot 1.7725 = 5.568
$$

$$
Y^m = (1.0)^3 = 1.0
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 1.2 \times 10^{-11} \cdot (-0.5) \cdot 1.0 \cdot 5{,}832{,}000 \cdot 5.568
$$

Compute progressively:

$$
1.2 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 5{,}832{,}000 = 6.998 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
6.998 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 5.568 = 3.897 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
3.897 \times 10^{-4} \cdot (-0.5) = -1.949 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Ratio:**

$$
N_f = \frac{27.19}{-1.949 \times 10^{-4}} \quad \text{(numerator positive because denominator negative)}
$$

$$
N_f = \frac{27.19}{1.949 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.395 \times 10^{5} \text{ cycles}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_f \approx 1.4 \times 10^{5} \text{ cycles}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Paris Integration:** When $m > 2$, the term $(1 - m/2)$ is **negative**, making the denominator negative. Students forget to take the absolute value of the numerator $(a_c^{1-m/2} - a_i^{1-m/2})$ which is also negative. Final $N_f$ must be **positive**.
>
> 2. **Unit Consistency:** Crack lengths **must be in meters** (since $K_{IC}$ is in MPa·$\sqrt{\text{m}}$). Mixing mm with MPa·$\sqrt{\text{m}}$ is a common 2-mark loss.
>
> 3. **Marin Factors Skipped:** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if students use $S'_e = 0.5 S_{ut}$ **without** applying size, surface, and loading factors.
>
> 4. **Goodman vs Gerber Confusion:** When asked for the *most conservative* criterion, answer is **Soderberg** (uses $S_y$); for *moderate* it is **Goodman** (uses $S_{ut}$); for *least conservative* (ductile) it is **Gerber**.
>
> 5. **Inspection Interval = $N_f / 2$**: KTU often asks for the inspection schedule. Use a safety factor of **2** on the predicted life.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Things to Remember Before Entering the KTU Exam Hall**

- [ ] **Two pillars**: $S$–$N$ approach (HCF, $> 10^4$ cycles) and $\varepsilon$–$N$ approach (LCF, $< 10^4$ cycles).
- [ ] **Basquin:** $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N)^b$ with $b \approx -0.1$ for steels.
- [ ] **Coffin–Manson:** $\Delta\varepsilon_p / 2 = \varepsilon'_f (2N)^c$ with $c \approx -0.5$.
- [ ] **Miner's Rule:** $\sum n_i / N_i = 1$ — cumulative damage index. Failure occurs when sum reaches **unity**.
- [ ] **Raw Endurance Limit:** $S'_e = 0.5 S_{ut}$ **only** when $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa and ferrous.
- [ ] **Six Marin factors:** Surface ($k_a$), Size ($k_b$), Loading ($k_c$), Temperature ($k_d$), Reliability ($k_e$), Miscellaneous ($k_f$).
- [ ] **Goodman is the KTU default** for ductile materials; Soderberg is most conservative; Gerber is least.
- [ ] **Stress Intensity Factor:** $K = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ — geometry $Y$ typically $1.0$ for center crack, $1.12$ for edge crack.
- [ ] **Paris Law Region II:** $da/dN = C (\Delta K)^m$ — integrates from $a_i$ to $a_c$ for $N_f$.
- [ ] **Three crack growth regions:** (I) threshold $\Delta K_{th}$, (II) stable Paris, (III) unstable near $K_{IC}$.
- [ ] **Inspection interval** = $N_f / 2$ for damage tolerance.
- [ ] **Always convert** crack lengths to meters before computing $K$.
- [ ] **Always check** $n \ge 1$ for safe design; otherwise redesign.
- [ ] **Materials with $S_{ut} > 1400$ MPa**: no endurance limit — use $S$–$N$ curve directly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Fracture Mechanics in Design Practice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application of Fracture Mechanics in Design Practice

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Application of Fracture Mechanics in Design Practice** refers to the systematic engineering methodology that integrates Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM) and Elastic-Plastic Fracture Mechanics (EPFM) principles into the structural design, life prediction, and integrity assessment of load-bearing components. It explicitly accounts for the presence of flaws (cracks, voids, inclusions, weld defects) that are assumed to exist in all engineering structures, rather than treating the material as a flawless continuum as in classical stress-based design.

The discipline enables the engineer to answer three fundamental design questions:

1. **Critical Crack Size (aₒ):** What is the maximum tolerable flaw size that can be tolerated at a given service stress without catastrophic brittle fracture?
2. **Critical Stress (σc):** What is the maximum allowable operating stress for a given initial flaw size and material fracture toughness?
3. **Critical Stress Intensity Factor (KIc):** What is the intrinsic material resistance to unstable crack propagation under plane-strain conditions?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Fracture mechanics design philosophy categorizes components into three categories: **Safe-Life Design**, **Fail-Safe Design**, and **Damage-Tolerant Design**. The choice depends on the consequence of failure, inspection accessibility, and material's crack-propagation characteristics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The Glass Window Principle**
>
> Imagine you have a windowpane made of ordinary glass. If you press on it gently, it does not break — even though there are countless microscopic cracks and surface flaws already present in the glass from manufacturing. **Classical strength theory** would say, "as long as stress is below the ultimate strength of glass, it is safe." But we have all witnessed the bizarre phenomenon of a small stone chip causing the *entire* glass panel to shatter explosively into tiny cubes. Why?
>
> Classical mechanics says stress at the chip is local and small. **Fracture mechanics** answers: at the tip of that chip, the *local stress field* (called the stress intensity) reaches a critical value — the **fracture toughness (KIc)** of the glass. The glass did not fail because of *average stress* but because the **amplified stress at the crack tip** crossed a material property threshold.
>
> Fracture mechanics design simply says: *"Don't design as if the material were perfect — design assuming flaws are present, and ensure the crack-tip driving force (K) never exceeds the material's inherent resistance (KIc)."*

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Fracture Toughness, KIc:** Critical value of Mode-I stress intensity factor (units: **MPa·√m** or **ksi·√in**). Plane-strain condition requires thickness **B ≥ 2.5(KIc/σys)²**.
- **Geometry Factor, Y:** Dimensionless correction factor (typically **1.0 ≤ Y ≤ 2.0**) accounting for crack shape, location, and component geometry.
- **Paris Law Exponent, m:** Typically **2.5 ≤ m ≤ 4.0** for steels; **m ≈ 3** is commonly assumed in textbook calculations.
- **Crack Growth Coefficient, C:** Empirical constant (units: **mm/cycle · (MPa√m)^(-m)**), derived experimentally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in design:** Traditional **stress-based design** uses a *Factor of Safety (FoS)* on yield/ultimate strength. **Fracture-mechanics-based design** uses a *Factor of Safety on toughness* — ensuring the applied stress intensity factor **K_applied < KIc / FoS_K**. This is the only way to rationally quantify the safety of flawed, welded, or high-strength structural components.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fracture Mechanics Design Philosophy

The application of fracture mechanics in design follows a structured decision hierarchy:

### Step 1 — Identify the Failure Mode
- Determine whether the component is susceptible to **brittle fracture**, **fatigue**, **stress corrosion cracking**, or **creep**.
- Brittle fracture is governed by **KIc**; fatigue crack growth is governed by **Paris Law**; stress corrosion is governed by **KISCC**.

### Step 2 — Characterize the Flaw Population
- Use Non-Destructive Evaluation (NDE) such as **ultrasonic testing, radiography, dye-penetrant testing, eddy-current testing**.
- Define: **a₀ = initial detectable flaw size**, **aᵢ = inspection interval threshold**, **a_c = critical crack size**.

### Step 3 — Quantify the Crack-Tip Driving Force
- For Mode-I (opening) loading — the most common and most critical mode:

$$K_I = Y \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a}$$

where the geometry factor **Y** is a function of crack length, component width, and loading configuration.

### Step 4 — Compare Driving Force to Material Resistance
- Apply the **fracture criterion**: **K_I ≤ K_Ic** (for brittle fracture).
- For fatigue: **da/dN ≤ allowable growth rate**.

### Step 5 — Establish Inspection / Replacement Intervals
- For damage-tolerant design: ensure inspection interval < time for crack to grow from aᵢ to a_c.

## 2.2 Three Design Philosophies

| Design Philosophy | Underlying Assumption | Application | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Safe-Life Design** | Component operates crack-free for entire design life | Aircraft wing, rotating machinery shafts | Requires very high FoS; inefficient |
| **Fail-Safe Design** | Multiple load paths; one crack does not cause collapse | Aircraft fuselage (redundant stringers), bridges | Adds weight and complexity |
| **Damage-Tolerant Design** | Cracks exist and grow; periodic inspection catches them before reaching a_c | Aerospace, pressure vessels, pipelines | Requires reliable NDE |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why each philosophy matters:** A fracture-mechanics engineer must choose the *philosophy* before doing the math. The math (K = Yσ√(πa), Paris integration) is identical across all three — only the design criteria and safety factors differ.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **mandatory for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination)** in PEMET744. Memorize the relationships, not just the formulas.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Stress intensity factor (Mode I) for through-thickness crack | MPa√m |
| 2 | $a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \left(\dfrac{K_{Ic}}{Y \sigma}\right)^2$ | Critical crack length at operating stress | m or mm |
| 3 | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{K_{Ic}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a}}$ | Critical fracture stress for a given crack length | MPa |
| 4 | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$ | Paris Law for fatigue crack growth rate | m/cycle |
| 5 | $\Delta K = Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Cyclic stress intensity factor range | MPa√m |
| 6 | $N_f = \dfrac{a_c^{\,(1 - m/2)} - a_0^{\,(1 - m/2)}}{C \left(\Delta \sigma\right)^m \pi^{m/2} Y^m \left(1 - \dfrac{m}{2}\right)}$ | Fatigue life (cycles) from a₀ to a_c, valid for **m ≠ 2** | cycles |
| 7 | $B \geq 2.5 \left(\dfrac{K_{Ic}}{\sigma_{ys}}\right)^2$ | Minimum thickness for plane-strain (KIc) validity | m |
| 8 | $K_{ISCC} = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Threshold for stress-corrosion cracking | MPa√m |

> **Critical Reminder on Table Symbols:** In LaTeX-rendered tables, absolute-value bars like $\vert x \vert$ must use `\vert` or `\mid`, **never** the raw pipe `|` character, to avoid breaking markdown table column boundaries.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Determines inspection intervals for fuselage lap-joints, wing spars, and engine disks. Damage-tolerance certification per **FAA/EASA CS-25.571** is mandatory.
- **Pressure Vessels & Pipelines:** ASME Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code Section XI mandates fracture-mechanics-based fitness-for-service evaluations.
- **Bridges & Offshore Structures:** Used in assessing fatigue life of welded connections under cyclic wave/wind loading.
- **Nuclear Industry:** Leak-Before-Break (LBB) analysis uses fracture mechanics to prove that a through-wall crack will leak before becoming unstable.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Crack Size (a_c) from Griffith–Irwin Criterion

### Starting Point: The Irwin Fracture Criterion

For a linear-elastic isotropic material containing a central through-thickness crack of length **2a** under remote tensile stress **σ**, the stress intensity factor is given by:

$$K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

At the point of **unstable fracture**, the applied stress intensity factor equals the material's plane-strain fracture toughness:

$$K_I = K_{Ic}$$

### Step-by-Step Algebraic Derivation

**Step 1:** Set up the equality at the critical condition.

$$Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a_c} = K_{Ic}$$

**Step 2:** Divide both sides by the term containing the geometry factor and stress. This isolates the square root of the critical crack length on the left-hand side.

$$\sqrt{\pi a_c} = \dfrac{K_{Ic}}{Y \sigma}$$

**Step 3:** Square both sides to remove the square root on the left-hand side. Squaring produces a π factor in the denominator, which we will transpose next.

$$\pi a_c = \left(\dfrac{K_{Ic}}{Y \sigma}\right)^2$$

**Step 4:** Divide both sides by π to isolate the critical crack length a_c as the final unknown.

$$a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{Ic}}{Y \sigma}\right)^2$$

**Result (Equation 1):**

$$\boxed{\,a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{Ic}}{Y \sigma}\right)^2\,}$$

### Numerical Worked Example

> **Given:** A high-strength steel plate (σ_ys = 1200 MPa, K_Ic = 75 MPa·√m) contains a central through-thickness crack. Operating tensile stress σ = 300 MPa. Geometry factor Y = 1.0. Determine the critical crack length.

**Step 1:** Identify the design formula to use — Equation 1 directly gives a_c.

$$a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{Ic}}{Y \sigma}\right)^2$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values into the formula.

$$a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{75 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}}{1.0 \times 300 \text{ MPa}}\right)^2$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the ratio inside the parentheses. The MPa units cancel, leaving √m, which is squared in the next step.

$$a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}(0.25 \text{ m}^{1/2})^2$$

**Step 4:** Square the inner term to get an answer in meters (m).

$$a_c = \dfrac{0.0625}{\pi} \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Divide by π = 3.14159 to obtain the final numerical value.

$$a_c = 0.01989 \text{ m} \approx 19.9 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6:** Compute total through-thickness crack length = 2a_c = 39.8 mm. If detected crack length exceeds this, the plate must be replaced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** A 40 mm crack is highly visible. Why does it require such sophisticated analysis? Because in *welded* or *high-strength steel* components, fracture toughness is lower and operating stresses are higher, so critical crack sizes can drop to **2–5 mm** — invisible without NDE.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fatigue Life Using Paris Law (Variable Amplitude → Constant Amplitude Approximation)

### Starting Point: Paris–Erdogan Equation

For cyclic loading with constant amplitude Δσ, the rate of fatigue crack growth is:

$$\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m = C \left(Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}\right)^m$$

### Step-by-Step Integration

**Step 1:** Separate the variables — crack length **a** on left, cycles **N** on right. This converts the differential equation into an integrable form.

$$da = C \left(Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi}\right)^m a^{m/2} \, dN$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange so that all terms in **a** are on the left and **dN** is on the right. This prepares the equation for direct integration.

$$a^{-m/2} \, da = C \left(Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi}\right)^m \, dN$$

**Step 3:** Integrate both sides from initial crack length **a₀** to critical crack length **a_c**, and from cycle count **0** to total life **N_f**.

$$\int_{a_0}^{a_c} a^{-m/2} \, da = \int_{0}^{N_f} C \left(Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi}\right)^m \, dN$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the left-hand integral using the standard power rule $\int a^n \, da = \dfrac{a^{n+1}}{n+1}$. Here $n = -m/2$, so the exponent becomes $1 - m/2$.

$$\left[\dfrac{a^{1 - m/2}}{1 - m/2}\right]_{a_0}^{a_c} = C \left(Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi}\right)^m N_f$$

**Step 5:** Apply the limits of integration on the left side, subtracting the lower bound from the upper bound.

$$\dfrac{a_c^{\,1 - m/2} - a_0^{\,1 - m/2}}{1 - m/2} = C \left(Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi}\right)^m N_f$$

**Step 6:** Multiply both sides by $(1 - m/2)$ to clear the denominator on the left.

$$a_c^{\,1 - m/2} - a_0^{\,1 - m/2} = C \left(Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi}\right)^m \left(1 - \dfrac{m}{2}\right) N_f$$

**Step 7:** Isolate N_f by dividing both sides by the coefficient on the right. Separate $Y^m (\Delta \sigma)^m \pi^{m/2}$ from the parenthesis.

$$N_f = \dfrac{a_c^{\,1 - m/2} - a_0^{\,1 - m/2}}{C \left(\Delta \sigma\right)^m \pi^{m/2} Y^m \left(1 - \dfrac{m}{2}\right)}$$

> **Result (Equation 2):**
> $$\boxed{\,N_f = \dfrac{a_c^{\,(1 - m/2)} - a_0^{\,(1 - m/2)}}{C \left(\Delta \sigma\right)^m \pi^{m/2} Y^m \left(1 - \dfrac{m}{2}\right)}\,}$$

### Numerical Worked Example (Fatigue Life)

> **Given:** Steel component, m = 3, C = 1.5 × 10⁻¹¹ (units consistent with MPa·√m and m/cycle). Y = 1.0, Δσ = 200 MPa. Initial crack a₀ = 1 mm = 0.001 m. Critical crack a_c = 20 mm = 0.02 m. Find N_f.

**Step 1:** Calculate the exponent $1 - m/2 = 1 - 1.5 = -0.5$.

**Step 2:** Compute $a_c^{\,-0.5}$ and $a_0^{\,-0.5}$.

$$a_c^{-0.5} = (0.02)^{-0.5} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{0.02}} = 7.071 \text{ m}^{-1/2}$$

$$a_0^{-0.5} = (0.001)^{-0.5} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{0.001}} = 31.623 \text{ m}^{-1/2}$$

**Step 3:** Numerator (note: since exponent is negative, $a_0^{-0.5} > a_c^{-0.5}$, so the difference is negative — this is expected).

$$a_c^{-0.5} - a_0^{-0.5} = 7.071 - 31.623 = -24.552 \text{ m}^{-1/2}$$

**Step 4:** Denominator components.

$$\left(\Delta \sigma\right)^m = (200)^3 = 8.0 \times 10^6 \text{ MPa}^3$$

$$\pi^{m/2} = \pi^{1.5} = 5.568$$

$$Y^m = 1.0$$

$$1 - \dfrac{m}{2} = -0.5$$

$$C \times (\Delta\sigma)^m \times \pi^{m/2} \times Y^m \times (1 - m/2)$$

$$= 1.5 \times 10^{-11} \times 8.0 \times 10^6 \times 5.568 \times 1.0 \times (-0.5)$$

$$= -3.341 \times 10^{-4} \text{ (units consistent)}$$

**Step 5:** Final division. The two negatives cancel.

$$N_f = \dfrac{-24.552}{-3.341 \times 10^{-4}} = 73,490 \text{ cycles}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The component can sustain approximately **73,500 load cycles** before the 1 mm initial crack grows to the critical 20 mm size. This sets the **inspection interval** for damage-tolerant design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Stress from Known Flaw Size

Useful when NDE has detected a flaw of length **2a**, and the engineer must specify the maximum allowable operating stress.

### Starting from K_I = K_Ic:

$$Y \sigma_c \sqrt{\pi a} = K_{Ic}$$

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1:** Divide both sides by $Y \sqrt{\pi a}$ to isolate σ_c.

$$\sigma_c = \dfrac{K_{Ic}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a}}$$

> **Result (Equation 3):**
> $$\boxed{\,\sigma_c = \dfrac{K_{Ic}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a}}\,}$$

### Numerical Example

> **Given:** K_Ic = 50 MPa√m, Y = 1.1, detected surface flaw depth a = 2.5 mm = 0.0025 m.

**Step 1:** Compute $Y \sqrt{\pi a}$.

$$Y \sqrt{\pi a} = 1.1 \times \sqrt{\pi \times 0.0025} = 1.1 \times \sqrt{0.007854} = 1.1 \times 0.08862 = 0.09748$$

**Step 2:** Compute critical stress.

$$\sigma_c = \dfrac{50}{0.09748} = 512.9 \text{ MPa}$$

> **Interpretation:** If the applied stress exceeds 513 MPa, the detected 2.5 mm flaw will propagate unstably. **Fitness-for-service** requires either reducing stress, repairing the flaw, or replacing the part.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Design Equation

| Engineering Question Asked | Use This Equation | Required Inputs |
|---|---|---|
| "What is the largest flaw we can tolerate at design stress?" | Equation 1 (a_c) | σ, K_Ic, Y |
| "What is the max allowable stress for a detected flaw?" | Equation 3 (σ_c) | a, K_Ic, Y |
| "How many cycles before this crack becomes critical?" | Equation 2 (N_f) | a₀, a_c, Δσ, C, m, Y |
| "Is the thickness sufficient to apply K_Ic?" | Inequality B ≥ 2.5(K_Ic/σ_ys)² | σ_ys, K_Ic |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Fracture Mechanics Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Component Design Brief] --> B[Identify Failure Mode]
    B --> B1[Brittle Fracture]
    B --> B2[Fatigue]
    B --> B3[Stress Corrosion]
    B1 --> C[Determine K_Ic and sigma_ys]
    B2 --> D[Determine Paris Constants C and m]
    B3 --> E[Determine K_ISCC]
    C --> F[Select Design Philosophy]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> F1[Safe-Life]
    F --> F2[Fail-Safe]
    F --> F3[Damage-Tolerant]
    F1 --> G1[Compute a_c using Eq 1]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G2[Set NDE Detection Limit a_0]
    G2 --> G3[Compute N_f using Eq 2]
    G3 --> H[Establish Inspection Interval]
    G1 --> H
    H --> I{Does a_c exceed a_0 by safety factor 2 or more?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Design Acceptable]
    I -- No --> K[Redesign: Lower stress or Use tougher material]
    K --> C
    J --> L[End: Finalize Drawings and Specifications]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fracture Mechanics Inputs & Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[Input Parameters]
        IN1[sigma: Applied Stress MPa]
        IN2[a: Crack Length m]
        IN3[K_Ic: Fracture Toughness MPa sqrt m]
        IN4[Y: Geometry Factor]
        IN5[C and m: Paris Constants]
        IN6[Delta sigma: Cyclic Stress Range]
    end
    subgraph ENGINE[Fracture Mechanics Engine]
        ENG1[LEFM Module]
        ENG2[Paris Integration Module]
        ENG3[EPFM Module J integral]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS[Design Outputs]
        OUT1[a_c Critical Crack Size]
        OUT2[sigma_c Critical Stress]
        OUT3[N_f Fatigue Life Cycles]
        OUT4[Inspection Interval]
    end
    IN1 --> ENG1
    IN2 --> ENG1
    IN3 --> ENG1
    IN4 --> ENG1
    ENG1 --> OUT1
    ENG1 --> OUT2
    IN2 --> ENG2
    IN5 --> ENG2
    IN6 --> ENG2
    ENG2 --> OUT3
    OUT3 --> OUT4
    IN1 --> ENG3
    IN3 --> ENG3
    ENG3 --> OUT2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Crack Growth Regimes (da/dN vs ΔK)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Region I: Near Threshold] -->|Slow growth, K approaching K_th| B[Region II: Paris Law]
    B -->|Steady power-law growth| C[Region III: Unstable Tearing]
    C -->|K approaches K_c, rapid growth| D[Final Fracture at K_Ic]
    A -.- A1[da/dN less than 10 to power minus 7 mm per cycle]
    B -.- B1[da/dN = C times Delta K to power m]
    C -.- C1[da/dN accelerates dramatically]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Damage-Tolerant Design Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Fabricate Component] --> P2[Put into Service]
    P2 --> P3[Operate for Interval T]
    P3 --> P4[Scheduled NDE Inspection]
    P4 --> P5{Crack Detected?}
    P5 -- No --> P2
    P5 -- Yes, but a less than a_i --> P2
    P5 -- Yes, a between a_i and a_c --> P6[Repair or Replace]
    P6 --> P1
    P5 -- Yes, a exceeds a_c --> P7[Component has failed: Investigate root cause]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define fracture toughness (KIc) and explain its significance in design practice.**

**Model Answer:**
Fracture toughness, K_Ic, is defined as the critical value of the Mode-I stress intensity factor at which a sharp crack in a linear-elastic material begins to propagate unstably under plane-strain conditions. It is measured in units of **MPa·√m** and represents the intrinsic resistance of the material to brittle fracture.

In design practice, K_Ic is significant because it provides a quantitative limit on the crack-tip driving force. The design criterion $K_I \leq K_{Ic}/n$ (where n is the safety factor) ensures that flaws of any size — up to the largest detectable by NDE — will not cause catastrophic failure at the operating stress. Unlike yield strength, K_Ic accounts for the presence of cracks and is therefore the appropriate material property for designing flawed, welded, or high-strength components.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between safe-life and damage-tolerant design philosophies.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Safe-Life Design | Damage-Tolerant Design |
|---|---|---|
| **Assumption** | Component is flaw-free during service | Cracks exist from fabrication or service |
| **Design Action** | High factor of safety on fatigue life | Inspection-based life management |
| **Use Case** | Rotating machinery, automotive parts | Aircraft structures, pressure vessels |
| **Failure Criterion** | S-N curve based | a_c from K_Ic; Paris Law for growth |
| **Limitation** | Inefficient for high-performance structures | Requires reliable NDE infrastructure |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** A steel plate has a central through-thickness crack of length 2a = 10 mm. The plate is loaded in tension to a stress of 350 MPa. The fracture toughness of the steel is K_Ic = 60 MPa·√m. Assuming geometry factor Y = 1.0, determine:
1. The critical crack length at which unstable fracture occurs.
2. The factor of safety on operating stress with respect to brittle fracture.
3. Comment on the design adequacy.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Using Paris Law, derive the expression for the number of cycles required for a crack to grow from an initial length a₀ to a critical length a_c under constant-amplitude cyclic loading. State the assumptions made.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Solution:

**Step 1: Identify given data.**

- $a_0 = 5 \text{ mm} = 0.005 \text{ m}$
- $\sigma = 350 \text{ MPa}$
- $K_{Ic} = 60 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$
- $Y = 1.0$

**Step 2: Compute critical crack length a_c using Equation 1.**

$$a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{Ic}}{Y \sigma}\right)^2$$

Substitute the values:

$$a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{60}{1.0 \times 350}\right)^2$$

Compute the inner ratio:

$$a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}(0.17143)^2$$

$$a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}(0.02939)$$

$$a_c = 0.009355 \text{ m} \approx 9.36 \text{ mm}$$

So total through-thickness crack length at fracture: $2a_c = 18.7 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 3: Compute factor of safety on operating stress.**

Using Equation 3, the critical stress for the current crack:

$$\sigma_c = \dfrac{K_{Ic}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a_0}}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$Y \sqrt{\pi a_0} = 1.0 \times \sqrt{\pi \times 0.005} = \sqrt{0.01571} = 0.1253 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_c = \dfrac{60}{0.1253} = 478.8 \text{ MPa}$$

Factor of Safety:

$$FoS = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\sigma_{applied}} = \dfrac{478.8}{350} = 1.37$$

**Step 4: Comment on design adequacy.**

The factor of safety of **1.37 is low** for a structural component. The crack only needs to grow from 5 mm to 9.36 mm — a mere **4.4 mm of crack growth** — before catastrophic brittle fracture occurs. This design is **inadequate** for safety-critical applications. Recommended actions:
- Reduce operating stress to ≤ 250 MPa to obtain FoS ≈ 2.
- Select a steel with higher K_Ic (e.g., 80 MPa·√m).
- Specify NDE inspection every 1000 cycles.

---

#### Part (b) — Derivation:

**Starting equation (Paris Law):**

$$\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$$

**Substitute ΔK:**

$$\Delta K = Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

$$\dfrac{da}{dN} = C \left(Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a}\right)^m = C (Y \Delta\sigma)^m \pi^{m/2} a^{m/2}$$

**Separate variables:**

$$da \cdot a^{-m/2} = C (Y \Delta\sigma)^m \pi^{m/2} \, dN$$

**Integrate from a₀ to a_c, and 0 to N_f:**

$$\int_{a_0}^{a_c} a^{-m/2} \, da = C (Y \Delta\sigma)^m \pi^{m/2} \int_{0}^{N_f} dN$$

**Evaluate:**

$$\left[\dfrac{a^{1 - m/2}}{1 - m/2}\right]_{a_0}^{a_c} = C (Y \Delta\sigma)^m \pi^{m/2} N_f$$

**Solve for N_f:**

$$N_f = \dfrac{a_c^{\,1 - m/2} - a_0^{\,1 - m/2}}{C (Y \Delta\sigma)^m \pi^{m/2} (1 - m/2)}$$

**Assumptions Made:**

1. Linear elastic fracture mechanics is applicable throughout (small-scale yielding).
2. Constant-amplitude cyclic loading (Δσ constant).
3. Geometry factor Y is independent of crack length.
4. Paris constants C and m are valid throughout the integration range (Region II only).
5. No retardation or acceleration effects (overload, mean stress, environment).
6. Through-thickness crack with no change in crack-front geometry.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** A pressure vessel is fabricated from a steel with K_Ic = 110 MPa·√m and yield strength σ_ys = 900 MPa. The vessel contains a semi-elliptical surface flaw of depth a = 8 mm and length 2c = 40 mm. Hoop stress in the vessel wall is 280 MPa. Use geometry factor Y = 1.2.
1. Calculate the applied stress intensity factor K_I.
2. Determine whether fracture will occur.
3. What is the minimum wall thickness to ensure plane-strain (valid K_Ic) conditions?

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the three design philosophies (safe-life, fail-safe, damage-tolerant) with suitable examples. Discuss how fracture mechanics equations are used in each.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Solution:

**Step 1: Compute the applied K_I.**

$$K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

Substitute the values (convert a to meters: 8 mm = 0.008 m):

$$K_I = 1.2 \times 280 \times \sqrt{\pi \times 0.008}$$

Compute $\pi \times 0.008 = 0.02513$.

Compute $\sqrt{0.02513} = 0.1585$.

Therefore:

$$K_I = 1.2 \times 280 \times 0.1585 = 53.26 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$$

**Step 2: Compare K_I to K_Ic.**

$$K_I = 53.26 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}} \quad \text{vs} \quad K_{Ic} = 110 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$$

Since $K_I < K_{Ic}$, the vessel will **not fracture** at the current stress level.

Compute factor of safety on toughness:

$$FoS_K = \dfrac{K_{Ic}}{K_I} = \dfrac{110}{53.26} = 2.07$$

This is acceptable for most pressure-vessel design codes.

**Step 3: Compute minimum thickness for plane strain.**

$$B_{min} = 2.5 \left(\dfrac{K_{Ic}}{\sigma_{ys}}\right)^2$$

$$B_{min} = 2.5 \left(\dfrac{110}{900}\right)^2$$

Compute the ratio: $110/900 = 0.1222$.

Square it: $(0.1222)^2 = 0.01494$.

Multiply by 2.5:

$$B_{min} = 2.5 \times 0.01494 = 0.03735 \text{ m} = 37.4 \text{ mm}$$

The vessel wall must be at least **37.4 mm thick** for K_Ic to be a valid material property. If wall thickness is below this, the use of K_Ic is non-conservative (the actual fracture resistance is lower than K_Ic).

---

#### Part (b) — Design Philosophies Explanation:

**1. Safe-Life Design:**

- **Principle:** The component is designed such that no crack (above the NDE detection limit) will reach critical size during the entire service life. Inspection is not relied upon.
- **Fracture Mechanics Use:** S-N curves are supplemented with fracture-mechanics calculations to confirm that the largest undetected flaw in the material, when subjected to the design stress spectrum, will never grow to $a_c$.
- **Example:** Aircraft landing gear, helicopter rotor masts, automotive crankshafts. The component is **retired before crack initiation**.
- **Equation Used:** Primarily $a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{Ic}}{Y \sigma}\right)^2$ to find the largest tolerable flaw, which is then used as the NDE acceptance criterion.

**2. Fail-Safe Design:**

- **Principle:** The structure is designed with **multiple load paths** (crack arresters, doublers, redundant members). If one element fails, the load redistributes to alternate paths.
- **Fracture Mechanics Use:** Determines the **inspection interval** to detect cracking before the *second* load path is compromised. It also establishes **crack-arrestor geometry** (e.g., stiffener spacing, hole locations) to ensure slow stable crack growth.
- **Example:** Aircraft fuselage (crack in one stringer → load redistributes to adjacent stringers), bridge trusses, ship hulls.
- **Equation Used:** $N_f = \dfrac{a_c^{(1 - m/2)} - a_0^{(1 - m/2)}}{C (\Delta\sigma)^m \pi^{m/2} Y^m (1 - m/2)}$ to compute the time required for crack to traverse from one stiffener to the next.

**3. Damage-Tolerant Design:**

- **Principle:** Acknowledge that cracks exist (from manufacturing or service). Design such that cracks grow **slowly and predictably**, and that **periodic inspection** will detect them before they reach $a_c$.
- **Fracture Mechanics Use:** Calculates inspection intervals based on Paris Law integration between NDE detection limit $a_0$ and critical crack size $a_c$.
- **Example:** Commercial aircraft wings (per FAA AC 25.571), nuclear pressure vessels (ASME Section XI), oil & gas pipelines.
- **Equation Used:** Both Equation 1 (to compute $a_c$) and Equation 2 (Paris integration to compute $N_f$ → inspection interval). The inspection interval is typically set at $N_f / 2$ or $N_f / 3$ for safety.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Most Commonly Lose Marks in PEMET744:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (3–4 marks lost):** Mixing **mm** and **m** in the same calculation. If a is in mm in the numerator but √(πa) is in √m, the units will cancel correctly only if you are consistent. **Always convert crack length to meters** before computing √(πa).
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Geometry Factor Y (2–3 marks lost):** Setting Y = 1.0 universally. In reality, Y ranges from 0.8 to 2.0 depending on crack shape, location (edge vs. center), and finite-width effects. The KTU paper will specify Y or expect you to look it up from a table.
>
> 3. **Confusing a (half-crack length) with 2a (total crack length):** Many formulas use **a** as the half-crack. The critical crack length equation gives **half-crack a_c**. If the question asks "total crack length," multiply by 2.
>
> 4. **Paris Law exponent error:** When $m = 4$, the exponent $1 - m/2 = -1$. Many students forget the minus sign and write $+1$.
>
> 5. **Plane-strain thickness check:** If the wall thickness B < 2.5(K_Ic/σ_ys)², the answer based on K_Ic is non-conservative. Examiners will deduct marks if you do not check this.
>
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** In any derivation question (especially Paris Law), explicitly listing assumptions earns **at least 2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Application of Fracture Mechanics in Design Practice**

- [ ] **Fracture criterion:** Failure occurs when $K_I = K_{Ic}$.
- [ ] **General Mode-I equation:** $K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$.
- [ ] **Critical crack length:** $a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{Ic}}{Y \sigma}\right)^2$.
- [ ] **Critical fracture stress:** $\sigma_c = \dfrac{K_{Ic}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a}}$.
- [ ] **Paris Law:** $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$, with $\Delta K = Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$.
- [ ] **Fatigue life (m ≠ 2):** $N_f = \dfrac{a_c^{(1 - m/2)} - a_0^{(1 - m/2)}}{C (\Delta\sigma)^m \pi^{m/2} Y^m (1 - m/2)}$.
- [ ] **Plane-strain validity:** $B \geq 2.5 (K_{Ic}/\sigma_{ys})^2$.
- [ ] **Three crack-growth regimes:** Region I (near threshold $K_{th}$), Region II (Paris Law, steady growth), Region III (unstable tearing toward $K_{Ic}$).
- [ ] **Three design philosophies:** Safe-Life (crack-free), Fail-Safe (redundant load paths), Damage-Tolerant (periodic inspection).
- [ ] **Typical values:** For ductile steels, $K_{Ic} = 50$–$150 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$; for aluminum alloys, $K_{Ic} = 20$–$50 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$; for brittle ceramics, $K_{Ic} = 1$–$5 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$.
- [ ] **Geometry factor Y:** Always given in KTU problems; Y = 1.0 only for central through-thickness crack in infinite plate.
- [ ] **Stress corrosion threshold:** Below $K_{ISCC}$, cracks do not propagate in aggressive environments regardless of stress.
- [ ] **Real-world standards:** ASME Section XI (nuclear), FAA AC 25.571 (aircraft damage tolerance), BS 7910 (fitness-for-service in UK).

> **Final Examiner Tip:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, internal choice in Part B means you should attempt **one** full 14-mark question. Budget your time: **~6 minutes for Part A × 2 questions** and **~18–20 minutes for Part B (full question)**. Always draw a **labeled sketch of the cracked component** — KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** for free-body diagrams even in numerical problems.
