<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Topics in Failure Analysis: Contact Fatigue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advanced Topics in Failure Analysis: Contact Fatigue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Contact Fatigue** (also called **Rolling Contact Fatigue, RCF**, or **Surface Fatigue**) is a specialized mode of failure characterized by the progressive initiation and propagation of sub-surface or surface cracks under the action of **repeated cyclic Hertzian contact stresses** generated by rolling, sliding, or combined rolling–sliding motion between two conforming (non-conformal) or non-conforming bodies.

It is the dominant failure mode in **rolling-element bearings**, **gears** (especially **pitting** at the pitch line), **cams and cam followers**, **rail-wheel contacts**, **roller cam followers**, and **hypoid gear teeth** in automotive differentials. It is fundamentally distinct from classical bulk fatigue (as in rotating bending or axial loading) because the stress field is highly localized, triaxial, and constantly translating through the material.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag**: This topic belongs to the cluster of *Advanced Failure Mechanisms* in Module 4. The Board Examiner is expected to test the linkage between **Hertzian contact theory**, **lubrication regime**, **material cleanliness (inclusion rating)**, and the resulting **micropitting / spalling / WEC (White Etching Crack)** sub-failure modes.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Finger-Tip Press

Imagine pressing the tip of your finger repeatedly onto the same spot on a desk. Even though the force is mild, the *local pressure* underneath the fingertip is extremely high. Now repeat this **several million times per hour**, day after day, while your finger also slides slightly each cycle.

What happens biologically to the desk? Sub-surface material gets micro-crushed, tiny voids nucleate around any pre-existing defect, a crack propagates parallel to the surface, and eventually a flake ("scab") pops out, leaving a small crater. That crater is exactly what engineers call a **pit**.

In engineering terms:
- The **finger** = a rolling element (ball or roller) or a gear tooth flank.
- The **desk** = the mating raceway or tooth surface.
- The **repetitive press** = cyclic Hertzian loading at the contact patch.
- The **scab popping out** = **pitting / spalling failure**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics govern the quantitative analysis of contact fatigue:

- **Maximum Hertzian Contact Pressure ($p_0$)** — typically between **1.5 GPa and 4.0 GPa** in heavily loaded gear/bearing contacts.
- **Subsurface Orthogonal Shear Stress ($\tau_{0,max}$)** — maximum shear stress located at depth ≈ **0.48 a** (for point contact) or **0.78 b** (for line contact) below the surface.
- **Contact Pressure Ratio ($\Lambda$)** or **Lambda Ratio ($\lambda$)** — dimensionless parameter $\lambda = h_{min} / (R_q)$ characterizing the **lubrication regime**.
- **L10 Bearing Life** — the fatigue life at which **10%** of a population of nominally identical bearings will have failed.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU valuation, mentioning the **L10 / Weibull slope $b$** concept from the **ANSI/ABMA/ISO 281** standard instantly conveys examiner-level depth.

## 1.4 Three Lubrication Regimes (Critical Classification)

| Regime | Lambda $\lambda$ Value | Surface Separation | Typical Failure Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Boundary Lubrication** | $\lambda < 1$ | Direct asperity contact | **High** — adhesive wear + micropitting |
| **Mixed Lubrication** | $1 \le \lambda \le 3$ | Partial fluid film | **Medium** — micro-pitting likely |
| **Full-Film (EHL / Hydrodynamic)** | $\lambda > 3$ | Complete fluid separation | **Low** — fatigue is dominant, not wear |

This lubrication regime classification is the *primary decision lens* an examiner will use to grade any contact-fatigue analysis answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hertzian Contact Stress Field — Conceptual Breakdown

When two curved bodies are pressed together by a normal load $F_n$, the contact is NOT point-wise (which would give infinite stress) but rather spreads over a finite area because the bodies deform elastically. **Heinrich Hertz (1882)** solved this for two perfectly elastic, homogeneous, isotropic solids with smooth, non-conforming surfaces.

The result is a **semi-ellipsoidal** (point contact) or **semi-cylindrical** (line contact) pressure distribution that is **elliptical/parabolic** in shape and characterized by:
- **Maximum pressure $p_0$ at the center**.
- **Zero pressure at the periphery** of the contact patch.
- A **subsidiary shear-stress field** whose peak is *not* on the surface but **below** it.

### 2.1.1 Why the Maximum Shear Stress is Subsurface

This is a counterintuitive but critical fact that Board Examiners love to test:

- On the **surface**, hydrostatic compression dominates; the principal stresses are all compressive.
- **Just below the surface** at depth ≈ **0.48 a** (for point contact) or **0.78 b** (for line contact), the **orthogonal shear stress** reaches its maximum.
- Therefore, **cracks nucleate below the surface first**, not at the visible surface.

This is the **subsurface-origin failure mode**.

### 2.1.2 The Surface-Origin Exception

When surface conditions are *poor* (roughness, contamination, low lambda ratio, tensile residual stresses from grinding), the maximum shear stress *location shifts toward the surface*, and **cracks nucleate at the surface itself**. This produces **micropitting** and **grey staining** (a precursor to micropitting).

## 2.2 Stages of Contact Fatigue Failure

A complete contact fatigue failure proceeds through **four well-defined stages**, mirroring classical bulk fatigue:

1. **Stage I — Crack Initiation.** Nucleation at inclusions (typically **TiN, MnS, Al$_2$O$_3$**), voids, or surface defects. Time: ≈ 60–80% of total life.
2. **Stage II — Crack Propagation.** A shear-mode crack grows along the direction of principal shear stress, often at ≈ **30°–45°** to the surface, then turns to propagate parallel to the surface at ≈ depth of maximum shear.
3. **Stage III — Spalling / Flaking.** The crack branches upward to the surface, releasing a **flake-shaped piece of metal** that leaves a shallow crater ("pit" or "spall").
4. **Stage IV — Catastrophic Failure.** Spalls propagate, vibration and noise increase, the raceway or tooth flank is destroyed, leading to seizure or shaft fracture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU Part B answers, an examiner expects you to **draw the Hertzian contact patch, mark $p_0$, mark the depth of $\tau_{0,max}$, and arrow the crack trajectory at ≈ 30°–45°**. This single figure is worth 4–5 marks.

## 2.3 Failure Morphology Terminology (Examiner's Vocabulary)

| Term | Morphology | Location | Typical Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pitting** | Crater of diameter ≈ 0.5–3 mm | Pitch line of gear / bearing race | Subsurface inclusion-initiated |
| **Spalling** | Larger, irregular flake | Bearing raceway | Advanced pitting coalescence |
| **Micropitting** | Grey/black surface frosting, sub-100 µm | Surface | Mixed/boundary lubrication, low $\lambda$ |
| **Macropitting** | Visible craters > 1 mm | Tooth flank | Subsurface-origin, high Hertzian pressure |
| **White Etching Cracks (WEC)** | Brittle white-etching subsurface network | Below surface of bearings | Hydrogen embrittlement + slip-tongue cracks |
| **Brinelling** | Indentations matching ball spacing | Raceway | Static overload / shock loading |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe `|` symbol is forbidden inside table rows because it breaks markdown parsing. All absolute-value, determinant, and set-membership notations have been rewritten using LaTeX `\vert` and `\mid`.

### 2.4.1 Hertzian Contact Geometry

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduced Radius (Point Contact):} \quad \frac{1}{R'} &= \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \\
\text{Reduced Modulus:} \quad \frac{1}{E^{*}} &= \frac{1-\nu_1^{2}}{E_1} + \frac{1-\nu_2^{2}}{E_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4.2 Point Contact (Sphere-on-Sphere / Ball-on-Race)

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \left( \frac{3 F_n R'}{4 E^{*}} \right)^{1/3} \quad \text{(contact patch semi-axis)} \\
p_{0} &= \frac{3 F_n}{2 \pi a^{2}} \quad \text{(maximum contact pressure)} \\
\tau_{0,\max} &\approx 0.30 \, p_{0} \quad \text{at depth} \approx 0.48 a
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4.3 Line Contact (Cylinder-on-Cylinder / Roller)

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \sqrt{\frac{4 F_n R'}{\pi L E^{*}}} \quad \text{(half-contact width)} \\
p_{0} &= \frac{2 F_n}{\pi b L} \quad \text{(maximum pressure)} \\
\tau_{0,\max} &\approx 0.30 \, p_{0} \quad \text{at depth} \approx 0.78 b
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4.4 Bearing Fatigue Life (ISO 281)

$$
L_{10} = \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^{p}
$$

where $p = 3$ for ball bearings and $p = 10/3$ for roller bearings; $C$ = basic dynamic load rating, $P$ = equivalent dynamic load.

### 2.4.5 Lambda Ratio

$$
\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^{2} + R_{q2}^{2}}}
$$

where $R_q$ = RMS surface roughness.

### 2.4.6 Master Formula Table

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p_0 = \dfrac{3 F_n}{2 \pi a^{2}}$ | $F_n$ = normal load, $a$ = contact semi-axis | Point contact — center of patch |
| 2 | $a^{3} = \dfrac{3 F_n R'}{4 E^{*}}$ | $R'$ = reduced radius, $E^{*}$ = reduced modulus | Hertzian contact patch size |
| 3 | $p_0 = \dfrac{2 F_n}{\pi b L}$ | $b$ = half-width, $L$ = contact length | Line contact — center of strip |
| 4 | $\tau_{0,\max} \approx 0.30 \, p_0$ | depth ≈ $0.48 a$ or $0.78 b$ | Subsurface shear peak |
| 5 | $\tau_{xy,\max} \approx 0.32 \, p_0$ | at surface boundary | Surface-origin crack driver |
| 6 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^{2} + R_{q2}^{2}}}$ | $h_{min}$ = min film thickness | Lubrication regime |
| 7 | $L_{10} = (C/P)^{3}$ | Ball bearings | Basic rating life |
| 8 | $L_{10} = (C/P)^{10/3}$ | Roller bearings | Basic rating life |
| 9 | $\sigma_{H,\max} = \zeta \sqrt{\dfrac{F_n}{b} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R'} \cdot \dfrac{1}{L}}$ | AGMA gear contact stress | Gear flank pitting |
| 10 | $h_{min} \approx 7.43 \, (1 - 0.85 e^{\,-0.31 k}) \cdot \mu^{0.65} \, \alpha^{0.56} \, U^{0.69} \, W^{-0.10} \, R'$ | Dowson–Higginson EHL film thickness | Gear & bearing lubrication analysis |

> **Units reminder**: Force in **N**, length in **m**, pressure in **Pa (or MPa/GPa)**, modulus in **Pa**, Poisson's ratio is dimensionless, dimensionless exponents are unitless.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Contact fatigue analysis is foundational in:
- **Aerospace main-shaft bearings** — drives the design margin for jet engine mainshafts.
- **Wind-turbine gearboxes** — millions of variable-load tooth engagements, micropitting driven by fluctuating lambda ratio.
- **Railway axle-box bearings** — classic L10 life governs replacement intervals.
- **Hypoid gears in EV differentials** — sliding + rolling mixed contact, prone to micropitting.
- **Cam-follower systems in IC engines** — line contact, sliding-rolling combined.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Hertzian Point Contact Pressure

We consider two elastic spheres pressed together by normal force $F_n$. We aim to find the contact patch radius $a$ and the maximum contact pressure $p_0$.

### Step 1 — Set up the geometry

Let the two spheres have radii $R_1$ and $R_2$, Young's moduli $E_1$, $E_2$, and Poisson's ratios $\nu_1$, $\nu_2$. Define a **reduced radius** $R'$ such that the system behaves like a single sphere of radius $R'$ in contact with a rigid flat:

$$
\frac{1}{R'} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the reduced modulus

To combine two different materials into one equivalent stiffness, use the reduced modulus $E^{*}$:

$$
\frac{1}{E^{*}} = \frac{1 - \nu_{1}^{2}}{E_{1}} + \frac{1 - \nu_{2}^{2}}{E_{2}}
$$

This combines both elastic compliances into a single number.

### Step 3 — Assume the pressure distribution

Hertz postulated a **hemispherical (paraboloid of revolution) pressure profile** within a circular contact patch of radius $a$:

$$
p(r) = p_{0} \, \sqrt{1 - \frac{r^{2}}{a^{2}}}
$$

This shape is assumed, not derived. Its integral over the contact area must equal the total applied force.

### Step 4 — Apply the equilibrium of normal force

The total load must equal the integral of pressure over the patch area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_n &= \int_{0}^{a} p(r) \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr \\
F_n &= \int_{0}^{a} p_{0} \, \sqrt{1 - \frac{r^{2}}{a^{2}}} \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr \\
F_n &= 2 \pi p_{0} \int_{0}^{a} r \, \sqrt{1 - \frac{r^{2}}{a^{2}}} \, dr
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $u = 1 - r^{2}/a^{2}$, so $du = -2r \, dr / a^{2}$, giving $r \, dr = -a^{2} du / 2$. Limits: when $r = 0$, $u = 1$; when $r = a$, $u = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_n &= 2 \pi p_{0} \cdot \int_{1}^{0} \sqrt{u} \cdot \left(-\frac{a^{2}}{2}\right) du \\
F_n &= \pi p_{0} a^{2} \int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{u} \, du \\
F_n &= \pi p_{0} a^{2} \cdot \left[ \frac{2}{3} u^{3/2} \right]_{0}^{1} \\
F_n &= \frac{2}{3} \pi p_{0} a^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Equate to elastic displacement (compatibility)

The vertical displacement of either surface at the centre is given by elasticity (Boussinesq–Cerruti solution):

$$
\delta = \frac{1 - \nu^{2}}{\pi E} \cdot \frac{F_n}{a}
$$

For two bodies, this becomes (using the reduced modulus):

$$
\delta = \frac{F_n}{\pi E^{*} a}
$$

Simultaneously, geometric approach of two sphere centres (sum of two parabolic flattenings):

$$
\delta = \frac{a^{2}}{R'}
$$

### Step 6 — Eliminate $\delta$ to solve for $a$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{a^{2}}{R'} &= \frac{F_n}{\pi E^{*} a} \\
a^{3} &= \frac{F_n R'}{\pi E^{*}}
\end{aligned}
$$

But Hertz's exact solution gives:

$$
\boxed{\,a = \left( \frac{3 F_n R'}{4 E^{*}} \right)^{1/3}\,}
$$

### Step 7 — Solve for $p_0$

From $F_n = \tfrac{2}{3} \pi p_0 a^{2}$:

$$
\boxed{\,p_{0} = \frac{3 F_n}{2 \pi a^{2}}\,}
$$

This is the **maximum Hertzian contact pressure**.

### Step 8 — Subsurface stress at depth $z = 0.48 a$

By analysing the principal stress tensor under Hertzian pressure, the **maximum orthogonal shear stress** in the $zx$-plane occurs at:

$$
\tau_{0,\max} \approx 0.30 \, p_{0} \quad \text{at depth} \approx 0.48 a
$$

This is the **theoretical site of crack initiation** in subsurface-origin contact fatigue.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

**Problem:** A steel ball of radius $R_1 = 10 \text{ mm}$ is pressed against a flat steel raceway ($R_2 = \infty$) by a normal load $F_n = 500 \text{ N}$. Given $E_1 = E_2 = 210 \text{ GPa}$ and $\nu_1 = \nu_2 = 0.30$, calculate:
- (a) The contact patch radius $a$
- (b) The maximum contact pressure $p_0$
- (c) The depth and magnitude of the maximum shear stress

### Solution

**Step 1 — Reduced radius:**

$$
\frac{1}{R'} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} = \frac{1}{0.010} + 0 = 100 \text{ m}^{-1} \implies R' = 0.010 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Reduced modulus:**

$$
\frac{1}{E^{*}} = \frac{1 - 0.30^{2}}{210 \times 10^{9}} + \frac{1 - 0.30^{2}}{210 \times 10^{9}} = \frac{2 \times 0.91}{210 \times 10^{9}}
$$

$$
E^{*} = \frac{210 \times 10^{9}}{2 \times 0.91} = 1.154 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa} = 115.4 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Contact patch radius $a$:**

$$
a = \left( \frac{3 F_n R'}{4 E^{*}} \right)^{1/3} = \left( \frac{3 \times 500 \times 0.010}{4 \times 1.154 \times 10^{11}} \right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
a = \left( \frac{15}{4.616 \times 10^{11}} \right)^{1/3} = \left( 3.249 \times 10^{-11} \right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
a = 3.18 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.318 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum pressure $p_0$:**

$$
p_0 = \frac{3 F_n}{2 \pi a^{2}} = \frac{3 \times 500}{2 \pi \times (3.18 \times 10^{-4})^{2}} = \frac{1500}{6.357 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

$$
p_0 = 2.36 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa} = 2.36 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Depth of maximum shear stress:**

$$
z_{\tau_{0,\max}} = 0.48 \, a = 0.48 \times 0.318 = 0.153 \text{ mm below surface}
$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude of maximum shear stress:**

$$
\tau_{0,\max} = 0.30 \, p_{0} = 0.30 \times 2.36 = 0.708 \text{ GPa} \approx 708 \text{ MPa}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Although the applied load is only 500 N, the *local* shear stress reaching ≈ 708 MPa is enormous — easily exceeding the endurance limit of many engineering steels. This is why contact fatigue can occur at apparently "light" loads.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Influence Diagram

| Parameter | Increase ⇒ Effect on Contact Fatigue Life |
|---|---|
| Contact load $F_n$ ↑ | $p_0 \uparrow \propto F_n^{1/3}$; life $\downarrow$ sharply |
| Reduced radius $R'$ ↑ | Larger patch, lower $p_0$, life ↑ |
| Reduced modulus $E^{*}$ ↓ | Larger patch, lower $p_0$, life ↑ (elastomers better) |
| Surface roughness $R_q$ ↑ | $\lambda \downarrow$, micropitting risk ↑ |
| Viscosity $\mu$ ↑ | $h_{min} \uparrow$, $\lambda \uparrow$, life ↑ |
| Inclusion size ↑ | Crack-initiation threshold load ↓, life ↓ |
| Hardness ↑ | $p_0 \uparrow$ but endurance limit also ↑ — net life ↑ (up to ≈ 62 HRC) |
| Residual tensile stress ↑ | Crack initiation ↑, life ↓ |
| Sliding-to-rolling ratio ↑ | Frictional heating, $\lambda \downarrow$, micropitting ↑ |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define contact fatigue and distinguish it from conventional bulk fatigue.**

**Model Answer:**
Contact fatigue is the progressive fracture of a material's surface or sub-surface region under repeated Hertzian contact stresses generated by rolling, sliding, or combined loading. Unlike conventional bulk fatigue (rotating bending, axial loading) where the *entire* volume carries uniform tensile stress, in contact fatigue the stress field is **highly localized** at the contact patch and **translates** through the material with each cycle. The maximum damaging stress is a **subsurface shear stress** ≈ 0.30 $p_0$ at depth ≈ 0.48 $a$ for point contact, whereas in bulk fatigue the critical stress is the **surface tensile stress**. Crack initiation site, morphology, and propagation path are therefore entirely different.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the Lambda ratio? How is it used to classify the lubrication regime in a gear mesh?**

**Model Answer:**
The Lambda ratio $\lambda$ is defined as $\lambda = h_{min} / \sqrt{R_{q1}^{2} + R_{q2}^{2}}$, where $h_{min}$ is the minimum EHD film thickness and $R_q$ is the RMS surface roughness of the two contacting flanks. It is dimensionless. Classification:
- $\lambda < 1$: **Boundary lubrication** — asperity contact dominates.
- $1 \le \lambda \le 3$: **Mixed lubrication** — partial film, micropitting risk.
- $\lambda > 3$: **Full-film (EHL)** — fatigue (not wear) is the limiting failure mode.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]
**Question A:**
**(a)** Derive the expression for **maximum Hertzian contact pressure $p_0$** and **contact patch radius $a$** for two elastic spheres under a normal load $F_n$. State the assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 20 mm diameter steel ball is pressed against a flat steel plate with $F_n = 1000$ N. Given $E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$, calculate $a$, $p_0$, and the depth of maximum shear stress. Comment on the failure implications. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Derivation

**(See Section 3.1 above for the full 8-step derivation.)** The final boxed results are:

$$
\boxed{a = \left( \frac{3 F_n R'}{4 E^{*}} \right)^{1/3}}, \quad \boxed{p_{0} = \frac{3 F_n}{2 \pi a^{2}}}
$$

**Assumptions:**
- Materials are **homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic**.
- Surfaces are **smooth and frictionless** (no tangential traction).
- Contact area is **small** compared with body dimensions.
- Strains are **small** (linear elasticity valid).
- No surface tractions; **normal contact only**.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $R_1 = 10$ mm = 0.010 m, $R_2 = \infty$, $F_n = 1000$ N, $E_1 = E_2 = 210$ GPa, $\nu_1 = \nu_2 = 0.30$.

**Step 1 — Reduced radius:**

$$
R' = R_1 = 0.010 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Reduced modulus:**

$$
E^{*} = \frac{E}{2(1-\nu^{2})} = \frac{210 \times 10^{9}}{2 \times 0.91} = 115.4 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Contact patch radius $a$:**

$$
a = \left( \frac{3 \times 1000 \times 0.010}{4 \times 115.4 \times 10^{9}} \right)^{1/3} = \left( 6.49 \times 10^{-11} \right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
a = 4.02 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.402 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum pressure $p_0$:**

$$
p_0 = \frac{3 \times 1000}{2 \pi \times (4.02 \times 10^{-4})^{2}} = \frac{3000}{1.015 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
p_0 = 2.96 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Depth of maximum shear stress:**

$$
z = 0.48 \, a = 0.193 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude of maximum shear stress:**

$$
\tau_{0,\max} = 0.30 \times 2.96 = 0.888 \text{ GPa} \approx 888 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Failure Implication:** $\tau_{0,\max} \approx 888$ MPa exceeds the endurance limit of most through-hardened bearing steels (≈ 600–700 MPa). Subsurface-origin pitting fatigue is therefore expected at this loading condition. To improve life, increase the contact radius (use a larger ball), reduce load, or use a **cleaner vacuum-arc-remelted (VAR)** steel with fewer inclusions.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Question B:**
**(a)** Explain the **four stages of contact fatigue failure** with neat sketches. Compare **subsurface-origin** vs **surface-origin** failure modes. (7 marks)

**(b)** A gear pair operates with $p_0 = 1.8$ GPa at the pitch line. The minimum EHL film thickness is $h_{min} = 0.6 \, \mu$m and combined RMS roughness is $R_q = 0.3 \, \mu$m. Classify the lubrication regime and recommend a corrective action. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Four Stages of Contact Fatigue Failure

**Stage 1 — Crack Initiation (≈ 60–80% of life):**
A microcrack nucleates at a **subsurface inclusion** (TiN, MnS, Al$_2$O$_3$) or at a **surface defect** if $\lambda$ is low. Inclusion-driven initiation is governed by the **stress concentration around the inclusion** and by the **inclusion size distribution** (controlled by steel cleanliness ratings such as ASTM E45).

**Stage 2 — Crack Propagation in Shear:**
The crack grows along the **principal shear stress direction** at ≈ 30°–45° below the surface, propagating inward and along the rolling direction.

**Stage 3 — Crack Turn and Sub-surface Parallel Growth:**
The crack branches and turns to propagate **parallel to the surface** at depth ≈ 0.48 $a$, forming a sub-surface "butterfly wing" or "white etching area".

**Stage 4 — Spalling / Pitting Eruption:**
The crack finally turns upward, reaching the surface and releasing a flake of metal, leaving a **pit** of diameter 0.5–3 mm. Coalescence of pits produces large-scale spalling, leading to noise, vibration, and eventual seizure.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Subsurface-Origin | Surface-Origin |
|---|---|---|
| Initiation site | Inclusion below surface | Surface defect / asperity |
| Critical stress | $\tau_{0,\max}$ at 0.48 $a$ | Surface shear at patch edge |
| Controlling factor | Steel cleanliness | Surface finish + $\lambda$ |
| Morphology | Pitting, spalling | Micropitting, grey staining |
| Mitigation | VAR/ESR steel, lower $p_0$ | Higher $\lambda$, smoother finish |

#### Part (b) — Lubrication Regime Classification

**Given:** $h_{min} = 0.6 \, \mu$m, $R_q = 0.3 \, \mu$m.

**Lambda Ratio:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^{2} + R_{q2}^{2}}} = \frac{0.6}{0.3} = 2.0
$$

**Classification:** Since $1 \le \lambda \le 3$, the gear pair is in **mixed lubrication regime**. This regime is most dangerous for **micropitting** and **grey-staining failure**.

**Corrective Action Recommendations (each worth 1–2 marks in valuation):**

1. **Increase oil viscosity** (e.g., switch from ISO VG 100 to VG 320) to raise $h_{min}$ to ≈ 1.0 µm.
2. **Improve surface finish** (super-finish to $R_q \approx 0.1 \, \mu$m) to raise $\lambda$ to ≈ 5.
3. **Use EP (extreme-pressure) additive** with anti-micropitting chemistry (e.g., ZDDP + MoDTC).
4. **Reduce $p_0$** by widening the face width or using a larger module / helix angle correction.
5. **Apply surface-engineered coatings** such as DLC (diamond-like carbon) or CrN with lower roughness.

Target: achieve $\lambda > 3$ for full-film EHL operation.

---

## 5.4 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to square the Poisson's ratio** in the reduced modulus formula. The $1-\nu^{2}$ term is the #1 silent error.
> 2. **Confusing contact patch radius $a$** (in metres) with ball radius $R_1$ (in metres). A student once wrote $a = 0.010$ m — full 3-mark penalty.
> 3. **Reporting $\tau_{0,\max} = 0.5 \, p_0$** — the correct multiplier is **0.30**, not 0.5. This is a frequent textbook typo.
> 4. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** Always carry Pa, MPa, GPa explicitly.
> 5. **Confusing Hertzian pressure $p_0$** with **bearing dynamic load rating $C$**. They are different concepts ($C$ is in N, $p_0$ is in Pa).
> 6. **Omitting the diagram of the Hertzian pressure profile** in the 14-mark question. Examiners explicitly state in the scheme of evaluation that a sketch of $p(r)$ vs $r$ with $p_0$ marked at the centre carries **2 separate marks**.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Contact Fatigue (Module 4)**

- [ ] Contact fatigue arises from **cyclic Hertzian stresses**, NOT from bulk tensile loading.
- [ ] Maximum contact pressure $p_0 = 3 F_n / (2 \pi a^{2})$ for point contact.
- [ ] Contact patch radius $a = [3 F_n R' / (4 E^{*})]^{1/3}$.
- [ ] Subsurface shear peak: $\tau_{0,\max} \approx 0.30 \, p_0$ at depth **0.48 a** (point) or **0.78 b** (line).
- [ ] Surface shear peak: $\tau_{xy,\max} \approx 0.32 \, p_0$ at the trailing edge of the patch.
- [ ] Reduced radius: $1/R' = 1/R_1 + 1/R_2$.
- [ ] Reduced modulus: $1/E^{*} = (1-\nu_1^2)/E_1 + (1-\nu_2^2)/E_2$.
- [ ] Lambda ratio $\lambda = h_{min} / \sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}$ classifies lubrication regime.
- [ ] $\lambda < 1$: boundary (adhesive wear); $1 \le \lambda \le 3$: mixed (micropitting); $\lambda > 3$: full film (fatigue).
- [ ] L10 bearing life: $L_{10} = (C/P)^3$ (ball), $(C/P)^{10/3}$ (roller).
- [ ] Dowson–Higginson: $h_{min}$ increases with $\mu^{0.65}$, $U^{0.69}$, $R'^{1.0}$.
- [ ] **Four failure stages**: nucleation → shear propagation → parallel growth → spalling.
- [ ] **Subsurface origin** is governed by inclusion size distribution (steel cleanliness).
- [ ] **Surface origin** is governed by lambda ratio and surface roughness.
- [ ] **White Etching Cracks (WEC)** are driven by hydrogen + slip-tongue mechanism — premium bearing steels now specify "WEC-resistant" processing.
- [ ] **Hardness sweet-spot**: bearing steels peak in fatigue life at ≈ 58–62 HRC; higher hardness paradoxically reduces life due to retained austenite / quench cracks.
- [ ] **AGMA Hertzian contact stress** for gears: $\sigma_H = \zeta \sqrt{F_n E / (b \cos\phi \, R')}$.
- [ ] **Tip**: In any numerical, end with a **physical interpretation sentence** — it earns the "engineering judgment" marks KTU examiners now award under NEP 2020 outcome-based grading.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High-Temperature Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** High-Temperature Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**High-Temperature Effects** in failure analysis refer to the time-dependent, thermally activated degradation mechanisms that occur in engineering materials when they are subjected to sustained loads (mechanical, thermal, or chemical) at homologous temperatures generally exceeding **T/T_m > 0.4**, where T is the operating temperature and T_m is the absolute melting temperature of the material. These effects include **creep, stress rupture, high-temperature fatigue, thermal fatigue, oxidation, hot corrosion, microstructural instabilities (overaging, precipitate coarsening, graphitization, sigma-phase embrittlement), and thermal shock failure.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** "A material's high-temperature regime begins when atomic diffusion rates become significant enough to cause time-dependent plastic flow under static load, a phenomenon fundamentally absent in room-temperature design."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine stretching a **chewing gum stick** slowly between your fingers at room temperature — it stretches and returns (elastic). Now, place the same stick near a **hot oven** (without melting it completely) and pull it gently. Over many hours, it will slowly stretch permanently — and eventually snap. The material did not melt, but it "crept" to failure.

- The hotter the oven → the **faster** it stretches
- The harder you pull → the **faster** it fails
- The longer you wait → the **more it deforms**

This slow, silent, time-dependent permanent deformation is **CREEP** — the central villain of high-temperature failure analysis.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reference Values for KTU Numericals:**
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)
> - **Homologous Temperature Threshold:** T/T_m > 0.4 (steels), > 0.5 (ceramics), > 0.6 (polymers)
> - **Typical Creep Test Temperatures:** 0.3T_m to 0.8T_m
> - **Strain Rate Range of Engineering Interest:** 10⁻¹⁰ to 10⁻³ s⁻¹
> - **Activation Energy (Self-Diffusion, γ-Fe):** Q ≈ 280 kJ/mol
> - **Activation Energy (Lattice Diffusion):** Q_L ≈ 280 kJ/mol
> - **Activation Energy (Grain Boundary Diffusion):** Q_gb ≈ 0.6 Q_L

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Stages of Creep (Primary, Secondary, Tertiary)

When a material is loaded at constant stress and constant high temperature, strain evolves as a function of time following the **classic creep curve**:

### Stage I — Primary (Transient) Creep
- Strain rate **decreases** with time (work hardening dominates).
- Dislocation multiplication and tangling.
- Material exhibits **strain hardening** faster than recovery.

### Stage II — Secondary (Steady-State) Creep
- Strain rate is **constant and minimum** (ε̇ = ε̇_ss = ε̇_min).
- Dynamic equilibrium between **work hardening and recovery (annihilation)**.
- This is the **design-relevant regime** — most engineering lifetime is calculated here.
- Governed by **Norton's Power Law**.

### Stage III — Tertiary Creep
- Strain rate **accelerates rapidly** until rupture.
- Causes: internal cavitation, grain boundary separation, necking, precipitate coarsening, oxidation-assisted cracking.
- Terminated by **rupture (failure)**.

## 2.2 Mechanisms of Creep (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Mechanism | Operative Range | Governing Equation (Norton form) |
|---|---|---|
| **Dislocation (Power-Law) Creep** | Intermediate σ, T > 0.5T_m | ε̇ = A σⁿ exp(-Q/RT), n = 3–8 |
| **Diffusion Creep (Nabarro-Herring)** | Low σ, fine grain, T > 0.5T_m | ε̇ ∝ (σΩ D_L d⁻²) exp(-Q_L/RT) |
| **Diffusion Creep (Coble)** | Low σ, fine grain, lower T | ε̇ ∝ (σΩ D_gb δ d⁻³) exp(-Q_gb/RT) |
| **Grain Boundary Sliding** | T > 0.5T_m, contributes to ε̇ | Contributes 30–60% of total strain |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** At high stress and high temperature, **dislocation climb** controls creep. At low stress and very fine grains, **Nabarro-Herring** or **Coble** diffusion creep dominates. Selection of **large grains or single crystals** suppresses diffusion creep — this is why **turbine blades are directionally solidified or single-crystal**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No `|` symbols used. Use `\vert` for absolute value notation.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = A \sigma^n \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Norton's Power Law (Secondary Creep) | s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\log \dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = \log A + n \log \sigma - \dfrac{Q}{2.303\,RT}$ | Linearized form for graphical analysis | — |
| 3 | $P_{LM} = T \left[\log t_r + C\right]$ | Larson-Miller Parameter (t_r in hours) | K·log(h) |
| 4 | $P_{SD} = \dfrac{Q}{2.303 R} \cdot \left[\log t_r - \log \epsilon_{ss}\right]$ | Sherby-Dorn Parameter | K |
| 5 | $P_{MH} = \dfrac{\log t_r - \log t_a}{T - T_a}$ | Manson-Haferd Parameter | log(h)/K |
| 6 | $\dfrac{t_{r1}}{t_{r2}} = \exp\left[\dfrac{Q}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$ | Life-fraction (Dorn) rule for isothermal comparison | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} \cdot t_r^{m} = C_{MG}$ | Monkman-Grant Relationship (m ≈ 0.85–1.0) | constant |
| 8 | $t_f = \dfrac{\epsilon_{r}}{\dot{\epsilon}_{ss}}$ | Approximate rupture life (Robinson's rule) | s |
| 9 | $\sigma = \sigma_0 \left(\dfrac{\dot{\epsilon}}{A}\right)^{1/n} \exp\left(\dfrac{Q}{nRT}\right)$ | Inverse Norton for design stress | MPa |
| 10 | $\Delta \sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E \alpha \Delta T}{1 - \nu}$ | Thermal stress (constrained bar) | MPa |
| 11 | $\sigma_{design} = \dfrac{\sigma_{rupture}}{n_{creep}}$ | Creep design with safety factor n_creep ≥ 1.5–2.5 | MPa |

Where:
- **A** = structure-dependent material constant
- **n** = stress exponent (3–8 for dislocation creep, 1 for diffusional creep)
- **Q** = activation energy for creep (J/mol)
- **T** = absolute temperature (K)
- **R** = 8.314 J/(mol·K)
- **C** = Larson-Miller constant ≈ 20 (for steels), 15–25 (Ni-based superalloys)
- **d** = grain diameter (m), **Ω** = atomic volume, **δ** = GB width ≈ 2 atomic diameters

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Applications of High-Temperature Failure Analysis:**
> - **Power Generation:** Boiler tubes (T = 540–620 °C), superheater headers, steam turbine blades
> - **Aerospace:** Jet engine turbine blades (T = 900–1100 °C), combustion chambers, afterburners
> - **Petrochemical:** Fired heater tubes, reformer catalyst tubes, ethylene cracking coils
> - **Nuclear:** Zircaloy fuel cladding, reactor pressure vessel internals
> - **Automotive:** Exhaust manifolds, turbocharger housings, diesel engine valves

**The economic impact is enormous:** A single unplanned outage of a 500 MW boiler due to a creep rupture can cost **$1–5 million per day** in lost generation. Hence, **creep life prediction** is one of the most heavily tested topics in KTU failure analysis examinations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Norton's Power Law from Dislocation Climb

### Step 1: Orowan's Equation for Strain Rate

The macroscopic strain rate is related to the motion of dislocations:

$$
\dot{\epsilon} = \rho_b \, b \, v
$$

where:
- $\rho_b$ = density of mobile dislocations (m⁻²)
- $b$ = Burgers vector magnitude (m)
- $v$ = average dislocation velocity (m/s)

### Step 2: Relate Dislocation Velocity to Stress via Climb

Dislocation climb is the rate-controlling step in high-temperature creep. The climb velocity is given by:

$$
v_{climb} = \dfrac{D_L \, \sigma \, b^3}{k_B \, T}
$$

The climb force per unit length on a dislocation is $F = \sigma \, b$, and the diffusion flux of vacancies drives the climb. Net dislocation velocity $v \propto v_{climb}$:

$$
v = \beta \cdot \dfrac{D_L \, \sigma \, b^3}{k_B \, T}
$$

where $D_L = D_0 \exp(-Q_L / RT)$ is the lattice self-diffusion coefficient.

### Step 3: Dislocation Density as a Function of Stress

Empirically and from hardening theory, mobile dislocation density scales with applied stress:

$$
\rho_b = \rho_0 \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\sigma_0}\right)^2
$$

This reflects the Taylor relation $\tau = \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho_b}$.

### Step 4: Combine via Orowan

Substitute into the Orowan equation:

$$
\dot{\epsilon} = \rho_b \, b \, v = \rho_0 \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\sigma_0}\right)^2 \cdot b \cdot \beta \cdot \dfrac{D_L \, \sigma \, b^3}{k_B \, T}
$$

Group the terms:

$$
\dot{\epsilon} = \underbrace{\dfrac{\rho_0 \, \beta \, b^4 \, D_0}{k_B \, T \, \sigma_0^2}}_{A} \cdot \sigma^{3} \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q_L}{RT}\right)
$$

### Step 5: Final Norton's Power Law Form

$$
\boxed{\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = A \, \sigma^{n} \, \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)}
$$

with **n = 3** for climb-controlled dislocation creep (typical for many pure metals and Class M alloys). In dispersion-strengthened superalloys, **n can rise to 5–8**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Larson-Miller Parameter

### Step 1: Assume Steady-State Creep Governs Rupture

If secondary creep is the dominant life-consuming stage, then rupture time $t_r$ is inversely proportional to the steady-state strain rate:

$$
t_r \propto \dfrac{1}{\dot{\epsilon}_{ss}}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute Norton's Law

$$
t_r = \dfrac{1}{A \sigma^n \exp(-Q/RT)} = \dfrac{1}{A \sigma^n} \exp\left(\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)
$$

### Step 3: Take Natural Logarithm

$$
\ln t_r = -\ln A - n \ln \sigma + \dfrac{Q}{RT}
$$

### Step 4: Rearrange into the Standard Larson-Miller Form

Multiply by 2.303 (to convert to base-10 log) and rearrange:

$$
2.303 \log t_r = -2.303 \log A - 2.303 \, n \log \sigma + \dfrac{Q}{RT}
$$

$$
\dfrac{Q}{2.303 R} = T \left[ \log t_r + \log A + n \log \sigma \right]
$$

Define the **Larson-Miller parameter**:

$$
\boxed{P_{LM} = T \left[ \log t_r + C \right] = \text{function of stress alone}}
$$

where $C = 2.303 \log A$ is the material constant (≈ **20** for most steels and Ni-based superalloys).

**Interpretation:** All combinations of (T, t_r) that give the same P_LM will result in the same rupture stress. This unifies short-term high-T tests with long-term low-T service.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Monkman-Grant Relationship

From numerous experimental observations:

$$
\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} \cdot t_r^{m} = C_{MG}
$$

Taking logarithms:

$$
\log \dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = \log C_{MG} - m \log t_r
$$

For many engineering alloys, **m ≈ 0.85 to 1.0** and **C_MG** is a constant. This empirical relation allows estimation of rupture life from a single short-term creep test (e.g., 1000-hour test extrapolated to 100,000-hour design life).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical:** A 1Cr-0.5Mo steel component operates at 600 °C under a steady stress of 80 MPa. Given: n = 5, A = 1.0 × 10⁻¹² MPa⁻⁵ s⁻¹ (at reference T), Q = 240 kJ/mol, C = 20, E = 200 GPa, α = 12 × 10⁻⁶ /K. Estimate (a) the steady-state creep rate, (b) the Larson-Miller rupture parameter, and (c) the predicted rupture time at this stress.

### (a) Steady-State Creep Rate

Temperature: T = 600 + 273 = **873 K**

$$
\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = A \, \sigma^n \, \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)
$$

Compute the exponent:

$$
\dfrac{Q}{RT} = \dfrac{240{,}000}{8.314 \times 873} = \dfrac{240{,}000}{7258.1} = 33.07
$$

Exponential term:

$$
\exp(-33.07) = 4.41 \times 10^{-15}
$$

Stress term:

$$
\sigma^n = 80^5 = 3{,}276{,}800{,}000 = 3.277 \times 10^{9} \text{ MPa}^{5}
$$

Now combine:

$$
\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = 1.0 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.277 \times 10^{9} \times 4.41 \times 10^{-15}
$$

$$
\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = 1.445 \times 10^{-17} \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** This is an extremely low strain rate — typical of a well-designed boiler tube in secondary creep.

### (b) Larson-Miller Parameter

Assume we wish to operate the component safely. The rupture time $t_r$ depends on the alloy's rupture data. For a typical 1Cr-0.5Mo at 80 MPa and 600 °C, experimental rupture data give $t_r \approx 100{,}000$ h.

$$
P_{LM} = T \left[\log t_r + C\right] = 873 \left[\log(100{,}000) + 20\right]
$$

$$
P_{LM} = 873 \left[5 + 20\right] = 873 \times 25 = 21{,}825 \text{ K·log(h)}
$$

### (c) Predicted Rupture Time from L-M

If the same alloy has a Larson-Miller constant C = 20 and the data at another temperature (say 650 °C = 923 K) gives the same P_LM, the rupture time at 600 °C is:

$$
t_r = 10^{(P_{LM}/T - C)} = 10^{(21825/873 - 20)} = 10^{25 - 20} = 10^{5} \text{ hours}
$$

This matches the design life.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The High-Temperature Failure Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Service at T greater than 0.4 Tm] --> B[Atomic Diffusion Becomes Significant]
    B --> C[Primary Creep Stage]
    B --> D[Microstructural Evolution]
    B --> E[Environmental Attack]

    C --> F[Secondary Steady State Creep]
    F --> G[Tertiary Creep Acceleration]

    D --> H1[Precipitate Coarsening Ostwald Ripening]
    D --> H2[Grain Growth]
    D --> H3[Sigma Phase Formation]
    D --> H4[Graphitization]

    E --> I1[Surface Oxidation]
    E --> I2[Hot Corrosion Sulfidation]
    E --> I3[Carburization]

    G --> J1[Internal Cavitation]
    G --> J2[Grain Boundary Wedge Cracks]
    G --> J3[Necking]

    H1 --> K[Loss of Strengthening]
    H2 --> K
    H3 --> K
    H4 --> K

    I1 --> L[Section Loss]
    I2 --> L
    I3 --> L

    K --> M[Final Rupture]
    J1 --> M
    J2 --> M
    J3 --> M
    L --> M

    M --> N[Design Intervention Required]
    N --> O1[Material Upgrade Superalloy]
    N --> O2[Reduce Operating Stress]
    N --> O3[Lower Operating Temperature]
    N --> O4[Apply Protective Coating]
    N --> O5[Redesign Geometry Reduce Triaxiality]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: The Three Stages of Creep and Their Governing Physics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Stage 1 Primary Creep]
        A1[Loading at T high] --> A2[Dislocation Multiplication]
        A2 --> A3[Work Hardening Dominates]
        A3 --> A4[Strain Rate Decreases with Time]
    end

    subgraph S2[Stage 2 Secondary Steady State]
        B1[Hardening balanced by Recovery] --> B2[Climb and Cross Slip Active]
        B2 --> B3[Constant Minimum Strain Rate]
        B3 --> B4[Norton Power Law Region]
    end

    subgraph S3[Stage 3 Tertiary]
        C1[Internal Damage Accumulation] --> C2[Cavitation and Necking]
        C2 --> C3[Strain Rate Accelerates]
        C3 --> C4[Rupture]
    end

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3

    B4 -.->|used for design| D[Creep Life Prediction via L-M Parameter]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Logic: Material Selection for High-Temperature Service

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Design Temperature and Stress Known]) --> Q1{Operating Temperature}
    Q1 -->|below 400 C| P1[Carbon Steel or Low Alloy]
    Q1 -->|400 to 600 C| P2[Cr-Mo Steels 1Cr 2.25Cr 9Cr]
    Q1 -->|600 to 800 C| P3[Austenitic SS 304H 321H 347H]
    Q1 -->|800 to 1000 C| P4[Ni-Fe Superalloys Inconel 600]
    Q1 -->|above 1000 C| P5[Ni-Based Superalloys Inconel 718 Waspaloy]
    Q1 -->|above 1100 C| P6[Single Crystal Superalloys CMSX-4 Rene N5]

    P2 --> Check1[Hot Corrosion Risk?]
    Check1 -->|Yes| Coat1[Apply Aluminide Coating]
    Check1 -->|No| Use1[Direct Use]

    P5 --> Check2[Stress Level?]
    Check2 -->|High| DS[Directionally Solidified Blades]
    Check2 -->|Very High| SX[Single Crystal Blades]

    P6 --> Coat2[TBC Thermal Barrier Coating Required]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question Paper]**

### Q1. Define creep and state the homologous temperature above which creep becomes a design concern for steels.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Creep is the time-dependent permanent deformation of a material subjected to a constant load or stress at elevated temperature. The deformation accumulates even though the applied stress may be well below the yield strength measured at room temperature.
- **Homologous threshold (1 mark):** For steels, creep becomes a design concern when the homologous temperature $T / T_m > 0.4$, where T is in Kelvin. For example, plain carbon steel (T_m ≈ 1530 °C ≈ 1803 K) begins to show significant creep above **~720 K (~447 °C)**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question Paper]**

### Q2. What is the Larson-Miller parameter? Why is it used in high-temperature design?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The Larson-Miller parameter is an empirical time-temperature parameter used to correlate creep rupture data, defined as $P_{LM} = T \left[\log t_r + C\right]$, where T is in Kelvin, $t_r$ is the rupture time in hours, and C ≈ 20 for most engineering alloys.
- **Use 1 (1 mark):** It enables extrapolation of short-duration high-temperature test data to predict long-term service life, since direct testing for 100,000–200,000 hours is impractical.
- **Use 2 (1 mark):** It unifies temperature and time into a single parameter, allowing construction of a **master rupture curve** of stress vs. P_LM that is valid across a wide range of service conditions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — University Question Paper Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Creep Mechanisms, Stages & Norton's Law*

#### (a) Draw a typical creep curve and explain the three stages of creep. Derive Norton's power law for steady-state creep. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Creep Curve (3 marks):**
The creep curve is a plot of strain (ε) vs. time (t) at constant stress (σ) and constant temperature (T). It exhibits three stages:

- **Stage I — Primary Creep:** Strain increases at a **decreasing rate**. Work hardening from dislocation tangling dominates over recovery. Dislocation density rises rapidly. The strain rate is initially high but decays exponentially.

- **Stage II — Secondary (Steady-State) Creep:** Strain increases at a **constant minimum rate** $\dot{\epsilon}_{ss}$. A dynamic balance exists between **strain hardening** and **thermal recovery** (annihilation of dislocations by climb and cross-slip). This stage typically consumes the largest fraction of the component's life and is the basis for design calculations.

- **Stage III — Tertiary Creep:** Strain rate **accelerates rapidly** until rupture. Mechanisms include internal void nucleation at grain boundaries, cavity growth, microcrack coalescence, necking of the cross-section, and loss of strengthening due to precipitate coarsening.

**Derivation of Norton's Power Law (4 marks):**

1. From Orowan's equation: $\dot{\epsilon} = \rho_b b v$
2. Mobile dislocation density scales as: $\rho_b = \rho_0 (\sigma / \sigma_0)^2$
3. Climb-controlled dislocation velocity: $v = \beta D_L \sigma b^3 / (k_B T)$
4. Lattice diffusivity: $D_L = D_0 \exp(-Q_L / RT)$
5. Substituting and grouping:

$$
\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = A \sigma^{n} \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)
$$

with $n = 3$ for climb-controlled pure metals and $n = 5–8$ for dispersion-strengthened alloys.

#### (b) A superalloy component operates at 800 °C under a stress of 150 MPa. The Norton constants are A = 5 × 10⁻⁴⁰ MPa⁻⁵ s⁻¹, n = 5, Q = 300 kJ/mol. Calculate the steady-state creep rate. Given Larson-Miller constant C = 22, predict the rupture life. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Temperature: $T = 800 + 273 = 1073$ K

**Steady-State Creep Rate (4 marks):**

$$
\dfrac{Q}{RT} = \dfrac{300{,}000}{8.314 \times 1073} = \dfrac{300{,}000}{8920.9} = 33.63
$$

$$
\exp(-33.63) = 2.62 \times 10^{-15}
$$

$$
\sigma^{n} = 150^{5} = 7.594 \times 10^{10} \text{ MPa}^{5}
$$

$$
\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = 5 \times 10^{-40} \times 7.594 \times 10^{10} \times 2.62 \times 10^{-15}
$$

$$
\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = 9.95 \times 10^{-44} \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

This is an extremely low rate, indicating excellent creep resistance.

**Rupture Life (3 marks):** Using Robinson's rule with a typical rupture ductility $\epsilon_r \approx 0.10$ (10%):

$$
t_f = \dfrac{\epsilon_r}{\dot{\epsilon}_{ss}} = \dfrac{0.10}{9.95 \times 10^{-44}} = 1.005 \times 10^{42} \text{ seconds}
$$

Converting:

$$
t_f = \dfrac{1.005 \times 10^{42}}{3600 \times 24 \times 365} \approx 3.18 \times 10^{34} \text{ years}
$$

This unphysically high value shows the limitations of simple Robinson extrapolation; **L-M parameter** is preferred. Using $P_{LM}$ and the alloy's master curve, a more realistic estimate is **50,000–100,000 hours** for a Ni-based superalloy at 800 °C / 150 MPa.

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *High-Temperature Damage Mechanisms & Design*

#### (a) Explain the different mechanisms of high-temperature failure in metallic components. Discuss the role of oxidation, hot corrosion, and microstructural instability. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Creep and Stress Rupture (2 marks):** The dominant mechanical degradation. As discussed above, time-dependent flow at constant load ultimately leads to rupture. Intergranular cavity formation at GBs perpendicular to the stress axis is the classical fracture mode.

**2. Oxidation (2 marks):** At high temperatures, metals react with atmospheric oxygen to form oxides. The rate follows parabolic kinetics $\Delta m^2 = k_p t$ for protective scales (Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃, SiO₂) or linear kinetics for non-protective scales (FeO). Loss of effective cross-section reduces load-bearing capacity. **Design rule:** Select alloys that form **continuous, adherent, slow-growing, and stress-tolerant** scales. Alumina formers (Ni-Al, FeCrAlY) outperform chromia formers above 1000 °C.

**3. Hot Corrosion (1.5 marks):** Accelerated oxidation induced by molten salt deposits (Na₂SO₄, V₂O₅) in combustion environments (e.g., gas turbines, marine engines). Two types: **Type I** (above 900 °C, basic fluxing) and **Type II** (600–800 °C, acidic fluxing). Counter-measures: high-Cr content, MCrAlY coatings, TBCs.

**4. Microstructural Instabilities (1.5 marks):** Long-term exposure causes:
- **Overaging & precipitate coarsening (Ostwald ripening):** Loss of γ' strengthening in Ni-superalloys
- **Sigma-phase formation:** Hard, brittle Fe-Cr-Mo phase embrittles stainless steels after 10,000+ hours
- **Graphitization:** Carbon steels in 400–600 °C service convert carbides to graphite, drastically reducing creep life
- **Cavitation at grain boundaries** — wedge cracks, r-type voids

#### (b) A boiler superheater tube is made of 2.25Cr-1Mo steel and is designed for 100,000 hours of service at 540 °C. The Larson-Miller constant is C = 22. From short-term tests, the rupture stress at P_LM = 20,000 is 100 MPa. Calculate (i) the maximum allowable stress for the design life, and (ii) whether the design is safe if the actual hoop stress is 70 MPa. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Maximum Allowable Stress (3.5 marks):**

Calculate the P_LM for design life:

$$
P_{LM} = T \left[\log t_r + C\right] = (540 + 273) \left[\log(100{,}000) + 22\right]
$$

$$
P_{LM} = 813 \times [5 + 22] = 813 \times 27 = 21{,}951 \text{ K·log(h)}
$$

**The short-term data is at P_LM = 20,000**, but our design needs P_LM = 21,951 — i.e., a **higher L-M value** at the same temperature requires **lower stress**. Using the standard master curve slope for 2.25Cr-1Mo:

$$
\dfrac{d(\log \sigma)}{dP_{LM}} \approx -2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per K·log(h) in this range}
$$

$$
\Delta(\log \sigma) = -2.0 \times 10^{-4} \times (21{,}951 - 20{,}000) = -0.390
$$

$$
\sigma_{allow} = 100 \times 10^{-0.390} = 100 \times 0.407 = 40.7 \text{ MPa}
$$

Applying a safety factor of 2:

$$
\sigma_{design} \approx \dfrac{40.7}{2} = 20.4 \text{ MPa (conservative)}
$$

**(ii) Safety Check (3.5 marks):**

Actual hoop stress = 70 MPa > 40.7 MPa (rupture) and vastly > 20.4 MPa (design).

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert °C → K (K = °C + 273). This single error cascades through every calculation. **Always convert first.**
> - **Mistake 2:** Using $\log_{10}$ vs $\ln$ inconsistently. Norton's law may use either, but the Larson-Miller parameter is **strictly base-10 logarithm**. Mixing them is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> - **Mistake 3:** Omitting units in intermediate steps. The examiner expects to see MPa⁻⁵ s⁻¹ for A, J/mol for Q, and J/(mol·K) for R. Unitless answers get **partial credit only**.
> - **Mistake 4:** Confusing **stress exponent n** (Norton's law) with **strain-hardening exponent n** (Hollomon's equation $\sigma = K \epsilon^n$). They are entirely different quantities in entirely different contexts.
> - **Mistake 5:** Reporting rupture time in seconds when the L-M parameter requires **hours**. Always convert to hours.
> - **Mistake 6:** Ignoring the **safety factor**. KTU examiners typically deduct **1 mark** if a real engineering design is reported without a safety factor n_creep.

**Conclusion:** The component is **NOT SAFE** with a 70 MPa hoop stress. The operating stress must be reduced to **~20 MPa** (with n = 2) or the material upgraded to a higher-grade alloy (e.g., 9Cr-1Mo-V or austenitic SS).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Pin This on Your Wall!):**

- [ ] **Definition:** Creep = time-dependent permanent deformation at $T > 0.4\,T_m$ under constant load.
- [ ] **Three Stages:** Primary (decelerating) → Secondary (steady, design-relevant) → Tertiary (accelerating, leads to rupture).
- [ ] **Master Equation:** $\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = A \sigma^n \exp(-Q / RT)$. Memorize it in **two forms** — direct and linearized.
- [ ] **Stress Exponent n:** n = 1 (diffusion creep), 3 (dislocation climb, pure metals), 5–8 (particle-strengthened alloys).
- [ ] **Larson-Miller:** $P_{LM} = T [\log t_r + C]$, with C ≈ 20. Master curve is **σ vs P_LM**.
- [ ] **Sherby-Dorn:** Alternative parameter based on $\log t_r - \log \dot{\epsilon}_{ss}$. Used when steady-state rate is well-characterized.
- [ ] **Manson-Haferd:** Linearizes data with two constants $t_a$ and $T_a$. Often used for complex alloys with non-linear L-M behavior.
- [ ] **Monkman-Grant:** $\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} \cdot t_r^m = C_{MG}$. Quick life prediction from one short test.
- [ ] **Robinson's Rule:** $t_f = \epsilon_r / \dot{\epsilon}_{ss}$. First-order estimate; over-predicts for low-ductility alloys.
- [ ] **Microstructural Instabilities to Remember:** Ostwald ripening of γ', sigma phase, graphitization, carbide degeneration.
- [ ] **Environmental Attack:** Oxidation (parabolic kinetics), hot corrosion (Type I and II), carburization, nitridation.
- [ ] **Design Counter-Measures:** Material upgrade (single-crystal superalloys), TBCs (thermal barrier coatings), MCrAlY bond coats, lower operating stress, larger grain size, controlled impurity levels.
- [ ] **Safety Factor:** Always use $n_{creep} \geq 1.5$ to 2.5 in design. Unfactored rupture stress is **not** a design stress.
- [ ] **Units Discipline:** Q in J/mol, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), T in K, $t_r$ in **hours** for L-M, σ in MPa.

> **End of Module 4 — High-Temperature Effects Notes**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Corrosion** is defined (per ISO 8044 and ASTM G15) as the *physico-chemical interaction between a metal and its environment* which results in a partial or complete degradation of the engineering properties of the metal. In thermodynamic terms, it is the *reversion of a refined metal to its thermodynamically more stable oxide, sulphide, carbonate, or hydroxide form* through an electrochemical (or in some rare cases, purely chemical) reaction pathway.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Examiners frequently ask students to distinguish between *corrosion* (electrochemical, requires electrolyte) and *oxidation* (direct chemical reaction with O₂). Marks are awarded for explicitly stating that corrosion is fundamentally an **electrochemical process** involving simultaneous **anodic dissolution** and **cathodic reduction** reactions coupled by an ionic current through an electrolyte and an electronic current through the metal.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Iron Lung of Metals"

Imagine you spent enormous energy at a steel plant to *lift* iron ore (Fe₂O₃) up an energy hill — converting brittle, useless rock into strong, ductile structural steel. **Corrosion is simply the iron "rolling back downhill"** to its native, low-energy oxide state. Every scratch, every crack, every raindrop is providing an opportunity for that downhill slide.

Think of a wet boat hull in seawater:
- The **hull metal** = the high-energy platform (anode region).
- The **seawater** = the conductive electrolyte.
- The **brass propeller** in contact with the hull = a different metal (cathode region).
- A **battery forms** between them, and the current that flows literally *dissolves your boat*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric Used by Engineers:** Corrosion rate is universally reported in **mpy (mils per year)** in the USA or **mm/year** in SI units. Industrial acceptance limits for carbon steel in atmospheric service are typically **≤ 5 mpy (≈ 0.13 mm/year)**.

## 1.3 Why Corrosion Matters in Failure Analysis

| Sector | Annual Loss / Risk | Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Oil & Gas (offshore) | Pipeline ruptures | Pitting + SCC |
| Bridges (infrastructure) | Section loss in rebars | Chloride-induced |
| Aerospace (riveted Al) | Exfoliation, fatigue | Intergranular |
| Marine propellers | Cavitation-erosion | Synergistic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The global direct cost of corrosion is estimated at **~3–4 % of the world's GDP** (~$2.5 trillion/year). For an engineer designing a component, *predicting and mitigating corrosion* is as essential as the mechanical stress calculation itself.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrochemical Cell of Corrosion

A corrosion cell is, by definition, a **short-circuited galvanic cell**. It has four mandatory components — remove any one and corrosion stops:

1. **Anode** — the site of *oxidation* (M → M^n+ + ne⁻). Metal dissolves here.
2. **Cathode** — the site of *reduction* (e.g., O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻).
3. **Electrolyte** — ionic conductor (water, soil, salt solutions) connecting the two electrodes.
4. **Metallic path** — the parent metal itself provides the electron conduction between anode and cathode.

### 2.1.1 Common Cathodic Reactions

$$\text{Dissolved oxygen (neutral/alkaline):} \quad O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^- \quad E^0 = +0.40 \text{ V (SHE)}$$

$$\text{Dissolved oxygen (acidic):} \quad O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O \quad E^0 = +1.23 \text{ V (SHE)}$$

$$\text{Hydrogen evolution (acidic):} \quad 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 \quad E^0 = 0.00 \text{ V (SHE)}$$

$$\text{Metal ion reduction:}\quad M^{n+} + ne^- \rightarrow M$$

## 2.2 Thermodynamic Tendency — The Nernst Equation

To know *whether* corrosion can occur, we calculate the cell potential:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{a_{oxidized}}{a_{reduced}} \right) \\
\text{At standard state (T = 298 K):}\quad E &= E^0 + \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} \left( \frac{a_{ox}}{a_{red}} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

Where **R = 8.314 J/mol·K**, **F = 96485 C/mol (Faraday's constant)**, *n* = electrons transferred, and *a* = activity (≈ concentration for dilute solutions).

> [!NOTE]
> A *positive* ΔE (E_cathode − E_anode) means the reaction is thermodynamically **spontaneous**. A *negative* ΔE means corrosion is thermodynamically **not favored** under those conditions (refer to **Pourbaix diagrams** for pH dependence).

## 2.3 Kinetics — The Mixed Potential Theory (Evans Diagram)

Thermodynamics tells us *if* corrosion will happen. Kinetics tells us *how fast*. The intersection of anodic and cathodic polarization curves on a **Evans diagram** gives:

- **E_corr** — the corrosion potential (mixed potential).
- **i_corr** — the corrosion current density (A/cm²), which is directly proportional to mass-loss rate.

### 2.3.1 Tafel Equation (Activation Polarization)

$$\eta = a \pm b \log_{10}(i)$$

Where *η* is the overpotential, *a* and *b* are Tafel constants. This relation is used in **potentiodynamic polarization** tests to extrapolate back to E_corr and obtain i_corr.

## 2.4 Faraday's Laws and Corrosion Rate

Faraday's First Law: *the mass of substance liberated at an electrode is proportional to the total electric charge passed*.

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

Faraday's Second Law: *the mass liberated is proportional to the equivalent weight*.

$$\frac{m_1}{m_2} = \frac{E_1}{E_2} \qquad \text{where } E = \frac{M}{n} = \text{equivalent weight (g/eq)}$$

### 2.4.1 Engineering Formulas for Corrosion Rate

Using Faraday's law and dimensional conversions, the corrosion rate in practical engineering units becomes:

$$\boxed{\;CR \;(\text{mpy}) = \frac{534 \, W}{D \, A \, T}\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;CR \;(\text{mm/yr}) = \frac{87.6 \, W}{D \, A \, T}\;}$$

Where:
- **W** = mass loss in **mg**
- **D** = density of the metal in **g/cm³**
- **A** = exposed surface area in **in²** (for mpy) or **cm²** (for mm/yr)
- **T** = exposure time in **hours**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Magic Numbers Demystified:** The constants 534 and 87.6 come from combining Faraday's constant (96485 C/mol), the conversion of hours → years, and the thickness conversion (mils or mm). Students should *memorize* the formula but understand its origin for KTU derivations.

## 2.5 Pourbaix Diagrams (pH–Potential Maps)

These diagrams (plotted for a given metal at 25 °C and unit activity of dissolved species) divide the stability fields into:

| Zone | Meaning | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Immunity** | Metal is thermodynamically stable | Cathodic protection target |
| **Corrosion** | Soluble metal ions exist | Material degrades |
| **Passivation** | Stable insoluble oxide/hydroxide film | Protective layer (e.g., Al₂O₃ on Al) |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often asks: *"For a steel structure at pH 8 in aerated water, would you expect corrosion or passivation?"* — Refer to the Fe–H₂O Pourbaix diagram.

## 2.6 Forms / Types of Corrosion (KTU Frequently Tested)

The **Eight Forms** recognized by NACE/ASM are:

1. **Uniform (General) Attack** — even thinning; easiest to predict.
2. **Galvanic (Dissimilar Metal) Corrosion** — driven by potential difference when two metals are electrically coupled in an electrolyte.
3. **Crevice Corrosion** — localized inside gaps, under gaskets, lap joints (oxygen-depleted zone).
4. **Pitting Corrosion** — deep, narrow, autocatalytic pits (most dangerous: small area, high depth).
5. **Intergranular Corrosion** — attack along grain boundaries (e.g., sensitized 304 SS).
6. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** — tensile stress + corrosive environment → brittle cracks.
7. **Erosion–Corrosion** — mechanical wear + corrosion (e.g., impingement attack).
8. **Corrosion Fatigue** — cyclic stress + corrosive environment (synergistic reduction in fatigue life).

## 2.7 Prevention Strategies (KTU Module Outcome)

| Strategy | Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Material selection | Use noble alloys | Monel, Ti, SS in seawater |
| Coatings | Barrier protection | Epoxy paint, galvanizing |
| Cathodic protection (sacrificial anode) | Galvanic suppression | Zn anode on ship's hull |
| Impressed current CP | External DC source | Pipeline protection |
| Inhibitors | Adsorption/passivation | Chromates, phosphates |
| Design modification | Avoid crevices, drain traps | Welded joints vs. bolted |

## 2.8 Real-World Utility

- **Offshore platforms:** Cathodic protection with Al–Zn–In sacrificial anodes prevents **$millions/year** in steel replacement.
- **Boilers:** Dissolved-oxygen scavengers (hydrazine) prevent pitting in economizer tubes.
- **Reinforced concrete:** Epoxy-coated rebar + Ca(NO₂) inhibitor combats chloride-induced pitting.
- **Aerospace:** Alodine (chromate conversion coating) on Al alloys creates a passive layer — this is exactly the passivation zone on the Pourbaix diagram.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Faraday's Law to the mpy Equation

This derivation is a **high-frequency 7-mark KTU problem**. Let us derive the mpy formula rigorously.

**Step 1 — Mass loss from Faraday's First Law:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} \\
\text{where } Q &= I \cdot t \\
\therefore \quad m &= \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* This is the pure electrochemical mass liberated per Faraday of charge. It assumes 100% current efficiency (every electron contributes to metal dissolution).

**Step 2 — Substitute the definition of current density:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= i_{corr} \cdot A \\
\therefore \quad m &= \frac{i_{corr} \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* Current density *i_corr* is the current per unit area, so multiplying by *A* returns total current.

**Step 3 — Introduce density to convert mass into volume:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{m}{D} \\
\therefore \quad V &= \frac{i_{corr} \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot D}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* Density links mass to the volume of metal removed — this is the geometric basis of "depth of attack".

**Step 4 — Compute the linear corrosion depth *d* by dividing volume by area:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{V}{A} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot D}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* Volume ÷ Area = uniform thickness removed. This is the engineering "corrosion depth".

**Step 5 — Convert time from seconds to hours (for mpy convention):**

$$t \, (\text{seconds}) = t \, (\text{hours}) \times 3600$$

**Step 6 — Convert meters to mils:**

$$1 \text{ mil} = 2.54 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Multiplier} = \frac{1}{2.54 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 39370$$

**Step 7 — Convert hours to years:**

$$1 \text{ year} = 365.25 \times 24 = 8766 \text{ hours} \approx 8760 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 8 — Assemble the constants:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CR \, (\text{mpy}) &= \frac{i_{corr} \cdot t_h \cdot M \cdot 39370 \cdot 8760}{n \cdot F \cdot D \cdot 3600} \\
&= i_{corr} \, (\mu A/cm^2) \times \frac{M}{n \cdot D} \times 0.129 \\
&\approx \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T} \quad \text{(using measured mass loss form)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The conversion chain above is the most-tested derivation in **PEMET744 Module 4**. Students often lose marks by missing the *time unit conversion* or by confusing *current density* units (μA/cm² vs. mA/cm²). Always state the units in **bold** in the final equation.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A cylindrical steel coupon of diameter 2 cm and length 5 cm is exposed to a corrosive medium for 30 days. The mass loss measured is 320 mg. Density of steel = 7.85 g/cm³. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mpy** and **mm/year**.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Surface area:} \quad A &= \pi d L + 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \\
A &= \pi (2)(5) + 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(2)^2 \\
A &= 10\pi + 2\pi = 12\pi \text{ cm}^2 \\
A &\approx 37.70 \text{ cm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Convert area to in}^2: \quad A_{in^2} &= 37.70 \times \frac{1}{6.4516} \\
A_{in^2} &\approx 5.844 \text{ in}^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Time in hours:} \quad T &= 30 \times 24 = 720 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
CR \, (\text{mpy}) &= \frac{534 \times W}{D \times A_{in^2} \times T} \\
CR &= \frac{534 \times 320}{7.85 \times 5.844 \times 720} \\
CR &= \frac{170880}{33036.6} \\
\boxed{CR &\approx 5.17 \text{ mpy}}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
CR \, (\text{mm/yr}) &= \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A_{cm^2} \times T} \\
CR &= \frac{87.6 \times 320}{7.85 \times 37.70 \times 720} \\
CR &= \frac{28032}{213070.8} \\
\boxed{CR &\approx 0.1315 \text{ mm/yr}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Verdict:** A rate of 5.17 mpy for carbon steel in *generic* exposure is borderline. If the medium is seawater, this is **unacceptable** — redesign with coating or cathodic protection is needed. This kind of "engineer's comment" earns the **impression mark** in KTU valuation.

## 3.3 Galvanic Series (for Galvanic Corrosion Calculation)

When two metals are coupled, the **one higher in the series** (more active) becomes the **anode** and corrodes. The potential difference ΔE determines the driving force.

| Metal / Alloy | Typical E (V vs. SHE, in seawater) |
|---|---|
| Magnesium | −1.60 |
| Zinc | −1.10 |
| Aluminum alloys | −0.95 to −0.75 |
| Mild steel | −0.65 |
| Lead | −0.50 |
| Tin | −0.45 |
| Copper | −0.30 |
| Stainless steel (304, passive) | −0.10 |
| Titanium | −0.05 |
| Graphite / Carbon | +0.30 |
| Gold | +1.00 |

**Rule of Thumb:** ΔE ≥ **0.25 V** in the galvanic series → *significant* galvanic corrosion risk. Always use the **galvanic series in the specific environment** — *not* the standard EMF series.

## 3.4 Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)

For stainless steels, pitting resistance is quantified by:

$$PREN = \%Cr + 3.3 \times \%Mo + 16 \times \%N$$

- PREN < 25 → low resistance (e.g., 304 SS = 18)
- PREN 25–32 → moderate (e.g., 316 SS = 24–26)
- PREN > 32 → high (e.g., 904L, super-duplex)

> [!NOTE]
> This empirical formula is *not* in textbooks for PEMET744, but examiners sometimes give a stainless-steel composition and ask whether it is suitable for chloride service. Always compute PREN.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Corrosion rate (mpy) | 534 W / (D A T) | mpy | Assumes uniform attack |
| Corrosion rate (mm/yr) | 87.6 W / (D A T) | mm/yr | Assumes uniform attack |
| Nernst equation | E° + (0.0592/n) log(a_ox/a_red) | V | T = 298 K |
| Tafel | η = a + b log(i) | V | Activation-controlled |
| Faraday mass | m = I t M / (n F) | g | 100% current efficiency |
| Galvanic risk | ΔE ≥ 0.25 V | V | Use series in *environment* |
| Pitting (PREN) | %Cr + 3.3%Mo + 16%N | — | Empirical |
| SCC susceptibility | K_ISCC / K_IC < 1 | — | Specific alloy-environment |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Corrosion Cell

The following block diagram maps the four mandatory components and energy flows of a working corrosion cell.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["ANODE SITE\nMetal dissolution\nM to Mn+ plus ne-"]
    nodeB["METALLIC PATH\nElectron flow inside metal\nHigh conductivity required"]
    nodeC["CATHODE SITE\nReduction reaction\nO2 or H+ accepts electrons"]
    nodeD["ELECTROLYTE\nIonic conduction\nSeawater or soil moisture"]
    nodeE["CORROSION PRODUCT\nMn+ plus OH- to M OH n\nForms rust or scale"]
    nodeF["LOADED STRUCTURE\nForce environment\nSustains electron flow"]

    nodeA -- electrons e- --> nodeB
    nodeB -- electrons e- --> nodeC
    nodeC -- ions in solution --> nodeD
    nodeD -- ions back to anode --> nodeA
    nodeA -- dissolves M --> nodeE
    nodeF -- external tensile stress --> nodeA

    classDef anode fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef cathode fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#2c3e80,color:#000
    classDef elec fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b08a2e,color:#000
    classDef product fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#555,color:#000
    classDef stress fill:#e2d6f0,stroke:#6c3483,color:#000

    class nodeA anode
    class nodeC cathode
    class nodeD elec
    class nodeE product
    class nodeF stress
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow — Corrosion Prevention Strategy Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Identify Corrosion Form\nEight NACE Types"]
    nodeQ1{"Service\nEnvironment?"}
    nodeAtm["Atmospheric\nRural Marine Industrial"]
    nodeSub["Submerged\nSoil Seawater Concrete"]
    nodeChem["Chemical\nAcid Alkali Organic"]
    nodeMat["Step 1\nMaterial Selection\nUse noble alloys or composites"]
    nodeCoat["Step 2\nSurface Coatings\nBarrier plus inhibitor"]
    nodeCP["Step 3\nCathodic Protection\nSacrificial or Impressed"]
    nodeInh["Step 4\nChemical Inhibitors\nAdsorption or passivation"]
    nodeMon["Step 5\nMonitoring and Inspection\nCoupon LPR EIS"]
    nodeEnd["Validated Design\nAcceptable CR less than 5 mpy"]

    nodeStart --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -- Atmosphere --> nodeAtm
    nodeQ1 -- Submerged --> nodeSub
    nodeQ1 -- Chemical --> nodeChem
    nodeAtm --> nodeMat
    nodeAtm --> nodeCoat
    nodeSub --> nodeMat
    nodeSub --> nodeCP
    nodeChem --> nodeMat
    nodeChem --> nodeInh
    nodeMat --> nodeMon
    nodeCoat --> nodeMon
    nodeCP --> nodeMon
    nodeInh --> nodeMon
    nodeMon --> nodeEnd
```

## 4.3 Type-Of-Corrosion Identification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ0{"Observed\nDamage Pattern?"}
    nodeUniform["Even thinning\nAlloy more noble\nthan expected"]
    nodeGalv["Two metals coupled\nOne severely\nattacked"]
    nodePit["Deep narrow holes\nAutocatalytic\nMnCl2 inside"]
    nodeCrev["Under gaskets\nO2 starved zones\nChloride rich"]
    nodeIGC["Cracking along\ngrain boundaries\nSensitization"]
    nodeSCC["Branching cracks\nTensile stress\nplus environment"]
    nodeErosion["Directional\nscalloped pattern\nFlow accelerated"]
    nodeCF["Multiple transgranular\ncracks plus corrosion\nproducts"]

    nodeQ0 -- Uniform --> nodeUniform
    nodeQ0 -- Two metals --> nodeGalv
    nodeQ0 -- Deep holes --> nodePit
    nodeQ0 -- Under joints --> nodeCrev
    nodeQ0 -- Grain path --> nodeIGC
    nodeQ0 -- Brittle cracks --> nodeSCC
    nodeQ0 -- Scallops --> nodeErosion
    nodeQ0 -- Cyclic loading --> nodeCF
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Pourbaix Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Metal plus Water\nplus Environment\nAt 25C"]
    nodeQ{"pH and\nPotential E?"}
    nodeImm["IMMUNITY ZONE\nE less than Ea\nNo corrosion\nApply Cathodic\nProtection here"]
    nodeCorr["CORROSION ZONE\nMn plus aq\nActive dissolution\nApply coatings\nor inhibitors"]
    nodePass["PASSIVATION ZONE\nStable oxide film\nSelf limiting\nAlloy with Cr Al Ti"]
    nodeA --> nodeQ
    nodeQ -- Very negative E --> nodeImm
    nodeQ -- Mid E plus neutral pH --> nodeCorr
    nodeQ -- Mid E plus high pH --> nodePass
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

Define corrosion. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the mechanism of electrochemical corrosion of iron in a neutral aqueous environment with dissolved oxygen.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Corrosion is the *physico-chemical interaction between a metal and its environment* resulting in degradation of the metal's useful properties.

In neutral aerated water, iron corrodes electrochemically:

- **At the anode:** Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ (oxidation/dissolution)
- **At the cathode:** O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻ (reduction)
- **Overall reaction:** 2Fe + O₂ + 2H₂O → 2Fe(OH)₂

The ferrous hydroxide further oxidizes to hydrated ferric oxide (Fe₂O₃·H₂O = rust). The metal itself serves as the electron path, and water acts as the electrolyte.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for both half-cell reactions, 1 mark for overall reaction and role of metal/electrolyte.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

Differentiate between *galvanic corrosion* and *crevice corrosion*. Give one engineering example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Galvanic Corrosion | Crevice Corrosion |
|---|---|---|
| Cause | Two dissimilar metals coupled | Same metal, stagnant electrolyte in a gap |
| Driving force | EMF difference | Differential aeration cell (O₂-rich vs. O₂-starved) |
| Location | At the more active metal | Inside crevices, under gaskets, lap joints |
| Example | Brass propeller on steel hull | Bolted plate joints in seawater |

*(1 mark each for cause + driving force, 1 mark for location + example, 1 mark for completeness.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Question (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** Explain the *Pourbaix diagram* for the Fe–H₂O system. Discuss how it is used in selecting corrosion prevention strategies, with special reference to cathodic protection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel coupon of surface area 10 cm² lost 480 mg after 240 hours of immersion in a 3 % NaCl solution. Density of steel = 7.8 g/cm³. Calculate the corrosion rate in **(i) mpy** and **(ii) mm/year**. Comment on the acceptability of this rate for an offshore structural member. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Step 1 — Define Pourbaix Diagram (2 marks):**
The Pourbaix diagram is a *potential (E) vs. pH* plot constructed using the Nernst equation, depicting thermodynamic stability regions of a metal and its compounds (oxides, hydroxides, ions) in aqueous solution at 25 °C.

**Step 2 — Describe the Three Zones for Fe–H₂O (3 marks):**
- *Immunity zone* (low E, low pH and high E, low pH): Fe is stable. Useful for materials selection.
- *Corrosion zone* (mid E, wide pH): Fe²⁺, Fe³⁺ soluble ions — metal dissolves.
- *Passivation zone* (high pH, mid-to-high E): stable Fe₂O₃ or Fe(OH)₃ film forms, protecting the metal.

**Step 3 — Link to Cathodic Protection (2 marks):**
Cathodic protection forces the metal's potential into the *immunity zone* by supplying electrons externally (impressed current) or via a more active sacrificial anode (Zn, Mg, Al). The Nernst equation quantifies the protective voltage: roughly −0.85 V vs. Cu/CuSO₄ reference for steel.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Given:**
W = 480 mg, D = 7.8 g/cm³, T = 240 h, A = 10 cm² (≈ 1.55 in²)

**Step 1 — Convert area to in² (1 mark):**
A_in² = 10 ÷ 6.4516 ≈ 1.55 in²

**Step 2 — Compute mpy (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
CR \, (\text{mpy}) &= \frac{534 \times 480}{7.8 \times 1.55 \times 240} \\
&= \frac{256320}{2901.6} \\
&\approx \boxed{88.34 \text{ mpy}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute mm/yr (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
CR \, (\text{mm/yr}) &= \frac{87.6 \times 480}{7.8 \times 10 \times 240} \\
&= \frac{42048}{18720} \\
&\approx \boxed{2.246 \text{ mm/yr}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Comment (1 mark):**
A rate of **88 mpy** is *grossly unacceptable* for offshore service (acceptable limit is ≤ 5 mpy). The component must be protected by *coating + cathodic protection* (e.g., Zn sacrificial anodes) or replaced with a more resistant alloy (e.g., 316 SS, monel).

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the *Eight Forms of Corrosion* recognized by NACE/ASM with one industrial example for each. Briefly discuss how stress-corrosion cracking (SCC) is influenced by the synergism of mechanical and chemical factors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the concept of *cathodic protection*. Differentiate between **sacrificial anode** and **impressed current cathodic protection (ICCP)** methods, with their relative merits. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Eight Forms (5 marks):**

| # | Form | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uniform | Atmospheric rusting of a steel fence |
| 2 | Galvanic | Brass impeller on cast-iron pump body |
