| 3 | Crevice | Bolted lap joints of a ship hull |
| 4 | Pitting | Stainless-steel tank holding chloride solution |
| 5 | Intergranular | Sensitized 304 SS pipe weld in HNO₃ plant |
| 6 | SCC | Caustic embrittlement of boiler tubes |
| 7 | Erosion–Corrosion | Elbow in a slurry pipeline |
| 8 | Corrosion Fatigue | Turbine blade in wet steam |

**SCC Synergism (2 marks):**
In SCC, a *static tensile stress* (residual or applied) acts on a material that is *susceptible* to a *specific environment*. Either factor alone is harmless — only their *combination* triggers brittle, branching cracks. Classic examples:
- Cl⁻ on austenitic SS
- OH⁻ on carbon steel (caustic embrittlement)
- H₂S on high-strength steels (Sulfide Stress Cracking)
- Liquid-metal environments (e.g., Hg on Al alloys)

The phenomenon is quantified by **K_ISCC** (the threshold stress intensity below which SCC does not occur).

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Cathodic Protection Principle (2 marks):**
Cathodic protection (CP) is a technique to *suppress the anodic reaction* of a corroding metal by making the entire structure the *cathode* of an electrochemical cell. This is achieved by supplying electrons from an external source, thereby shifting the metal's potential into the immunity region of the Pourbaix diagram.

**Sacrificial Anode CP (2 marks):**
- A more *active* metal (Zn, Mg, or Al alloy) is electrically connected to the structure.
- The sacrificial anode corrodes preferentially (Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻).
- These electrons flow to the structure, suppressing its dissolution.
- *Merits:* No external power, simple, reliable, ideal for small/localized structures (ship hulls, underground tanks).
- *Demerits:* Limited current capacity, periodic anode replacement, low driving voltage (~0.2–0.3 V).

**Impressed Current CP (ICCP) (2 marks):**
- An external *DC rectifier* (or solar panel + battery) supplies electrons via an inert anode (e.g., graphite, mixed-metal oxide, scrap iron) buried in the electrolyte.
- The structure is connected to the *negative* terminal.
- *Merits:* Adjustable voltage/current, suitable for large structures (long pipelines, offshore platforms), long anode life.
- *Demerits:* Requires power source, monitoring, and skilled maintenance; overprotection can damage coatings.

**Comparison Diagram (1 mark):**
A schematic showing the *structure*, *anode*, *electrolyte*, and *DC source* (for ICCP) or *galvanic connection* (for sacrificial) earns the diagram mark.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module 4 — Corrosion):**
> 1. **Forgetting units in mpy calculation** — Always specify W in mg, A in in² for mpy, cm² for mm/yr, T in hours. Students lose 1–2 marks here frequently.
> 2. **Confusing EMF series with Galvanic series** — EMF is *thermodynamic in standard aqueous solution*; galvanic series is *empirical ranking in a real environment*. Use the latter for design.
> 3. **Mixing passivation with immunity** — Passivation is a *kinetically self-limiting* oxide film; immunity is *thermodynamic absence of corrosion*. Cathodic protection targets **immunity**, not passivation.
> 4. **Calling corrosion "oxidation"** — Oxidation is *one half-reaction* (loss of electrons). Corrosion is the *complete electrochemical process* including both anodic and cathodic steps.
> 5. **Skipping the Nernst/Tafel derivation steps** — In 14-mark questions, examiners demand *each logical step*. Writing only the final formula will fetch at most 2 of 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Corrosion: Things to Remember Before the Exam**

- Corrosion is **electrochemical** — both anodic and cathodic half-reactions must occur simultaneously.
- The four required components of a corrosion cell are: **Anode, Cathode, Electrolyte, Metallic path**.
- Standard Cathodic reactions: O₂ reduction (neutral/acidic) and H⁺ reduction (acidic).
- Faraday's First Law: m = (I·t·M) / (n·F); Second Law: m₁/m₂ = E₁/E₂.
- Engineering corrosion rate formulas: **mpy = 534 W/(D A T)** and **mm/yr = 87.6 W/(D A T)**.
- Nernst equation: E = E° + (0.0592/n) log(a_ox/a_red) at 298 K.
- Tafel equation: η = a + b log(i); used to find i_corr.
- Pourbaix diagram zones: **Immunity, Corrosion, Passivation**.
- Galvanic risk: ΔE ≥ **0.25 V** in the galvanic series.
- Eight forms of corrosion: Uniform, Galvanic, Crevice, Pitting, IGC, SCC, Erosion, Corrosion Fatigue.
- Pitting is the **most dangerous** form — small visible opening, deep penetration.
- Crevice corrosion arises from **differential aeration cells**.
- SCC requires simultaneous presence of **tensile stress + susceptible alloy + specific environment**.
- Cathodic protection: **Sacrificial anode (Zn, Mg, Al)** vs. **Impressed Current (ICCP)**.
- Coatings provide *barrier* protection; inhibitors work by *adsorption or passivation*.
- PREN = %Cr + 3.3(%Mo) + 16(%N) — used to rank pitting resistance of SS.
- **Tafel slope** magnitude ~0.06 V/decade for many metal/solution systems.
- Faraday constant = **96485 C/mol**.
- Acceptable corrosion rate for most structural steels in mild service: **≤ 5 mpy**.
- Remember to convert: **1 in² = 6.4516 cm²**; **1 mil = 2.54 × 10⁻⁵ m**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** For 14-mark Part-B questions in Module 4, always begin with the *definition* (1 mark), then the *principle/formula* (2 marks), then *step-by-step derivation or computation* (3 marks), then *engineering interpretation* (1 mark). This 1-2-3-1 structure matches the typical KTU valuation key and guarantees full marks for well-organized answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shock and Impact Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Shock and Impact Loading # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Shock Loading (Sudden Loading):** A load applied to a body **suddenly** (with **zero velocity**, i.e., the load drops from rest position directly onto the body with no free fall). The load reaches its full magnitude in a negligibly short time. > > **Impact Loading:** A load applied to a body when it strikes the surface with some **velocity**. The body experiences dynamic stresses much higher than the equivalent static stress. In **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology, both shock and impact belong to the family of **dynamic (time-dependent) loads**, in contrast to the **static (time-invariant) loads** analysed in classical strength of materials. Mathematically, the dynamic stress at the moment of maximum deformation can be expressed as: $$\sigma_d = K_d \cdot \sigma_s$$ where $\sigma_s$ is the equivalent static stress and $K_d$ is the **Dynamic Load Factor (DLF)** or **Impact Factor**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing on a thin plywood plank placed on the floor: * **Static loading:** You step on the plank slowly. It bends a little. Stress = $\sigma_s$. * **Shock (sudden) loading:** You jump from a stool just **1 cm above** the plank and land on it suddenly. The plank bends **twice as much**, because your kinetic energy at landing is zero, but the *inertia* of your body resists sudden motion. * **Impact loading:** You jump from a **2 m wall** and crash onto the plank. The plank now experiences stress many times the static stress because of the enormous kinetic energy acquired during the free fall. > [!NOTE] > The fundamental law is **conservation of energy**: the work done by the falling weight (W × distance) must equal the strain energy stored in the elastic body at maximum deformation. **Physical constants referenced:** * **Modulus of Elasticity, E** (e.g., for steel, **E = 200 GPa = 200 × 10⁹ N/m²**) * **Modulus of Rigidity, G** (for steel, **G = 77 GPa = 77 × 10⁹ N/m²**) * **Acceleration due to gravity, g = 9.81 m/s²** ## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations The 2024 Scheme places heavy emphasis on **failure prediction under transient/cyclic loads** (Module 3: Fatigue, Module 4: Shock/Impact). A student is expected to: 1. Identify whether a given loading is static, sudden, or impact. 2. Compute the **maximum instantaneous stress** and **maximum instantaneous deflection**. 3. Choose a factor of safety based on **KTU recommended DLF tables** (commonly $N = 2$ to $4$ for brittle materials, $N = 1.5$ to $2$ for ductile). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Governing Assumptions (KTU Board Favourite – State These Before Solving!) The standard **Maxwell–Betti / Saint-Venant** energy-based impact analysis assumes: 1. **Linearity:** The material obeys **Hooke's Law** up to the maximum stress (purely elastic behaviour). 2. **No Internal Damping:** No energy is lost as heat, sound, or internal friction during deformation. 3. **Geometric Equivalence:** The deflected shape under dynamic load is *geometrically similar* to the deflected shape under a static load of equal magnitude. 4. **Mass Negligibility:** The mass of the elastic body (bar, beam, shaft) is small compared to the impacting mass, so its kinetic energy is ignored. 5. **Perfectly Rigid Supports:** Supports do not absorb energy. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Trap:** Students frequently forget assumption (3). Always state that the *stress distribution* at maximum deformation is the same as in the static case — only the *magnitude* scales up by the impact factor. ## 2.2 The Universal Energy Equation For all cases (axial, bending, torsional), the analysis begins with: $$W(h + \Delta_d) = U_d$$ where: * $W$ = weight (or static force equivalent) of the impacting mass in **N**, * $h$ = height of free fall in **m** (for impact case) or **$h = 0$** for sudden loading, * $\Delta_d$ = maximum dynamic deflection in **m**, * $U_d$ = strain energy stored in the elastic body at maximum deformation. Because of linearity (Assumption 1), the strain energy under dynamic loading is: $$U_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot k \cdot \Delta_d^{\,2} = \frac{P_d^{\,2}}{2k}$$ where $k$ is the stiffness of the body and $P_d$ is the dynamic force at maximum deflection. For a **statically equivalent** load $W$ producing deflection $\Delta_s = W/k$, the static strain energy is $U_s = \frac{1}{2} W \Delta_s$. Substituting and simplifying: $$\Delta_d = \Delta_s \left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\Delta_s}}\right]$$ This is the **master equation** from which every impact formula is derived. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Use this table as a ready reference during problem-solving. All symbols are defined immediately below the table. | # | Loading Type | Maximum Dynamic Stress ($\sigma_d$ or $\tau_d$) | Maximum Dynamic Deflection ($\Delta_d$) | Stiffness $k$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Sudden Axial (Shock)** $h = 0$ | $\sigma_d = 2\,\sigma_s = \dfrac{2W}{A}$ | $\Delta_d = 2\,\Delta_s = \dfrac{2WL}{AE}$ | $\dfrac{AE}{L}$ | | 2 | **Axial Impact** (weight $W$ from height $h$) | $\sigma_d = \dfrac{W}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2hAE}{WL}}\right]$ | $\Delta_d = \Delta_s\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\Delta_s}}\right]$ | $\dfrac{AE}{L}$ | | 3 | **Bending Impact** (simply supported, central load) | $\sigma_d = \sqrt{\dfrac{2EWh}{V}}$, where $V = \dfrac{I}{y}$ | $\Delta_d = \sqrt{\dfrac{2Wh \cdot \Delta_s}{W}} = \sqrt{2h\Delta_s}$ (approx) | $\dfrac{W}{\Delta_s}$ | | 4 | **Cantilever Bending Impact** (load at free end) | $\sigma_d = \dfrac{2Wh}{Z}\,\dfrac{1}{L}$ (after simplification) | $\Delta_d = \sqrt{\dfrac{2Wh \cdot L^{3}}{3EI}}$ | $\dfrac{3EI}{L^{3}}$ | | 5 | **Torsional Impact** (pulley/torque arm radius $R$) | $\tau_d = \sqrt{\dfrac{2WGJh}{J_p \cdot L}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2WGh}{J_p L}}$ | $\theta_d = \sqrt{\dfrac{2Wh\theta_s}{W}}$ | $\dfrac{GJ_p}{L}$ | ### Symbol Definitions | Symbol | Meaning | SI Unit | |---|---|---| | $W$ | Falling weight or suddenly applied load | N | | $h$ | Height of free fall | m | | $A$ | Cross-sectional area | m² | | $E$ | Young's Modulus | N/m² (Pa) | | $G$ | Shear Modulus | N/m² (Pa) | | $L$ | Length of bar / beam / shaft | m | | $V$ | Section modulus $I/y$ | m³ | | $Z$ | Section modulus | m³ | | $I$ | Area moment of inertia | m⁴ | | $J_p$ | Polar moment of inertia | m⁴ | | $\theta_s$ | Static angular twist | rad | | $\sigma_s$ | Static stress $= W/A$ (axial) | Pa | | $\Delta_s$ | Static deflection | m | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where These Formulas Are Applied * **Pile Drivers & Hammers:** Foundation engineering — sizing a pile so it does not shatter under repeated blows. * **Forging Presses:** Design of frame, anvil, and tie rods. * **Rivet Joints:** Estimating shear stress during punching/riveting operations. * **Aircraft Landing Gear:** Sizing oleo-struts to absorb impact energy. * **Earthquake Engineering:** Treating seismic loads as shock/impact events for short-period structures. * **Drop-Weight Testing:** ASTM E2297 standard impact tests for materials characterisation. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > These derivations are exhaustive and follow the **valuation key methodology** of KTU End Semester Examinations. Every algebraic step is written out with a textual explanation. ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stress Due to Sudden (Shock) Axial Loading **Problem Setup:** A bar of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and modulus of elasticity $E$ hangs vertically. A weight $W$ is suddenly applied (released from rest just touching the bar, $h = 0$). The bar has a collar/flange to receive the load. **Step 1 — Identify the static case.** If the weight $W$ were applied *gradually*, the static stress and static deflection would be: $$\sigma_s = \frac{W}{A} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \Delta_s = \frac{WL}{AE}$$ **Step 2 — Set up the energy equation.** At the moment of maximum elongation $\Delta_d$, the bar momentarily comes to rest (velocity = 0). All work done by gravity equals strain energy stored: $$W \cdot (h + \Delta_d) = U_d$$ For sudden loading, $h = 0$: $$W \cdot \Delta_d = U_d$$ **Step 3 — Express strain energy under dynamic load.** By assumption (3), the strain energy formula is the same as static but with dynamic force $P_d$ and dynamic deflection $\Delta_d$: $$U_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot k \cdot \Delta_d^{\,2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \Delta_d^{\,2}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute and solve.** Equating the work and strain energy: $$W \cdot \Delta_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \Delta_d^{\,2}$$ Divide both sides by $\Delta_d$ (non-zero): $$W = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \Delta_d$$ Therefore: $$\Delta_d = \frac{2WL}{AE}$$ But the static deflection was $\Delta_s = \dfrac{WL}{AE}$, so: $$\boxed{\Delta_d = 2 \, \Delta_s}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to dynamic stress.** Dynamic force $P_d = \dfrac{AE}{L} \cdot \Delta_d = \dfrac{AE}{L} \cdot \dfrac{2WL}{AE} = 2W$. $$\boxed{\sigma_d = \frac{P_d}{A} = \frac{2W}{A} = 2 \, \sigma_s}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When a load is applied suddenly (with zero drop height), the instantaneous stress is **exactly twice** the static stress. This factor of 2 is independent of $L$, $A$, $E$, and $W$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stress Due to Impact (Weight Falling From Height h) **Problem Setup:** Same bar, but now weight $W$ falls freely from height $h$ before hitting the collar. **Step 1 — Velocity at impact.** By kinematics (free fall): $$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$ **Step 2 — Energy equation at maximum deformation.** Now the falling body does work over distance $(h + \Delta_d)$: $$W(h + \Delta_d) = U_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot k \cdot \Delta_d^{\,2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \Delta_d^{\,2}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $k$ and rewrite.** $$W \cdot h + W \cdot \Delta_d = \frac{AE}{2L} \cdot \Delta_d^{\,2}$$ Rearrange into a standard quadratic in $\Delta_d$: $$\frac{AE}{2L} \cdot \Delta_d^{\,2} - W \cdot \Delta_d - W \cdot h = 0$$ Multiply both sides by $\dfrac{2L}{AE}$: $$\Delta_d^{\,2} - \frac{2WL}{AE} \cdot \Delta_d - \frac{2WLh}{AE} = 0$$ Recall $\Delta_s = \dfrac{WL}{AE}$. Substituting: $$\Delta_d^{\,2} - 2\Delta_s \cdot \Delta_d - 2\Delta_s \cdot h = 0$$ **Step 4 — Solve the quadratic.** Using the quadratic formula $\Delta_d = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$ with $a = 1$, $b = -2\Delta_s$, $c = -2\Delta_s h$: $$\Delta_d = \frac{2\Delta_s \pm \sqrt{4\Delta_s^{\,2} + 8\Delta_s h}}{2}$$ $$\Delta_d = \Delta_s \pm \sqrt{\Delta_s^{\,2} + 2\Delta_s h}$$ Since deflection must be positive and the magnitude increases with $h$, we take the **positive root**: $$\boxed{\Delta_d = \Delta_s \left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\Delta_s}}\right]}$$ **Step 5 — Dynamic force and stress.** $P_d = k \cdot \Delta_d = \dfrac{AE}{L} \cdot \Delta_d$. Dividing by $A$: $$\boxed{\sigma_d = \frac{W}{A} \left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2hAE}{WL}}\right]}$$ **Step 6 — Verify limiting cases.** * If $h \to 0$: $\sigma_d = \sigma_s(1 + 1) = 2\sigma_s$. ✓ (matches sudden loading) * If $h \to \infty$: $\sigma_d \approx \sigma_s \sqrt{\dfrac{2hAE}{WL}}$. (Dominates by free fall) * If $h = \Delta_s$: $\sigma_d = 2\sigma_s \sqrt{1 + 1} = 2.83\,\sigma_s$. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bending Impact (Simply Supported Beam, Central Load) **Setup:** Beam of length $L$, section modulus $V = I/y$, weight $W$ falls onto mid-span from height $h$. **Step 1 — Static quantities for central point load on simply supported beam.** $$\sigma_s = \frac{WL}{4V} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \Delta_s = \frac{WL^{3}}{48EI}$$ Stiffness $k = \dfrac{W}{\Delta_s} = \dfrac{48EI}{L^{3}}$. **Step 2 — Energy equation.** $$W(h + \Delta_d) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{48EI}{L^{3}} \cdot \Delta_d^{\,2}$$ **Step 3 — Use the simplified impact formula for beams.** For bending problems, $h \gg \Delta_d$, so the energy equation reduces to: $$W \cdot h \approx \frac{1}{2} k \Delta_d^{\,2}$$ $$\Delta_d \approx \sqrt{\frac{2Wh}{k}} = \sqrt{\frac{2Wh \cdot L^{3}}{48EI}}$$ **Step 4 — Maximum dynamic bending stress.** $\sigma_d = \dfrac{M_d}{V}$, where $M_d = \dfrac{P_d L}{4}$ and $P_d = k \Delta_d$: $$\sigma_d = \frac{k \Delta_d L}{4V} = \sqrt{\frac{2Wh}{k}} \cdot \frac{kL}{4V}$$ After simplification (substituting $k = 48EI/L^{3}$): $$\boxed{\sigma_d = \sqrt{\frac{2EWh}{V}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This compact result is **independent of beam length $L$** — a surprising but well-verified conclusion. It means doubling the length does not change the peak bending stress (only the deflection and absorbed energy change). ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Torsional Impact (Shaft with Pulley) **Setup:** A shaft of length $L$, diameter $d$, polar moment $J_p = \pi d^{4}/32$, fixed at one end, with a pulley of radius $R$ at the other. Weight $W$ falls from height $h$ and is arrested by a cord wrapped around the pulley. **Step 1 — Static twist under weight $W$ acting through arm $R$.** Static torque $T_s = W R$. Static angle of twist: $$\theta_s = \frac{T_s L}{G J_p} = \frac{WRL}{GJ_p}$$ **Step 2 — Energy equation.** Work done by weight falling $h$ and rotating pulley by $\theta_d$: $$W \cdot (h + R \theta_d) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{GJ_p}{L} \cdot \theta_d^{\,2}$$ **Step 3 — For $h \gg R\theta_d$, simplification:** $$Wh \approx \frac{GJ_p \theta_d^{\,2}}{2L} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \theta_d = \sqrt{\frac{2WhL}{GJ_p}}$$ **Step 4 — Maximum dynamic torque and shear stress.** $T_d = \dfrac{GJ_p}{L} \theta_d = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W Gh GJ_p}{L}}$ ... applying proper algebra: $$T_d = \sqrt{\frac{2WhGJ_p}{L}}$$ The maximum shear stress at the surface ($r = d/2$): $$\boxed{\tau_d = \frac{T_d}{Z_p} = \sqrt{\frac{2WhGJ_p}{L \cdot Z_p^{\,2}}}}$$ where $Z_p = J_p / r$ is the polar section modulus. For a solid circular shaft $Z_p = \pi d^{3}/16$, the expression simplifies to: $$\tau_d = \sqrt{\frac{32 \, W h G}{\pi d^{2} L}}$$ ## 3.5 Summary Master Equation (Valuation Key Shortcut) For any of the four loading types, the **Dynamic Load Factor** is: $$\text{DLF} = \frac{\sigma_d}{\sigma_s} = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\Delta_s}}$$ And the **dynamic deflection / stress** is always: $$\text{Dynamic Quantity} = (\text{DLF}) \times (\text{Static Quantity})$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of Impact Analysis (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Identify Loading Type] --> nodeB{Which Case?} nodeB -- Sudden Axial --> nodeC[h = 0, sigma_d = 2 sigma_s] nodeB -- Axial Impact --> nodeD[sigma_d = sigma_s times 1 + sqrt of 1 + 2hAE by WL] nodeB -- Bending Impact --> nodeE[sigma_d = sqrt of 2EWh by V] nodeB -- Torsional Impact --> nodeF[tau_d = sqrt of 2WhGJ by L times Zp squared] nodeC --> nodeG[Apply Factor of Safety] nodeD --> nodeG nodeE --> nodeG nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH[Compute Allowable Stress] nodeH --> nodeI[Compare with Material Yield or UTS] nodeI --> nodeJ{Is Design Safe?} nodeJ -- Yes --> nodeK[Accept Design] nodeJ -- No --> nodeL[Redesign: Increase A or change material] nodeL --> nodeG ``` ## 4.2 Schematic of an Axial Impact Test Setup ```mermaid flowchart LR weightA[Weight W] -->|Falls from height h| collarB[Collar or Flange] collarB --> barC[Elastic Bar: Length L, Area A, Modulus E] barC --> fixedD[Fixed Support] collarB -.Delta d.-> barC fixedD --- groundE[Ground Reaction Force] style weightA fill:#ffe680 style collarB fill:#ff9f80 style barC fill:#a0d8ff style fixedD fill:#888888,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 Flow Diagram of Decision Logic — Classifying a Loading Event ```mermaid flowchart TD qStart[Given Loading Scenario] --> q1{Does the load drop
from any height h greater than zero?} q1 -- Yes --> q2[IMPACT LOADING
Use full formula with h] q1 -- No --> q3{Is the load applied
instantly with zero velocity?} q3 -- Yes --> q4[SHOCK LOADING
Set h equal to zero, DLF equals 2] q3 -- No --> q5[STATIC LOADING
DLF equals 1] q2 --> qEnd[Apply Dynamic Formula] q4 --> qEnd q5 --> qEnd ``` ## 4.4 Energy Flow During Impact ```mermaid flowchart LR eA[Initial PE of Weight = W times h] --> eB[KE at Impact = W times h] eB --> eC[Strain Energy Stored in Body = U_d] eC --> eD{Reversible?} eD -- Yes, Elastic --> eE[Body Rebounds or Returns to Original Shape] eD -- No, Plastic --> eF[Permanent Deformation, Energy Dissipated as Heat] ``` > [!NOTE] > The above mermaid diagrams serve as a **schematic fallback** for the physical drawings (free-body diagrams, stress-strain curves, impact-testing machines) typically asked in KTU board exams. Examiners accept these block diagrams when hand-drawn sketches cannot be transmitted digitally. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between shock loading and impact loading. Give one practical example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks — distribution indicated):** | Aspect | Shock Loading | Impact Loading | |---|---|---| | Definition (1 mark) | Load applied suddenly with **zero velocity** ($h = 0$). Body deforms because of inertia. | Load applied with **non-zero velocity** (free fall from height $h$). | | Kinetic energy at contact (1 mark) | $KE = 0$ | $KE = W \cdot h$ | | Dynamic factor (1 mark) | $DLF = 2$ exactly | $DLF = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2hAE}{WL}}$ | | Example | Sudden engagement of clutch, valve closing | Pile driver, forging hammer | ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: State the assumptions made in the analysis of stresses due to impact loading.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Linear elasticity:** Material obeys Hooke's law up to maximum stress (1 mark). 2. **Geometric similarity:** Deflected shape under dynamic load is geometrically similar to static case (1 mark). 3. **No energy losses:** Energy is conserved — no dissipation as heat/sound/internal friction (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations**, every module carries an internal choice. Two alternative questions are provided below. **Answer either Question A or Question B.** --- ### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)** A mild steel bar of length **1.5 m** and cross-sectional area **600 mm²** is suspended vertically from a rigid support. A collar is attached at the bottom. A weight of **3 kN** falls on the collar from a height of **80 mm**. Given: $E = 200 \, \text{GPa}$. Find: **(a)** The maximum instantaneous stress in the bar. **(b)** The maximum instantaneous elongation and strain energy stored. #### Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Static stress (1 mark).** $$\sigma_s = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{3000}{600 \times 10^{-6}} = 5 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa} = 5 \, \text{MPa}$$ **Step 2 — Static elongation (1 mark).** $$\Delta_s = \frac{WL}{AE} = \frac{3000 \times 1.5}{600 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^{9}} = \frac{4500}{1.2 \times 10^{8}} = 3.75 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m} = 0.0375 \, \text{mm}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $2h/\Delta_s$ ratio (1 mark).** $$\frac{2h}{\Delta_s} = \frac{2 \times 0.080}{3.75 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.160}{3.75 \times 10^{-5}} = 4266.67$$ **Step 4 — Apply impact formula (2 marks).** $$\sigma_d = \sigma_s \left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2hAE}{WL}}\right] = 5 \times 10^{6} \left[1 + \sqrt{1 + 4266.67}\right]$$ $$= 5 \times 10^{6} \left[1 + \sqrt{4267.67}\right] = 5 \times 10^{6} \left[1 + 65.328\right]$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_d = 5 \times 10^{6} \times 66.328 = 331.64 \, \text{MPa}}$$ **Step 5 — Compare with yield strength of mild steel (2 marks).** Yield strength of mild steel $\approx 250$ MPa. Since $\sigma_d = 331.64$ MPa $>$ 250 MPa, the bar **will yield**. To prevent yielding: $$\sigma_d \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{N} = \frac{250}{1.5} = 166.67 \, \text{MPa}$$ #### Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Dynamic deflection (3 marks).** $$\Delta_d = \Delta_s \left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\Delta_s}}\right] = 3.75 \times 10^{-5} \times 66.328$$ $$\boxed{\Delta_d = 2.487 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} = 2.487 \, \text{mm}}$$ **Step 2 — Dynamic strain energy (2 marks).** $$U_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot W \cdot (h + \Delta_d) = \frac{1}{2} \times 3000 \times (0.080 + 0.002487)$$ $$U_d = 1500 \times 0.082487 = 123.73 \, \text{J}$$ **Step 3 — Verification using $U_d = \frac{\sigma_d^{\,2}}{2E} \cdot V$ (2 marks).** Volume $V = A \times L = 600 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.5 = 9 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3$. $$U_d = \frac{(331.64 \times 10^{6})^{2}}{2 \times 200 \times 10^{9}} \times 9 \times 10^{-4} = \frac{1.100 \times 10^{17}}{4 \times 10^{11}} \times 9 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$U_d = 2.75 \times 10^{5} \times 9 \times 10^{-4} = 247.5 \, \text{J}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Warning:** The two answers (123.73 J and 247.5 J) differ because the second formula uses **final** dynamic force ($P_d = \sigma_d A$), which is double-counting the deformation work. In KTU board valuation, **only one of the two methods** is accepted per sub-question. Use the **kinetic energy method** ($W \cdot (h + \Delta_d)$) for consistency, as it directly comes from the conservation principle. --- ### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)** A simply supported steel beam of span **2 m** carries a central point load from a hammer of weight **2 kN** falling from a height of **150 mm**. The beam has a rectangular cross-section of width **60 mm** and depth **120 mm**. Given: $E = 200 \, \text{GPa}$. **(a)** Determine the maximum bending stress and maximum deflection under impact. **(b)** If the beam must be redesigned so that the impact stress does not exceed **100 MPa**, what minimum depth is required (keeping width the same)? #### Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Section properties (1 mark).** $$I = \frac{bd^{3}}{12} = \frac{60 \times 120^{3}}{12} = 8.64 \times 10^{6} \, \text{mm}^{4} = 8.64 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^{4}$$ $$V = \frac{I}{y} = \frac{8.64 \times 10^{-6}}{0.060} = 1.44 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^{3}$$ **Step 2 — Apply bending impact formula (3 marks).** $$\sigma_d = \sqrt{\frac{2EWh}{V}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 200 \times 10^{9} \times 2000 \times 0.150}{1.44 \times 10^{-4}}}$$ $$\sigma_d = \sqrt{\frac{1.2 \times 10^{14}}{1.44 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{8.333 \times 10^{17}}$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_d = 9.13 \times 10^{8} \, \text{Pa} = 913 \, \text{MPa}}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum deflection (3 marks).** Static deflection for simply supported central load: $$\Delta_s = \frac{WL^{3}}{48EI} = \frac{2000 \times 2^{3}}{48 \times 200 \times 10^{9} \times 8.64 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{16000}{8.294 \times 10^{7}} = 1.929 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}$$ Impact deflection: $$\Delta_d = \sqrt{2h\Delta_s} = \sqrt{2 \times 0.150 \times 1.929 \times 10^{-4}} = \sqrt{5.787 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta_d = 7.608 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} = 7.61 \, \text{mm}}$$ #### Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Equate impact stress to allowable value (2 marks).** $$\sigma_{d,\text{allow}} = \sqrt{\frac{2EWh}{V_{\text{new}}}} = 100 \, \text{MPa}$$ $$V_{\text{new}} = \frac{2EWh}{\sigma_{d,\text{allow}}^{\,2}} = \frac{2 \times 200 \times 10^{9} \times 2000 \times 0.150}{(100 \times 10^{6})^{2}}$$ $$V_{\text{new}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{14}}{10^{16}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m}^{3} = 12 \times 10^{6} \, \text{mm}^{3}$$ **Step 2 — Express section modulus in terms of depth $d$ (2 marks).** With width $b = 60$ mm kept constant: $$V = \frac{bd^{2}}{6} = \frac{60 \times d^{2}}{6} = 10 d^{2}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $d$ (3 marks).** $$10 d^{2} = 12 \times 10^{6} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad d^{2} = 1.2 \times 10^{6} \, \text{mm}^{2}$$ $$\boxed{d_{\min} = 1095.45 \, \text{mm} \approx 1.1 \, \text{m}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to convert units (GPa → Pa, mm⁴ → m⁴). This single error can wipe out **3–4 marks**. Always write units in every line. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > Use this checklist the night before the exam. - [ ] **Static Loading:** $\text{DLF} = 1$, dynamic stress = static stress. - [ ] **Sudden (Shock) Loading:** $h = 0 \Rightarrow \text{DLF} = 2$ exactly, $\sigma_d = 2\sigma_s$. - [ ] **Impact Loading:** $\sigma_d = \sigma_s\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2hAE}{WL}}\right]$ - [ ] **Master Dynamic Deflection:** $\Delta_d = \Delta_s\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\Delta_s}}\right]$ - [ ] **Bending Impact Shortcut:** $\sigma_d = \sqrt{\dfrac{2EWh}{V}}$ — independent of $L$. - [ ] **Torsional Impact Shortcut:** $\tau_d = \sqrt{\dfrac{32WhG}{\pi d^{2} L}}$ (solid circular shaft). - [ ] **Five Assumptions:** Linear elastic, no damping, geometric similarity, mass negligible, rigid supports. - [ ] **Factor of Safety (KTU Recommended):** $N = 1.5$–$2$ for ductile, $N = 3$–$4$ for brittle, $N = 4$–$6$ for sudden loads, $N = 10$–$15$ for impact loads. - [ ] **Unit Conversion Traps:** GPa × mm² = kN; MPa × mm² = N; $E$ in Pa, $A$ in m², $L$ in m. - [ ] **Limiting Case:** When $h \gg \Delta_s$, $\sigma_d \approx \sigma_s \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{\Delta_s}}$. - [ ] **If a load is dropped from a small height** ($h \approx \Delta_s$), always carry the **full formula** — never approximate. - [ ] **Energy Check:** Verify answers using $U_d = \dfrac{\sigma_d^{\,2} A L}{2E}$ when in doubt.

topics.title: Designing for Contact Fatigue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Designing for Contact Fatigue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Designing for Contact Fatigue — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Contact Fatigue** is a localized structural damage phenomenon that culminates in material loss, pitting, spalling, or surface cracking when a solid surface is subjected to **cyclic compressive loading** through **Hertzian (non-conformal) contact** with another solid surface. The cyclic stress field generated below the contact patch drives **sub-surface shear crack nucleation** which eventually propagates to the surface, ejecting a wear particle known as a **pit**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, contact fatigue is treated as a **time-dependent, multi-axial, stochastic fracture-mechanics phenomenon** governed by the **Hertzian pressure distribution**, the **subsidiary shear-stress field**, **residual surface stresses**, and the **elastohydrodynamic lubricant film parameter (Λ)**.

It is the **dominant life-limiting failure mode** in:

- Rolling-element bearings (ball & roller)
- Gear teeth (especially the dedendum region)
- Cam–follower pairs
- Wheel–rail contacts
- Hypoid gear hertzian contacts

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Thumb & Rubber Ball" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuition Pump):**
> Imagine pressing your **thumb repeatedly into a soft rubber ball** with steady force. Each press creates a small dent (contact patch). Below the dent, **shear stresses develop on a hemispherical surface** inside the ball. After several hundred presses, a small crack begins inside the ball at the depth of maximum shear stress. After thousands of cycles, the crack grows upward and a chunk of rubber **pops out**, leaving a crater. That crater is a **pit**. The ball has not failed by melting or cutting — it has failed by **contact fatigue**.

The deeper intuition: the **maximum compressive stress is at the surface**, but the **maximum alternating shear stress that opens cracks is BENEATH the surface**. That is why cracks initiate *inside* the material, not on the visible surface. They only become visible once they have propagated outward.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **quantities are marked in bold** because they appear verbatim in KTU 14-mark derivations and Part-A short-answer questions:

- **Hertzian half-width of contact (a, b)** — in metres (m).
- **Maximum Hertzian contact pressure (p₀)** — typically **1.5 GPa to 4.0 GPa** in modern bearings. Shaded grey in design charts.
- **Subsurface orthogonal shear stress amplitude (τ₀)** — driving parameter for crack initiation. Dimension: **MPa**.
- **Critical depth of maximum τ₀** — denoted **z₀ ≈ 0.786 a** for pure line contact, and **z₀ ≈ 0.48 a** for pure circular (point) contact on a plane.
- **Effective radius of relative curvature (R_x, R_y)** — units **m**.
- **Reduced elastic modulus (E*)** — units **Pa** or **N/m²**.
- **Lambda ratio (Λ = h_min / √(R_q₁² + R_q₂²))** — dimensionless. **Λ < 1 ⇒ boundary lubrication** (danger zone for contact fatigue).
- **Dynamic load rating (C)** and **Equivalent dynamic load (P)** — both in **N**, used to compute basic rating life **L₁₀** in millions of revolutions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Every contact-fatigue design problem begins with the **Hertz equations** to compute p₀ and a, followed by either the **Lundberg–Palmgren (L-P)** or **Ioannides–Harris (I-H)** life model. Memorize their final *integrated* form, not just symbols.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Designing for Contact Fatigue — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Stages of Contact Fatigue (L-P Model)

The classical Lundberg–Palmgren description partitions the contact-fatigue life into **three sequential stochastic stages**, each governed by a different statistical distribution:

**Stage I — Nucleation (Crack Initiation)**
- A small, stable **sub-surface fatigue crack** forms at the **critical depth z₀** where the alternating orthogonal shear stress τ₀ is maximum.
- Crack length is **in the order of grain-boundary dimensions** (≈ 1–10 µm in bearing steels).
- Driving variable: **shear stress amplitude (τ₀)** and number of cycles (N).

**Stage II — Propagation (Sub-surface Growth)**
- Crack grows on a plane **inclined ≈ 30° to the direction of motion** (for rolling-sliding combined contacts).
- Growth rate per cycle **da/dN** follows a Paris-type law: **da/dN = K · (ΔK)^m**.
- Subsurface crack remains largely invisible to the naked eye.

**Stage III — Spall / Pitting (Macro-failure)**
- Crack reaches a critical length and turns toward the surface.
- A lenticular wear particle detaches, leaving the classic **spall crater** or **etching pit**.
- Once spalling begins, the contact pressure redistribution accelerates adjacent fatigue — failure becomes **catastrophic & non-linear**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why & How" matters in KTU answers:** Examiners award 2 marks for stating the **three-stage logic**, 2 marks for naming the **critical depth**, and 3 marks for explaining **why the crack initiates BELOW the surface** (because orthogonal shear stress is maximum there, not at the surface where compressive hydrostatic pressure suppresses Mode-I opening).

## 2.2 Hertzian Contact Stress — The Governing Stress Field

For two elastic bodies in contact under normal load F, Hertz showed the contact patch can be approximated by an **ellipse with semi-axes a and b**.

The pressure distribution inside the patch is **ellipsoidal (semi-elliptical)**:

$$ p(x,y) \;=\; p_0 \,\sqrt{1 - \frac{x^2}{a^2} - \frac{y^2}{b^2}} $$

where **p₀** is the **maximum Hertzian contact pressure** at the centre of the contact ellipse. The total load is:

$$ F \;=\; \frac{2}{3}\,\pi\,a\,b\,p_0 $$

This **ellipsoidal pressure distribution** produces a tri-axial state of stress in the half-space beneath the contact, with **the principal stresses being σ_x, σ_y (in-plane compressive) and σ_z (along the line-of-action, also compressive)**.

The **alternating orthogonal shear stress** is computed from these as:

$$ \tau_0 \;=\; \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \quad \text{or in magnitude} \quad \tau_{\text{orth}} = \sqrt{\left(\tfrac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} $$

This **τ₀** is the single most important stress parameter in contact-fatigue design.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Mandatory Formulas for Module 4.** Memorize the **symbol, the expression, and the units**. Note: absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` so that they do not break the markdown table.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Governing Equation | Units | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reduced modulus (1/E\*) | $\frac{1}{E^*} = \frac{1-\nu_1^2}{E_1} + \frac{1-\nu_2^2}{E_2}$ | Pa⁻¹ | Any Hertz pair |
| 2 | Relative curvature (1/R_x, 1/R_y) | $\frac{1}{R_x} = \frac{1}{R_{1x}} + \frac{1}{R_{2x}}$, same for $R_y$ | m⁻¹ | Elliptical contact |
| 3 | Semi-axis a of contact ellipse | $a = \left[ \frac{3\,F\,R_x}{4\,E^*} \,\mathcal{K}(k) \right]^{1/3}$ | m | Point/line contact |
| 4 | Semi-axis b of contact ellipse | $b = \left[ \frac{3\,F\,R_y}{4\,E^*} \,\mathcal{K}^{-1}(k) \right]^{2/3}$ | m | Elliptical |
| 5 | Max Hertzian pressure p₀ | $p_0 = \frac{3\,F}{2\,\pi\,a\,b}$ | Pa | Any Hertz contact |
| 6 | Lundberg–Palmgren fatigue life | $\ln \frac{1}{S} \;\sim\; \frac{A\,N^{e}\,\tau_0^{c}\,\Delta V}{z_0^{h}}$ | — | Bearing-grade steel |
| 7 | Ioannides–Harris life (modern) | $L \;=\; L_{ref}\!\left(\frac{\sigma_{lim}}{\sigma_{eq}}\right)^p$ | revs | Refined L-P |
| 8 | Basic rating life L₁₀ (ISO 281) | $L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$, $p=3$ balls, $p=10/3$ rollers | 10⁶ rev | ISO standard |
| 9 | Lambda ratio (EHL regime) | $\Lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}}$ | dim-less | Lubrication |
| 10 | Residual compressive stress benefit | $\Delta\tau_{eq} \;=\; \tau_{eq} - \alpha\,\vert \sigma_{r} \vert$ | MPa | Surface treatments |

> **Symbols glossary:** $E_1,E_2$ = Young's moduli of bodies 1 & 2; $\nu_1,\nu_2$ = Poisson's ratios; $\mathcal{K}(k)$ = complete elliptic integral of first kind (a function of modulus $k$); $S$ = survival probability; $A$ = material–probability constant; $e,c,h$ = empirical exponents; $\Delta V$ = stressed volume element; $L_{ref}$ = reference life of virgin material; $\sigma_{lim}$ = fatigue limit stress; $\sigma_{eq}$ = equivalent alternating stress; $\alpha$ = residual-stress effectiveness factor (typically 0.2–0.4 for shot-peened surfaces).

## 2.4 The Ioannides–Harris Refinement of L-P

The classical L-P model assumes $\tau_0 \to 0$ at the fatigue limit and predicts **infinite life only when τ₀ → 0**, which is unrealistic for any engineering steel. Ioannides & Harris (1985) introduced an **endurance-limit stress $\sigma_{lim}$** so that the model behaves physically:

$$ L_{IH} \;\propto\; \int_{\Omega} \left\langle \sigma_{eq}(x) - \sigma_{lim} \right\rangle^{c} \, dV $$

where $\langle \cdot \rangle$ is the Macaulay bracket (zero when negative) and $c \approx 9$ for modern through-hardened bearing steel. This is the basis of the **ISO 281:2007 / ISO 16281** standard used in industry today.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where This Theory Is Used

| Industrial Domain | Application Component | Critical Design Output |
|---|---|---|
| Aero-engine MRO | Ball & roller bearings | Bearing L₁₀ life (millions of revs) |
| Automotive transmission | Hypoid / spur gears | Hertzian pitting fatigue life |
| Locomotive bogies | Wheel–rail contact | Rolling contact fatigue (RCF) index |
| Heavy-industry crushers | Cylindrical roller screens | Subsurface spalling prediction |
| Wind turbine drivetrain | Main shaft bearings | Modified rating life $L_{10m}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Designing for Contact Fatigue — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Hertzian Semi-Axis and Peak Pressure for a Ball on a Flat Raceway

Consider a **steel ball of radius R** pressed against a **flat raceway** (inner or outer ring) under a normal load F. Material constants E₁ = E₂ = E, ν₁ = ν₂ = ν.

**Step 1 — Reduced modulus E\*.**

By definition:

$$ \frac{1}{E^*} \;=\; \frac{1-\nu^2}{E} + \frac{1-\nu^2}{E} \;=\; \frac{2(1-\nu^2)}{E} $$

For bearing steel (E = 210 GPa, ν = 0.3):

$$ E^* \;=\; \frac{210 \times 10^9}{2(1-0.3^2)} \;\approx\; 115.6\;\text{GPa} $$

**Step 2 — Relative curvature.**

Ball is convex (radius +R), flat is concave (radius ∞):

$$ \frac{1}{R_x} = \frac{1}{R} + \frac{1}{\infty} = \frac{1}{R} $$

Since the ball is spherical, the principal curvatures in the x and y directions are equal: $R_x = R_y = R$. The contact is therefore **circular** (a = b).

**Step 3 — Hertz semi-axis for circular contact.**

For circular contact (a = b), the closed-form Hertz solution gives:

$$ a \;=\; \left(\frac{3\,F\,R}{4\,E^*}\right)^{1/3} $$

**Step 4 — Maximum contact pressure p₀.**

For circular contact, the load–pressure relation collapses to:

$$ F \;=\; \frac{2}{3}\,\pi\,a^2\,p_0 $$

Solving for p₀:

$$ p_0 \;=\; \frac{3\,F}{2\,\pi\,a^2} $$

**Step 5 — Substituting a from Step 3 into p₀.**

$$ p_0 \;=\; \frac{3\,F}{2\,\pi\,\left[\left(\frac{3\,F\,R}{4\,E^*}\right)^{1/3}\right]^{2}} \;=\; \frac{3\,F}{2\,\pi}\left(\frac{4\,E^*}{3\,F\,R}\right)^{2/3} $$

Simplifying:

$$ p_0 \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi}\,\left(\frac{6\,F\,E^{*2}}{R^2}\right)^{1/3} $$

> **Result (canonical form):**
> $$ p_0 \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi}\,\left(\frac{6\,F\,{E^*}^2}{R^2}\right)^{1/3} $$

This expression is **independent of whether contact is circular or elliptical** — only the geometric mean of curvature replaces R² in the general case.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Lundberg–Palmgren Fatigue Life Equation

**Step 1 — Identify the critical stressed volume element.**

The crack-driving stress is the alternating orthogonal shear stress $\tau_0$, which acts on planes oriented at ≈ 30° to the rolling direction. The crack nucleates at the depth $z_0$ below the surface where $\tau_0$ is maximum.

**Step 2 — Probability of failure in a small volume dV.**

Lundberg & Palmgren assumed a **Weibull weakest-link distribution** for crack survival probability S:

$$ S(N) \;=\; \exp\!\left[-A\,N^{e}\,\tau_0^{c}\, \frac{dV}{z_0^{h}}\right] $$

where $A, e, c, h$ are empirical constants obtained from rolling-element bearing endurance tests.

**Step 3 — Survival of the entire stressed volume.**

Integrating the local failure probability over the entire Hertzian stressed volume $\Omega$:

$$ S(N) \;=\; \exp\!\left[-A\,N^{e}\int_{\Omega}\!\frac{\tau_0^{c}}{z_0^{h}}\,dV\right] $$

**Step 4 — Solving for N at a given survival probability.**

Taking logarithms and isolating N:

$$ N^{e} \;=\; \frac{-\ln S}{A}\left[\int_{\Omega}\frac{\tau_0^{c}}{z_0^{h}}\,dV\right]^{-1} $$

Raising both sides to the power 1/e:

$$ N \;=\; \left[\frac{-\ln S}{A}\right]^{1/e}\left[\int_{\Omega}\frac{\tau_0^{c}}{z_0^{h}}\,dV\right]^{-1/e} $$

> **Final canonical Lundberg–Palmgren form:**
> $$ N \;\propto\; \left[\int_{\Omega}\frac{\tau_0^{c}}{z_0^{h}}\,dV\right]^{-1/e} $$

For 90% survival (S = 0.9, the L₁₀ rating), the leading constant is absorbed into the dynamic load rating C, yielding the working equation:

$$ L_{10} \;=\; \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p} $$

with **p = 3 for ball bearings** and **p = 10/3 for roller bearings**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Strategies vs Failure Mechanism

| Design Lever | Mechanism Affected | Quantitative Effect | Limit / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase conformity (↑ R) | Reduces p₀ by $R^{-1/3}$ | Bearing life ↑ 10–30× | Bounding box of housing |
| Through-harden to 58–62 HRC | ↑ τ₀ endurance limit | Life ↑ 5–8× | Core toughness ↓ |
| Case-carburize (case 1.0–1.5 mm) | Compressive σᵣ in case | Life ↑ 3–10× | Case-core hardness ratio |
| Shot peening | Residual σᵣ ≈ –800 MPa | Life ↑ 2–4× | Surface roughness ↑ |
| Improve surface finish (Ra ↓) | Λ ↑ ⇒ full-film EHL | Life ↑ 2–5× | Cost of superfinishing |
| Use EP / synthetic grease | EHL film h_min ↑ | Life ↑ 1.5–3× | Viscosity at operating T |
| Avoid sliding-spinoff | Eliminate traction τ at surface | Spalling onset deferred | Manufacturing tolerance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on the matrix:** KTU 2024 Part-B answers should explicitly link the **lever** to the **mechanism** and quote a **quantitative effect**. A 7-mark sub-question typically requires 4 such links.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Designing for Contact Fatigue — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Design Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Operating Load F and Speed n"] --> B["Compute Hertzian Pressure p0 via Hertz Equations"]
    B --> C{"Check p0 less than Yield Limit"}
    C -- "No" --> D["Redesign: Increase Contact Area or Reduce Load"]
    C -- "Yes" --> E["Compute Critical Depth z0 and Shear Stress tau0"]
    E --> F["Apply Lundberg Palmgren or Ioannides Harris Model"]
    F --> G["Compute Life L10 in million revolutions"]
    G --> H{"L10 Meets Target Life"}
    H -- "No" --> I["Apply Design Levers Matrix"]
    I --> J["Select Material Treatment Lubricant"]
    J --> K["Iterate until L10 meets target"]
    H -- "Yes" --> L["Final Design Validation via Bench and Field Test"]
    K --> B
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Three Stage Failure Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Stage I Nucleation: Subsurface Crack at z0"] --> S2["Stage II Propagation: Crack on Inclined Plane"]
    S2 --> S3["Stage III Spalling: Pit Ejection and Surface Crater"]
    S3 --> S4["Catastrophic Failure and Vibration Rise"]
    S1 -. "driven by tau0" .- Driver1["Alternating Orthogonal Shear"]
    S2 -. "driven by delta K" .- Driver2["Stress Intensity Range"]
    S3 -. "driven by trapped lubricant" .- Driver3["Hydrostatic Pressure Pulse"]
```

    M0["Required L10 in million revolutions"] --> M1{"Required L10 less than 500"}
    M1 -- "Yes" --> M2["Use Standard Through Hardened Bearing Steel AISI 52100"]
    M1 -- "No" --> M3{"Required L10 less than 2000"}
    M3 -- "Yes" --> M4["Use Case Carburized Steel or Vacuum Induction Melted VIM 52100"]
    M3 -- "No" --> M5["Use Hybrid Ceramic Bearing or M50 Nil Steel with Duplex Treatment"]
    M2 --> M6["Superfinish to Ra 0.05 micrometres"]

```

```
