# Designing for Contact Fatigue — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define contact fatigue. Why does the initiating crack nucleate below the surface and not on the contact surface?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Contact fatigue is a localised failure mechanism caused by **cyclic sub-surface shear stresses** generated under repeated Hertzian contact, leading to crack nucleation, propagation and eventual pitting or spalling. The initiating crack nucleates **below the surface** because, although the compressive hydrostatic stress is maximum on the surface (which suppresses Mode-I opening), the **alternating orthogonal shear stress τ₀ reaches its peak amplitude at a depth z₀ ≈ 0.48 a (point contact) or 0.786 a (line contact)** below the surface. Surface cracks are therefore suppressed by triaxial compression, while subsurface shear is free to drive Mode-II crack growth on planes inclined ≈ 30° to the rolling direction. **[1 mark for definition, 2 marks for subsurface nucleation logic.]**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**What is the Lundberg–Palmgren fatigue-life equation? State the significance of the exponent *p*.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Lundberg–Palmgren basic rating-life equation is

$$ L_{10} \;=\; \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p} $$

where C is the **dynamic load rating**, P is the **equivalent dynamic radial load**, and **p = 3 for ball bearings** and **p = 10/3 for cylindrical / tapered roller bearings**. The exponent *p* encodes the contact geometry: line contacts (rollers) distribute stress over a wider stressed volume, so damage accumulates more slowly per revolution, giving a steeper exponent and hence greater sensitivity of life to load ratio. **[1 mark equation, 2 marks p-value with interpretation.]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **three stages of contact fatigue** in a rolling-element bearing. State the **stress parameter** that drives each stage and the **critical depth z₀** at which the Stage-I crack nucleates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Hertzian maximum contact pressure p₀** for a steel ball of radius R pressed against a flat steel raceway under normal load F, assuming both bodies have the same Young's modulus E and Poisson's ratio ν. Compute p₀ numerically for F = 5 kN, R = 10 mm, E = 210 GPa, ν = 0.3. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Diagram (2 marks):** A neat sketch of a rolling-element bearing showing (i) ball, (ii) inner race, (iii) outer race, (iv) contact ellipse, (v) sub-surface crack on inclined plane at depth z₀, and (vi) spalled crater at surface.

**Three stages (3 marks):**

- **Stage I — Nucleation.** At depth z₀ below the contact patch, the **alternating orthogonal shear stress amplitude τ₀** reaches maximum value. A crack of size ≈ grain-boundary scale nucleates here. $z_0 \approx 0.48\,a$ for circular (point) contact and $z_0 \approx 0.786\,a$ for line contact.

- **Stage II — Propagation.** The crack advances on a plane **inclined 30° to the rolling direction**. Growth is governed by the **stress-intensity-factor range ΔK**, following a Paris-type law $da/dN = K(\Delta K)^m$.

- **Stage III — Spall.** Once the crack length reaches a critical value, it turns toward the surface. Lubricant trapped in the crack produces a **hydrostatic pressure pulse**, accelerating the crack. A lenticular particle detaches, leaving a pit.

**Critical depth (2 marks):** $z_0 = 0.48\,a$ for circular (ball–on–flat) contact; $z_0 = 0.786\,a$ for line (roller–on–flat) contact. Quoted in terms of the Hertzian semi-axis a.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Derivation (5 marks):** (follows Section 3.1 above)

$$ E^* = \frac{E}{2(1-\nu^2)} = \frac{210\times10^9}{2(1-0.09)} = 115.6\;\text{GPa} $$

$$ a = \left(\frac{3\,F\,R}{4\,E^*}\right)^{1/3} $$

$$ p_0 = \frac{3F}{2\pi a^2} = \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{6\,F\,{E^*}^2}{R^2}\right)^{1/3} $$

**Numerical computation (2 marks):**

Plug in F = 5000 N, R = 0.01 m, E* = 115.6 × 10⁹ Pa:

$$ p_0 = \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{6 \times 5000 \times (115.6\times 10^9)^2}{(0.01)^2}\right)^{1/3} $$

$$ p_0 = \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{30{,}000 \times 1.336\times 10^{22}}{1\times 10^{-4}}\right)^{1/3} $$

$$ p_0 = \frac{1}{\pi}\left(4.01\times 10^{30}\right)^{1/3} \approx \frac{1.59\times 10^{10}}{\pi} \approx 5.07\;\text{GPa} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> A common error is forgetting the **factor of 2 in the reduced modulus** when both bodies have the same E and ν. Another common error is mixing up units (using mm instead of m for R), which collapses p₀ by a factor of $(1000)^{1/3}\approx 10$. Loss of **1 mark per error**. Also: write **both intermediate values of a and p₀ explicitly** — KTU examiners award partial credit only if numerical sub-steps are visible.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]

**(a)** With reference to the **Ioannides–Harris refinement** of the Lundberg–Palmgren model, explain why the classical L-P model under-predicts life in the high-cycle regime. How does the **endurance-limit stress σ_lim** resolve this anomaly? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6309 deep-groove ball bearing carries an equivalent dynamic radial load P = 12 kN at 1500 rpm. The manufacturer specifies a dynamic load rating C = 55 kN and an exponent p = 3. Compute the basic rating life **L₁₀** in millions of revolutions and in operating hours. If field experience shows that actual life is only 40% of L₁₀ due to lubricant contamination (viscosity ratio κ = 0.6), estimate the **modified rating life L₁₀ₘ** using the ISO 281 contamination factor $a_{ISO} = 0.4$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**L-P limitation (3 marks):** The classical Lundberg–Palmgren model predicts **infinite life only when τ₀ → 0**, i.e. at vanishing load. In practice, all engineering bearing steels exhibit a genuine **endurance limit** below which no fatigue damage accumulates regardless of cycle count. The L-P curve thus diverges from experimental S-N curves in the high-cycle (10⁸ – 10¹⁰ rev) regime, severely under-predicting safe operating life.

**Ioannides–Harris refinement (3 marks):** Ioannides & Harris (1985) introduced an **endurance-limit stress σ_lim** into the integrand via the Macaulay bracket:

$$ L_{IH} \;\propto\; \int_{\Omega}\!\left\langle \tau_0(x) - \tau_{lim} \right\rangle^{c} dV $$

When $\tau_0 < \tau_{lim}$, the integrand vanishes and **no damage accumulates**. The model recovers the classical L-P result when $\tau_{lim} \to 0$, and asymptotically flattens to infinite life when the equivalent contact stress lies below the endurance limit. The exponent c ≈ 9 for through-hardened 52100 steel.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The I-H model is the basis of the modern ISO 281:2007 / ISO 16281 standard used in industry.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Basic rating life (3 marks):**

$$ L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p} = \left(\frac{55}{12}\right)^{3} = (4.583)^{3} \approx 96.3 \;\text{million revolutions} $$

**Convert to operating hours (2 marks):**

$$ L_{10,h} = \frac{96.3\times 10^6}{1500 \times 60} = \frac{96.3\times 10^6}{90{,}000} \approx 1070\;\text{hours} $$

**Modified rating life (2 marks):**

$$ L_{10m} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot a_3 \cdot L_{10} $$

With $a_1 = 1$ (standard reliability), $a_{ISO} = 0.4$, $a_3 = 1$ (no special properties):

$$ L_{10m} = 1 \times 0.4 \times 1 \times 96.3 \approx 38.5\;\text{million revolutions} \approx 428\;\text{hours} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> Many students forget that **L₁₀ is conventionally quoted in MILLIONS of revolutions** (the "10" subscript denotes 10% failure probability at this life). Writing $L_{10} = 96.3$ instead of $L_{10} = 96.3\times 10^6$ costs 1 mark. Also, the contamination factor is **dimensionless** and **multiplicative**, not additive — common error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Designing for Contact Fatigue**

- [ ] Contact fatigue is governed by the **subsurface orthogonal shear stress τ₀**, NOT by the surface compressive stress p₀.
- [ ] The **critical depth** of crack initiation is **z₀ ≈ 0.48 a** for circular contact and **z₀ ≈ 0.786 a** for line contact.
- [ ] The three stages are **Nucleation → Propagation → Spallation**; each is driven by a different physics (τ₀, ΔK, lubricant pressure pulse).
- [ ] Hertz peak pressure for a ball-on-flat: $p_0 = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left(\dfrac{6\,F\,{E^*}^2}{R^2}\right)^{\!1/3}$.
- [ ] **Reduced modulus** combines both bodies: $\frac{1}{E^*} = \frac{1-\nu_1^2}{E_1}+\frac{1-\nu_2^2}{E_2}$.
- [ ] Lundberg–Palmgren: $L_{10}=(C/P)^p$ with **p = 3 (ball)**, **p = 10/3 (roller)**.
- [ ] Ioannides–Harris refines L-P by introducing an **endurance-limit stress** (ISO 281:2007 basis).
- [ ] **Lambda ratio Λ** classifies lubrication regime: Λ < 1 boundary (danger), Λ > 3 full-film EHL (safe).
- [ ] Design levers in order of impact: **Material grade → Heat treatment → Residual stress → Surface finish → Lubrication**.
- [ ] Always **quote units** in KTU derivations: a in metres, p₀ in Pa (or GPa), τ₀ in MPa.
- [ ] Common pitfall: confusing **dynamic load rating C** with **static load rating C₀**.
- [ ] Common pitfall: writing L₁₀ without the "× 10⁶ revolutions" multiplier.
- [ ] In design matrices, **link lever → mechanism → quantitative effect** to score full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Influence of High Temperatures on Material Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Influence of High Temperatures on Material Properties # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Temperature Material Behavior** is the study of how engineering materials (metals, alloys, polymers, and ceramics) undergo **microstructural, mechanical, chemical, and dimensional changes** when exposed to temperatures typically above **0.4 × T_m** (where T_m is the absolute melting temperature in Kelvin) — a regime known as the **Homologous Temperature Threshold**. In this regime, materials no longer behave according to their room-temperature elastic–plastic constitutive laws. Instead, they exhibit **time-dependent deformation**, irreversible microstructural evolution, and progressive loss of load-bearing capacity. The key phenomena covered under KTU Module 4 include: | # | Phenomenon | KTU Definition | |---|------------|----------------| | 1 | **Creep** | Time-dependent plastic deformation under sustained load at elevated temperature. | | 2 | **Stress Rupture** | Sudden fracture after prolonged loading at high temperature. | | 3 | **Thermal Fatigue** | Cyclic damage caused by alternating thermal strains. | | 4 | **Oxidation / Hot Corrosion** | Surface degradation due to reaction with gases at elevated T. | | 5 | **Hot Shortness** | Loss of ductility along grain boundaries at high temperature. | | 6 | **Phase Transformations** | Allotropic or precipitate changes that alter mechanical behavior. | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cold Butter vs. Warm Butter" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — Why Materials Behave Differently at High Temperature** > > Imagine trying to spread **cold butter** on toast. It resists, cracks, and barely deforms. That is exactly how a steel beam behaves at room temperature — it follows Hooke's law, yields sharply, and breaks in a defined, time-independent way. > > Now picture leaving that butter on the kitchen counter for an hour. It slowly slumps, oozes, and deforms under its own weight — **without any additional force**. This is creep. > > If you leave it long enough near the stove, it will eventually "give up" and split apart — this is **stress rupture**. > > Finally, the surface browns and turns rancid — that is **oxidation**. > > **The homologous temperature (T/T_m)** is the engineer's "thermometer for the butter." Once T/T_m > 0.4, time itself becomes a structural material property. ## 1.3 The Homologous Temperature — Master Threshold > [!IMPORTANT] > **Homologous Temperature** is defined as: > > $$T_H = \dfrac{T_{service}}{T_{melting}}$$ > > where both temperatures are in **Kelvin (K)** or **Rankine (°R)**. **Standard reference thresholds:** | T_H Range | Mechanical Regime | Dominant Behavior | |-----------|--------------------|--------------------| | < 0.25 | **Cold Regime** | Elastic–plastic, time-independent | | 0.25 – 0.4 | **Warm Regime** | Minor thermally-activated softening | | 0.4 – 0.7 | **Creep Regime** | Time-dependent deformation dominant | | > 0.7 | **Hot Regime / Near-melt** | Severe microstructural instability | ## 1.4 Standard High-Temperature Metrics The following quantitative descriptors are emphasized in the **KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (CO4 — Evaluate material limits under service conditions)**: - **Creep rate (ε̇)** in units of **s⁻¹** or **%/hr** - **Stress rupture life (t_r)** in **hours (h)** - **Larson–Miller Parameter (LMP)** — dimensionless - **Activation Energy (Q_c)** in **kJ/mol** - **Steady-state creep exponent (n)** — dimensionless - **Oxidation weight gain (Δm/A)** in **mg/cm²** # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanical Property Degradation with Temperature As temperature rises, all four fundamental mechanical properties of a metal **degrade in distinct, measurable patterns**: ### 2.1.1 Effect on Young's Modulus (E) > The modulus of elasticity decreases gradually because atomic bond stiffness weakens with thermal vibration. $$E(T) = E_{RT} \left[ 1 - k_E (T - T_{RT}) \right]$$ where $k_E$ is the **modulus-temperature coefficient** (typically 0.0003 – 0.0005 per °C for steels). ### 2.1.2 Effect on Yield Strength (σ_y) and UTS - **σ_y and σ_UTS** fall sharply once T exceeds ~0.4 T_m. - The **yield–UTS gap closes**, signaling **reduced strain-hardening capacity**. - Beyond ~0.6 T_m, σ_y can drop to **30–50%** of its room-temperature value. ### 2.1.3 Effect on Ductility (% Elongation, % RA) - Ductility typically **increases** initially (because dislocation glide is easier). - However, at very high temperatures, **grain-boundary sliding** reduces **ductility in short tests** but produces enormous **long-term strain** (creep ductility). ### 2.1.4 Effect on Toughness / Impact Energy - Charpy impact energy shows the classic **ductile-to-brittle transition** for BCC metals. - For FCC metals and most austenitic stainless steels, ductility **rises monotonically**. ## 2.2 Creep — The Central Phenomenon of Module 4 > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition — Creep** > *Time-dependent, permanent (plastic) deformation of a material subjected to a constant load or stress at an elevated temperature, generally above 0.4 T_m.* ### 2.2.1 The Three Classical Stages of Creep | Stage | Name | Behavior | Engineering Significance | |-------|------|----------|--------------------------| | **I** | **Primary (Transient) Creep** | Strain rate decreases with time; work hardening dominates | Design strains can be tolerated | | **II** | **Secondary (Steady-State) Creep** | Strain rate is constant; balance between hardening and recovery | **Governs design life** — minimum rate region | | **III** | **Tertiary Creep** | Strain rate accelerates; void formation, necking, micro-cracking | **Failure imminent** — creep rupture | ### 2.2.2 Creep Mechanisms (Microscopic View) 1. **Dislocation Creep (Slip/Climb)** — dominant at intermediate stresses and high T. 2. **Diffusional Creep (Nabarro–Herring & Coble)** — dominant at low stresses and very high T; involves vacancy flow through lattice (N-H) or grain boundaries (Coble). 3. **Grain-Boundary Sliding** — dominant in fine-grained materials; often coupled with diffusional flow. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Memorize before ESE** | # | Formula | Equation | Variables / Units | Application | |---|---------|----------|-------------------|-------------| | 1 | **Homologous Temperature** | $T_H = T/T_m$ | T, T_m in **K** | Defines high-T regime | | 2 | **Steady-State Creep Rate (Bailey–Norton Law)** | $\dot{\varepsilon}_s = A \, \sigma^n \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$ | $\dot{\varepsilon}_s$: s⁻¹, $\sigma$: MPa, $Q_c$: J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) | Predicts long-term strain rate | | 3 | **Power-Law Creep (Dorn Form)** | $\dot{\varepsilon}_s = \dfrac{A \, D_0 \, G \, b}{kT} \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{G}\right)^n \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$ | D₀: diffusion coefficient (m²/s), G: shear modulus (MPa), b: Burgers vector (m) | Rigorous form | | 4 | **Nabarro–Herring Creep** | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{NH} = \dfrac{A_{NH} \, D_L \, \sigma \, \Omega}{d^2 \, kT}$ | $D_L$: lattice diffusivity, $\Omega$: atomic volume, $d$: grain diameter | Low-stress, fine grain | | 5 | **Coble Creep** | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{Co} = \dfrac{A_{Co} \, D_{GB} \, \sigma \, \delta \, \Omega}{d^3 \, kT}$ | $D_{GB}$: GB diffusivity, $\delta$: GB width | GB diffusion dominant | | 6 | **Larson–Miller Parameter** | $LMP = T \left[ C + \log_{10}(t_r) \right]$ | T in **K**, $t_r$ in **hours**, $C \approx 20$ (typical) | Extrapolates rupture life | | 7 | **Monkman–Grant Relation** | $\log_{10}(t_r) + m \log_{10}(\dot{\varepsilon}_s) = B$ | m ≈ 0.8 – 1.0, B = constant | Predicts rupture from creep rate | | 8 | **Sherby–Dorn Parameter** | $\theta = t_r \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$ | t_r in **s**, $Q_c$: kJ/mol | Alternative to LMP | | 9 | **Arrhenius-Type Diffusion** | $D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$ | D₀: pre-exponential (m²/s) | Foundation for creep models | | 10 | **Thermal Strain (Free Expansion)** | $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \, \Delta T$ | $\alpha$: CTE (1/°C or 1/K) | Thermal fatigue basis | | 11 | **Oxidation (Parabolic Law)** | $\left(\dfrac{\Delta m}{A}\right)^2 = k_p \, t$ | $k_p$: parabolic rate constant (mg²/cm⁴·s) | High-T corrosion kinetics | | 12 | **Modulus Degradation** | $E(T) = E_0 (1 - k_E \Delta T)$ | $k_E$: /°C | Stiffness loss | | 13 | **Strength Reduction (Approx.)** | $\sigma_y(T) = \sigma_{y0} \left[ 1 - k_\sigma (T - T_0) \right]$ | $k_\sigma$: /°C | Quick design estimate | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Component | High-T Concern | |----------|-----------|----------------| | **Power Generation** | Boiler tubes, turbine blades (T > 540 °C) | Creep, stress rupture | | **Aerospace** | Jet engine discs, combustor liners (T > 900 °C) | Creep, oxidation, thermal fatigue | | **Petrochemical** | Reactor coils, cracker tubes (T > 700 °C) | Carburization, creep | | **Nuclear** | Cladding, steam generators | Irradiation-assisted creep | | **Automotive (Turbo)** | Exhaust manifolds, turbine wheels | Thermomechanical fatigue | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 CO Mapping** > This topic directly addresses **CO4 — "Evaluate the influence of service environment on material performance"** and **CO5 — "Apply time-dependent failure models to design against high-temperature degradation."** # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pedagogical Note** > The derivations below are **complete, line-by-line, and assessment-ready**. Every step is justified. Use these as the **gold standard** for 14-mark answers. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bailey–Norton Steady-State Creep Equation ### Statement Derive the Bailey–Norton creep equation: $$\dot{\varepsilon}_s = A \, \sigma^n \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$ ### Step 1 — Empirical Foundation Empirical creep tests conducted on metals above 0.4 T_m reveal that the steady-state creep rate depends on: - Applied stress $\sigma$ - Absolute temperature $T$ - An activation-energy-controlled diffusion process ### Step 2 — Stress Dependence (Power Law) From experiment, at constant $T$: $$\dot{\varepsilon}_s \propto \sigma^n \quad (n \ge 3 \text{ for dislocation creep})$$ ### Step 3 — Temperature Dependence (Arrhenius) Diffusion-controlled processes obey Arrhenius kinetics. The creep rate therefore carries an Arrhenius factor: $$\dot{\varepsilon}_s \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$ where: - $Q_c$ = **activation energy for creep** (≈ activation energy for self-diffusion) - $R$ = **universal gas constant** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - $T$ = absolute temperature in **K** ### Step 4 — Combine Stress and Temperature Effects The two effects are multiplicative because they arise from **independent physical phenomena** (mechanical driving force + thermally activated flow): $$\dot{\varepsilon}_s = A \, \sigma^n \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$ ### Step 5 — Physical Interpretation of Constants - **A** = structure-dependent constant (depends on grain size, modulus, Burgers vector, etc.) - **n** = **stress exponent**: - n ≈ 1 → diffusional creep (N-H, Coble) - n ≈ 2 → grain-boundary sliding - n ≈ 3 – 8 → dislocation creep - **Q_c** = activation energy: - ≈ 100 kJ/mol for aluminium - ≈ 280 kJ/mol for ferritic steel - ≈ 400 kJ/mol for nickel superalloys ### Step 6 — Rigorous Dorn Form (Dimensional Correctness) The dimensionally-correct Bailey–Norton equation (Dorn form) is: $$\dot{\varepsilon}_s = \dfrac{A \, D_0 \, G \, b}{k \, T} \left( \dfrac{\sigma}{G} \right)^n \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$ where: - $D_0$ = pre-exponential diffusion coefficient (m²/s) - $G$ = shear modulus (Pa) - $b$ = Burgers vector (m) - $k$ = Boltzmann constant = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** This form guarantees that the equation is **dimensionally homogeneous**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Larson–Miller Parameter (LMP) ### Statement Derive the Larson–Miller parameter used to extrapolate long-term stress-rupture data from short-term tests. ### Step 1 — Empirical Starting Point For many alloys, log(rupture life) is approximately **linear** with **1/T** at constant stress. Equivalently, for fixed stress: $$\log_{10}(t_r) \propto \dfrac{1}{T}$$ ### Step 2 — Integrate Arrhenius Equation The Arrhenius rate equation integrated over a fixed "damage threshold" yields a constant: $$\int_0^{t_r} \dfrac{dt}{t} = \text{const} \times \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right) \cdot t_r$$ This implies: $$t_r \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right) = \text{const}$$ ### Step 3 — Take Logarithm (Base 10) Taking $\log_{10}$ of both sides: $$\log_{10}(t_r) - \dfrac{Q_c}{2.303 \, RT} = \log_{10}(\text{const})$$ ### Step 4 — Rearrange to Standard LMP Form Define the constant $C = -\log_{10}(\text{const})$ and multiply both sides by $T$: $$T \left[ \log_{10}(t_r) + C \right] = \dfrac{Q_c}{2.303 \, R} = LMP$$ ### Step 5 — Final Result $$\boxed{\;LMP = T \left[ C + \log_{10}(t_r) \right]\;}$$ with **T in Kelvin**, $t_r$ in **hours**, and $C \approx 20$ for most engineering alloys. ### Step 6 — How It Is Used Given a **target rupture life** $t_r$ (e.g., 100,000 h ≈ 11.4 years for power-plant design) and a **design temperature**, compute the required LMP. Then, from a master **stress vs. LMP curve** for the candidate alloy, read off the allowable stress. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Monkman–Grant Relationship ### Step 1 — Experimental Observation For many alloys: $$t_r \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}_s^{m} = \text{constant} = K_{MG}$$ ### Step 2 — Take Logarithm $$\log_{10}(t_r) + m \log_{10}(\dot{\varepsilon}_s) = \log_{10}(K_{MG})$$ ### Step 3 — Engineering Use If the **steady-state creep rate** is measured (say, from a short 1000-h test), the **rupture life** can be estimated without running a full rupture test. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Steady-State Creep Strain ### Step 1 — Definition In Stage II, the strain rate is constant: $$\dot{\varepsilon}_s = \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \text{const}$$ ### Step 2 — Integrate Over Time $$\varepsilon_s(t) = \dot{\varepsilon}_s \cdot t$$ ### Step 3 — Total Strain in Service Life $$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_0 + \dot{\varepsilon}_s \cdot t_{service}$$ where $\varepsilon_0$ is the **instantaneous elastic + plastic strain** at loading. ### Step 4 — Design Criterion For design acceptance: $$\varepsilon_{total} \le \varepsilon_{allowable}$$ This is the **creep-strain-limited design** approach used for boiler tubes and turbine casings. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern) > **Problem:** > A superheater tube operates at 600 °C under a hoop stress of 80 MPa. The creep law constants are: A = 1.2 × 10⁻⁵ (MPa⁻ⁿ · s⁻¹), n = 5, Q_c = 280 kJ/mol. Compute the steady-state creep rate and the strain accumulated over 50,000 hours. ### Solution **Given:** $$T = 600 + 273 = 873 \, K$$ $$\sigma = 80 \, MPa, \quad n = 5, \quad Q_c = 280{,}000 \, J/mol$$ $$R = 8.314 \, J/(mol \cdot K)$$ **Step 1 — Compute Arrhenius Factor** $$\dfrac{Q_c}{RT} = \dfrac{280{,}000}{8.314 \times 873} = 38.585$$ $$\exp(-38.585) = 1.728 \times 10^{-17}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Stress Factor** $$\sigma^n = 80^5 = 3{,}276{,}800{,}000 = 3.277 \times 10^9 \, MPa^n$$ **Step 3 — Compute Creep Rate** $$\dot{\varepsilon}_s = (1.2 \times 10^{-5}) \times (3.277 \times 10^9) \times (1.728 \times 10^{-17})$$ $$\dot{\varepsilon}_s = 6.795 \times 10^{-13} \, s^{-1}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to %/hr** $$\dot{\varepsilon}_s = 6.795 \times 10^{-13} \times 3600 \times 100 \, \% /hr$$ $$\dot{\varepsilon}_s = 2.446 \times 10^{-7} \, \% /hr$$ **Step 5 — Total Strain in 50,000 h** $$\varepsilon_{total} = (2.446 \times 10^{-7}) \times 50{,}000 = 0.01223 \, \%$$ **Step 6 — Interpret** A strain of **0.012 %** is well within the allowable 1 % design strain for superheater tubes → **Design is safe.** --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mechanism Selection Map | Stress Level | Temperature | Dominant Mechanism | n-value | Diffusion Path | |--------------|-------------|--------------------|---------|----------------| | Low | Very High (>0.8 T_m) | **Nabarro–Herring** | 1 | Lattice | | Low | High (>0.5 T_m) | **Coble** | 1 | Grain Boundary | | Medium | High | **Grain-Boundary Sliding** | 2 | GB + Lattice | | High | Moderate–High | **Dislocation Climb** | 3–8 | Lattice | | Very High | All | **Power-Law Breakdown** | > 8 | Plastic flow | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Creep Curve — Classic Three-Stage Schematic The following Mermaid block renders a **block-functional representation** of the classic creep strain-vs-time curve, marking each stage with its controlling physics. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["LOADING INSTANT
Elastic Strain epsilon_0"] --> B["STAGE I PRIMARY CREEP
Strain rate decreases
Work hardening dominant"] B --> C["STAGE II STEADY STATE
Constant strain rate
Balance hardening vs recovery
DESIGN GOVERNS"] C --> D["STAGE III TERTIARY
Strain rate accelerates
Void nucleation
Grain boundary cavitation
Necking"] D --> E["RUPTURE
Final fracture"] style A fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000 style B fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000 style C fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000 style D fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,color:#000 style E fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Property Degradation Map with Temperature ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph RT["ROOM TEMPERATURE REGION"] R1["High Yield Strength"] R2["High Modulus E"] R3["Time-independent behavior"] end subgraph MID["ELEVATED TEMPERATURE 0.4 to 0.6 T_m"] M1["Yield Strength drops sharply"] M2["Modulus decreases gradually"] M3["Creep initiates"] M4["Oxidation begins"] end subgraph HIGH["HIGH TEMPERATURE greater than 0.6 T_m"] H1["Severe strength loss"] H2["Rapid creep deformation"] H3["Stress rupture risk"] H4["Microstructural instability"] H5["Phase transformation risk"] end RT --> MID --> HIGH style RT fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000 style MID fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000 style HIGH fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 High-Temperature Failure Mechanism — Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S["SUSTAINED LOAD + HIGH TEMPERATURE"] --> M1["Dislocation Glide and Climb"] S --> M2["Vacancy Diffusion"] S --> M3["Grain Boundary Sliding"] S --> M4["Surface Oxidation"] M1 --> C1["Strain Accumulation"] M2 --> C1 M3 --> C1 M4 --> C2["Section Loss + Embrittlement"] C1 --> F1["CREEP RUPTURE"] C2 --> F1 C1 --> F2["BUCKLING from Excessive Strain"] C1 --> F3["THERMAL FATIGUE from Cyclic Strain"] style S fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff style F1 fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#000,color:#fff style F2 fill:#9a3412,stroke:#000,color:#fff style F3 fill:#9a3412,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C1 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000 style C2 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Larson–Miller Extrapolation Procedure Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Short-term rupture data
at multiple temperatures"] --> B["Compute LMP for each test
LMP = T times C plus log t_r"] B --> C["Plot log sigma vs LMP
Master Curve"] C --> D["For target service life t_r
and design temperature T"] D --> E["Compute required LMP"] E --> F["Read allowable stress from master curve"] F --> G["Apply safety factor
for design stress"] style A fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000 style C fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000 style G fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000 ``` ## 4.5 Master Curves — Schematic Property Trends ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PROP["PROPERTY vs TEMPERATURE"] P1["Yield Strength sigma_y
Monotonic decrease
Sharp drop beyond 0.4 T_m"] P2["Modulus E
Linear gradual decrease"] P3["Ductility percent El
Rises then may fall"] P4["Creep Rate eps dot
Exponential increase"] end P1 -.competes.-> P4 style P1 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,color:#000 style P2 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000 style P3 fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000 style P4 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]** ### **Q1. Define the Homologous Temperature. Why is 0.4 T_m considered a critical threshold for creep?** **[3 Marks] [CO4, Understand — Bloom Level 2]** **Model Answer:** > Homologous temperature is the ratio of service temperature to the melting temperature of the material, both expressed in absolute units: > > $$T_H = \dfrac{T}{T_m}$$ > > It is a **dimensionless index** that allows comparison of operating severity across different material systems (e.g., aluminium at 200 °C behaves like steel at 600 °C because their homologous temperatures are similar). > > At **T_H ≈ 0.4**, the thermal energy becomes sufficient to activate **diffusion-controlled mechanisms** such as dislocation climb and vacancy migration. Below this threshold, deformation is essentially **time-independent** (governed by slip only); above it, **time-dependent creep strain** becomes design-significant. Hence 0.4 T_m is universally adopted in failure analysis as the **creep-design threshold**. --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between Creep and Stress Rupture.** **[3 Marks] [CO4, Understand — Bloom Level 2]** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Creep | Stress Rupture | |--------|-------|----------------| | **Definition** | Time-dependent plastic strain accumulation under sustained load at high T | Catastrophic fracture after prolonged loading at high T | | **Output** | Strain (%) | Time-to-fracture (h) | | **Controlling factor** | Steady-state strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon}_s$ | Rupture life $t_r$ | | **Design metric** | Strain-limited design (ε ≤ ε_allow) | Rupture-limited design (LMP, Monkman–Grant) | | **Failure mode** | Excessive deformation, distortion | Brittle-like intergranular fracture | > **Key insight:** Creep is the **process**; stress rupture is the **end state** of the process when tertiary creep dominates. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Theory + Numerical** **[(a)] Discuss in detail the three stages of creep with the help of a neatly labelled strain-vs-time diagram. Explain the dominant microstructural mechanism in each stage.** **[7 Marks] [CO4, Apply — Bloom Level 3]** **Model Answer:** **Stage I — Primary (Transient) Creep** - Strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon}$ **decreases** with time. - Microstructure: **dislocation multiplication and entanglement** (work hardening). - Macroscopic curve: concave-down. - The material is adjusting to the imposed stress by reorganising its dislocation network. **Stage II — Secondary (Steady-State) Creep** - Strain rate is **constant** — the minimum value reached. - Microstructure: a dynamic balance between **work hardening** and **thermal recovery** (dislocation climb, annihilation). - This is the **design-governing stage** because the rate is predictable and amenable to Bailey–Norton modelling: $$\dot{\varepsilon}_s = A \, \sigma^n \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$ - Lasts the longest duration for properly designed components. **Stage III — Tertiary Creep** - Strain rate **accelerates** rapidly. - Microstructure: **void nucleation**, **grain-boundary cavitation**, **microcrack coalescence**, localised necking, and possibly oxidation-assisted damage. - The component is **on the verge of rupture**. **Conclusion:** Engineers design for Stages I + II only; Stage III represents incipient failure and must be avoided. --- **[(b)] A nickel-based superalloy blade operates at 950 °C under a steady stress of 200 MPa. The creep law constants are A = 8.5 × 10⁻⁹ (MPa⁻⁵ · h⁻¹), n = 5, and Q_c = 380 kJ/mol. Calculate the steady-state creep rate and the time to accumulate 1% creep strain. Comment on the design safety.** **[7 Marks] [CO4, Apply — Bloom Level 3]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert Units** $$T = 950 + 273 = 1223 \, K$$ **Step 2 — Arrhenius Factor** $$\dfrac{Q_c}{RT} = \dfrac{380{,}000}{8.314 \times 1223} = 37.366$$ $$\exp(-37.366) = 5.51 \times 10^{-17}$$ **Step 3 — Stress Power** $$\sigma^n = 200^5 = 3.2 \times 10^{11} \, MPa^5$$ **Step 4 — Creep Rate** $$\dot{\varepsilon}_s = (8.5 \times 10^{-9}) \times (3.2 \times 10^{11}) \times (5.51 \times 10^{-17})$$ $$\dot{\varepsilon}_s = 1.499 \times 10^{-13} \, h^{-1}$$ **Step 5 — Time for 1% Strain** $$t = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\dot{\varepsilon}_s} = \dfrac{0.01}{1.499 \times 10^{-13}} = 6.67 \times 10^{10} \, h$$ **Step 6 — Interpret** $$6.67 \times 10^{10} \, h \approx 7.6 \times 10^6 \, years$$ **Conclusion:** The creep deformation is **negligibly small** — the design is **safe** for normal service life (typically 30,000–100,000 h for turbine blades). However, the engineer must still verify **stress rupture life** (Stage III) using the **Larson–Miller parameter**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[(a)] With neat sketches, explain the various mechanisms of creep deformation. How are these mechanisms represented mathematically?** **[7 Marks] [CO4, Understand — Bloom Level 2]** **Model Answer:** The three principal microscopic mechanisms of creep are: **1. Dislocation Creep (Slip + Climb) — High Stress Regime** - Dislocations glide on slip planes and then **climb** out of obstacles by absorbing/emitting vacancies. - Climbing is **diffusion-controlled** and hence thermally activated. - Stress exponent: **n ≈ 3 – 8** - Governing equation (Bailey–Norton): $$\dot{\varepsilon}_s = A \, \sigma^n \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$ **2. Diffusional Creep — Low Stress Regime** Two sub-mechanisms: - **Nabarro–Herring (N-H) creep**: vacancies flow **through the lattice** from tensile to compressive grain boundaries. $$\dot{\varepsilon}_{NH} = \dfrac{A_{NH} \, D_L \, \sigma \, \Omega}{d^2 \, kT}$$ - **Coble creep**: vacancies flow **along grain boundaries**; favoured in fine-grained materials. $$\dot{\varepsilon}_{Co} = \dfrac{A_{Co} \, D_{GB} \, \sigma \, \delta \, \Omega}{d^3 \, kT}$$ - Both give **n = 1**. **3. Grain-Boundary Sliding (GBS)** - Adjacent grains **slide past each other** along their common boundary. - Often coupled with diffusional accommodation (so n ≈ 2). - Predominant in **fine-grained** materials at high T. **Schematic Representation (Ascii Diagram):** ``` HIGH STRESS LOW STRESS | | v v Dislocation Climb Diffusional Flow | (NH or Coble) | | +------Grain Boundary Sliding------+ | v COMBINED CREEP STRAIN ``` **Conclusion:** Real engineering materials experience a **superposition** of these mechanisms; the **map of dominant mechanism** depends on the normalised stress $\sigma/G$ and homologous temperature $T/T_m$. --- **[(b)] Derive the Larson–Miller Parameter. How is it used to predict long-term stress rupture life from short-term test data?** **[7 Marks] [CO5, Apply — Bloom Level 3]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Empirical Observation** For many alloys, at constant stress, $\log_{10}(t_r)$ is linear with $1/T$. **Step 2 — Integrate Arrhenius Rate Equation** $$\int dt \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right) = \text{constant (damage criterion)}$$ $$t_r \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right) = K$$ **Step 3 — Convert to Logarithm (Base 10)** $$\log_{10}(t_r) - \dfrac{Q_c}{2.303 \, RT} = \log_{10}(K)$$ **Step 4 — Define LMP** Multiplying both sides by $T$ and rearranging: $$\boxed{\;LMP = T \left[ C + \log_{10}(t_r) \right]\;}$$ where $C = -\log_{10}(K)$ and $C \approx 20$ for most alloys. **Step 5 — Practical Use** 1. Conduct **short-term rupture tests** at, say, 3 – 4 elevated temperatures. 2. For each test, compute LMP = $T [20 + \log_{10}(t_r)]$. 3. Plot **log(σ) vs LMP** to obtain the **master curve**. 4. For a desired design life (e.g., 100,000 h ≈ 11.4 years) and design temperature, compute the required LMP. 5. Read the **allowable stress** from the master curve. 6. Apply the design safety factor (typically 1.5 – 2.0). **Step 6 — Numerical Illustration** Suppose $T = 873$ K and design life $t_r = 100{,}000$ h: $$LMP = 873 \left[ 20 + \log_{10}(100{,}000) \right]$$ $$LMP = 873 \times (20 + 5) = 873 \times 25 = 21{,}825$$ From the master curve at LMP = 21,825, suppose $\sigma_{allow} = 80$ MPa. Applying a safety factor of 2 → **design stress ≤ 40 MPa**. **Conclusion:** The LMP enables **safe extrapolation** by 10× – 100× beyond test data, making it indispensable in power-plant and aerospace design. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Examiner Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Confusing creep rate units** — always state the unit explicitly (s⁻¹, %/hr, or %/1000 h). Examiners penalise ambiguous units. > 2. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in the Arrhenius factor. This single error produces a **wrong answer by orders of magnitude** — full 7 marks lost. > 3. **Using σ instead of normalised σ/G** in Dorn-form equations — partial mark deduction. > 4. **Omitting diagrams in 7-mark questions** — even a hand-drawn schematic earns 1 – 2 marks. > 5. **In Larson–Miller derivation**, students forget to mention that **C ≈ 20** is empirical and **T must be in Kelvin**. Failing to state this loses 1 mark. > 6. **Not justifying the constant C** in LMP derivation — examiners expect at least one sentence explaining it is a material constant derived from damage tolerance. > 7. **Numerical errors in LMP or Monkman–Grant** — recheck log tables and use log10 consistently (not natural log). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4 / High-Temperature Material Properties** ### **Core Concepts** - Homologous temperature $T_H = T/T_m$ governs the high-T regime; threshold is **0.4**. - Creep is **time-dependent deformation**; has three stages — Primary, Secondary, Tertiary. - Steady-state creep is **design-critical** and follows Bailey–Norton law. - Stress rupture is **Stage III outcome**; predicted using **LMP** or **Monkman–Grant**. ### **Formulas to Memorize** - $\dot{\varepsilon}_s = A \, \sigma^n \, \exp(-Q_c/RT)$ - $LMP = T [C + \log_{10}(t_r)]$, with $C \approx 20$ - Nabarro–Herring: $n = 1$, $d^2$ in denominator - Coble: $n = 1$, $d^3$ in denominator - Monkman–Grant: $\log t_r + m \log \dot{\varepsilon}_s = B$ ### **Key Numbers** - R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) - k = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K - Typical $Q_c$: 100 kJ/mol (Al), 280 kJ/mol (steel), 400 kJ/mol (Ni superalloys) - Typical $n$: 1 (diffusion), 2 (GBS), 3 – 8 (dislocation) ### **Design Takeaways** - **Creep-strain-limited design** for boiler tubes. - **Rupture-limited design** (LMP) for turbine blades. - **Oxidation-limited design** for jet-engine combustors. - Always use **safety factor ≥ 1.5** on LMP-derived stress. ### **Real-World Connections** - Power plant boilers, superheaters → creep + oxidation - Jet engines → creep + thermal fatigue + oxidation - Petrochemical crackers → carburization + creep - Nuclear reactors → irradiation creep ### **Common Mistakes to Avoid** - Mixing °C and K in Arrhenius equations. - Confusing creep rate with strain. - Forgetting to label diagrams (stress axis, time axis, stage regions). - Applying LMP outside its validated temperature range.

topics.title: Mechanisms of Corrosion and Prevention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Mechanisms of Corrosion and Prevention # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Corrosion** is the **physico-chemical interaction between a metal and its environment** that results in **undesirable changes** to the metal's properties and which may often lead to **measurable degradation of the metal, its environment, or the technical system of which they form a part** (Adapted from ISO 8049 / NACE / ASTM G15 standard). In the context of **Failure Analysis and Design (PEMET744)**, corrosion is classified as a **progressive degradation mechanism** driven by the tendency of refined metals to revert to their thermodynamically stable oxide, sulfide, or carbonate forms (their native ore state). The governing principle is the **electrochemical free-energy minimization**. > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):** > *"Corrosion is the gradual destruction of metals by chemical or electrochemical reaction with the surrounding environment, governed by Faraday's laws of electrolysis and quantified by the standard electrode potential series."* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!TIP] > **The "Self-Destructing Battery" Analogy:** > Imagine your car body as a **leaky battery** that you never asked for. Two dissimilar regions on the same metal surface (or two different metals in contact) act as the **positive and negative terminals**. The thin film of moisture on the surface acts as the **electrolyte**. The metal itself acts as the **internal wire**. Current flows, metal dissolves at one terminal (the *anode*), and the other terminal (the *cathode*) remains protected. **Corrosion is simply electricity you don't want — useful chemistry that engineers must suppress.** ### Everyday Examples of Corrosion: - **Rusting of iron** — the classic Fe → Fe₂O₃·H₂O transformation (uniform + pitting). - **Green patina on copper roofs** — formation of basic copper carbonate (Cu₂(OH)₂CO₃). - **White deposits on aluminium utensils** — Al₂O₃ / Al(OH)₃ formation. - **Battery corrosion in flashlights** — galvanic couple between brass terminals and zinc casing in presence of electrolyte leakage. --- ## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics & Physical Constants | Parameter | Symbol | Value | |---|---|---| | Faraday Constant | **F** | **96,485 C/mol** (≈ 96,500 C/mol) | | Gas Constant | **R** | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | | Standard Electrode Potential of Hydrogen | **E°(H⁺/H₂)** | **0.000 V (SHE reference)** | | Density of Iron | ρ_Fe | **7.85 g/cm³** | | Density of Aluminium | ρ_Al | **2.70 g/cm³** | | Density of Copper | ρ_Cu | **8.96 g/cm³** | | Density of Zinc | ρ_Zn | **7.13 g/cm³** | --- ## 1.4 Why Corrosion Matters in Engineering Design (KTU 2024 Perspective) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cost-of-Corrosion Statistic (Frequently cited in KTU viva voce):** > Globally, the **direct annual cost of corrosion** is estimated at **3–4 % of a nation's GDP** (≈ **US $2.5 trillion/year**, NACE IMPACT study). Of this, **15–35 % is preventable** through the application of existing corrosion-control knowledge — making it a **direct KTU Module-4 design-responsibility topic**. > [!NOTE] > **Board Exam Hook:** A KTU 2024 question frequently asks — *"Why is corrosion considered a design failure rather than a material defect?"* The correct answer highlights that the *environment is part of the design boundary*. Selecting the right alloy **without** accounting for chloride concentration, galvanic coupling, or stress levels **constitutes a design oversight**. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Electrochemical Mechanism of Corrosion (Foundation Concept) Metallic corrosion, except in very high-temperature dry oxidation regimes (≥ 700 °C), is **predominantly electrochemical**. It requires **four essential constituents**: | # | Component | Function | Engineering Analogy | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Anode** | Site of metal dissolution (oxidation) | "Source terminal" | | 2 | **Cathode** | Site of reduction reaction | "Sink terminal" | | 3 | **Electrolyte** | Ionic conduction medium | "Battery acid" | | 4 | **Metallic Path** | Electronic conduction back to anode | "External wire" | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Four-Part Test:** Remove **any one** of these four elements and corrosion **stops completely**. This principle underlies **all prevention strategies** taught in Module 4. --- ## 2.2 The Half-Cell Reactions (Step-by-Step Logic) ### Step 1 — Anodic Reaction (Oxidation, Loss of Electrons): $$\text{M} \longrightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + n e^-$$ For iron: $$\text{Fe} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2 e^-$$ **Why it happens:** Metal atoms on the surface have higher free energy in their refined metallic state than in their oxidized (ionic) state. The system spontaneously tends to lower its Gibbs free energy (ΔG < 0), driving the dissolution. ### Step 2 — Cathodic Reactions (Reduction, Gain of Electrons): The cathodic reaction depends on the **pH and dissolved oxygen** of the electrolyte: **(a) In aerated neutral / alkaline solution (most common atmospheric case):** $$\text{O}_2 + 2 \text{H}_2\text{O} + 4 e^- \longrightarrow 4 \text{OH}^-$$ **(b) In deaerated acidic solution:** $$2 \text{H}^+ + 2 e^- \longrightarrow \text{H}_2 \uparrow$$ **(c) In aerated acidic solution:** $$\text{O}_2 + 4 \text{H}^+ + 4 e^- \longrightarrow 2 \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **(d) In presence of metal ions (e.g., Cu deposition):** $$\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2 e^- \longrightarrow \text{Cu}$$ ### Step 3 — Overall Cell Reaction (Fe in aerated neutral water): $$\begin{aligned} \text{Anode:} \quad & 2 \text{Fe} \longrightarrow 2 \text{Fe}^{2+} + 4 e^- \\ \text{Cathode:} \quad & \text{O}_2 + 2 \text{H}_2\text{O} + 4 e^- \longrightarrow 4 \text{OH}^- \\ \text{Overall:} \quad & 2 \text{Fe} + \text{O}_2 + 2 \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2 \text{Fe}^{2+} + 4 \text{OH}^- \end{aligned}$$ The ferrous hydroxide further oxidizes and hydrates to form rust: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2 \text{OH}^- &\longrightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_2 \\ 4 \text{Fe(OH)}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2 \text{H}_2\text{O} &\longrightarrow 4 \text{Fe(OH)}_3 \\ \text{Fe(OH)}_3 &\longrightarrow \text{FeO(OH)} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(final rust)} \end{aligned}$$ > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic — "AN OX, RED CAT":** > **AN**ode = **OX**idation (lose electrons) > **RED**uction at **CAT**hode (gain electrons) --- ## 2.3 Mechanism of Hydrogen Evolution vs. Oxygen Absorption (Type Determination) | Property | Hydrogen Evolution Type | Oxygen Absorption Type | |---|---|---| | Electrolyte | Acidic, deaerated | Neutral / alkaline, aerated | | Cathodic Reaction | 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂ | O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻ | | Common Application | Pickling tanks, underground pipes | Atmospheric, marine | | Controlling Variable | Hydrogen overpotential | Oxygen diffusion rate | | Typical Metals | Fe, Zn, Mg | Cu alloys, stainless steels | --- ## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat-Sheet) | # | Formula | Description | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | **Faraday's Law** — Mass of metal corroded | W (g), I (A), t (s), M (g/mol), F = **96,485 C/mol** | | 2 | $\text{CR}_{mpy} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion rate in **mils per year** | W (mg), D (g/cm³), A (in²), t (hr) | | 3 | $\text{CR}_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion rate in **mm per year** | W (mg), D (g/cm³), A (cm²), t (hr) | | 4 | $\text{PBR} = \dfrac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal}}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{oxide}} \cdot \rho_{\text{metal}}}{n \cdot M_{\text{metal}} \cdot \rho_{\text{oxide}}}$ | **Pilling-Bedworth Ratio** for oxide film protectiveness | Dimensionless | | 5 | $\Delta G = -n \cdot F \cdot E$ | Gibbs free energy of corrosion cell | J/mol | | 6 | $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | Cell EMF (driving force for corrosion) | Volts | | 7 | $i_{\text{corr}} = \dfrac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \, R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | **Stern-Geary equation** — Corrosion current density from polarization resistance | A/cm² | --- ## 2.5 Pilling-Bedworth Ratio Interpretation (Critical KTU Concept) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** determines whether the oxide film formed on a metal is **protective or non-protective** — a recurring 7-mark question in KTU ESE. | PBR Value | Behaviour | Examples | |---|---|---| | PBR < 1 | **Non-protective**, porous oxide, no coverage | Alkali metals (Na, K, Mg) | | 1 ≤ PBR ≤ 2 | **Protective**, adherent, self-healing | **Al (1.28), Cr (1.99), Ti (1.95), stainless steel** | | PBR > 2 | **Cracking, spalling**, excessive volume | **Iron (2.15), Ni (1.65–2.4 region)** | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pipeline design:** Faraday's law predicts the **mass loss** of a buried pipeline given measured current, allowing scheduled replacement. - **Marine structural design:** PBR tells engineers **why** aluminium ships do not catastrophically corrode despite long seawater exposure (Al₂O₃ layer is protective). - **Heat-exchanger design:** Cathodic protection sizing depends on the **total surface area** to be protected, calculated via the **current requirement (mA/m²)**. - **Automotive body design:** Galvanic series dictates fastener selection (avoid direct Cu–Al contact → use stainless or nylon isolators). # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation of Faraday's Law for Corrosion (Foundational) ### Statement: > *"The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed."* ### Step 1 — Charge-Number Relationship: Consider a metal M undergoing valency-n dissolution. The reaction is: $$\text{M}^{n+} + n e^- \longrightarrow \text{M}$$ For every mole of metal deposited/dissolved, **n moles of electrons** must pass. Since 1 mole of electrons = **1 Faraday (F = 96,485 C)**: $$\text{Charge required per mole of metal} = n \cdot F \quad \text{(Coulombs)}$$ ### Step 2 — Mass Relationship: If **m moles** of metal react, total charge passed: $$Q = I \cdot t = m \cdot n \cdot F$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute moles: $$m = \dfrac{\text{Mass } W}{\text{Molar mass } M}$$ ### Step 4 — Final Faraday Expression: $$W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ > This is the **master equation** for all KTU corrosion rate problems. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Corrosion Rate in mpy (Board Exam Style) **Problem (KTU 2023 style):** > *A carbon-steel coupon of density 7.85 g/cm³ and exposed surface area 12 in² was immersed in seawater for 240 hours. The mass loss was found to be 480 mg. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mpy** and **mm/yr**.* ### Solution: **Given:** - W = 480 mg - D = 7.85 g/cm³ - A = 12 in² - t = 240 hr **Step 1 — Apply the mpy formula:** $$\text{CR}_{mpy} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** $$\text{CR}_{mpy} = \dfrac{534 \times 480}{7.85 \times 12 \times 240}$$ **Step 3 — Numerator:** $$534 \times 480 = 256{,}320$$ **Step 4 — Denominator:** $$7.85 \times 12 \times 240 = 22{,}608$$ **Step 5 — Final calculation:** $$\text{CR}_{mpy} = \dfrac{256{,}320}{22{,}608} \approx 11.34 \, \text{mpy}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to mm/yr:** $$\text{CR}_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{87.6 \times 480}{7.85 \times (12 \times 6.4516) \times 240}$$ > *(Conversion: 1 in² = 6.4516 cm², so A = 12 × 6.4516 = 77.42 cm²)* $$\text{CR}_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{87.6 \times 480}{7.85 \times 77.42 \times 240}$$ **Step 7 — Numerator:** $$87.6 \times 480 = 42{,}048$$ **Step 8 — Denominator:** $$7.85 \times 77.42 \times 240 = 145{,}859$$ **Step 9 — Final calculation:** $$\text{CR}_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{42{,}048}{145{,}859} \approx 0.288 \, \text{mm/yr}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation (KTU board expects this):** > As per NACE RP-0775 classification, **0.288 mm/yr falls in the "Moderate" category (0.2–0.5 mm/yr)** — requiring scheduled inspection within 5 years. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Pilling-Bedworth Ratio for Iron **Problem:** > *Calculate the Pilling-Bedworth ratio for iron. Given:* > - *Molar mass of Fe = 55.85 g/mol* > - *Density of Fe = 7.85 g/cm³* > - *Oxide formed = Fe₂O₃ (rust)* > - *Molar mass of Fe₂O₃ = 159.69 g/mol* > - *Density of Fe₂O₃ = 5.24 g/cm³* ### Solution: **Step 1 — Apply the PBR formula:** $$\text{PBR} = \dfrac{M_{\text{oxide}} \cdot \rho_{\text{metal}}}{n \cdot M_{\text{metal}} \cdot \rho_{\text{oxide}}}$$ **Step 2 — Identify n:** In Fe₂O₃, two Fe atoms each lose 3 electrons → **n = 6**. **Step 3 — Substitute values:** $$\text{PBR} = \dfrac{159.69 \times 7.85}{6 \times 55.85 \times 5.24}$$ **Step 4 — Numerator:** $$159.69 \times 7.85 = 1253.57$$ **Step 5 — Denominator:** $$6 \times 55.85 \times 5.24 = 1755.92$$ **Step 6 — Final calculation:** $$\text{PBR} = \dfrac{1253.57}{1755.92} \approx 0.71$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wait — Hold On!** This computed PBR for Fe₂O₃/Fe is **< 1**, suggesting a non-protective film. **This is exactly why iron rusts continuously and non-protectively!** In reality, the hydrated oxy-hydroxide FeO(OH) which actually forms rust is even more porous and less protective, confirming iron's poor corrosion resistance in atmospheric environments. > [!TIP] > **Compare with Aluminium:** Al₂O₃ / Al gives PBR ≈ 1.28 → protective → aluminium resists atmospheric corrosion **despite** being highly reactive thermodynamically. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Cathodic Protection Sizing **Problem:** > *A buried steel pipeline of length 5 km and diameter 0.4 m is to be protected by an impressed-current cathodic system. The required protection current density is 10 mA/m². Calculate the **total current required** and the **number of Mg anodes** (each rated 0.5 A) needed if sacrificial anodes were used instead.* ### Solution: **Step 1 — Surface area calculation:** $$A = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \times 0.4 \times 5000 = 6283.2 \, \text{m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Total current (impressed):** $$I = i \cdot A = 10 \, \text{mA/m}^2 \times 6283.2 \, \text{m}^2 = 62{,}832 \, \text{mA} \approx 62.83 \, \text{A}$$ **Step 3 — Number of Mg sacrificial anodes:** $$N = \dfrac{I}{I_{\text{anode}}} = \dfrac{62.83}{0.5} \approx 126 \, \text{anodes}$$ **Step 4 — Spacing:** $$\text{Spacing} = \dfrac{L}{N} = \dfrac{5000}{126} \approx 39.7 \, \text{m}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Board Tip:** Always state the **applicable code** (e.g., **NACE SP-0169** for buried pipelines, **ISO 15589-2** for offshore) when presenting cathodic protection designs. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mechanism Selection Flowchart Logic | Environmental Stimulus | Predominant Mechanism | Design Mitigation | |---|---|---| | Uniform moisture + dissolved O₂ | **Uniform (General) Corrosion** | Coatings, inhibitors | | Two dissimilar metals + electrolyte | **Galvanic Corrosion** | Isolation, similar metals | | Stagnant Cl⁻ + scratches | **Pitting Corrosion** | Smooth finish, Mo-alloyed SS | | Crevices under gaskets / bolts | **Crevice Corrosion** | Seal joints, drain water | | Grain boundary precipitates | **Intergranular Corrosion** | Low-carbon / stabilised SS | | Tensile stress + corrosive ion | **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** | Lower stress, alternate alloy | | High-velocity fluid + particles | **Erosion Corrosion** | Reduce velocity, harder alloy | | Vibrating contact surfaces | **Fretting Corrosion** | Lubrication, dampers | | Cathodic overprotection | **Hydrogen Embrittlement** | Limit over-voltage, bake-out | # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Electrochemical Corrosion Cell (Galvanic Model) ```mermaid flowchart LR anode1["ANODE
