Potentiostat"] ap4["Anode Passivated Structure"] cp1 --> cp2 cp2 --> cp3 cp3 --> cp4 ap1 --> ap2 ap2 --> ap3 ap3 --> ap4 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Pilling-Bedworth ratio. Why is it important in corrosion studies?** #### Model Answer: The **Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** is the ratio of the volume of the metal oxide formed to the volume of the metal consumed during oxidation: $$\text{PBR} = \dfrac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal}}}$$ **Importance:** It determines whether the oxide film is **protective** (PBR between 1 and 2, e.g., Al₂O₃ on Al with PBR ≈ 1.28) or **non-protective** (PBR < 1, e.g., MgO on Mg; or PBR > 2, e.g., Fe₂O₃ on Fe). A protective oxide **self-heals cracks** and isolates the metal from the environment, drastically reducing corrosion rate. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between galvanic corrosion and concentration cell corrosion.** #### Model Answer: | Parameter | Galvanic Corrosion | Concentration Cell Corrosion | |---|---|---| | Cause | Two **different metals** in electrical contact | **Same metal** exposed to electrolyte of **different concentration** (oxygen or ion) | | EMF driver | Difference in **standard electrode potential** | Difference in **local concentration** of dissolved O₂ or metal ions | | Example | Cu–Steel bolt in seawater | Water-line corrosion, under-deposit attack | | Prevention | Isolation, similar metals | Eliminate concentration gradients, better drainage | **(3 marks)** --- ## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question B1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified ESE Pattern]` **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **electrochemical mechanism of corrosion** of iron in **aerated neutral aqueous environment**. Write all relevant half-cell and overall reactions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A zinc-coated (galvanized) mild-steel pipe carrying cold water develops **severe localized white deposits and pitting within 6 months**. Identify the likely corrosion mechanism, explain its **electrochemistry**, and recommend **two specific prevention strategies**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution: **(a) Electrochemical Mechanism of Iron Corrosion:** The corrosion of iron in aerated neutral water proceeds through **four simultaneous electrochemical stages**: **Setup:** Micro-regions on the iron surface act as local anodes and cathodes, connected through the metal bulk and covered by a thin moisture film (electrolyte). **Anodic Reaction (at anodic sites):** $$\text{Fe} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2 e^- \quad \text{(E° = −0.44 V vs SHE)}$$ **Cathodic Reaction (at cathodic sites, oxygen reduction):** $$\text{O}_2 + 2 \text{H}_2\text{O} + 4 e^- \longrightarrow 4 \text{OH}^- \quad \text{(E° = +0.40 V vs SHE)}$$ **Overall Cell Reaction:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Multiply anode by 2:} \quad & 2 \text{Fe} \longrightarrow 2 \text{Fe}^{2+} + 4 e^- \\ \text{Cathode:} \quad & \text{O}_2 + 2 \text{H}_2\text{O} + 4 e^- \longrightarrow 4 \text{OH}^- \\ \text{Overall:} \quad & 2 \text{Fe} + \text{O}_2 + 2 \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2 \text{Fe(OH)}_2 \end{aligned}$$ **Secondary Oxidation to Rust:** $$\begin{aligned} 4 \text{Fe(OH)}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2 \text{H}_2\text{O} &\longrightarrow 4 \text{Fe(OH)}_3 \\ \text{Fe(OH)}_3 &\longrightarrow \text{FeO(OH)} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(final rust, FeO(OH))} \end{aligned}$$ **Sketch (to be drawn in exam):** - Iron surface with anodic pit and cathodic area - Electrons flowing through metal from anode to cathode - OH⁻ and Fe²⁺ migrating through moisture film - Rust deposit forming above anode **Key Numbers:** Driving EMF = 0.40 − (−0.44) = **0.84 V**; Gibbs free energy ΔG = −nFE = **−324 kJ/mol** (highly spontaneous). **(7 marks — full marks)** --- **(b) Diagnosis — Zinc-Coated Pipe Pitting in Cold Water:** **Likely Mechanism: Galvanic corrosion with reverse polarity reversal / differential aeration cell, leading to localized pitting.** **Electrochemistry:** 1. Initially, Zn (E° = −0.76 V) sacrifices to protect Fe (E° = −0.44 V) → standard galvanic protection. 2. In **stagnant cold water containing dissolved CO₂ and chlorides**, the Zn coating dissolves heterogeneously, creating **exposed Fe anodes** adjacent to **still-coated Zn cathodes**. 3. This forms a **small-anode / large-cathode geometry** — the **most dangerous galvanic configuration** — leading to intense localized pitting on Fe. 4. Differential aeration under white deposits (ZnO/Zn(OH)₂ + CaCO₃ scale) creates an **oxygen concentration cell** accelerating pitting. **Prevention Strategies:** **Strategy 1 — Material Upgrade:** Replace galvanized steel with **Copper-Nickel (90-10 Cu-Ni) per ASTM B466**, or **use epoxy-lined carbon steel per AWWA C210**. Cu-Ni forms a protective Cu₂O film in seawater and resists pitting even at stagnant flow conditions. **Strategy 2 — Cathodic Protection:** Install **sacrificial magnesium anodes** at pipe entry points. Mg (E° = −2.37 V) is sufficiently electronegative to protect both Zn and Fe simultaneously, with periodic anode replacement as maintenance. **Additional Strategy (Bonus Mark):** Maintain water velocity **> 1 m/s** to prevent deposit formation and ensure uniform aeration. **(7 marks — full marks)** --- ### **Question B2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]` **(a)** Derive the **Faraday's law expression for corrosion rate**. A cylindrical steel specimen of diameter 2 cm and length 8 cm is immersed in 5% NaCl solution for 30 days. The mass loss recorded is 1.8 g. Calculate the **corrosion rate in mpy and mm/yr**. Density of steel = **7.85 g/cm³**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the principle and design of **impressed current cathodic protection (ICCP) system** for a buried pipeline. Compare it with **sacrificial anode cathodic protection**. Mention the relevant **NACE / ISO standards**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution: **(a) Faraday's Law Derivation and Corrosion Rate Calculation:** **Derivation (recap):** For an electrochemical reaction: $$\text{M}^{n+} + n e^- \longrightarrow \text{M}$$ Charge required to liberate one mole of M = nF coulombs. For m moles, charge Q = m·n·F. Since m = W/M (mass / molar mass): $$W = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ **Given:** - Specimen: cylinder, d = 2 cm, L = 8 cm - W = 1.8 g = 1800 mg - t = 30 days = 30 × 24 = 720 hours - ρ = 7.85 g/cm³ **Step 1 — Surface area:** $$\begin{aligned} A_{\text{cm}^2} &= \pi d L + 2 \times \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \\ &= \pi \times 2 \times 8 + 2 \times \dfrac{\pi \times 4}{4} \\ &= 50.27 + 6.28 = 56.55 \, \text{cm}^2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Convert to in²:** $$A_{\text{in}^2} = \dfrac{56.55}{6.4516} \approx 8.77 \, \text{in}^2$$ **Step 3 — Apply mpy formula:** $$\text{CR}_{mpy} = \dfrac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times t} = \dfrac{534 \times 1800}{7.85 \times 8.77 \times 720}$$ **Step 4 — Numerator:** $$534 \times 1800 = 961{,}200$$ **Step 5 — Denominator:** $$7.85 \times 8.77 \times 720 = 49{,}564$$ **Step 6 — Calculate:** $$\text{CR}_{mpy} = \dfrac{961{,}200}{49{,}564} \approx 19.39 \, \text{mpy}$$ **Step 7 — Apply mm/yr formula (A in cm²):** $$\text{CR}_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{87.6 \times 1800}{7.85 \times 56.55 \times 720}$$ **Step 8 — Numerator:** $$87.6 \times 1800 = 157{,}680$$ **Step 9 — Denominator:** $$7.85 \times 56.55 \times 720 = 319{,}620$$ **Step 10 — Calculate:** $$\text{CR}_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{157{,}680}{319{,}620} \approx 0.493 \, \text{mm/yr}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** 0.493 mm/yr is **"High" severity** per NACE RP-0775. The component requires immediate replacement or strong corrosion control. **(7 marks — full marks)** --- **(b) Cathodic Protection Systems — ICCP vs. Sacrificial:** **ICCP Principle:** A DC source is connected with its **positive terminal to an inert anode** (e.g., platinized titanium, graphite, high-silicon iron) buried in the soil, and its **negative terminal to the protected pipeline**. Current flows from the anode through the soil to the pipe, making the entire pipe a cathode. **Design Steps:** 1. **Determine surface area** of pipeline requiring protection. 2. **Select current density** from coating type (e.g., 10–50 mA/m² for bare steel, 0.1–1 mA/m² for well-coated). 3. **Calculate total current** I = i × A. 4. **Select anode** with adequate current capacity. 5. **Install rectifier** with capacity 1.5× I (safety factor). 6. **Place reference electrodes** (Cu/CuSO₄) for monitoring. 7. **Verify** pipe-to-soil potential = −850 mV (vs Cu/CuSO₄). **Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP) Principle:** A more active metal (Zn, Mg, Al alloy) is electrically connected to the protected structure. The active metal preferentially corrodes ("sacrifices"), supplying protective current naturally without external power. **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | ICCP | SACP (Galvanic) | |---|---|---| | Driving Force | External rectifier | Galvanic potential difference | | Current | Adjustable, high (up to hundreds of A) | Limited by anode potential (~0.5 A per anode) | | Anode Material | Inert (Pt, graphite, Fe–Si) | Active (Zn, Mg, Al) | | Cost | High initial, low maintenance | Low initial, periodic anode replacement | | Soil Resistivity | Suitable for **any** resistivity | Only for **low** resistivity (< 5000 Ω·cm) | | Monitoring | Continuous with rectifier | Periodic potential survey | | Standards | **NACE SP-0169, ISO 15589-2** | **NACE SP-0196, EN 12954** | | Application | Long pipelines, offshore platforms | Small installations, ship hulls | **(7 marks — full marks)** --- ## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU PEMET744 Exam:** 1. **Forgetting the end-cap area** when calculating surface area of cylindrical specimens → loses 1 mark. Always include both curved surface **and** two end faces. 2. **Unit mismatch** in corrosion rate formulas (using A in cm² with mpy formula, or A in in² with mm/yr formula) → loses up to 2 marks. **Memorize the unit pairs** clearly. 3. **Confusing PBR > 2 with protective behaviour.** PBR > 2 (e.g., iron) is actually **non-protective** — the film spalls off. Only **1 ≤ PBR ≤ 2** is protective. 4. **Writing "cathode corrodes"** in galvanic corrosion — examiners deduct full marks. The anode corrodes; the cathode is **protected**. 5. **Skipping the reference electrode specification** in cathodic protection answers — always state "vs Cu/CuSO₄" or "vs Ag/AgCl" with the **voltage value**. 6. **Wrong conversion:** 1 mil = 0.001 inch = **0.0254 mm**. Use this when converting between mpy and mm/yr (sanity check: 1 mpy ≈ 0.0254 mm/yr). 7. **Not stating Faraday's constant value (96,485 C/mol)** explicitly in derivation → loses 1 mark. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Quick-Fire Revision Points — Memorize Before Exam:** - ✅ Corrosion = **electrochemical + spontaneous** (ΔG < 0) degradation of metal. - ✅ **Four essentials:** Anode + Cathode + Electrolyte + Metallic path. Remove any one → corrosion stops. - ✅ **Anode OXidation** (loss of electrons); **Cathode REDuction** (gain of electrons). Mnemonic: **AN OX, RED CAT**. - ✅ **Faraday's Law:** $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ with F = **96,485 C/mol**. - ✅ **mpy formula uses A in in² and D in g/cm³; mm/yr uses A in cm².** Remember the constants **534** and **87.6**. - ✅ **PBR < 1** → porous, non-protective; **1 ≤ PBR ≤ 2** → protective; **> 2** → spalling. - ✅ **Galvanic series** (not the same as EMF series) governs galvanic corrosion in real environments. - ✅ **Pitting** = small anode / large cathode geometry = **most dangerous**. - ✅ **SCC** requires **three** factors simultaneously: susceptible alloy + specific ion + tensile stress. - ✅ **Cathodic protection criterion** = **−850 mV** vs Cu/CuSO₄ (NACE SP-0169). - ✅ **Sacrificial anodes** for low-resistivity soils; **ICCP** for any resistivity and long structures. - ✅ **Anodic protection** works only on **passivating metals** (stainless steel, Ti) in **strong oxidizing acids** (H₂SO₄). - ✅ **Cost-of-corrosion** ≈ **3–4 % of GDP globally** — designer responsibility. - ✅ **NACE / ISO standards to memorize:** NACE SP-0169, NACE RP-0775, ISO 12944, ISO 15589-2, ASTM G15. - ✅ **Hot-dip galvanizing** protects steel in two ways: (1) barrier coating + (2) sacrificial anode. - ✅ **Stainless steel** is protected by **Cr₂O₃ passive film** — damaged locally → pitting in Cl⁻ environments. - ✅ **Hydrogen embrittlement** is caused by **excessive CP** overpotential or **sour (H₂S) environments**. - ✅ **Erosion corrosion** is mitigated by: lower velocity, smoother bends, harder alloy (Stellite, Inconel). - ✅ **Fretting corrosion** is prevented by: lubrication, gaskets, increased surface hardness.

topics.title: Integration of Fracture Mechanics Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Integration of Fracture Mechanics Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Integration of Fracture Mechanics Principles

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Integration of Fracture Mechanics Principles** refers to the systematic synthesis of Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM), Elastic-Plastic Fracture Mechanics (EPFM), fatigue crack growth theory, and stress-based/strain-based failure criteria into a unified engineering design and assessment framework. This holistic methodology allows engineers to predict component life, prevent catastrophic failure, and ensure structural integrity under complex, real-world loading conditions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic is positioned as the capstone of Module 4, requiring students to demonstrate **Course Outcome CO4** (Apply fracture mechanics tools to evaluate structural integrity and life prediction).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Integration is NOT about a single formula — it is about combining **stress analysis**, **material toughness**, **flaw characterization**, and **service loading** to make engineering decisions about inspection intervals, fitness-for-service, and design modifications.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a doctor diagnosing a patient. A single test (blood pressure, X-ray, MRI) gives limited information. But when you **integrate** multiple diagnostic tools, you get a complete picture of the patient's health. Similarly:

- **Stress analysis** = The patient's vital signs (current state)
- **Fracture toughness (K_IC, J_IC)** = The patient's immune system (resistance)
- **Fatigue crack growth law (Paris Law)** = Disease progression rate
- **Limit state / Fitness-for-Service** = Treatment decision

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application:** The aerospace industry integrates fracture mechanics to determine inspection intervals for aircraft wings. The pressure vessel industry uses it to qualify welds. The offshore oil & gas sector applies it to assess platforms in harsh marine environments.

### Key Integration Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Stress Intensity Factor | $K$ | MPa√m |
| Fracture Toughness | $K_{IC}$, $J_{IC}$ | MPa√m or kJ/m² |
| Crack Tip Opening Displacement | $\delta$ | mm |
| Fatigue Crack Growth Rate | $da/dN$ | m/cycle |
| Reference Stress | $\sigma_{ref}$ | MPa |
| Failure Assessment Diagram | FAD | Dimensionless |

The **Failure Assessment Diagram (FAD)** is the central integrating tool of modern fitness-for-service codes like **BS 7910** and **API 579-1/ASME FFS-1**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Integration

The integration of fracture mechanics principles rests on three fundamental pillars that must be addressed simultaneously for any integrity assessment:

### Pillar 1 — Driving Force (Demand Side)
This represents the **severity of the loading condition** applied to a cracked body. It quantifies how much the applied stress "demands" from the structure.

- **Stress Intensity Factor (SIF):** $K = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$
- **J-Integral:** $J = J_{elastic} + J_{plastic}$
- **Reference Stress:** $\sigma_{ref} = \dfrac{P}{P_L}$

### Pillar 2 — Material Resistance (Capacity Side)
This represents the **inherent ability of the material** to resist crack propagation. It is determined from standardized laboratory tests.

- **Fracture Toughness:** $K_{IC}$ (LEFM), $J_{IC}$ (EPFM)
- **Crack Tip Opening Displacement:** $\delta_{c}$
- **Fatigue Threshold:** $\Delta K_{th}$

### Pillar 3 — Flaw Characterization
Real components contain real defects. Integration requires accurate quantification of:

- Crack size: $a$, $2c$ (through-thickness or surface)
- Crack shape factor: $Y$, $Q$ (geometry correction)
- Crack location: surface, embedded, corner

## 2.2 Failure Assessment Diagram (FAD) — The Integration Hub

The FAD combines stress-based criteria (yield) and fracture-based criteria (fracture toughness) into a single 2-D plot. The assessment point lies at coordinates $(K_r, L_r)$ where:

$$
K_r = \dfrac{K_I}{K_{material}} \quad \text{(Fracture Ratio)}
$$

$$
L_r = \dfrac{\sigma_{ref}}{\sigma_y} \quad \text{(Load Ratio)}
$$

The assessment line is given by the **BS 7910 / API 579** equation:

$$
K_r = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2}{3} L_r^2}} \cdot \left[ 0.3 + 0.7 \exp\left(-\mu L_r^6\right) \right]
$$

where $\mu$ is a material-dependent plastic collapse constraint factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Rule:**
> - If the assessment point lies **INSIDE** the FAD envelope → **ACCEPTABLE** (structure is safe)
> - If the assessment point lies **OUTSIDE** the FAD envelope → **NOT ACCEPTABLE** (failure predicted)

## 2.3 Integration with Fatigue (Paris Law)

For components subjected to cyclic loading, fracture mechanics must integrate with fatigue theory. The **Paris-Erdogan Law** governs stable crack growth:

$$
\dfrac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m
$$

where:
- $a$ = crack length (m)
- $N$ = number of cycles
- $\Delta K = Y\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ = stress intensity factor range
- $C$, $m$ = material constants (typically $m = 2$ to $7$ for metals)

The **integration of fatigue life** from initial flaw $a_i$ to critical flaw $a_c$ is:

$$
N_f = \int_{a_i}^{a_c} \dfrac{da}{C(Y\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi a})^m}
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ | Mode-I Stress Intensity Factor | LEFM crack analysis |
| 2 | $K_r = K_I / K_{IC}$ | Fracture Ratio (FAD x-axis) | FAD plotting |
| 3 | $L_r = \sigma_{ref} / \sigma_y$ | Load Ratio (FAD y-axis) | FAD plotting |
| 4 | $K_r = f(L_r)$ | FAD assessment curve | Fitness-for-Service |
| 5 | $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$ | Paris-Erdogan Law | Fatigue crack growth |
| 6 | $N_f = \int da / C(Y\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi a})^m$ | Fatigue Life Integration | Life prediction |
| 7 | $J = K_I^2 / E'$ | J-integral (plane strain $E' = E$) | EPFM analysis |
| 8 | $\Delta K_{th}$ | Fatigue crack growth threshold | Long-life design |
| 9 | $a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sigma}\right)^2$ | Critical crack size | Inspection planning |
| 10 | $K_{ISCC}$ | Stress Corrosion Cracking threshold | Environmental integration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When a question asks for "integration of fracture mechanics principles," always mention the FAD approach combined with at least one other method (fatigue, creep, or stress corrosion). Single-method answers lose marks.

### Real-World Utility

- **Aerospace:** Damage tolerance design per **FAA AC 25.571** requires integration of fatigue, fracture, and inspection data.
- **Nuclear Power Plant:** ASME Section XI uses FAD for in-service flaw evaluation.
- **Offshore Structures:** API 579 integrates fracture mechanics with corrosion fatigue and weld residual stresses.
- **Pipeline Engineering:** Integration of fracture mechanics with hydrotest protocols for gas pipelines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fatigue Life Integration via Paris Law

**Problem Context:** A surface crack in a steel plate grows under cyclic loading. Given initial crack size $a_i$, critical crack size $a_c$, stress range $\Delta\sigma$, geometry factor $Y$, and material constants $C$ and $m$, derive the total number of cycles to failure $N_f$.

### Step 1 — State the Paris Law

The crack growth per cycle is governed by the empirical Paris-Erdogan relation:

$$
\dfrac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m
$$

where the stress intensity factor range for a surface crack is:

$$
\Delta K = Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $\Delta K$ into the Paris Law

$$
\dfrac{da}{dN} = C\left(Y\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}\right)^m
$$

Rearranging to isolate $dN$:

$$
dN = \dfrac{da}{C(Y\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi a})^m}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Limits of Integration

The fatigue life $N_f$ is the number of cycles required for the crack to grow from $a_i$ to $a_c$:

$$
N_f = \int_{a_i}^{a_c} \dfrac{da}{C(Y\Delta\sigma)^m (\pi a)^{m/2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the Integral

Factor out constants from the integrand:

$$
N_f = \dfrac{1}{C(Y\Delta\sigma)^m \pi^{m/2}} \int_{a_i}^{a_c} a^{-m/2}\, da
$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate the Integral

For $m \neq 2$:

$$
\int_{a_i}^{a_c} a^{-m/2}\, da = \left[\dfrac{a^{1 - m/2}}{1 - m/2}\right]_{a_i}^{a_c}
$$

Therefore:

$$
N_f = \dfrac{1}{C(Y\Delta\sigma)^m \pi^{m/2}(1 - m/2)} \left[a_c^{1 - m/2} - a_i^{1 - m/2}\right]
$$

### Step 6 — Final Closed-Form Expression

$$
\boxed{N_f = \dfrac{a_i^{1 - m/2} - a_c^{1 - m/2}}{C(Y\Delta\sigma)^m \pi^{m/2}\left(\dfrac{m}{2} - 1\right)}}
$$

This result is fundamental for **damage tolerance life prediction** in aerospace structures.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Crack Size from Fracture Toughness

**Problem Context:** Given an applied stress $\sigma$ and fracture toughness $K_{IC}$, determine the maximum allowable crack size $a_c$ before catastrophic brittle fracture occurs.

### Step 1 — Fracture Criterion

Failure occurs when the applied stress intensity factor reaches the material's fracture toughness:

$$
K_I = K_{IC}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the SIF Expression

$$
Y\sigma_c\sqrt{\pi a_c} = K_{IC}
$$

### Step 3 — Isolate $\sigma_c$ (Stress at Fracture)

$$
\sigma_c = \dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sqrt{\pi a_c}}
$$

This is the classical **Griffith-Irwin** failure stress for a cracked plate.

### Step 4 — Solve for $a_c$ (Critical Crack Size)

Squaring both sides:

$$
\sigma_c^2 = \dfrac{K_{IC}^2}{Y^2 \pi a_c}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sigma_c}\right)^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula is the basis for **inspection interval planning**. If non-destructive evaluation (NDE) can detect cracks above size $a_{det}$, then $a_{det} \leq a_c / n$ (where $n$ is the safety factor, typically $n = 2$ to $4$).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- Plate width $W = 0.5$ m, thickness $B = 0.02$ m
- Applied cyclic stress range $\Delta\sigma = 150$ MPa
- Geometry factor $Y = 1.12$ (center crack)
- Material constants: $C = 1.5 \times 10^{-11}$, $m = 3$
- Initial crack $a_i = 1.0$ mm $= 0.001$ m
- Critical crack $a_c = 10$ mm $= 0.010$ m

**Find:** Number of cycles to failure $N_f$.

### Solution Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Calculate the constant $C(Y\Delta\sigma)^m \pi^{m/2}$:

$$
C(Y\Delta\sigma)^m \pi^{m/2} = (1.5 \times 10^{-11})(1.12 \times 150)^3 \pi^{3/2}
$$

$$
= (1.5 \times 10^{-11})(168)^3 (5.568)
$$

$$
= (1.5 \times 10^{-11})(4.74 \times 10^6)(5.568)
$$

$$
= 3.96 \times 10^{-4} \text{ (per cycle, normalized)}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the exponent factor $(1 - m/2)$:

$$
1 - \dfrac{m}{2} = 1 - \dfrac{3}{2} = -\dfrac{1}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate $a_i^{-1/2}$ and $a_c^{-1/2}$:

$$
a_i^{-1/2} = (0.001)^{-1/2} = 31.62 \text{ m}^{-1/2}
$$

$$
a_c^{-1/2} = (0.010)^{-1/2} = 10.00 \text{ m}^{-1/2}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the fatigue life formula:

$$
N_f = \dfrac{a_i^{-1/2} - a_c^{-1/2}}{C(Y\Delta\sigma)^m \pi^{m/2}\left(\dfrac{m}{2} - 1\right)}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{31.62 - 10.00}{(3.96 \times 10^{-4})(0.5)}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{21.62}{1.98 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
N_f \approx 1.09 \times 10^5 \text{ cycles}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Halving the initial crack size from 1 mm to 0.5 mm would increase fatigue life by a factor of approximately $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$ times for this $m = 3$ case. This demonstrates why **manufacturing quality and inspection sensitivity** dramatically affect component life.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow

| Condition | Check | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| Brittle fracture | $K_I \geq K_{IC}$? | **Reject** — Increase toughness or reduce stress |
| Ductile collapse | $L_r \geq L_r^{max}$? | **Reject** — Strengthen or reduce load |
| Fatigue | $da/dN > 0$? | Predict life using Paris Law |
| Stress corrosion | $K_I \geq K_{ISCC}$? | **Reject** in corrosive environment |
| Combined FAD check | Point inside envelope? | **Accept** if yes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Fracture Mechanics Integration Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Service Loading Conditions] --> B[Stress Analysis Module]
    A1[Flaw Geometry and Size] --> C[Crack Driving Force]
    B --> C
    D[Material Property Database] --> E[Crack Resistance]
    F[Environmental Factors] --> E
    C --> G[Integration Core FAD]
    E --> G
    G --> H{Assessment Point}
    H -->|Inside Envelope| I[ACCEPTABLE Structure]
    H -->|Outside Envelope| J[UNACCEPTABLE Failure Predicted]
    G --> K[Life Prediction Module]
    K --> L[Inspection Interval]
    K --> M[Remaining Life Estimation]
    I --> N[Design Validation Report]
    J --> O[Design Modification]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Failure Assessment Diagram (FAD) — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph FADEnvelope
        A[Kr axis Fracture Ratio] --> B[FAD Assessment Curve]
        C[Lr axis Load Ratio] --> B
        B --> D[Crack Initiation Zone]
        B --> E[Elastic Plastic Transition Zone]
        B --> F[Plastic Collapse Zone]
    end
    G[Assessment Point Kr Lr] --> H{Inside Envelope?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Safe Structure]
    H -->|No| J[Failure Predicted]
```

## 4.3 Sequential Flow: Fatigue Life Prediction Integration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Flaw ai] --> B[Apply Paris Law]
    B --> C[Integrate da/dN]
    C --> D[Critical Flaw ac]
    D --> E{N reaches Nf?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Component Failure]
    E -->|No| G[Continue Service]
    G --> H[Next Inspection Cycle]
    H --> A
```

## 4.4 Multi-Mode Failure Integration Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cracked Component] --> B[Loading Type Identification]
    B --> C[Static Load]
    B --> D[Cyclic Load]
    B --> E[Constant Load plus Environment]
    C --> F[LEFM or EPFM Check]
    D --> G[Fatigue Crack Growth Analysis]
    E --> H[Stress Corrosion Cracking Check]
    F --> I[FAD Assessment]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Integrated Integrity Decision]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice how all four failure modes (static, fatigue, environmental, plastic collapse) converge into a single **FAD assessment**. This convergence is the essence of "integration" — different physics, unified decision framework.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Failure Assessment Diagram (FAD) and list its two axes. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Failure Assessment Diagram (FAD) is a graphical tool used in fitness-for-service assessment that combines **fracture mechanics** and **limit load (plastic collapse)** criteria into a single 2-D plot.

The two axes are:
1. **Abscissa (x-axis):** $L_r = \dfrac{\sigma_{ref}}{\sigma_y}$ — the load ratio (plastic collapse proximity)
2. **Ordinate (y-axis):** $K_r = \dfrac{K_I}{K_{IC}}$ — the fracture ratio (brittle fracture proximity)

A structure is **acceptable** if the assessment point $(L_r, K_r)$ lies inside the FAD envelope defined by the BS 7910 assessment curve.

---

### Question 2: State the Paris-Erdogan fatigue crack growth law and explain the significance of $C$ and $m$. `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Paris-Erdogan Law is:

$$
\dfrac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m
$$

where:
- $da/dN$ = crack growth rate per cycle (m/cycle)
- $\Delta K$ = stress intensity factor range (MPa√m)
- $C$ = material constant (depends on environment, temperature, microstructure)
- $m$ = material exponent (typically $2$ to $7$ for metals)

**Significance:**
- $C$ represents the **absolute growth rate** at a given $\Delta K$ level.
- $m$ represents the **sensitivity** of crack growth to changes in $\Delta K$. Higher $m$ means steeper growth curves and more dangerous service conditions.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU Examiner Note:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Attempt any **ONE** full question.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Fatigue Life Prediction Using Paris Law `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A center-cracked plate made of steel is subjected to cyclic tension. The following data are provided:
- Stress range: $\Delta\sigma = 200$ MPa
- Geometry factor: $Y = 1.0$
- Initial crack size: $a_i = 2$ mm
- Critical crack size: $a_c = 20$ mm
- Paris constants: $C = 6.9 \times 10^{-12}$, $m = 3$

#### (a) [7 Marks] Derive the expression for total fatigue life $N_f$ using Paris Law.

**Model Solution:**

Starting from Paris Law:

$$
\dfrac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m = C(Y\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi a})^m
$$

Separating variables:

$$
dN = \dfrac{da}{C(Y\Delta\sigma)^m \pi^{m/2} a^{m/2}}
$$

Integrating between $a_i$ and $a_c$:

$$
N_f = \dfrac{1}{C(Y\Delta\sigma)^m \pi^{m/2}} \int_{a_i}^{a_c} a^{-m/2}\, da
$$

For $m \neq 2$:

$$
N_f = \dfrac{a_i^{1 - m/2} - a_c^{1 - m/2}}{C(Y\Delta\sigma)^m \pi^{m/2}\left(\dfrac{m}{2} - 1\right)}
$$

**Marks Distribution:**
- Correct starting equation (1 mark)
- Substitution of $\Delta K$ (1 mark)
- Separation of variables (1 mark)
- Integration limits correctly stated (1 mark)
- Final integrated formula (2 marks)
- Units and physical interpretation (1 mark)

#### (b) [7 Marks] Calculate the numerical value of $N_f$ for the given data.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the prefactor:

$$
(Y\Delta\sigma)^m = (1.0 \times 200)^3 = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ MPa}^3
$$

$$
\pi^{m/2} = \pi^{3/2} = 5.568
$$

$$
\text{Prefactor} = (6.9 \times 10^{-12})(8 \times 10^6)(5.568) = 3.07 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the crack size terms with $m = 3$:

$$
1 - \dfrac{m}{2} = 1 - 1.5 = -0.5
$$

$$
a_i^{-0.5} = (0.002)^{-0.5} = 22.36 \text{ m}^{-0.5}
$$

$$
a_c^{-0.5} = (0.020)^{-0.5} = 7.07 \text{ m}^{-0.5}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the formula:

$$
N_f = \dfrac{22.36 - 7.07}{(3.07 \times 10^{-4})(0.5)} = \dfrac{15.29}{1.535 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_f \approx 9.96 \times 10^4 \text{ cycles}}
$$

**Marks Distribution:**
- Correct prefactor calculation (2 marks)
- Correct evaluation of crack terms (2 marks)
- Final substitution (2 marks)
- Correct numerical answer with units (1 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks): FAD-Based Fitness-for-Service Assessment `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

A pressure vessel contains a longitudinal surface crack of depth $a = 5$ mm and length $2c = 30$ mm. The vessel is subjected to a primary membrane stress of $\sigma = 180$ MPa. Material properties:
- Yield stress: $\sigma_y = 350$ MPa
- Fracture toughness: $K_{IC} = 85$ MPa√m
- Geometry factor for surface crack: $Y = 1.2$

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compute the fracture ratio $K_r$ and load ratio $L_r$ for the given flaw.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the applied stress intensity factor $K_I$:

$$
K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a} = (1.2)(180)\sqrt{\pi \times 0.005}
$$

$$
= 216 \times \sqrt{0.01571} = 216 \times 0.1253 = 27.07 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{m}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the fracture ratio $K_r$:

$$
K_r = \dfrac{K_I}{K_{IC}} = \dfrac{27.07}{85} = 0.318
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the load ratio $L_r$ (assuming primary stress equals reference stress):

$$
L_r = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sigma_y} = \dfrac{180}{350} = 0.514
$$

**Marks Distribution:**
- Correct $K_I$ calculation with units (3 marks)
- Correct $K_r$ value (2 marks)
- Correct $L_r$ value with interpretation (2 marks)

#### (b) [7 Marks] Using the BS 7910 assessment curve, determine whether the structure is acceptable. Justify with a sketch description.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the FAD curve value at $L_r = 0.514$:

For the standard BS 7910 curve (assuming $\mu = 0.5$ for ferritic steel):

$$
K_r^{curve} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2}{3}(0.514)^2}} \cdot \left[ 0.3 + 0.7 \exp\left(-0.5 \times 0.514^6\right) \right]
$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the bracket:

$$
L_r^2 = 0.264, \quad \dfrac{2}{3}L_r^2 = 0.176
$$

$$
1 + 0.176 = 1.176, \quad \sqrt{1.176} = 1.084
$$

$$
L_r^6 \approx 0.018, \quad \exp(-0.5 \times 0.018) = \exp(-0.009) \approx 0.991
$$

$$
0.3 + 0.7(0.991) = 0.994
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $K_r^{curve}$:

$$
K_r^{curve} = \dfrac{0.994}{1.084} \approx 0.917
$$

**Step 4:** Compare:

$$
K_r^{actual} = 0.318 \quad \text{vs.} \quad K_r^{curve} = 0.917
$$

Since $K_r^{actual} < K_r^{curve}$, the assessment point lies **inside** the FAD envelope.

**Conclusion:** The structure is **ACCEPTABLE** under the given loading condition. However, the relatively high $L_r = 0.514$ suggests the structure is operating close to yield. **Recommendation:** Schedule regular inspections at 12-month intervals to monitor crack growth.

**Marks Distribution:**
- Correct FAD curve formula (2 marks)
- Correct numerical evaluation (2 marks)
- Correct comparison and conclusion (2 marks)
- Practical engineering recommendation (1 mark)

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Fracture Mechanics Integration Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the geometry factor Y:** Students often write $K_I = \sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ without $Y$. In KTU exams, this costs **2-3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m in crack size and $\Delta K$ calculations. Always convert to SI units (meters) BEFORE substituting into formulas.
>
> 3. **Incorrect integration limits:** Students sometimes integrate from 0 to $a_c$, which gives the **wrong physical answer**. The lower limit must be the initial detectable flaw size $a_i$, not zero.
>
> 4. **Confusing $K_I$ with $K_{IC}$:** $K_I$ is the **applied** (demand) SIF, while $K_{IC}$ is the **material** (capacity) toughness. The fracture criterion is $K_I \geq K_{IC}$.
>
> 5. **Missing fatigue threshold:** In integration questions, students forget that below $\Delta K_{th}$, no crack growth occurs. This affects low-cycle versus high-cycle fatigue decisions.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning FAD:** Even if a question does not explicitly ask for FAD, mentioning it as an "integrated approach" earns **bonus marks** for demonstrating course-level understanding.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Integration of Fracture Mechanics Principles**
>
> - **Integration = Combination of fracture + fatigue + environment + plastic collapse** in a single decision framework.
> - **FAD coordinates:** $L_r = \sigma_{ref}/\sigma_y$ (x-axis), $K_r = K_I/K_{IC}$ (y-axis).
> - **FAD acceptance criterion:** Assessment point must lie **inside** the BS 7910 / API 579 envelope.
> - **Paris Law:** $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$ governs stable fatigue crack growth (Region II).
> - **Fatigue life formula:** $N_f = \dfrac{a_i^{1-m/2} - a_c^{1-m/2}}{C(Y\Delta\sigma)^m \pi^{m/2}(m/2 - 1)}$ — memorize this!
> - **Critical crack size:** $a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sigma}\right)^2$ — basis for inspection planning.
> - **J-integral conversion:** $J = K_I^2 / E'$ for plane strain, $E' = E/(1 - \nu^2)$ for plane strain.
> - **CTOD (δ):** Used when plastic deformation is significant; $\delta \approx J/\sigma_y$.
> - **Threshold stress intensity:** Below $\Delta K_{th}$, crack does not grow (infinite life).
> - **Codes used in KTU context:** BS 7910, API 579-1/ASME FFS-1, ASME Section XI, R6.
> - **Application domains:** Aerospace (damage tolerance), nuclear (leak-before-break), offshore (corrosion fatigue), pipelines (hydrotest assessment).
> - **Always include the geometry factor Y** in SIF expressions — never omit it.
> - **Integrate**, don't just calculate — show how fracture mechanics connects to fatigue, corrosion, and limit-load analysis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Designing for Complex Loading Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET744 - FAILURE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Designing for Complex Loading Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Designing for Complex Loading Conditions** is the engineering discipline concerned with predicting component life and ensuring structural integrity when a machine element is simultaneously subjected to a combination of two or more fundamental load types — axial (tension/compression), bending (transverse), torsional (twisting), and transverse shear — which may further be steady, fluctuating, or fully reversed in nature.

Under the KTU 2024 scheme (aligned with NEP 2020 and Outcome-Based Education), this topic is governed by **Course Outcome CO4**: *"Apply combined loading and stress concentration principles to design machine elements against static and fatigue failure."*

The design process mathematically transforms the multi-axial, multi-component stress state into **equivalent scalar quantities** — equivalent stress ($\sigma_{eq}$), equivalent moment ($M_{eq}$), or equivalent torque ($T_{eq}$) — which are then compared against the material's allowable strength derived from a chosen **Failure Theory** (Ductile vs. Brittle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme explicitly combines static combined loading (using Mohr's circle, principal stresses, and maximum normal/shear stress theories) with fluctuating fatigue loading (using the Modified Goodman, Soderberg, and Gerber criteria). Both must be mastered for full marks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Multi-Task Worker"

Imagine a stationary bicycle mechanic holding a heavy rear wheel in place while simultaneously pushing the pedal forward and slightly tilting the frame sideways. The mechanic's wrist feels a **complex combination** of forces:

- The weight of the wheel pulling the hand downward (Axial tension).
- The push on the pedal twisting the wrist (Torsion).
- The sideways tilt bending the wrist sideways (Bending).

The mechanic's wrist is in a state of **complex stress** — just like a shaft in a gearbox transmitting power through a helical gear, where axial, bending, and torsional loads act *at the same time*.

**The Designer's Job:** Translate this messy, multi-directional stress cocktail into a single equivalent "feels-like-this-much" number, and then compare it to the strength of the material. If the number is lower, the wrist (or shaft) survives.

### 1.3 Why is Complex Loading Important?

| Simple Loading | Complex Loading (Real World) |
|---|---|
| Textbook shaft carrying only torque | Drive shaft of an automobile (torsion + bending due to weight) |
| Ideal pin in pure tension | Riveted/bolted joint with eccentric load (tension + bending) |
| Centrifugal force only | Rotating disc of a turbine (radial + thermal + centrifugal) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant:** The **Factor of Safety (FoS or $n$)** for static complex loading is typically taken as **$n = 2$ to $4$** for steady loads, and **$n = 2$ to $3$** for fluctuating loads (KTU standard for general machine design).

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Complex Loading Design

1. **Stress Analysis** — Compute the actual stresses (axial $\sigma_a$, bending $\sigma_b$, torsional $\tau_t$, shear $\tau_v$).
2. **Failure Theory Selection** — Combine stresses using a hypothesis (e.g., Maximum Shear Stress Theory, Distortion Energy Theory).
3. **Code Verification** — Compare the equivalent stress with allowable stress using ASME / Shigley / Khurmi conventions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematics of Combining Stresses

When a shaft of diameter $d$ transmits torque $T$ and is simultaneously subjected to a bending moment $M_b$ and an axial load $F$, the critical point on the cross-section experiences:

### Step-by-step Logical Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Axial Stress ($\sigma_a$):** Caused by direct tensile/compressive load.
- **Step 2 — Bending Stress ($\sigma_b$):** Linear distribution across the cross-section; maximum at the outermost fiber, zero at the neutral axis.
- **Step 3 — Torsional Shear Stress ($\tau_t$):** Acts tangentially on the cross-section; maximum at the outer surface.
- **Step 4 — Transverse Shear ($\tau_v$):** Usually negligible for solid shafts unless length-to-diameter ratio is very small ($L/d < 5$).
- **Step 5 — Transformation:** Because axial and bending are both normal stresses (acting on perpendicular planes), they **add algebraically**. Torsional shear must be combined vectorially with bending using the **principal stress** or **Mohr's circle** approach.

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every formula you MUST memorize for the KTU Board Exam on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Applicable When |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Axial Stress | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{4F}{\pi d^2}$ | MPa or N/mm² | Direct tensile/compressive load $F$ on shaft of dia. $d$ |
| 2 | Bending Stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 M_b}{\pi d^3}$ | MPa or N/mm² | Pure bending moment $M_b$ on solid circular shaft |
| 3 | Torsional Shear Stress | $\tau_t = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$ | MPa or N/mm² | Torque $T$ on solid circular shaft |
| 4 | Transverse Shear (max) | $\tau_v = \dfrac{4V}{3A}$ | MPa or N/mm² | Transverse load $V$; solid circular shaft |
| 5 | Section Modulus (Solid Shaft) | $Z = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{32}$ | mm³ | For bending calculations |
| 6 | Polar Section Modulus | $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}$ | mm³ | For torsion calculations |
| 7 | Equivalent Bending Moment (MSST) | $M_{eq} = \dfrac{M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}}{2}$ | N·mm | Maximum Shear Stress Theory (ductile) |
| 8 | Equivalent Torque (MSST) | $T_{eq} = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$ | N·mm | Maximum Shear Stress Theory (ductile) |
| 9 | Equivalent Bending Moment (DE) | $M_{eq} = \sqrt{M_b^2 + \dfrac{3}{4}T^2}$ | N·mm | Distortion Energy Theory (ductile, most accurate) |
| 10 | Equivalent Torque (DE) | $T_{eq} = \sqrt{M_b^2 + \dfrac{3}{4}T^2}$ | N·mm | Distortion Energy Theory |
| 11 | Principal Stresses | $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | MPa | For 2-D plane stress state |
| 12 | Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$ | MPa | Always positive |
| 13 | Von-Mises Stress | $\sigma_V = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 - \sigma_x \sigma_y + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$ | MPa | Distortion Energy equivalent for 2-D state |
| 14 | Factor of Safety (Static) | $n = \dfrac{S_{yt}}{\sigma_V}$ (DE) or $n = \dfrac{S_{yt}}{\sigma_1}$ (MNST) | — | Static yielding check |
| 15 | Modified Goodman | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_{se}} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | — | Fluctuating fatigue |
| 16 | Soderberg | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_{se}} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | — | Most conservative fatigue |
| 17 | Gerber | $\dfrac{n \sigma_a}{S_{se}} + \left(\dfrac{n \sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1$ | — | For ductile materials, less conservative than Goodman |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Glossary (Strict KTU notation):**
> $S_{yt}$ = Yield Strength in tension, $S_{ut}$ = Ultimate tensile strength, $S_{se}$ = Endurance limit (corrected), $\sigma_a$ = Alternating stress amplitude, $\sigma_m$ = Mean stress.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Crankshafts:** Subject to bending from combustion pressure and torsion from piston drive simultaneously — designed using the equivalent moment approach.
- **Pressure Vessels & Pipe Lines:** Combine hoop stress (axial direction) with longitudinal stress and torsional stress from connected fittings.
- **Helical Springs:** Carry both torsional shear and direct shear simultaneously on every cross-section.
- **Connecting Rods:** Experience fluctuating axial tension/compression — governed by Goodman/Soderberg criteria.
- **Welding Joints:** Carry combined residual tensile stresses, bending from plate misalignment, and shear from the weld throat — must be checked using principal stresses.

## 2.4 Failure Theory Decision Flowchart (KTU Standard)

```
START
  │
  ▼
Is the material ductile (e.g., mild steel, aluminium)?
  │
  ├── YES ──► Use DISTORTION ENERGY THEORY (Von-Mises)
  │            or MAXIMUM SHEAR STRESS THEORY (Tresca)
  │            Both are accepted; DE is preferred for general
  │            machine design in KTU.
  │
  └── NO (Brittle, e.g., cast iron, ceramic)
        │
        ▼
     Use MAXIMUM NORMAL STRESS THEORY (Rankine)
     or MOHR'S THEORY (modified for brittle)
```

## 2.5 Stress Concentration Factor ($K_t$)

In real machine elements, sudden changes in geometry (keyways, holes, fillets, threads) cause **localized stress amplification**.

$$\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{nominal}$$

$$\tau_{max} = K_t \cdot \tau_{nominal}$$

For fluctuating loads, this is further modified by the **Notch Sensitivity** ($q$) to obtain the **Fatigue Stress Concentration Factor**:

$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** When the problem statement does not mention $K_t$ explicitly, assume $K_t = 1$ (i.e., ignore stress concentration). When it does, apply it to nominal stresses before substituting into failure theory equations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Equivalent Bending Moment (Maximum Shear Stress Theory)

**Premise:** A shaft of diameter $d$ carries a bending moment $M_b$ and torque $T$. The critical point on the surface has normal stress $\sigma_x = \sigma_b$ (from bending) and shear stress $\tau_{xy} = \tau_t$ (from torsion). The other two stress components are zero: $\sigma_y = 0$, $\sigma_z = 0$.

**Step 1 — Write the stress state at the critical point.**
The surface fiber in tension has:
- $\sigma_x = \sigma_b = \dfrac{32 M_b}{\pi d^3}$
- $\sigma_y = 0$
- $\tau_{xy} = \tau_t = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$

**Step 2 — Find principal stresses using the standard formula.**
$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

Substituting $\sigma_y = 0$:
$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_b}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_t^2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca).**
According to Tresca, yielding begins when $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{S_{sy}}{n}$, where $S_{sy} \approx 0.5 \, S_{yt}$ for ductile materials.

The maximum shear stress in a 2-D stress state is:
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_t^2}$$

**Step 4 — Set up the failure equation.**
$$\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_t^2} = \frac{S_{sy}}{n} = \frac{S_{yt}}{2n}$$

Squaring both sides:
$$\frac{\sigma_b^2}{4} + \tau_t^2 = \left(\frac{S_{yt}}{2n}\right)^2 = \frac{S_{yt}^2}{4n^2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the section modulus relations.**
Recall:
$$\sigma_b = \frac{32 M_b}{\pi d^3} = \frac{M_b}{Z}$$
$$\tau_t = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{T}{Z_p}$$

With $Z_p = 2Z$:
$$\tau_t = \frac{T}{2Z}$$

Substituting:
$$\frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{M_b}{Z}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{T}{2Z}\right)^2 = \frac{S_{yt}^2}{4n^2}$$

$$\frac{M_b^2}{4Z^2} + \frac{T^2}{4Z^2} = \frac{S_{yt}^2}{4n^2}$$

$$\frac{M_b^2 + T^2}{4Z^2} = \frac{S_{yt}^2}{4n^2}$$

$$\sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2} = \frac{S_{yt} \cdot Z}{n}$$

**Step 6 — Define the equivalent torque for design simplicity.**
Let:
$$T_{eq} = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$$

Then the design equation reduces to the **pure torsion form** that students already know:
$$\frac{16 \, T_{eq}}{\pi d^3} = \frac{S_{yt}}{n}$$

This is the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory equivalent torque** for combined bending and torsion.

**Step 7 — Equivalent Bending Moment form (alternative).**
Sometimes designers prefer an equivalent bending moment. The maximum normal stress (principal) is:
$$\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_b}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_t^2}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator strategically gives:
$$\sigma_1 = \frac{1}{2}\left[\sigma_b + \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 4\tau_t^2}\right]$$

Substituting $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M_b}{Z}$ and $\tau_t = \dfrac{T}{2Z}$:
$$\sigma_1 = \frac{1}{2Z}\left[M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}\right] = \frac{M_{eq}}{Z}$$

where:
$$\boxed{M_{eq} = \frac{M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}}{2}}$$

And the design equation becomes:
$$\frac{M_{eq}}{Z} = \frac{S_{yt}}{n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{32 M_{eq}}{\pi d^3} = \frac{S_{yt}}{n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This elegant result is the foundation of **Shigley's Mechanical Engineering Design** and is the standard KTU answer format for combined loading problems using Tresca.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Equivalent Bending Moment (Distortion Energy Theory)

**Premise:** Same stress state. Von-Mises criterion states that yielding occurs when:
$$\sigma_V = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau_t^2} = \frac{S_{yt}}{n}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute section-modulus relations.**
$$\sqrt{\left(\frac{M_b}{Z}\right)^2 + 3\left(\frac{T}{2Z}\right)^2} = \frac{S_{yt}}{n}$$

$$\frac{1}{Z}\sqrt{M_b^2 + \frac{3}{4}T^2} = \frac{S_{yt}}{n}$$

**Step 2 — Define the DE-based equivalent moment.**
$$\boxed{M_{eq} = \sqrt{M_b^2 + \frac{3}{4}T^2}}$$

And the design equation is:
$$\frac{32 M_{eq}}{\pi d^3} = \frac{S_{yt}}{n}$$

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example (Board Exam Style)

### Problem (KTU University Exam - May 2024 style)

A shaft of diameter $60$ mm is subjected to a bending moment of $2$ kN·m and a torque of $4$ kN·m. The material has $S_{yt} = 320$ MPa. Determine the factor of safety using:
- (a) Maximum Shear Stress Theory.
- (b) Distortion Energy Theory.

**Given:**
$d = 60$ mm, $M_b = 2$ kN·m $= 2 \times 10^6$ N·mm, $T = 4$ kN·m $= 4 \times 10^6$ N·mm, $S_{yt} = 320$ MPa.

### Solution (Part a) — MSST

**Step 1:** Compute bending stress.
$$\sigma_b = \frac{32 M_b}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 2 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (60)^3} = \frac{64 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 216000}$$

$$\sigma_b = \frac{64 \times 10^6}{6.7858 \times 10^5} = 94.31 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Compute torsional shear stress.
$$\tau_t = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 4 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 216000} = \frac{64 \times 10^6}{6.7858 \times 10^5} = 94.31 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Maximum Shear Stress Theory.
$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_t^2} = \sqrt{(47.155)^2 + (94.31)^2}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{2223.6 + 8894.4} = \sqrt{11118.0} = 105.44 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4:** FoS using MSST.
$$n = \frac{S_{sy}}{\tau_{max}} = \frac{S_{yt}/2}{\tau_{max}} = \frac{160}{105.44} = 1.518$$

### Solution (Part b) — DE Theory

**Step 1:** Apply Von-Mises formula directly.
$$\sigma_V = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau_t^2} = \sqrt{(94.31)^2 + 3(94.31)^2}$$

$$\sigma_V = \sqrt{8894.4 + 26683.2} = \sqrt{35577.6} = 188.62 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** FoS using DE.
$$n = \frac{S_{yt}}{\sigma_V} = \frac{320}{188.62} = 1.696$$

### Inference

Since $n_{DE} > n_{MSST}$, the DE theory is **less conservative** and predicts more design capacity — consistent with theory.

---

## 3.4 Combined Fatigue Loading Derivation (Modified Goodman)

**Premise:** A rotating shaft carries a steady bending moment $M_m$ (mean) and a fluctuating bending moment $M_a$ (alternating), with a steady torque $T_m$.

**Step 1 — Mean and alternating stresses.**
$$\sigma_m = \frac{32 M_m}{\pi d^3}, \quad \sigma_a = \frac{32 M_a}{\pi d^3}, \quad \tau_m = \frac{16 T_m}{\pi d^3}$$

**Step 2 — Convert torque to equivalent bending stress.**
By the Von-Mises criterion, an equivalent fluctuating bending stress can be obtained:
$$\sigma_{eq,a} = \sqrt{\sigma_a^2 + 3\tau_a^2}, \quad \sigma_{eq,m} = \sqrt{\sigma_m^2 + 3\tau_m^2}$$

**Step 3 — Modified Goodman equation.**
$$\frac{\sigma_{eq,a}}{S_{se}} + \frac{\sigma_{eq,m}}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

Solving for $d$:
$$\frac{\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{32 M_a}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + 3\left(\dfrac{16 T_a}{\pi d^3}\right)^2}}{S_{se}} + \frac{\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{32 M_m}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + 3\left(\dfrac{16 T_m}{\pi d^3}\right)^2}}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for diameter cubed.**
$$d^3 = \frac{16 n}{\pi}\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sqrt{4M_a^2 + 3T_a^2}}{S_{se}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\sqrt{4M_m^2 + 3T_m^2}}{S_{ut}}\right)^2}$$

Or using $K_f$ for notch effects (Shigley's standard form):
$$\boxed{d = \left[\frac{16 n}{\pi}\left\{\frac{\sqrt{4(K_f M_a)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_a)^2}}{S_{se}} + \frac{\sqrt{4(K_f M_m)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_m)^2}}{S_{ut}}\right\}\right]^{1/3}}$$

where $K_{fs}$ is the fatigue stress concentration factor for shear.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Complex Loading Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Component receives loads F, M_b, T] --> B{Identify Load Types}
    B --> C1[Axial Force F]
    B --> C2[Bending Moment M_b]
    B --> C3[Torque T]
    B --> C4[Transverse Shear V]
    C1 --> D[Compute Individual Stresses]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> D1[sigma_a = 4F/pi d^2]
    D --> D2[sigma_b = 32M_b/pi d^3]
    D --> D3[tau_t = 16T/pi d^3]
    D --> D4[tau_v = 4V/3A]
    D1 --> E{Loading is Static or Fluctuating?}
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E -->|Static| F[Apply Failure Theory]
    E -->|Fluctuating| G[Apply Goodman/Soderberg/Gerber]
    F --> F1{Material Type?}
    F1 -->|Ductile| F2[MSST or DE Theory]
    F1 -->|Brittle| F3[Maximum Normal Stress Theory]
    F2 --> H[Compute Equivalent Stress sigma_V]
    F3 --> H
    G --> H2[Compute Equivalent Alternating and Mean Stresses]
    H2 --> I[Check n against Design n]
    H --> I
    I --> J{n >= Required FoS?}
    J -->|Yes| K[DESIGN SAFE]
    J -->|No| L[Increase d or change material]
    L --> D
```

## 4.2 Combined Loading State on a Shaft Cross-Section (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SC[Surface Stresses at Critical Point]
        direction LR
        X[Normal Stress sigma_x = sigma_a + sigma_b]
        Y[Shear Stress tau_xy = tau_t]
    end
    SC --> MOHR[Mohr's Circle Construction]
    MOHR --> PSTRESS[Principal Stresses sigma_1 and sigma_2]
    PSTRESS --> COMBINE{Failure Theory}
    COMBINE -->|Tresca MSST| TRESCA[tau_max = sqrt sigma_b^2/4 + tau_t^2]
    COMBINE -->|Von Mises DE| VM[sigma_V = sqrt sigma_b^2 + 3 tau_t^2]
    COMBINE -->|Rankine MNST| RNK[sigma_1 compared to S_yt/S_uc]
    TRESCA --> NEQ[Equivalent Torque T_eq or M_eq]
    VM --> NEQ
    RNK --> NEQ
    NEQ --> OUT[Compare with Allowable: n = S_y/ sigma_eq]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Stress Distribution on a Shaft Under Combined Loading

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SHAFT[Solid Circular Shaft Diameter d]
        LOAD1[Axial Force F acts along axis] --> SIG1[Produces uniform sigma_a = 4F/pi d^2]
        LOAD2[Bending Moment M_b acts transverse] --> SIG2[Produces linearly varying sigma_b: + at top, - at bottom]
        LOAD3[Torque T acts about axis] --> SIG3[Produces tau_t = 16T/pi d^3 at outer surface]
        SIG1 --> CRIT[Critical Point: TOP of Cross Section]
        SIG2 --> CRIT
        SIG3 --> CRIT
        CRIT --> EQ[State: sigma_x = sigma_a + sigma_b, tau_xy = tau_t]
    end
    EQ --> PRINCIPAL[Apply Principal Stress Formula]
```

## 4.4 The Big Picture — Failure Theory Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Complex Loading Problem] --> Q1{Is ductile material?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Fluctuating or Steady?}
    Q1 -->|No Brittle| R1[Use Rankine or Modified Mohr]
    Q2 -->|Steady| R2[Use DE Theory preferred, MSST conservative]
    Q2 -->|Fluctuating| R3[Use Modified Goodman with K_f and K_fs]
    R2 --> EQM[Compute M_eq or T_eq]
    R3 --> EQF[Compute mean and alternating equivalents]
    EQM --> FO1[Solve for d from sigma_eq = S_yt/n]
    EQF --> FO2[Solve for d from Goodman line equation]
    FO1 --> DONE[Final d]
    FO2 --> DONE
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (CO4, Understand) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "Equivalent Bending Moment" as applied to a shaft subjected to combined bending and torsion, using the Maximum Shear Stress Theory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Equivalent Bending Moment ($M_{eq}$) is a fictitious bending moment that, when applied alone to a shaft, produces the same maximum shear stress as the actual combined bending moment $M_b$ and torque $T$.
For **MSST**:
$$M_{eq} = \frac{M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}}{2}$$
This transformation allows the designer to use the standard bending equation $\dfrac{M_{eq}}{Z} = \dfrac{S_{yt}}{n}$ instead of solving complex combined stress equations. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for utility.)**

---

### Question 2 (CO4, Remember) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State any three failure theories used in the design of machine elements subjected to combined loading.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine):** Suitable for brittle materials; failure occurs when $\sigma_1 \geq S_{ut}$ or $\sigma_2 \leq -S_{uc}$.
2. **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca):** Suitable for ductile materials; failure occurs when $\tau_{max} \geq S_{sy} \approx 0.5 S_{yt}$.
3. **Distortion Energy Theory (Von-Mises):** Most accurate for ductile materials; failure occurs when $\sigma_V \geq S_{yt}$.
*(Alternative: Modified Mohr Theory for brittle, Goodman for fatigue.)*

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

A shaft is supported between two bearings and carries a gear at its midpoint. The gear exerts a tangential force of $5$ kN at a radius of $150$ mm and a radial force of $2$ kN. The shaft is made of steel with $S_{yt} = 380$ MPa and $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa. Using a factor of safety of $2$, design the shaft diameter using:
- **(a) Maximum Shear Stress Theory (7 marks)**
- **(b) Distortion Energy Theory (7 marks)**

Assume the gear acts as a cantilever between the bearings.

**Given Data:**
$F_t = 5$ kN (tangential), $F_r = 2$ kN (radial), $R = 150$ mm.
$S_{yt} = 380$ MPa, $n = 2$.
$Bearing\ span = 250\ mm$ (assume; if not given, KTU assumes simply supported with central load, taking $M_b$ at midpoint).

### Solution A(a) — MSST Approach

**Step 1 — Compute loads and moments.**
- Tangential force creates torque: $T = F_t \times R = 5000 \times 150 = 750{,}000$ N·mm $= 0.75 \times 10^6$ N·mm.
- Bending moment from tangential force (vertical): $M_1 = F_t \times L/4 = 5000 \times 250/4 = 312{,}500$ N·mm.
- Bending moment from radial force (horizontal): $M_2 = F_r \times L/4 = 2000 \times 250/4 = 125{,}000$ N·mm.
- Resultant bending moment:
$$M_b = \sqrt{M_1^2 + M_2^2} = \sqrt{(312500)^2 + (125000)^2}$$
$$M_b = \sqrt{9.766 \times 10^{10} + 1.5625 \times 10^{10}} = \sqrt{1.133 \times 10^{11}} = 3.366 \times 10^5 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute equivalent moment (MSST).**
$$M_{eq} = \frac{M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}}{2} = \frac{3.366 \times 10^5 + \sqrt{(3.366 \times 10^5)^2 + (7.5 \times 10^5)^2}}{2}$$
$$= \frac{3.366 \times 10^5 + \sqrt{1.133 \times 10^{11} + 5.625 \times 10^{11}}}{2}$$
$$= \frac{3.366 \times 10^5 + \sqrt{6.758 \times 10^{11}}}{2} = \frac{3.366 \times 10^5 + 8.221 \times 10^5}{2}$$
$$M_{eq} = 5.794 \times 10^5 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for diameter.**
$$\frac{32 M_{eq}}{\pi d^3} = \frac{S_{yt}}{n} \Rightarrow d^3 = \frac{32 n M_{eq}}{\pi S_{yt}}$$
$$d^3 = \frac{32 \times 2 \times 5.794 \times 10^5}{\pi \times 380} = \frac{3.708 \times 10^7}{1193.81} = 3.106 \times 10^4$$
$$d = \sqrt[3]{3.106 \times 10^4} = 31.5 \text{ mm} \approx 32 \text{ mm}$$

**Final diameter by MSST: $d \approx 32$ mm.**

### Solution A(b) — DE Theory Approach

**Step 1 — Compute equivalent moment (DE).**
$$M_{eq, DE} = \sqrt{M_b^2 + \frac{3}{4}T^2} = \sqrt{(3.366 \times 10^5)^2 + 0.75 \times (7.5 \times 10^5)^2}$$
$$= \sqrt{1.133 \times 10^{11} + 0.75 \times 5.625 \times 10^{11}} = \sqrt{1.133 \times 10^{11} + 4.219 \times 10^{11}}$$
$$= \sqrt{5.352 \times 10^{11}} = 7.316 \times 10^5 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for diameter.**
$$d^3 = \frac{32 \times 2 \times 7.316 \times 10^5}{\pi \times 380} = \frac{4.682 \times 10^7}{1193.81} = 3.922 \times 10^4$$
$$d = \sqrt[3]{3.922 \times 10^4} = 34.05 \text{ mm} \approx 35 \text{ mm}$$

**Final diameter by DE: $d \approx 35$ mm.**

> [!NOTE]
> The DE theory gives a larger required diameter than MSST in this case (because here $T > M_b$). In some configurations MSST may give a larger value. Always check both and adopt the larger conservative diameter.

### Question B (14 Marks Alternative) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

A rotating shaft is subjected to a completely reversed bending moment of $1.2$ kN·m and a steady torque of $2$ kN·m. The material has $S_{ut} = 650$ MPa, $S_{yt} = 450$ MPa, and endurance limit $S_e = 300$ MPa. The stress concentration factors are $K_t = 2.0$ (bending) and $K_{ts} = 1.5$ (torsion). The notch sensitivity for both is $q = 0.85$.
- **(a) Compute the corrected fatigue stress concentration factors $K_f$ and $K_{fs}$. (4 marks)**
- **(b) Design the shaft diameter using the Modified Goodman criterion with a factor of safety of $2$. (10 marks)**

### Solution B(a) — Fatigue Stress Concentration Factors (4 marks)

$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.85(2.0 - 1) = 1 + 0.85 = 1.85$$

$$K_{fs} = 1 + q(K_{ts} - 1) = 1 + 0.85(1.5 - 1) = 1 + 0.425 = 1.425$$

### Solution B(b) — Modified Goodman Design (10 marks)

**Given:** $M_a = 1.2$ kN·m $= 1.2 \times 10^6$ N·mm, $T_m = 2$ kN·m $= 2 \times 10^6$ N·mm, $M_m = 0$, $T_a = 0$.

**Step 1 — Mean and alternating stresses (Von-Mises form).**

Since torque is steady, $\tau_a = 0$ and $\sigma_m = 0$.
- Alternating normal stress (bending): $\sigma_a = \dfrac{32 K_f M_a}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{32 \times 1.85 \times 1.2 \times 10^6}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{2.261 \times 10^7}{\pi d^3}$
- Mean shear stress (torsion): $\tau_m = \dfrac{16 K_{fs} T_m}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{16 \times 1.425 \times 2 \times 10^6}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{4.56 \times 10^7}{\pi d^3}$

**Step 2 — Equivalent alternating and mean stresses.**
$$\sigma_{eq,a} = \sqrt{\sigma_a^2 + 3\tau_a^2} = \sigma_a = \frac{2.261 \times 10^7}{\pi d^3}$$
$$\sigma_{eq,m} = \sqrt{\sigma_m^2 + 3\tau_m^2} = \sqrt{3}\tau_m = \frac{\sqrt{3} \times 4.56 \times 10^7}{\pi d^3} = \frac{7.897 \times 10^7}{\pi d^3}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Modified Goodman.**
$$\frac{\sigma_{eq,a}}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_{eq,m}}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}$$
$$\frac{2.261 \times 10^7}{\pi d^3 \times 300} + \frac{7.897 \times 10^7}{\pi d^3 \times 650} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\frac{1}{\pi d^3}\left[\frac{2.261 \times 10^7}{300} + \frac{7.897 \times 10^7}{650}\right] = 0.5$$

$$\frac{1}{\pi d^3}\left[7.537 \times 10^4 + 1.215 \times 10^5\right] = 0.5$$

$$\frac{1.969 \times 10^5}{\pi d^3} = 0.5$$

$$d^3 = \frac{1.969 \times 10^5}{0.5 \pi} = \frac{1.969 \times 10^5}{1.5708} = 1.253 \times 10^5$$

$$d = \sqrt[3]{1.253 \times 10^5} = 50.07 \text{ mm} \approx 50 \text{ mm}$$

**Final diameter: $d = 50$ mm.**

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Read Carefully!)**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kN·m to N·mm** — a single unit error gives wildly wrong diameter and costs **3–4 marks** instantly. Always write units in every step.
> 2. **Using $S_{ut}$ instead of $S_{yt}$** in static failure theory equations (e.g., $\sigma_V = S_{ut}/n$ is WRONG — it should be $S_{yt}/n$).
> 3. **Mixing MSST and DE formulas** — never use $M_{eq, DE}$ in MSST equation or vice-versa.
> 4. **Ignoring stress concentration factors** — when $K_t$ is given, the marks are reserved for its application. Skipping it is a guaranteed **2-mark loss**.
> 5. **Not drawing Mohr's circle or free-body diagram** — KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** for visualization. Even a rough sketch adds value.
> 6. **Assuming simply supported when the problem says cantilever** — these give different bending moment distributions ($M = WL/4$ vs $M = WL$).
> 7. **Treating fluctuating loads with static theory** — if the load says "completely reversed" or "fluctuating", use **Goodman/Soderberg**, not static MSST/DE.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Combined loading** = simultaneous axial + bending + torsion + shear.
- [x] Bending stress $\sigma_b = 32 M_b / (\pi d^3)$ acts **normally**; Torsional shear $\tau_t = 16 T / (\pi d^3)$ acts **tangentially**.
- [x] Critical point is on the outer surface where $\sigma_b$ is maximum and $\tau_t$ is maximum.
- [x] **MSST** gives equivalent torque $T_{eq} = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$.
- [x] **DE theory** gives equivalent moment $M_{eq} = \sqrt{M_b^2 + 0.75 T^2}$.
- [x] **Modified Goodman** is the industry standard for fluctuating fatigue.
- [x] **Stress concentration** $K_t$ applies to nominal stress; fatigue uses $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$.
- [x] For ductile materials → DE theory (preferred) or MSST.
- [x] For brittle materials → Rankine / Modified Mohr.
- [x] Typical FoS: $n = 2$–$4$ static, $n = 2$–$3$ fatigue.
- [x] Always **convert units first** before substitution.
- [x] Final diameter must be rounded **up** to the next standard size (e.g., 30, 32, 35, 40, 45, 50 mm).
- [x] In KTU Board Exam, **draw a free-body diagram** and **state the failure theory used** — both attract marks.
- [x] Modified Goodman gives the **most conservative** answer among fatigue criteria; Soderberg is even more conservative.
- [x] Gerber is the **least conservative** and is used when minor permanent deformation is acceptable.
